heading: Framework Library
content:
BELCONNEN UNITED FC
TECHNICAL DEPARTMENT
——————————————
FOOTBALL FRAMEWORK LIBRARY
——————————————
2025 Edition | Aligned with European Football Leaders
——————————————
Prepared by:
Scott Conlon – Technical Director
Belconnen United FC
V2025.01

heading: 1. Introduction
headingPath: 1. Introduction
content:
The Belco Football Framework Library is a collection of one-page resources that define and simplify the key principles of play across the Belconnen United program.
Each framework translates a core tactical idea into clear, coachable language – outlining what the principle means, why it matters, and how it appears on the pitch.
The library complements the Belco Curriculum and Phase Handbooks, ensuring that coaches across every age phase use consistent terminology and training language.

heading: 2.1 Open Ball
headingPath: 2. In Possession Frameworks > 2.1 Open Ball
content:
(Internal – Coach Reference)
Definition
• An Open-Ball = when the ball carrier has time, space, and a forward-facing body shape — able to see the game and make a decisive action.• The Belco Way: Open Ball means go. It’s the cue for the entire team to shift from circulation to penetration — runs, bravery, and intent.• This is the cornerstone of Belco football — everything we build (compactness, overloads, tempo control) is designed to create the open-ball moment.
Why It Matters
• It’s the trigger that turns control into threat — when we see it, we go forward with purpose.• Drives our attacking identity: proactive, forward-thinking, and brave.• Connects all Belco principles: overloads create it, tempo control decides when to use it, compactness supports it.• Builds a shared understanding — everyone recognises the same cue and reacts together.• Teaches courage — the player in the open-ball moment must commit to the action, not hide from it.
Core Coaching Messages
Phase | Message
Kids (10–13) | “If your teammate’s got space — run forward!”
Tactical (14–15) | “Open Ball = attack mode — forward runs, combinations, bravery.”
Professional (16+) | “An open-ball moment decides the game — see it, commit, and penetrate.”
Team Actions on Open-Ball
When the open-ball appears, the whole team must come alive:
1. Forward Runs (Blind-Side Threat)
- Strikers and attacking mids run in behind or between defenders — especially on the blind side.
- The earlier the run, the harder it is to track.🔹 Belco Link: Overloads and Tempo Control — timing is everything.
2. Penetrating Action by the Ball Carrier
- Drive forward, play in behind, or find the third man — never safe, always progressive.
- If the lane opens, break it.
- Encourage players to take the moment — this is where goals start.
3. Support Underneath
- Provide the bounce option to recycle if the forward lane closes.
- Keep compact behind to secure balance and allow immediate counter-press if lost.
4. Wide Expansion
- Fullbacks and wingers stretch horizontally — open inside lanes for the carrier.
- Creates 2v1s or diagonal passing angles from the half-space.
5. Third-Man Play
- The trigger action: pass → set → forward run → through.
- Overload structure often creates this — the open-ball is the release point.
Out of Possession Relevance
• When the opponent achieves an open-ball:
- First defender must close immediately to delay their decision.
- Backline drops or adjusts diagonally to protect the space behind.
- Compactness is restored before the next pass.• The defensive goal: deny open-ball moments, exploit our own.• Belco teams defend to control tempo — never allow the opponent to play forward comfortably.
Coach Standards
• The open-ball moment must be highlighted in every session — it’s our ignition point.• Coach must constantly ask: Did we recognise it? Did we act?• Reward bravery — even if the action fails, the intent is right.• Freeze play to reinforce:
- “That’s your open ball — what now?”
- “Who’s running off the blind side?”• Players must know their roles and runs when it appears — automatic, not reactive.• Non-negotiable: when we see it, we go forward with purpose.
Session Examples
1. Youth (10–13) – Open-Ball Reaction Game
- Setup: 4v4 + 3 neutrals, central zone.
- Rule: when a neutral receives facing forward (open-ball), attackers must sprint beyond line immediately.
- Coaching Focus: recognition of cue, timing of forward runs, bravery to play through.
2. Tactical (14–15) – 6v6 Positional Game: Open-Ball Trigger to Break
- Setup: 3 zones — back, middle, front.
- Condition: once a player receives facing forward in middle zone, next pass must target penetration (in behind or third man).
- Coaching Detail: open body shape, scanning before touch, immediate forward movement.
- Progression: add scoring zones behind defensive line.
3. Professional (16+) – 11v11 Phase Play: Open-Ball Recognition
- Focus: middle third transitions — spotting and exploiting open-ball cues.
- Freeze Detail: check spacing, runs, and decision speed.
- Objective: turn open-ball moments into penetration within 3–5 seconds.
- Measure: successful penetrations per open-ball occurrence.
Summary
The Open Ball is the heartbeat of Belco football.It’s what we build for, it’s what triggers our runs, and it’s where our bravery shows.We don’t play safe when it appears — we commit, attack, and change the game.
Recognise it. React together. Be brave enough to use it.Open Ball = Go.

heading: 2.2 Overloads
headingPath: 2. In Possession Frameworks > 2.2 Overloads
content:
(Internal – Coach Reference)
Definition
• An overload = creating a numerical advantage (2v1, 3v2, etc.) in a specific zone of the pitch by advancing both the ball and a player into the next third.• The Belco Way: We don’t just move the ball forward — we move people with it. To overload, someone must join the play, not just pass and watch.
Why It Matters
• Defines how we progress — we don’t rely on long passes; we build numbers where the ball goes.• Makes our play dynamic and connected — we create superiority by movement, not just position.• Reinforces Belco principles: Overloads, Open Ball, Compactness, Tempo Control.• Develops game intelligence — players must read when and how to bring an extra man.• Croatian influence: attacking in waves — each action carries both the ball and support.
Core Coaching Messages
Phase | Message
Kids (10–13) | “Take the ball and a friend!”
Tactical (14–15) | “Don’t just pass it — join it. The overload moves with the ball.”
Professional (16+) | “Superiority is created when a player travels with the ball or supports the next line.”
How Overloads Are Created
An overload doesn’t exist by standing still — it’s earned through coordinated actions.
1. Carry (Dribble Progression)
- A player drives forward with the ball and body, taking a defender and creating a new number up in the next third.
- Example: CB steps into midfield, drawing a forward, creating 3v2 with CM + AM.🔹 Belco Link: Open Ball → Carry Forward → Compact Support.
2. Forward Run to Receive (Penetration Support)
- A player without the ball runs beyond the line and receives from the previous third.
- Brings a new body and angle to combine — now we’ve carried the overload forward.🔹 Belco Link: Compactness + Tempo Control.
3. Third-Man Run (Bounce and Go)
- A player passes, another sets (bounce), third man runs beyond to receive — the overload travels.
- This rotation drags defenders and shifts numbers in our favour.🔹 Belco Link: Overload → Rotation → Break Line.
4. One-Two Combination (Wall Pass Progression)
- A classic 2v1 solution: short give-and-go to beat a man and carry both ball + player forward.
- Turns static possession into dynamic superiority.🔹 Belco Link: Compactness and Timing of Support.
In Possession (By Thirds)
Build-Up (Back → Mid)
- Overload through carry or support run — CB or FB steps in, CM joins, creating a 3v2.
- If the ball moves and we don’t — no overload created.
Middle Third
- Use third-man actions, rotations, and wall passes to advance with a player.
- Objective: transport the overload from one zone to the next.
Final Third
- Overloads become finishing combinations: 2v1 wide, bounce + cut-back, late arriving midfielders.
- Key: someone must arrive, not wait for the ball to come back.
Out of Possession (Mirror Principle)
• When facing overloads, stay calm, delay, and recover shape.• Nearest player engages, others compact to protect inside lanes.• Teaches composure — we can’t always match numbers, but we can control angles and space.
Coach Standards
• Non-negotiable: every forward progression must include a supporting run — player + ball together.• Reinforce recognition: “Did we take a player with the ball?”• Praise third-man runs, carries, and wall passes as active overload creation.• Design sessions that require physical joining of play to move the overload forward.• Belco players must understand: superiority is never static — it travels.
Session Examples
1. Youth (10–13) – Carry & Combine 3v2
- Setup: 3 zones, 3v2 in middle → one player must dribble into final zone before passing.
- Rule: ball can’t go forward alone — must be carried or supported by a runner.
- Coaching Focus: awareness of when to go, when to support, how to arrive.
2. Tactical (14–15) – 7v7 Progression Game (Build Overload)
- Setup: half-pitch divided into thirds.
- Condition: team must carry the overload into next third (via dribble, run, or bounce-pass).
- Coaching Points:
- Read open-ball cues.
- Travel with possession.
- Trigger runs off bounce passes.
- Objective: deliberate overload creation through movement and connection.
3. Professional (16+) – 11v11 Phase Play: Moving Overloads
- Focus: tracking overload creation across thirds.
- Condition: goal only counts if the overload moves forward with both player + ball.
- Freeze Detail: “Did we bring someone with it?”
- Measure: number of successful overload carries and third-man entries per game.
Summary
Overloads are not given — they’re created by players who travel with the game.When we move the ball and a player into the next third, we control rhythm, create superiority, and open the pitch.That’s the Belco way — connected, progressive, and intelligent football.
Take the ball. Take a teammate. Take control.

heading: 2.3 Drive–Draw–Play
headingPath: 2. In Possession Frameworks > 2.3 Drive–Draw–Play
content:
(Internal – Coach Reference)
Definition
- Drive–Draw–Play is a simple coaching model that helps players understand how to progress the ball with purpose.
- It’s not a Belco “pillar,” but a teaching lens — a way to coach the detail behind breaking lines and creating advantages.
- Drive = carry the ball forward with intent.
- Draw = attract pressure, make the defender commit.
- Play = release at the right moment — to free the extra player or continue through yourself.
🔹 The Belco Way: We don’t pass for possession’s sake; we pass because we’ve created pressure and exploited it.
Why It Matters
- Turns ball mastery into meaningful actions — every touch has purpose.
- Connects directly to Overloads and Open Ball:
- Drive creates the open-ball.
- Draw creates the overload.
- Play is the release that breaks the line.
- Gives coaches and players a clear, repeatable teaching cycle: don’t just pass — drive, draw, then play.
- Builds understanding of timing — the key to penetration and clean combinations.
Timing Principle: The Art of Release
- Too Early: defender can recover to the receiver — advantage wasted.
- Too Late: defender blocks the passing lane — attack killed.
- Just Right: the defender commits, the pass is slipped, and the receiver is free.
- If the defender doesn’t commit, the carrier keeps driving to the next line.
🔹 Belco coaching cue: “Make them commit before you play — or punish them if they don’t.”
Core Coaching Messages
Phase | Message
Kids (10–13) | “Run at them, make them come, then pass.”
Tactical (14–15) | “Drive to commit, draw the defender, play to the free man.”
Professional (16+) | “Fix the opponent with your carry — release with perfect timing or keep going.”
Applications
In Possession
- Centre Backs: drive into open space to commit the striker — creates 3v2 or opens lane into midfield.
- Midfielders: drive toward the next line to pull one defender and open the next pass.
- Forwards: drive at centre-backs to drag them, then slip a runner in behind.
- Wingers: drive inside to draw fullback + centre-back, release fullback on the overlap.
🔹 Belco Links: Overloads, Open Ball, Tempo Control.
Out of Possession
• Pressing and duels follow the same logic:
- Drive the opponent into a set zone.
- Draw them into a trap (deny options).
- Play out quickly once possession is regained.
🔹 Belco Links: Pressing and Duel Mentality.
Coach Standards
- Encourage players to carry — don’t pass early out of fear.
- Freeze and ask: “Did you make them commit before you played?”
- Praise correct timing — not just outcome.
- Reinforce that driving doesn’t mean dribbling for show; it’s purposeful movement to fix an opponent.
- Constantly connect it back to identity: Drive–Draw–Play = Overload → Open Ball → Penetration.
Session Examples
1. Youth (10–13) – Drive–Draw–Play Mini-Game
- Setup: 3v3 + small goals.
- Rule: goals only count if they come from a drive–draw–play sequence.
- Coaching Detail:
- Encourage carriers to face forward and commit.
- Reward players who fix an opponent before passing.
2. Tactical (14–15) – 6v6 in Zones (Breaking the Line)
- Setup: middle and final third split.
- Condition: ball must be driven past the first line before a forward pass is allowed.
- Coaching Points: timing of pass, recognition of when defender commits, movement of supporting player.
- Progression: add 8s or 10s making third-man runs through.
3. Professional (16+) – Pattern Play: Build and Break
- Sequence: CB drives → CM receives → draws AM → plays forward to striker run.
- Focus: timing of each pass and trigger recognition.
- Coach Detail: “Did you play too early? Too late? Or perfectly when they bit?”
Summary
Drive–Draw–Play is how we teach players to turn space and pressure into opportunity.It’s not a tactic; it’s a way of thinking — fix before you pass, commit before you release.When the timing is right, the opponent is broken, and the line is gone.
Drive with intent. Draw their commitment. Play with perfect timing.

heading: 2.4 Building Through the Thirds
headingPath: 2. In Possession Frameworks > 2.4 Building Through the Thirds
content:
(Internal – Coach Reference)
Definition
• Building through the thirds = progressing the ball from back → middle → front using purposeful movement, combinations, and tempo control.• The Belco Way: We don’t skip the game — we learn to play through it. Every line, every phase, every player matters in the build.
Why It Matters
• Creates complete, intelligent players who can manage rhythm, not just play long.• Teaches possession with intent — every pass has a purpose: to move the opponent, not just the ball.• Embeds Belco principles: Tempo Control, Overloads, Open Ball, Compactness.• Makes us unpredictable: we can slow the game to draw pressure or break lines at full speed when the trigger appears.• Croatian Influence: structured play through all thirds builds calmness under pressure — they trust the ball to solve the problem.
Core Coaching Messages
Phase | Message
Kids (10–13) | “Back → Mid → Front — no skipping.”
Tactical (14–15) | “Use the middle man — find the extra player.”
Professional (16+) | “Break lines with control — arrive in the next third ready to play forward.”
In Possession
Back Third
- Build calmly with CBs, FBs, and GK — create overloads to invite pressure.
- Focus on angles and body shape — see the next pass before you receive.
- Avoid panic clearances — reset to play, not just to survive.🔹 Belco Links: Compactness (support lines), Open Ball (GK/CB triggers).
Middle Third
- The “decision zone” — midfielders connect lines, set tempo, and break pressure.
- Use bounce passes and third-man runs through the half-spaces.
- Recognise open-ball triggers: when the pass travels forward, we move to support forward.🔹 Belco Links: Overloads and Tempo Control.
Front Third
- Combine quickly — arrive, don’t wait.
- Timing of movements (9s, 10s, wingers) creates the difference between chance and chaos.
- Focus on cut-backs, late box arrivals, and third-man finishes.🔹 Belco Links: Open Ball → Penetration → Finish.
Out of Possession (Mirror Pressing Structure)
• Front Third Press: trigger off their first pass — press forward to kill their build.• Middle Third Block: stay compact, set traps, and control tempo — don’t chase, contain then pounce.• Defensive Third Compactness: narrow shape, force play wide, and prepare to transition fast once recovered.🔹 Belco Links: Compactness and Tempo Control.
Coach Standards
• Non-negotiable: we never skip the middle third — progression, not bypass.• Training sessions must reflect build-up phases — one third flows into the next.• Praise recognition: players who reset, switch, or bounce to build rhythm.• Encourage patience in the build, aggression after control.• Every Belco player must be comfortable receiving and playing in any third.• Analyse goals both ways — where did the build start, and what broke the line?
Session Examples
1. Youth (10–13) – 3-Zone Progression Game
- Setup: 5v5 in a 30x20m field divided into thirds.
- Condition: must pass through each zone before scoring.
- Coaching Detail: use support angles, move with the ball, play forward when safe.
- Focus: connection, patience, and awareness of spacing between lines.
2. Tactical (14–15) – 7v7 Build and Break
- Setup: half-pitch divided into thirds.
- Condition: team must play through the pivot (6 or 8) before entering final third.
- Coaching Focus: open body shape, timing of support, use of bounce passes to break the press.
- Objective: structured progression with rhythm changes.
3. Professional (16+) – 11v11 Phase Play – Line-Break Challenge
- Condition: team must break at least one line before entering next third.
- Coach Freeze: assess spacing, movement triggers, and support distances.
- Progression: add pressing opposition for realistic tempo control.
- Objective: link compact build-up with dynamic forward play.
Summary
Building Through the Thirds is how Belco teams learn to own the rhythm of the game.It develops calmness in build, clarity in connection, and conviction in penetration.It’s not just about passing — it’s about moving the opponent until the game opens.
Build with purpose. Play through, not around. Control the tempo, then break it.

heading: 2.5 Cut Backs / Assist Zone
headingPath: 2. In Possession Frameworks > 2.5 Cut Backs / Assist Zone
content:
(Internal – Coach Reference)
Definition
• The Assist Zone = the high-probability finishing area between the edge of the 6-yard box and the penalty spot — reached through wide or central penetration and finished with a cut-back, not a cross.• The Belco Way: We don’t cross and hope — we drive and deliver back with purpose.When we can’t finish directly, we attack the byline and cut back into the zone where defenders can’t recover.
Why We Use It
• Statistically superior: crosses are low-percentage; cut-backs produce clear, controlled finishes.• Tactically smart: defenders and goalkeepers are moving toward their own goal, leaving the zone unprotected.• Aligned with Belco DNA: purposeful tempo, open-ball triggers, forward runs, and second-line arrivals.• Represents our identity: intelligent, calculated attacking — we create, not hope.• In 2025 season — this framework directly underpinned our record goal-scoring year.
Core Coaching Messages
Phase | Message
Kids (10–13) | “Run to the line, pass it back!”
Tactical (14–15) | “Arrive in the assist zone — second line finishes.”
Professional (16+) | “Assist Zone = gold. Cut-backs decide matches.”
In Possession Cues
1. Wide Overload → Penetration → Cut-Back
- Fullback + winger combine to isolate the defender.
- Beat or bypass them (dribble or one-two) and drive into the byline.
- Deliver backwards, not square — ball angle around the penalty spot.🔹 Belco Link: Overloads + Open Ball (bravery to penetrate).
2. Midfield Penetration → Slip Pass to Runner
- Midfielder plays straight ball for diagonal run behind the line — easier to reach byline at speed.
- The runner doesn’t need to beat the defender; just get level with the goal line.
- First thought: can we go to goal? If not — cut back.
3. Open-Ball Trigger → Weak-Side Activation
- When the ball carrier is open and facing forward, weak-side forward runs blind-side into far-post cut-back lane.
- Arrive, don’t wait — timing beats defenders.
Out of Possession Impact
• Forces opponents to defend facing their own goal — hardest scenario to clear or track runners.• Compact teams stretched backwards — recovery delayed, lines broken.• Creates repeatable second-line finishing opportunities even when first pass doesn’t score.• Defensive rebound always favours us — we’re running onto it, they’re reacting to it.
Coach Standards
• Non-negotiable Belco principle: Our attacks target the Assist Zone.• Every finishing practice must include cut-back scenarios — from dribble, combination, or run in behind.• Wide players judged on cut-backs created, not crosses attempted.• Midfielders and forwards judged on timing and arrival into the zone.• Coach feedback:
- “Did we attack the byline?”
- “Was someone ready in the zone?”
- “Did we cut back with purpose?”
Session Examples
1. Youth (10–13) – Wide Channel Finishing
- Setup: 4v4 + GKs with wide zones.
- Condition: goals only count from cut-backs delivered from the assist zone.
- Focus: timing of run, awareness of delivery angle, second-line arrival.
2. Tactical (14–15) – Pattern Play: Straight Pass → Diagonal Run → Cut-Back
- Sequence:
- Midfielder plays straight pass behind fullback.
- Winger runs diagonally onto it → drives to byline → cut-back to arriving 10 or 9.
- Coaching Detail:
- No float crosses.
- Runners time from edge of box; late arrivals = best finishes.
3. Professional (16+) – Conditioned 8v8 or 11v11: Assist Zone Target Game
- Rule: goals only count if final pass comes from assist zone.
- Coach Focus: team shape to create entry, second-line movement, and box occupation.
- Measure: number of entries into assist zone per game; conversion rate from cut-backs.
4. End-Game Scenario – Final 10-Minute Repetition Block
- Condition: every goal must come from a cut-back.
- Objective: instil habit — “drive, don’t cross.”
- Outcome: automatic understanding of how to reach and exploit the zone.
Summary
The Assist Zone defines our attacking identity — no hopeful crosses, no wasted entries.We drive, deliver, and arrive.Every overload and open-ball moment is built to get someone to the byline and create the high-percentage chance.It’s calculated, rehearsed, and devastating when done together.
Get there. Cut it back. Finish clean.That’s Belco Football.

heading: 2.6 Level and Outside
headingPath: 2. In Possession Frameworks > 2.6 Level and Outside
content:
(Internal – Coach Reference)
Definition
• Level and Outside = positioning yourself on the same horizontal line as your direct defender, but just outside their shoulder line — not behind, not inside.• The Belco Way: we stand where they don’t want to look — outside their vision, ready to receive, face forward, and break lines.
Why It Matters
• Creates a blind spot — the defender can’t see you and the ball at the same time.• Draws defenders wide, opening the inside passing lane for the teammate or the bounce.• Triggers forward movement — being “outside” gives you a natural position to move forward (off the ball) to make the third-man run.• Improves first-touch efficiency — you receive with your far foot, away from pressure, facing forward.• Builds unpredictability — forces the defender to constantly adjust body shape and reference points.🔹 Belco DNA link: Open Ball, Overloads, and Tempo Control.
Core Coaching Messages
Phase | Message
Kids (10–13) | “Stand level and just outside your defender — see the ball and the goal.”
Tactical (14–15) | “Be outside their shoulder — that’s your space to receive or run.”
Professional (16+) | “Stay in their blind spot — your position shapes their body, your timing kills their press.”
In Possession Link
• Being level keeps you connected for combination play.• Being outside opens your body to receive forward, play around, or go beyond.• Sets up third-man actions: pass → set → spin inside the line you just created.• Enables “possession with penetration” — we can play around and through because the defender must turn to find you.
Out of Possession
• Once the ball is lost, the player who was “outside” tucks back in line quickly — restore compactness.• Opposite winger or 8 narrows to protect the opened lane.• Use same body shape in pressing: step “outside” their receiving line to block inside options.
Coach Standards
• Non-negotiable: never stand directly in front of your defender.• Check body shape — half-open stance, far foot ready to receive or accelerate.• Reinforce scanning: know both your defender and the ball before the pass arrives.• Praise players for timing their movement — arrive level and outside as the ball travels, not before.• Encourage manipulation: small feints inside before darting outside to receive.
Session Examples
- Youth (10–13) – Outside Shoulder Game (3v3 + 3 neutrals)
- Setup: 20x20m grid. Attackers earn double points when receiving outside the shoulder of their marker.
- Coaching Points:
- Arrive level as the pass travels.
- Receive with far foot.
- Quick first touch forward.
- Defender can’t see both you and ball — use that to create space.
- Tactical (14–15) – Positional Game: 6v6 + 2 Targets (Third-Man Focus)
- Shape: midfield zone with two target players behind defensive line.
- Condition: Target must start “outside” their defender and time movement to meet the bounce pass.
- Coaching Detail: open body, check shoulder, first step forward — timing > speed.
- Objective: practise level/outside reception → layoff → run through channel.
- Professional (16+) – 11v11 Phase Play (Build-to-Break)
- Focus: wide players and attacking mids working “level and outside” full-backs/CBs to open inside lanes.
- Coach Freeze: check visual reference — are they in line and outside? Can the defender see both?
- Rehearse third-man timing: ball in → bounce → spin → through.

heading: 2.7 Control the Tempo
headingPath: 2. In Possession Frameworks > 2.7 Control the Tempo
content:
(Internal – Coach Reference)
Definition
• Controlling the tempo = deciding when to slow and when to accelerate — in both possession and out of possession.• The Belco Way: We set the rhythm. Whether we’re building, pressing, or protecting a lead — the opponent never dictates our speed.
Why It Matters
• Gives us control of the game, even against faster or stronger teams.• Connects all Belco principles: Building Through Thirds (progression), Overloads (superiority), and Open Ball (timing).• Builds match intelligence and maturity — understanding when to breathe, when to break.• Defines our identity: organised, composed, and intentional — we play on our terms.• Croatian Influence: calm under pressure, explode with precision — the rhythm of control.
Core Coaching Messages
Phase | Message
Kids (10–13) | “Sometimes we go fast, sometimes we go slow.”
Tactical (14–15) | “Circulate until the gap — then accelerate.”
Professional (16+) | “We decide the rhythm — slow to draw them, fast to break them.”
In Possession
Slow Tempo (Control Phase)
- Circulate to move the opponent, not to rest.
- Reset, switch, and invite pressure to create space elsewhere.
- Rebuild composure after transition or overload.🔹 Belco Link: Compactness and Patience in Build.
Fast Tempo (Penetration Phase)
- Triggered by open-ball moments, third-man runs, or line-breaking opportunities.
- Accelerate through quick combinations or direct forward passes.
- The change of tempo is what unbalances the opposition — it’s the moment we strike.🔹 Belco Link: Open Ball, Overloads, and Building Through Thirds.
Player Cues:
- If pressure is tight, play fast to escape.
- If space is open, slow it down to draw them in.
- The best teams shift gears inside the same sequence.
Out of Possession
Slow the Opponent’s Tempo
- Mid-block shape, compact lines, control central space.
- Force them to play sideways or backwards — our shape dictates their speed.
- The team rests in control, not in chaos.
Speed the Opponent’s Tempo (Force Errors)
- When trigger appears (bad touch, back pass, open-ball), we press to accelerate their panic.
- Tempo control defensively means knowing when to jump and when to wait.🔹 Belco Link: Compactness and First Defender Triggers.
Belco Principle: Even without the ball, we control the rhythm of play.
Coach Standards
• Tempo must be a theme in every block — not a separate topic.• Reinforce the question: “Could we slow it? Should we speed it?”• Use freeze moments to highlight good tempo shifts — especially when possession changes third.• Encourage midfielders to act as the “rhythm section” — their choices dictate team speed.• Non-negotiable: Belco teams never rush blindly, never slow without purpose.
Session Examples
1. Youth (10–13) – Tempo Control 5v5
- Setup: 25x20m pitch. Coach calls “slow” or “fast” every 2 minutes.
- Slow: circulate, pass limit removed.
- Fast: two-touch rule, must attack within 6 seconds.
- Coaching Focus: understanding rhythm changes, control before speed.
2. Tactical (14–15) – 7v7 Positional Game (Switch and Break)
- Condition: team must complete 5 passes (slow phase) before allowed to play a forward ball (fast phase).
- Progression: add target zones for line-breaking passes.
- Coaching Detail: recognise open-ball triggers, coordinate support runs when shifting tempo.
3. Professional (16+) – 11v11 Game Management Phase
- Scenario: final 10 minutes of match — team must alternate between slow circulation and explosive countering.
- Coach Focus: communication between lines, leadership in control, when to “breathe” vs when to “strike.”
- Measure: number of deliberate tempo shifts leading to control or chance creation.
Summary
Controlling the tempo is the art of running the game, not chasing it.It’s how Belco teams dominate mentally and tactically — deciding when to rest, when to race, and when to break.It’s the rhythm that links every principle: build patiently, strike with intent, defend in control.
Slow when we choose. Fast when we want. Always on our terms.

heading: 3.1 Pressing
headingPath: 3. Out of Possession Frameworks > 3.1 Pressing
content:
(Internal – Coach Reference)
Definition
• Pressing = collective defensive action to win the ball high, force errors, and dictate opponent tempo.• The Belco Way: Press forward, press together, press brave.When we go, it’s 1v1 all over the park — no safety nets, no spare man at the back. Everyone rolls on a line, everyone commits.
Why We Press This Way
• Matches our identity: aggressive, proactive, compact — we defend forward, not backward.• Creates instant attacking chances close to goal.• Develops duel mentality: we trust our players to win 1v1s under pressure.• Builds senior habits: courage, trust, and coordination.• Croatian influence: pressing built on collective timing and tight structure — short distances, one heartbeat.
Core Coaching Messages
Phase | Message
Kids (10–13) | “When they mess up — we all go!”
Tactical (14–15) | “Press = 1v1 everywhere, no free players.”
Professional (16+) | “Trigger. Roll on. Lock the pitch.”
Pressing Triggers
We don’t guess; we go on clear cues the whole team recognises:
1. Poor Control / Bad Touch
- Ball travels too far from foot → first defender jumps.
2. Opponent Facing Backwards
- Pressure instantly — lock them into a direction.
3. Pass into Fullback / Sideline
- Trap zone → front three shift to one side; midfield and back line roll on.
4. GK or CB Under Pressure
- Forward line squeezes; 10 joins to press; CBs and FBs push high — we back our line.
5. Set-Piece Press (Goal Kicks)
- Set up 1v1 across the field.
- Pressing shape predetermined: striker screens one CB, 10 steps onto the other, wingers lock FBs, midfield squeezes high, back line holds halfway.
🔹 Belco Principle: “If one goes, all go — rolling-on together.”
Structural Variations
1. Push the 10 On
- ST + 10 press CBs; wide players lock FBs.
- Midfield squeezes up; CBs step in behind.
- Creates 1v1s across the pitch, high compactness.
2. Push a Fullback On
- Ball goes wide → nearest FB jumps to press.
- Opposite FB tucks in; midfield slides to support.
- Front three shift across — we trap on the sideline.
3. Slide Front Three
- Winger tucks inside to block central lane.
- ST angles press to force outside.
- Opposite winger ready to pounce on switch.
4. Rolling-On Principle
- As the first line presses, every other line steps up one line.
- No gaps, no safety space — the team moves as one aggressive unit.
- Back line holds high, confident in the duel.
Out of Possession Philosophy
• We press to attack, not just to defend.• Compactness behind the ball ensures if one duel is lost, another is immediately engaged.• The best press ends with a controlled regaining pass forward, not just a tackle.• When we can’t win it high, we stay connected and ready for the next trigger — always in position to go again.
Coach Standards
• Training games must feature pressing scenarios — goal kicks, turnovers, bad touches.• Players judged on:
- Reaction speed to the trigger.
- Courage in 1v1 duels.
- Compactness when rolling on.• Non-negotiable: we never leave a free player when we press.• Use freeze coaching: “Who’s rolled on behind? Where’s the spare man? (There isn’t one!)”• Every player understands their press role — direction, cover, and trigger cue.
Session Examples
1. Youth (10–13) – Trigger Press Game
- Setup: 5v5 + GKs in 30x25m.
- Rule: points only for winning the ball in attacking half.
- Coach Detail: identify triggers (bad touch, back pass), react fast, all go together.
2. Tactical (14–15) – 7v7 Rolling-On Press
- Setup: half-pitch, build-up team plays from GK.
- Condition: pressing team starts compact, triggers on poor touch or pass to fullback.
- Coaching Points: rolling-on coordination — midfield and back line step as front press.
- Progression: add restarts from goal kicks — teach structural set-ups.
3. Professional (16+) – 11v11 Pressing Phase Play
- Focus: pressing triggers from goal-kicks and build-up play.
- Structure: front three dictate side; 10 joins press; midfield and back line roll on.
- Coach Freeze: check distances (10–12m vertically, 8–10m horizontally), pressing angles, and communication cues.
- Measure: time to regain ball or force long clearance.
Summary
Pressing defines our Belco aggression and identity.
We defend to attack — 1v1 everywhere, compact and fearless.
When the cue appears, we roll on as one, lock the pitch, and win the duel.
That’s our mark: organised chaos, full commitment and total control.
See the trigger. Roll on. Win it back.That’s Belco Pressing.

heading: 3.2 Compactness
headingPath: 3. Out of Possession Frameworks > 3.2 Compactness
content:
(Internal – Coach Reference)
Definition
• Compactness = keeping short vertical and horizontal distances so the team defends as one unit — always close enough to press, cover, and recover.• The Belco Way: we defend forward and together — no gaps, no easy passes through, no free space between our lines.
Why It Matters
• Protects dangerous spaces — especially the central channel and between the lines.• Makes pressing efficient — short distances mean quicker pressure and immediate support.• Builds trust — players know there’s always cover behind and balance beside.• Belco DNA: defending isn’t retreating — it’s organised aggression that controls tempo and territory.
Core Coaching Messages
Phase | Message
Kids (10–13) | “Stay close, defend together.”
Tactical (14–15) | “Small to defend, big to attack.”
Professional (16+) | “Compactness kills their rhythm — expansion creates ours.”
In Possession Link
• Compactness behind the ball gives secure support lines for circulation and recovery if lost.• Expansion only happens after control — stretch the pitch when possession is safe.• When losing the ball, immediate collapse back into compactness — no transition gaps.🔹 Relates to Belco: Open Ball and Tempo Control.
Out of Possession
• Vertical spacing: 10–12m between lines.• Horizontal spacing: 8–10m between players.• Always shift together — the line closest to the ball squeezes, others adjust in sync.• Press to one side — create traps and limit exit routes.• First defender leads forward, second provides cover, third secures balance.• When the ball switches, we recover through the middle, not around — shortest path first.
Coach Standards
• Non-negotiable: we never defend stretched.• Every session must include a compactness rule — measurable zones or spacing constraints.• Praise players for delaying or covering when outnumbered — compactness before tackling.• Freeze and check distances during play — lines, cover, and body shape.• Compactness review after every match: line gaps, recovery timing, and collective response.
Session Examples
1. Youth (10–13) – 4v4 + Floaters (Compact Line Game)
- Setup: 20x25m grid, floaters outside.
- Condition: defenders earn points when they stay within a marked 6m band during defensive phase.
- Focus: shifting together, staying connected, covering passing lanes.
- Coaching Detail: body orientation, distances between players, instant recovery after loss.
2. Tactical (14–15) – 7v7 Compact Block Game
- Grid: 40x35m, two zones.
- Condition: defenders must remain within 15m vertically, 25m horizontally.
- Coaching Detail: trigger compactness when ball enters middle zone — shift, squeeze, trap.
- Objective: teach collective contraction and recovery timing.
3. Professional (16+) – 11v11 Phase Play – Compactness Check
- Focus: team compactness during mid-block and press.
- Coach Freeze: measure spacing — too stretched → reset shape and repeat.
- Progression: add switch triggers to test horizontal compactness and communication.

heading: 3.3 First Defender
headingPath: 3. Out of Possession Frameworks > 3.3 First Defender
content:
(Internal – Coach Reference)
Definition
• First Defender = the nearest player to the ball when out of possession, responsible for immediately engaging the opponent to control the situation.• The Belco Way: we defend forward and together. The first defender doesn’t just chase — they lead the press, set the line, and control the tempo of our defensive response.
Why It Matters
• Sets the team’s defensive trigger — everyone reacts off the first defender’s action.• Stops the opponent’s rhythm early — limits their time, space, and decision options.• Buys recovery time — good delay gives structure and compactness back to the team.• Builds our identity of organised aggression — we press with intent, not chaos.🔹 Belco DNA link: Compactness, Duel Mentality, and Tempo Control.
Core Coaching Messages
Phase | Message
Kids (10–13) | “You’re first — get close fast, don’t dive in.”
Tactical (14–15) | “Press or delay — you decide. Your body shape tells the team what happens next.”
Professional (16+) | “First defender controls tempo — one second too late, and the team loses shape.”
Key Roles and Behaviours
1. Close Down Quickly (Press)
- Sprint to engage the opponent as the ball travels.
- Angle your run to close both player and passing lane.
- Arrive under control — body low, balanced, front foot pressure.
- Trigger: poor opponent touch, open ball, or backwards body position.
2. Delay and Control
- If immediate pressure isn’t possible, slow the play.
- Stay 1–1.5m off — block forward options, force play backward or wide.
- Use body shape to guide the opponent where we want (toward help).
- Communicate: “Hold!” — signals team to recover and compact.
3. Lead the Press
- First defender = trigger for the rest.
- When you go, others must go — no solo pressing.
- Use visual cues: when you engage, second defender tightens, third covers.
- Lead with intent: your pressure dictates team distance and direction.
In Possession Link
• The first defender often becomes the first attacker in transition — after winning the duel or intercepting, play forward instantly.• Croatian methodology emphasises duel mentality — recover, then attack fast with purpose.
Out of Possession Link
• Compactness is built around the first defender’s line of pressure.• Communication between 1st, 2nd, and 3rd defenders determines if we press, hold, or drop.• The first defender’s decision decides our team height and tempo of recovery.
Coach Standards
• Non-negotiable: The nearest player reacts first. No hesitation.• Always demand recognition of cues — open touch = press, closed touch = delay.• Reinforce correct body orientation — show one way, don’t open both sides.• Praise correct decision-making (press vs delay) as much as winning the ball.• Train players to lead communication — “I’m in!”, “Hold!”, “Force left!”
Session Examples
- Youth (10–13) – 1v1 + Recovery Rondo
- Setup: 8x8m grid, 3 attackers vs 1 defender.
- Condition: as soon as ball is lost, the nearest player becomes first defender.
- Coaching Detail:
- Close fast, slow down 2m before contact.
- Stay balanced, delay or press based on opponent’s touch.
- Reinforce reaction speed — who goes first?
- Objective: build instinct to become first defender immediately after loss.
- Tactical (14–15) – Press or Delay Game (5v5 + 3 neutrals)
- Setup: 25x25m grid, transitions every 10–15 seconds.
- Condition: when ball is lost, nearest player must decide — press (if open touch) or delay (if under control).
- Coaching Points:
- Cue recognition (body shape of opponent).
- Team reaction to first defender’s action.
- Communication: “Press!”, “Hold!”, “Force left!”
- Progression: add gates for transition goals — reward turnovers within 3 passes.
- Professional (16+) – 11v11 Pressing Phase: Line Leadership
- Focus: front three and midfield lines — coordinated first defender triggers.
- Freeze Play: check distance to ball, body angle, and second defender’s cover.
- Objective: train pressing direction (force outside/inside), team height, and recovery tempo.
- Measure: compactness and reaction time after first defender engagement.
Perfect — this fits cleanly after your First Defender framework.Below is a Second Defender (Cover) framework written in the same internal Belco style, aligned with Croatian defensive logic and your shared language.

heading: 3.4 Second Defender – Cover
headingPath: 3. Out of Possession Frameworks > 3.4 Second Defender – Cover
content:
(Internal – Coach Reference)
Definition
- Second Defender = the closest supporting defender to the first defender, positioned to cover space behind, protect dangerous lanes, and intervene if the first defender is beaten.
- The Belco Way: the second defender provides security without killing aggression. We press forward — but always with cover.
Why It Matters
- Allows the first defender to press with confidence.
- Prevents direct penetration when the first duel is lost.
- Protects central space and goal-facing actions.
- Turns individual defending into collective defending.
🔹 Belco DNA link: Compactness, Overloads, Duel Mentality.
Core Coaching Messages
Phase | Message
Kids (10–13) | “Help your mate — stand where the danger is.”
Tactical (14–15) | “Read the duel. Cover the space, not the man.”
Professional (16+) | “Your position decides whether we press or protect.”
Key Roles and Behaviours
1. Provide Immediate Cover
- Move as the first defender moves — not after.
- Position yourself goal-side and inside whenever possible.
- Distance: 2–5m behind and slightly inside the first defender.
- Body half-turned — see ball + opponent + space.
Coaching cue: “Inside first. Goal first.”
2. Protect the Most Dangerous Space
- Priority order:
- Central lane
- Space behind first defender
- Opponent’s next action (shot/pass/drive)
- Do not chase the ball — hold the threat.
- If ball carrier shifts laterally, adjust on an arc, not straight lines.
🔹 Relates to Belco: Compactness
3. Read the Duel, Not the Ball
- If first defender is:
- Under control → hold and screen.
- Beaten or unbalanced → step in decisively.
- Timing is critical — arrive as the attacker escapes, not after.
Key error to correct: stepping too early and opening space behind.
4. Communicate and Organise
- Constant information to the first defender:
- “Inside!”
- “Hold!”
- “I’m here!”
- Communication reassures the press and stabilises team distances.
- Second defender often becomes the organiser of the local unit.
In Possession Link
- Second defender positioning prepares the counter-press.
- When ball is won:
- First defender often engages.
- Second defender often plays forward first.
- Croatian methodology values defensive readiness for attacking transition.
Out of Possession Link
- Cover defines whether the team can:
- Press aggressively
- Or must delay and recover
- Distance between 1st–2nd defender sets the team’s risk level.
- Poor cover = broken press.
Coach Standards
- Never accept flat or square cover positions.
- Demand inside-first positioning.
- Freeze moments to show:
- Passing lanes exposed
- Central space left open
- Praise correct cover positioning, not just interceptions.
- Reinforce the idea: “You defend for two.”
Session Examples
1. Youth (10–13) – 2v2 Recover & Cover
- Setup: 12x10m grid, 2v2.
- Condition: if first defender is beaten, second must delay for 3 seconds.
- Coaching Detail:
- Inside position.
- Stay connected — no straight chasing.
- Objective: understand helping roles, not ball chasing.
2. Tactical (14–15) – 3v3 + Target Zones
- Setup: 20x15m, central scoring zone.
- Condition: goals count double through the middle.
- Coaching Points:
- Second defender screens central lane.
- Timing of stepping in after first defender engagement.
- Progression: restrict defenders to one tackle — forces cooperation.
🔹 Relates to Belco: Overloads & Compactness
3. Professional (16+) – Unit Defending (Back 4 / Midfield 3)
- Format: 6v6+GK in two-thirds.
- Focus: first and second defender relationships.
- Freeze moments:
- Distance between defenders.
- Body shape relative to goal.
- Measure: central entries conceded after first contact.
Tactical Summary (Coach Language)
“First defender applies pressure.Second defender gives permission to press.”
Without cover, aggression becomes chaos.With cover, aggression becomes identity.

heading: 3.5 Third Defender – Balance
headingPath: 3. Out of Possession Frameworks > 3.5 Third Defender – Balance
content:
(Internal – Coach Reference)
Definition
- Third Defender = the next connected defender behind the first two, responsible for balancing the defensive action by protecting space away from the ball, controlling depth, and securing the team shape.
- The Belco Way: we don’t just win duels — we protect the game behind them. The third defender ensures our aggression never breaks our structure.
Why It Matters
- Prevents the opponent’s next action, not just the current one.
- Secures the team against switches, runs in behind, and second balls.
- Allows the first and second defenders to be assertive.
- Forms the foundation of rest-defence.
🔹 Belco DNA link: Compactness, Tempo Control, Overloads.
Core Coaching Messages
Phase | Message
Kids (10–13) | “Stay connected — don’t drift away from the game.”
Tactical (14–15) | “You protect space early so we don’t run later.”
Professional (16+) | “You control the line, the depth, and the next pass.”
Key Roles and Behaviours
1. Control Depth and Distance
- Position behind and across the first two defenders.
- Maintain optimal distance:
- Close enough to squeeze space.
- Far enough to see the whole picture.
- Constant micro-adjustments as the ball moves.
Coaching cue: “See the whole game.”
2. Protect the Far Side and Central Access
- Responsibility is space, not ball or man.
- Priorities:
- Space behind the defensive unit.
- Far-side runner.
- Switch of play.
- Never get attracted to the ball-side overload.
🔹 Relates to Belco: Overloads (defending them, not chasing them)
3. Maintain Compactness of the Unit
- Third defender sets:
- Line height.
- Horizontal balance.
- If the ball-side steps, the third defender slides and anchors.
- If the line breaks, they drop early to protect depth.
Common fault to correct: stepping too high and losing the space behind.
4. Be the Defensive Controller
- Constant communication:
- “Shift!”
- “Hold the line!”
- “Switch coming!”
- Acts as the reference point for others.
- Often the calmest defender — reads danger before it arrives.
In Possession Link
- Third defender is usually part of rest-defence structure.
- Positioning allows:
- Immediate counter-press if ball is lost.
- Protection against long balls and direct transitions.
- Croatian methodology prioritises preventing transition over chasing it.
Out of Possession Link
- Third defender determines:
- Whether the team can stay high.
- Or must drop and reset.
- Balance between pressure and protection.
- If the third defender is wrong, the whole team stretches.
Coach Standards
- Demand constant scanning — ball, opponents, teammates.
- Never allow ball-watching.
- Freeze moments to show:
- Open far side.
- Unprotected depth.
- Praise anticipation, not just recovery runs.
- Reinforce: “You defend what we can’t see.”
Session Examples
1. Youth (10–13) – 3v3 + Safe Zone
- Setup: 20x15m, with a protected end zone.
- Condition: third defender must stay connected to protect zone.
- Coaching Detail:
- Stay central.
- Adjust as ball moves.
- Objective: introduce balance without complexity.
2. Tactical (14–15) – 4v4 + Switch Threat
- Setup: 30x25m with wide switch zones.
- Condition: attackers score double after a switch.
- Coaching Points:
- Third defender anticipates switch.
- Early movement, not recovery sprints.
- Progression: neutral player added to force constant scanning.
🔹 Relates to Belco: Tempo Control & Compactness
3. Professional (16+) – Rest-Defence Game (7v7+3)
- Focus: back line + pivot relationships.
- Constraint: goals count double from direct transitions.
- Freeze Moments:
- Third defender’s distance to last line.
- Far-side protection during ball-side press.
- Measure: transitions conceded in 3 passes or less.
Defensive Triangle Summary (Belco Reference)
- 1st Defender: Pressure
- 2nd Defender: Cover
- 3rd Defender: Balance
“Pressure wins duels.Cover secures them.Balance protects everything else.”
Together, they create controlled aggression, not chaos.
If you want, next steps we can:
- Convert all three into a single one-page visual framework
- Or map them directly into Belco session templates (U13 / U15 / U18)

heading: 3.6 Closed Ball
headingPath: 3. Out of Possession Frameworks > 3.6 Closed Ball
content:
(Internal – Coach Reference)
Definition
• Closed Ball = a situation where the opponent’s ball carrier is under pressure or facing backward, and therefore cannot play forward or in behind.• The Belco Way: when the ball is closed, we press forward and squeeze the pitch — the threat of a through ball is gone, so we can be brave and compact.• This is the defensive mirror of our Open-Ball principle — it tells us when we can step, close space, and control territory.
Why It Matters
• Tells us when to press and compress. When the opponent can’t play forward, we move as one to trap and win it back.• Protects space intelligently: our line doesn’t drop unnecessarily — we read the ball, not fear the space.• Maintains Belco identity: organised aggression — defend forward, compact together, attack from pressure.• Connects to other principles:
- Compactness — shortest distances between lines.
- First Defender — pressure creates the closed-ball moment.
- Pressing — trigger to roll on and lock the field.
- Counter-Pressing — once it’s closed, we go win it.
🔹 Croatian Origin: The CFF curriculum defines “closed ball” as when the opponent cannot play a through pass due to pressure or body orientation — the signal for the team to move higher and compress space.
Core Coaching Messages
Phase | Message
Kids (10–13) | “If they can’t look forward — we move up!”
Tactical (14–15) | “Closed ball = step and squeeze together.”
Professional (16+) | “Recognise closed-ball moments — compress, lock, and hunt.”
Team Actions on Closed-Ball
1. Step Up and Squeeze
- Back line and midfield line both move forward.
- Reduce vertical space to 8–10m between lines.
- Win second balls and intercept short passes.
2. Shift Compactness Toward the Ball
- Team slides as one toward the ball side — close passing lanes, deny inside space.
- Maintain 8–10m horizontal gaps between players.
3. Press Aggressively
- First defender continues pressure; next line supports tight behind.
- “Rolling on” principle — each line steps forward to back the press.
- Lock them into one side and win it through overload pressure.
4. Prepare to Counter
- When the ball is closed, it’s also the moment before transition.
- Be ready to recover possession and immediately find the open-ball to go forward.
Recognising a Closed-Ball Moment
- Opponent’s ball carrier is facing backward or toward touchline.
- First defender is tight and controlling their direction.
- No visible lane to play forward or in behind.
- Pressure + cover already established (compact shape).When these cues align → the ball is closed → we squeeze together.
Out of Possession Application
• When the ball is open, we respect the threat in behind → back line holds.• When the ball is closed, we eliminate that threat → back line and midfield step forward.• The shift from open → closed defines our defensive rhythm:
- Open ball = caution and cover.
- Closed ball = aggression and compression.
In Possession Link
• When we have the ball and it’s closed (opponent tight or compact):
- Reset and recycle — create an open-ball before attempting penetration.• Understanding both sides of the concept helps players read when to attack and when to secure.
Coach Standards
• Train recognition of open vs closed situations — don’t just defend on instinct.• Praise defenders who step up when the ball is closed (not those who drop needlessly).• Use freeze moments: “Ball’s closed — what should our line do?”• Build language: “Closed — squeeze!” becomes a team cue.• Every pressing or defensive session must have clear open-ball / closed-ball cues.• Non-negotiable: we defend the moment, not the space.
Session Examples
1. Youth (10–13) – Open vs Closed Recognition Game
- Setup: 5v5 + GKs.
- Condition: Coach calls “open” or “closed” depending on ball carrier’s pressure.
- Coaching Focus: compact team shape, learning when to step or hold.
2. Tactical (14–15) – 7v7 Pressing Shape Drill
- Setup: half-pitch.
- Condition: When opponent’s CB faces back → team steps as one (closed-ball cue).
- Coaching Detail: timing of line movement, rolling-on coordination.
- Progression: live game transitions (closed-ball → press → counter).
3. Professional (16+) – 11v11 Defensive Phase Play
- Focus: reading open/closed ball to manage team height.
- Freeze: check distances between lines; measure compactness on closed-ball cue.
- Objective: coordinate back line and midfield movement to dominate territory.
Summary
The Closed-Ball principle completes our positional language.It’s the defensive signal to step forward, squeeze, and hunt together — the moment when bravery meets organisation.Croatian methodology calls it the permission to press; Belco calls it the moment to own the pitch.
Ball closed → lines rise → pressure wins.That’s Belco Defensive Intelligence.

heading: 3.7 Return Ball
headingPath: 3. Out of Possession Frameworks > 3.7 Return Ball
content:
(Internal – Coach Reference)
Definition
• Return Ball = when the opponent plays the ball backwards — either to recycle possession under pressure or to reset their build-up.• The Belco Way: when they play back, we step forward.A return ball is our cue to advance the team, compress space, and prepare to press or win territory.
• It’s the bridge moment between closed-ball and pressing — the opponent retreats, and we roll on together.
Why It Matters
• Natural pressing cue: a backwards pass means the opponent can’t hurt us — perfect moment to step and trap.• Reclaims territory: when they go back, we go up.• Connects directly to our defensive identity: defend forward, compact, aggressive.• Links key Belco principles:
- Closed Ball → establishes pressure.
- Return Ball → advances our shape.
- Pressing → completes the trap.• Croatian method alignment:The CFF curriculum teaches defenders to “move the line forward in relation to a return ball” — reading ball movement, not ball position.
Core Coaching Messages
Phase | Message
Kids (10–13) | “When they pass backwards, we move up!”
Tactical (14–15) | “Return ball = push the team, close the space.”
Professional (16+) | “Ball travels back, we roll on — it’s our moment to lock them in.”
Team Actions on a Return Ball
1. Step and Roll-On
- All lines advance one line — rolling-on principle.
- Striker presses the receiver; midfield follows up tight; back line holds high.
- Reduce the vertical gap between units to 8–10m.
2. Pressing Preparation
- The backwards pass is our “green light” to initiate press shape.
- Identify the next trigger (closed-ball or poor touch) and go together.
- Wide players anticipate side traps — step in to block switch.
3. Compact the Middle
- As the ball goes back, midfield line narrows and squeezes.
- Force play to the sideline or into our pressure zone.
- Maintain cover behind first defender — aggressive but balanced.
4. Psychological Message
- We send the signal: “You’re going backward — we’re coming forward.”
- It builds rhythm and confidence — team ownership of territory.
Recognising a Return-Ball Moment
- Ball is played backward (to CB, GK, or behind midfield).
- Opponent faces their own goal or plays under pressure.
- No immediate forward option visible.
- Our nearest players are already set compact (from closed-ball phase).
When these cues appear → we roll on as one and take ground.
Out of Possession Application
• Return Ball is a step trigger — the whole block advances.• Closed Ball is the squeeze trigger — the press engages.• Together they form the rhythm of our defensive phase:
- Open Ball = hold.
- Return Ball = step.
- Closed Ball = squeeze.🔹 Belco Link: Compactness + Pressing + First Defender.
In Possession Link
• We also use return balls intentionally in build-up to:
- Reset our rhythm.
- Draw the opponent forward to create open-ball space.• Croatian principle: “Back to go forward.”• Belco language: Return to re-open.
Coach Standards
• Reinforce that a backward opponent pass = team cue to step.• Training sessions must include return-ball recognition in pressing games.• Use freeze moments:
- “They’ve gone back — what should we do?”
- “Can we step, can we trap?”• Praise collective movement — one goes, all go.• Non-negotiable: Belco teams don’t drop when the opponent retreats.
Session Examples
1. Youth (10–13) – Return Reaction Game
- Setup: 5v5 in 30x25m.
- Condition: when attacking team plays back, defending team steps up 5m.
- Focus: learning the cue, compact team shape, pressing rhythm.
2. Tactical (14–15) – Press Trap from Return Ball
- Setup: 7v7 build-up game (opponent starts from GK).
- Condition: backward pass triggers press — striker presses, 10 steps, midfield rolls on.
- Coaching Detail: timing of movement, cover balance, body angles.
- Progression: add fullback traps and counter transitions.
3. Professional (16+) – 11v11 Phase Play: Step & Trap
- Scenario: defending mid-block against possession team.
- Trigger: opponent plays back → team steps, locks the side, initiates press.
- Coach Focus: line coordination, trigger recognition, distances.
- Measure: number of successful traps following a return ball.
Summary
The Return Ball is our signal to advance and control territory — the middle point between patience and aggression.When the opponent retreats, we take ground.It keeps us compact, brave, and always pressing on our terms.
Ball back → team forward → territory ours.That’s Belco Return-Ball Football.

heading: 3.8 Duel Mentality
headingPath: 3. Out of Possession Frameworks > 3.8 Duel Mentality
content:
(Internal – Coach Reference)
Definition
• Duel Mentality = the belief that every 1v1 moment can decide the game.• The Belco Way: if you win your duel, you change the game. Every player owns their battle — with or without the ball.
Why It Matters
• Modern pressing is built on duels — every matchup matters; one loss and the press collapses.• Attacking duels open the game — beat your man and the pitch opens (the “open-ball” moment).• Builds character — courage, resilience, and accountability: my duel matters.• Belco DNA: organised aggression and bravery under pressure.
Core Coaching Messages
Phase | Message
Kids (10–13) | “Win your battle — don’t back off.”
Tactical (14–15) | “Your duel decides the play.”
Professional (16+) | “Every 1v1 shapes the rhythm — win it, and you control the moment.”
Defensive Duels
• Mindset: proactive, defend forward, don’t wait — dictate the duel.• Body shape: side-on, low stance, show them where you want them.• Compactness Link: you’re never alone — cover and balance behind make your aggression possible.• Pressing Link: when we press, every player owns their man — no passengers.• Detail: stay tight enough to challenge, but patient enough to win. Timing is everything.
Attacking Duels
• Mindset: confident and purposeful — be the difference maker.• Decision-making: read the opponent’s body; if they’re square, attack; if they’re side-on, combine.• Execution: dribble, bounce, or run — but commit with conviction.• Open Ball Link: beating your defender creates the open-ball moment for your team.• Tempo Control: duels dictate the speed of the game — win one, and the tempo shifts in your favour.
Coach Standards
• Every training session must include duel moments — measurable, reviewed, praised.• Evaluate duel outcomes in match analysis (wins/losses by zone and phase).• Reward bravery, not just success — the willingness to engage matters.• Build duel habits early — use tight spaces, direction, and consequence (reward or punishment).• Reinforce the message: duels are team currency — win enough, and the result follows.
Session Examples
1. Youth (10–13) – 1v1 Duel Game to Mini-Goals
- Setup: 10x15m channels, two mini-goals each.
- Scoring: 1 point for beating the defender, 2 points for scoring.
- Progression: defender earns bonus point for regaining and countering.
- Coaching Focus: engage quick, low stance, courage to go forward or defend forward.
2. Tactical (14–15) – 4v4 + 2 Neutrals (Duel Restarts)
- Setup: 25x25m grid. Each restart begins with a 1v1 before play resumes.
- Objective: force repeated duel situations across different zones.
- Coaching Detail: awareness of support (compactness), immediate reaction after duel (transition).
- Belco Focus: individual duel feeds team shape — win yours, the press stays alive.
3. Professional (16+) – 11v11 Duel KPI Game
- Setup: full pitch, coach tracks duels per line (defence, midfield, attack).
- Condition: if a player loses three consecutive duels, coach freezes and repositions shape.
- Coaching Focus: decision-making under pressure, compactness and cover around duels, recovery after loss.
- Review Metric: duel win rate = pressing and tempo effectiveness indicator.
Summary
Duel Mentality is Belco’s competitive edge — it’s how we turn pressure into control and moments into matches.Winning duels is more than physical — it’s mental dominance, tactical awareness, and bravery to act.Every duel, every moment, every player — that’s where games are won.
Win your battle. Lead the fight. Change the game.

heading: 4.1 Secure or Counter
headingPath: 4. Transition Frameworks > 4.1 Secure or Counter
content:
Secure or Counter (Transition to Attack)
(Internal – Coach Reference)
Definition
• Secure or Strike = the decision-making framework for the first 3–4 seconds after regaining possession.• It defines what happens the instant we win the ball back — do we strike (counter-attack immediately) or secure (keep the ball and rebuild)?• The Belco Way: We recognise the moment, decide fast, and commit fully.This is where game intelligence meets bravery — the ability to read disorganisation and act with purpose.
Why It Matters
• Every regain is a potential attack. The question is: what kind of attack?• Sharp transition decisions separate intelligent teams from reactive ones.• Encourages bravery in fast counters while maintaining discipline to control tempo when the moment isn’t right.• Links directly to core Belco principles:
- Counter-Pressing (structure to win the ball back)
- Tempo Control (deciding when to accelerate or slow)
- Open Ball (recognising forward opportunity)
- Overloads (having support around the ball to play forward or secure)• Culturally aligned with Croatian methodology: “first look forward, then secure” — always positive, always smart.
Core Coaching Messages
Phase | Message
Kids (10–13) | “Win it, look forward first!”
Tactical (14–15) | “3-second rule — if they’re open, go! If not, keep it.”
Professional (16+) | “Recognise chaos — strike fast if they’re disorganised; secure if they’re set.”
The 3–4 Second Window
1. Read the Opponent’s Shape
- Are they disorganised? (stretched, open, wrong side of the ball)→ Strike immediately.
- Are they compact and behind the ball?→ Secure possession.
2. Strike Phase – Counter-Attack Mentality
- One thought: can we go to goal now?
- Play forward within 3 passes.
- Encourage directness and risk — no consequences for losing it quickly.
- Runners must explode forward — into blind sides and open channels.
- Ball carrier must commit defenders (Drive–Draw–Play).🔹 Belco Link: Open Ball, Tempo Control, Duel Mentality.
3. Secure Phase – Possession Control
- If the counter isn’t on, don’t force it.
- Move the ball 2–3 times to reset shape and draw pressure.
- Keep compact support — re-establish build-up structure.
- Wait for an open-ball moment to go again.🔹 Belco Link: Building Through Thirds, Compactness, Overloads.
4. Communication Cues
- “Go!” → signals strike.
- “Secure!” → signals circulate and rebuild.
- Players closest to the regain (CMs, CBs) lead the call.
- Train players to recognise visual cues (numbers, space, opponent body shape).
Player Roles in Transition
Role | Strike Phase | Secure Phase
Ball Winner | Play forward fast or drive into open space. | Protect the ball, connect first support.
Nearest Support | Offer forward run or wall pass. | Be safe outlet for recycle.
Far-Side Players | Sprint into space (width or depth). | Recover into structure.
Midfield Line | Break forward to join counter. | Form passing triangle around regain.
Back Line | Step up to compress space. | Re-form compact build-up shape.
Coach Standards
• Every session involving possession must include a transition element — what happens when we win it?• Reinforce the 3–4 second decision:
- “Could we go?”
- “If not, did we secure quickly?”• Praise intent, not just success — if we read disorganisation correctly, the risk is worth it.• Freeze and highlight examples:
- “Look at their spacing — that’s a strike moment.”
- “Too many behind the ball — that’s secure.”• Encourage shared language: Go or Secure becomes team shorthand.
Session Examples
1. Youth (10–13) – Transition Game: Go or Keep
- Setup: 5v5 in 30x25m.
- Rule: when team wins possession, they have 3 seconds to decide.
- If opponent is open: attack goal within 5 seconds.
- If compact: keep ball for 3 passes = 1 point.
- Coaching Focus: recognising moments, brave decisions.
2. Tactical (14–15) – 7v7 Half-Pitch Transition Play
- Setup: 3 zones (defensive, middle, attacking).
- Condition: every regain triggers a coach call — “Strike” or “Secure.”
- Coaching Detail: reaction speed, spacing for counter or retention.
- Progression: players make the decision themselves after regains.
3. Professional (16+) – 11v11 Transition Phase Play
- Scenario: controlled possession build → turnover → immediate transition.
- Coach Focus: team spacing, player cues, speed of recognition.
- Measure:
- Time to forward action when countering.
- Number of passes to secure when rebuilding.
Summary
Secure or Strike is Belco’s decision framework for positive transition.It teaches players to read chaos, act fast, and stay brave — we counter when the opponent is open, we secure when they’re not.It keeps our play intelligent, aggressive, and always in control of tempo.
Win it → Read it → Decide: Secure or Strike.That’s Belco Transition Football.

heading: 4.2 Counter-press or Compact
headingPath: 4. Transition Frameworks > 4.2 Counter-press or Compact
content:
Transition to Defence
(Internal – Coach Reference)
Definition
- Counter or Compact = the decision-making framework for the first 3–4 seconds after losing possession.
- It defines what happens instantly when we lose the ball — do we counter-press (win it back immediately), or do we compact (drop, recover shape, and protect)?
- The Belco Way: React together, read the moment, and commit fully.This is the defensive mirror of “Secure or Counter” — quick, intelligent, and united.
Why It Matters
- Every loss of possession is a danger moment — but it’s also a chance to regain control.
- Sharp transition decisions separate reactive teams from proactive ones.
- It teaches collective discipline: when to go win it back vs. when to get organised.
- Links directly to core Belco principles:
- Aggressive Recovery (press instantly when possible)
- Collective Compactness (stay tight when stretched)
- Tempo Control (choose intensity or patience based on the situation)
- Duel Mentality (nearest players act decisively)
- Culturally aligned with Croatian methodology: “Press if you can, close if you can’t.”(Pritisni ako možeš, zatvori ako ne možeš.)
Core Coaching Messages
Phase | Message
Kids (10–13) | “Lose it? Win it back fast!” / “Too far? Get back together!”
Tactical (14–15) | “First five seconds — if we’re close, press; if not, close!”
Professional (16+) | “Recognise the moment — counter-press if we can trap, compact if we can’t.”
The 3–4 Second Window
1. Read the State of Play
- Opponent disorganised or back turned? → Counter-Press.
- Opponent facing forward or spaced wide? → Compact.
- Nearest three players decide the reaction; everyone else supports.
2. Counter-Press Phase – React & Compress
- Objective: Win it back before they can play forward.
- Actions:
- Nearest 3–4 players close ball, passing lanes, and forward outlets.
- Back line squeezes to condense space.
- Ball-side players hunt; far side tucks in for protection.
- Belco Link: Aggressive Recovery / Duel Mentality / Tempo Control.
- Croatian Cue: “Prvih pet sekundi odlučuje” — “The first five seconds decide.”
3. Compact Phase – Recover & Reorganise
- Objective: Delay, protect the middle, re-form lines.
- Actions:
- Everyone drops together to compact distances vertically and horizontally.
- Nearest player delays; teammates reset block.
- No pressure? Stay disciplined — let them play sideways, not through.
- Belco Link: Collective Compactness / Build & Control the Tempo.
- Croatian Cue: “Zatvori prazninu” — “Close the gap.”
4. Communication Cues
- “Press!” → triggers immediate counter-press.
- “Compact!” → triggers regroup and block.
- Nearest midfielder or pivot leads the call.
- Train players to read visual cues: opponent’s body shape, distance to ball, support numbers.
Player Roles in Transition
Role | Counter-Press Phase | Compact Phase
Ball Loser | React instantly — chase or close lane. | Recover central space and delay.
Nearest Support | Jump to trap with first defender. | Drop to screen passing option.
Far-Side Players | Squeeze in to lock middle. | Drop to restore shape.
Midfield Line | Step forward to compress space. | Fall into horizontal compactness.
Back Line | Squeeze up for compression. | Drop to depth line, protect behind.
Coach Standards
- Every attacking session must also include “what if we lose it?” — train both transition sides.
- Reinforce the 3–4 second rule:
- “Could we press?”
- “If not, did we compact quickly?”
- Praise intent and coordination — it’s about the collective read, not individual chase.
- Freeze moments to highlight:
- “That was a press cue — they were disorganised.”
- “Too far, that’s compact — well read.”
- Encourage team shorthand:
- “Press!” = immediate squeeze.
- “Compact!” = collective retreat.
Session Examples
- Youth (10–13) – 4v4 Transition Trap Game
- Setup: 4v4 in 25×25m grid with end zones.
- Rule: On loss, nearest 3 press for 5 seconds; if not won, retreat behind halfway line.
- Focus: Reaction speed, collective read.
- Coaching Cue: “Press or Compact — decide fast!”
- Tactical (14–15) – 7v7 + GK Transition Play
- Setup: Build-up to turnover scenario.
- Rule: When team loses it, coach calls “Press” or “Compact.”
- Progression: Players decide call themselves.
- Focus: Coordination of first line, shape recovery, timing.
- Professional (16+) – 11v11 Controlled Transition Phase
- Scenario: Simulate losing possession in build-up and high areas.
- Measure:
- Seconds to regain when pressing.
- Distances between lines when compacting.
- Focus: Decision speed + structural recovery.
Summary
Counter or Compact defines Belco’s defensive transition identity.It’s the intelligent mirror of “Secure or Counter” — a fast, collective decision about how we react after loss.We don’t chase blindly; we recognise, react, and reorganise with purpose.
Lose it → Read it → Decide: Counter or Compact.That’s Belco Transition Football.

heading: 4.3 Counter-Pressing
headingPath: 4. Transition Frameworks > 4.3 Counter-Pressing
content:
Counter-Pressing Framework(Internal – Coach Reference)
Definition
• Counter-pressing = the immediate collective pressure applied in the moment possession is lost, with the goal of regaining the ball before the opponent can play out.• The Belco Way: because we play with controlled possession and compact support, we’re already in position to react instantly — the moment we lose it, we hunt it.• Counter-pressing isn’t chaos — it’s organised aggression within compact structure.
Why It Matters
• Protects against transition: we win the ball before the opponent’s counter even starts.• Turns loss into opportunity: the opponent is open and disorganised — our best attacking chances come right after losing the ball.• Reinforces Belco identity: proactive, connected, fearless — we defend forward, not backward.• Links directly to our possession principles:
- Compactness keeps distances tight.
- Overloads ensure support around the ball.
- Tempo Control allows balance to press or recover.• Cultural link: Croatian methodology teaches “reaction as discipline” — recovery pressure is a team reflex, not an individual effort.
Core Coaching Messages
Phase | Message
Kids (10–13) | “When we lose it — go get it back straight away!”
Tactical (14–15) | “React fast — 3-second rule: press, support, or screen.”
Professional (16+) | “Our structure gives us the right to counter-press — organised, instant, and ruthless.”
Key Principles of Counter-Pressing
1. Reaction (First Two Seconds)
- Nearest player presses immediately — no hesitation.
- Don’t look to foul; look to win.
- Speed beats shape — reaction time decides success.
2. Compactness (Support Distances)
- The reason we can counter-press effectively: our spacing in possession.
- Support players (2nd and 3rd defenders) are already within 8–12m — close enough to trap or intercept.
- Compact possession = immediate recovery potential.
3. Direction of Press (Control the Exit)
- Don’t chase blindly — press to block forward options.
- Nearest player presses ball; second locks the inside; third secures behind.
- Angle your run to force play where we have numbers.
4. Collective Triggers
- Ball lost in central or attacking third.
- Opposition receiver facing their own goal.
- Multiple Belco players in 15m radius (numerical proximity advantage).
- If we have compact support → we go.
- If we’re stretched → recover and regroup.
5. Transition to Attack
- If we win it back, go forward immediately — counter the counter.
- Opponent is disorganised; find the open-ball and attack quickly.
Out of Possession Link
• When counter-pressing fails, we snap into compact defensive block — no middle ground.• Last presser delays; teammates recover inside line.• Compactness regained before opponent can break rhythm.🔹 Belco Link: Compactness + First Defender Framework.
Coach Standards
• Every possession-based session must include transition reaction moments — what happens when we lose it?• Praise reaction speed and collective movement, not just regains.• Freeze sessions to highlight distances: “Were we close enough to go?”• Reinforce the “3-second mentality”:
- Press: nearest player goes.
- Support: next player locks the exit.
- Screen: third player protects behind.• Non-negotiable: Belco teams don’t drop off immediately after losing it — we react first, recover second.
Session Examples
1. Youth (10–13) – 3-Second Reaction Game
- Setup: 5v5 in 25x25m.
- Condition: when a team loses the ball, they have 3 seconds to win it back.
- Coaching Focus: immediate press, team reaction, compactness.
- Objective: build instinctive reaction to loss of possession.
2. Tactical (14–15) – 7v7 Transition Game (Press or Recover)
- Setup: half-pitch with zones.
- Condition: if ball is lost in attacking half → instant press; if lost in defensive half → recover compact.
- Coaching Detail: reading cues, team coordination, pressing direction.
- Progression: add target goals for quick recovery into attack.
3. Professional (16+) – 11v11 Phase Play: Counter-Press Triggers
- Focus: structured possession leading into instant recovery press.
- Freeze: identify spacing at moment of loss — were we close enough to go?
- Measure: number of regains within 5 seconds of loss.
- Coaching Focus: transition speed, pressing angle, communication.
Summary
Counter-Pressing is how Belco turns control into aggression.Because we play compact, our loss of possession is never panic — it’s our next opportunity.
We’re close, we’re organised, and we go together — pressing is the next pass.
Lose it. React. Win it back. Go again.That’s Belco Counter-Pressing.

heading: 5. Using the Frameworks
headingPath: 5. Using the Frameworks
content:
These frameworks are intended as working tools for Belco coaches.
They can be printed individually, discussed in coach education sessions, or referenced when designing training cycles.
Each framework links directly to the senior playing principles to create a unified learning pathway from junior to first grade.

heading: 6. Notes
headingPath: 6. Notes
content: [Space for additional frameworks or reflections.]

heading: U11 Coach Pack
content:
BELCONNEN UNITED FC
TECHNICAL DEPARTMENT
——————————————
COACH PACK YEAR 1
TECHNICAL DEVELOPMENT PHASE (U11)
——————————————
Foundation of the Belco player
——————————————
Prepared by:
Scott Conlon – Technical Director
Belconnen United FC
V2026.01

heading: 1. U11 Message to the Coaches
headingPath: COACH PACK 1 (U11) – TECHNICAL DEVELOPMENT PHASE > 1. U11 Message to the Coaches
content:
This phase is where our football culture is lived, not explained.
Your role isn’t to control the session — it’s to create the conditions for play. At U11, players must fall in love with the ball, enjoy taking risks, and learn to compete in duels. Mistakes are part of the game — allow them, expect them, and move on quickly.
Keep your voice simple and calm. Use short, positive cues that encourage action: “be brave,” “go again,” “help your mate.” Avoid over-instruction — the game will do most of the teaching.
Training should always feel like real football: players making decisions, moving with purpose, and competing for the ball. If there is direction and opposition, learning will follow.
