At this age, we are not chasing understanding or structure. We are building confidence, love of play, and habit through repetition. If players leave training wanting to play more, the session has done its job.
Teach them to love the ball, enjoy the duel, and play without fear.

heading: 2. U11 Player Characteristics
headingPath: COACH PACK 1 (U11) – TECHNICAL DEVELOPMENT PHASE > 2. U11 Player Characteristics
content:
U11 players are energetic, curious, and highly impressionable. Their coordination and perception are developing quickly, making this an important phase for lots of movement, ball contact, and repetition through play.
At this age, players learn best by doing, not by being told. They respond strongly to emotion, challenge, and success inside the game.
Typical traits:
- High enthusiasm and short concentration spans — they thrive on variety, competition, and movement.
- Strong desire to dribble, run, and show their skills; awareness of teammates develops gradually through games.
- Beginning to notice simple game situations (pressure, space, help) when experienced repeatedly, not explained.
- Learn best through visual demonstration and game constraints, rather than verbal instruction.
- Confidence grows when effort and bravery are rewarded; too much correction can quickly reduce risk-taking.
Coach takeaway: keep sessions active, competitive, and game-like. Let players explore solutions and make mistakes at speed. Short prompts and well-designed games are more effective than long explanations.

heading: 3. U11 Phase Overview
headingPath: COACH PACK 1 (U11) – TECHNICAL DEVELOPMENT PHASE > 3. U11 Phase Overview
content:
Area | Key Points
Age Phase | U11 – Technical Foundation (Play & Exploration Phase)
Development Focus | Building confidence with the ball, enjoying duels, improving coordination, and learning through repetition under pressure.
Session Rhythm | 2 sessions per week. Short activities, high engagement, frequent ball contact. Sessions flow quickly with minimal stoppages.
Training Balance | Game-dominant training with strong technical repetition inside realistic football situations. Tactical ideas are felt through play, not explained.
Game Focus | Encourage expression and bravery. Do not stop play to teach structure. Praise effort, intent, and recovery actions. Let players solve problems in the game.
Belco Identity (Introduced Through Play) | 🔹 Comfort on the ball (foundation for future tempo control) 🔹 Competing in duels with confidence 🔹 Helping teammates in simple overload situations
Coach Role | Create energy and rhythm. Intervene briefly and positively. Guide through task design and encouragement, not instruction. Be the facilitator of play, not the controller.

heading: 4. U11 Coaching Emphasis
headingPath: COACH PACK 1 (U11) – TECHNICAL DEVELOPMENT PHASE > 4. U11 Coaching Emphasis
content:
Coaching Dimension | Emphasis for This Phase | Practical Guidance
Session Rhythm | Fast-moving, playful, lots of touches | Short activities (4–6 mins). Rotate teams quickly. Keep the ball rolling.
Coaching Voice | Positive, minimal, encouraging action | Use simple words: “brave,” “go again,” “help,” “try it.” Avoid long explanations.
Correction Style | Mostly indirect and individual | Adjust the task or demonstrate briefly. Rarely stop the whole group.
Technical Focus | Comfort on the ball under pressure | Encourage changing direction, protecting the ball, and moving after actions.
Game Habits (Emergent) | Helping the ball carrier in simple ways | Let players discover support through 2v2 and 3v3 games. Do not name shapes or patterns.
Psychological Theme | Courage, freedom, enjoyment | Praise effort, ideas, and recovery — not just success.
Language Focus | Action-based, child-friendly cues | “Try again.” “Can you help?” “Go forward.”
Behaviours to Reward | Bravery, effort, engagement | Acknowledge players who keep trying, compete in duels, and react quickly.
Croatian Guideline | The game teaches more than the coach | Well-designed games do the coaching. Intervene only when needed.

heading: 5. U11 Technical Focus List
headingPath: COACH PACK 1 (U11) – TECHNICAL DEVELOPMENT PHASE > 5. U11 Technical Focus List
content:
U11 Technical Focus – Foundation Through Play
Purpose
At U11, technique is developed inside the game — with pressure, direction, and opposition.Players repeat actions through many game situations, not through isolated correction.The goal is confidence and instinct, not technical understanding.
1. Comfort on the Ball
What we want to see
- Players happy to receive, carry, and protect the ball
- Willingness to turn, escape, and try again
How it shows up
- 1v1 duels
- Directional 2v2 and 3v3 games
- Small goals and transition games
2. Changing Direction & Escaping Pressure
What we want to see
- Players turning away from pressure
- Using different directions and speeds
How it shows up
- Small-sided directional games
- Gates and zones that reward movement
- Free-play dribbling games
3. Helping a Teammate
What we want to see
- Players offering simple passing options
- Moving after passing to stay involved
How it shows up
- 2v2 and 3v3 games
- Overload games without explanation
- “Help the ball” challenges
4. Carrying the Ball Forward
What we want to see
- Players driving into space when it opens
- Confidence to move the game forward
How it shows up
- Directional SSGs
- End-zone and line-breaking games
- Score-by-carry challenges
5. Competing to Win the Ball
What we want to see
- Quick reaction when the ball is lost
- Desire to get close and compete
How it shows up
- Transition games
- 1v1 and 2v2 defending situations
- Win-it-back games
6. Finishing With Confidence
What we want to see
- Players wanting to score
- Variety in finishing attempts
How it shows up
- Dribble-to-score games
- Rebounds and second-chance goals
- Small-goal finishing
7. Awareness Through Play
What we want to see
- Players gradually noticing space, pressure, and teammates
- Better decisions emerging naturally
How it shows up
- Repetition in SSGs
- Constraints that encourage looking up
- Coach questions after play, not during
U11 End-of-Phase Technical Expectations
By the end of the U11 season, players will not all look the same. Development is uneven and individual.However, most players should regularly show the following behaviours in games:
1. Comfort With the Ball
- Willingly receive the ball under pressure
- Attempts to turn or protect rather than immediately pass
- Uses both feet naturally during play
Indicator: Player asks for the ball and keeps trying after mistakes.
2. Confidence in Duels
- Engages in 1v1 situations without hesitation
- Attempts to beat an opponent or shield the ball
- Competes immediately after losing possession
Indicator: Player does not hide from contact or pressure.
3. Ability to Move the Game Forward
- Recognises space and drives into it when available
- Passes to a teammate when pressure is high
- Moves after passing to stay involved
Indicator: Player contributes to attacks through action, not positioning.
4. Simple Cooperation
- Offers basic support to the ball carrier
- Uses teammates naturally in 2v2 and 3v3 situations
- Begins to combine without being instructed
Indicator: Player plays with others, not around them.
5. Reaction to Game Changes
- Reacts quickly when possession is won or lost
- Makes an effort to recover or support immediately
- Understands the game never stops
Indicator: Player is mentally connected to play transitions.
6. Desire to Score
- Looks to finish attacks
- Attempts different types of finishes
- Shows enjoyment in attacking moments
Indicator: Player attacks goal with intent, not fear.
Important Notes for Coaches
- These behaviours will appear often, not always
- Inconsistency is normal and expected
- Absence of these behaviours does not indicate failure
- Late developers may show fewer behaviours now and more later
The primary success measure at U11 is confidence and enjoyment in football.

heading: 6. U11 Language Bank
headingPath: COACH PACK 1 (U11) – TECHNICAL DEVELOPMENT PHASE > 6. U11 Language Bank
content:
(Coach Communication Library – Used Consistently Across All U11 Teams)
This chapter outlines the simple, shared words coaches use to support young players during training and games. At U11, language is not used to explain football or give instructions — it is used to encourage action, confidence, and engagement inside the game.
The purpose of language at this age is to help players play freely, stay involved, and enjoy solving problems with the ball. Cues are short, familiar, and positive. They are used to support effort and intent, not to dictate decisions or movements.
Language at U11 is supportive, not corrective. Coaches prompt with words that invite players to try again, help a teammate, or play forward when possible. Most learning still comes from the game itself — language simply nudges attention without interrupting flow.
This shared language helps create consistency across teams while protecting creativity, enjoyment, and curiosity. As players grow older, these same words will remain, but responsibility will gradually shift from the coach’s voice to the player’s awareness.
U11
1. On the Ball (Confidence & Bravery)
Coaching intention: encourage freedom, direction, and courage on the ball.
U11 Language Cues
- “Be brave.”
- “Go forward if you can.”
- “Change direction.”
- “Protect it.”
- “Try again.”
- “Good idea.”
Open body, half-turn, scanning, tempo language
2. Helping a Teammate (Simple Cooperation)
Coaching intention: passing as support, not structure.
U11 Language Cues
- “Help your mate.”
- “Can you be an option?”
- “Pass and move.”
- “Stay involved.”
- “Find a friend.”
Third-man, back–mid–front, middle man, triangles
3. Winning the Ball Back (Effort & Reaction)
Coaching intention: immediate reaction, duel mentality.
U11 Language Cues
- “Go win it.”
- “Get close.”
- “Be brave.”
- “React!”
- “Don’t stop.”
Press triggers, five-second rule, compact lines
4. After the Ball Changes (Transition Awareness)
Coaching intention: keep players mentally connected to the game.
U11 Language Cues
- “Win it and play.”
- “Go again.”
- “Help behind.”
- “Stay with the game.”
Balance, tempo switch, recover shape language
5. Mentality & Effort (Emotional Safety)
Coaching intention: reward courage, effort, and engagement.
U11 Language Cues
- “That’s brave.”
- “Good effort.”
- “Great reaction.”
- “You didn’t stop.”
- “Well done for trying.”
Leadership, verbal organisation, control language

heading: 7. U11 Annual Cycle Progression
headingPath: COACH PACK 1 (U11) – TECHNICAL DEVELOPMENT PHASE > 7. U11 Annual Cycle Progression
content:
Structure: The U11 season consists of 10 cycles (3-Week Cycles, 2 Sessions per Week ≈30 weeks, 60 sessions)
U11 cycles are numbered Cycle 1 to Cycle 10
Cycle | Theme | Week 1 | Week 2 | Week 3
1 | Be Friends With the Ball | Free Ball Play – Change & Protect Keep the Ball in Chaos | Direction Changes & Escapes Shield, Turn & Go | Ball Under Pressure Games Dribble Survival Games
2 | Beat Your Player | 1v1 Any Direction Attack the Space to Score | Fake, Go & Accelerate Beat Them to Finish | Duel Festival 1v1 → 2v2 Transition Games
3 | Play With a Mate | Pass & Move to Keep It Help the Ball Carrier | 2v1 – Use Your Mate Pass or Drive Games | Keep It Together Small Team Possession Games
4 | Win It Back | Chase & Delay Games Slow Them Down | Steal It & Go Forward Win It Back to Score | Regain + Counter Transition Battle Games
5 | Score in Different Ways | Dribble to Finish Finish After a Move | Quick Finishes in the Box One-Touch Scoring Games | Finish Under Pressure Rebound & Second-Chance Goals
6 | Win → Play → Defend | Fast Reaction Games Win & Play Forward | Counter or Recover Help Behind the Ball | Chaos Transition Games Transition Tournament
7 | Stay Close Together | Defend Your Zone Together Stay Close & Protect | Help the First Defender Nearest 3 Defend | Protect the Middle Stay Connected Game
8 | Find the Free Player | Look Before You Get It Find Space to Play | Switch the Game Use the Whole Pitch | Wide to Score Change the Picture
9 | Play Smart | When to Go Fast When to Keep the Ball | Score or Keep It Read the Moment Games | Simple Game Control Smart Decision Matches
10 | Festival of Football | Mixed Games – All Themes Team Challenges | Express Yourself Play Our Way | Belco Festival Celebrate the Season
How to Use It
- Each session theme = 60-70min session when we build content later.
- Week 1: teach the action (technique under pressure).
- Week 2: link the action (decision & cooperation).
- Week 3: apply the action (game integration & transition).
- Keep using Belco language cues in every block so identity builds naturally.

heading: 8. U11 Training Sessions
headingPath: COACH PACK 1 (U11) – TECHNICAL DEVELOPMENT PHASE > 8. U11 Training Sessions
content:
Belco training sessions at U11 are designed to support play, not control it.
At this age, structure exists only to create rhythm, energy, and repetition. Sessions must never limit creativity, curiosity, or freedom with the ball.
We are inspired by Croatian methodology — not by copying drills, but by copying how learning emerges through play.
Session Flow (Purpose-Based, Not Drill-Based)
Every U11 session follows a simple flow, not a fixed set of exercises:
- Prepare the player (uvodni)Wake up the body and the brain. Lots of touches, movement, and fun.
- Explore the main idea (glavni)Small-sided games where the key theme appears naturally through pressure and opposition.
- Let the game take over (završni)Free or guided play where players apply ideas without interruption.
These phases describe energy and engagement, not coaching detail.
Coach Expectations at U11
Coaches are not expected to run perfect activities.They are expected to:
- Keep the session moving and alive
- Let players play more than listen
- Increase realism by:
- adding direction
- adding opposition
- reducing space
- Use shared U11-safe language
- Step in briefly, then step out again
About Session Examples
The session examples that follow show one possible way to apply this approach.
Activities may be:
- changed
- simplified
- replaced
As long as:
- the flow remains
- the games are real
- the players stay engaged
Belco Principle (U11 Version)
We don’t copy exercises.
We create environments where football teaches itself.
If players are smiling, competing, and asking for the ball, the session is working.

heading: U11 Session Structure
headingPath: COACH PACK 1 (U11) – TECHNICAL DEVELOPMENT PHASE > 8. U11 Training Sessions > U11 Session Structure
content:
How Belco Training Sessions Are Structured (U11)
Belco U11 training sessions are built around a simple Croatian principle:players learn football by playing football — with the ball, with opponents, and with freedom.
Structure exists to support play, not to organise behaviour or teach tactics.
Each session is deliberately simple and designed to:
- build confidence and enjoyment
- maximise ball contact and repetition
- allow decisions to emerge naturally through play
- avoid tactical organisation or instruction
As the session progresses:
- early activities support success through space or numbers
- pressure increases naturally through opposition and realism
- coach input reduces, not increases
The session always flows through three connected phases.
Phase 1 – Play with the Ball
This phase helps players arrive ready to play, both physically and emotionally.
Priority
- comfort on the ball
- enjoyment and engagement
- rhythm and confidence
Players should experience:
- lots of ball contact
- freedom of movement
- early success
Overloads, extra space, and assisted situations are encouraged to help players settle and enjoy the ball.
This phase is not about teaching or correcting.It is about helping players feel good with the ball and ready to play.
Coaches should see:
- smiles
- natural energy building
- willingness to get involved
Phase 2 – Learning Game
This is the main play phase of the session.
Players learn through small-sided games that present simple football situations(e.g. 1v1, 2v1, 2v2, 3v3).
The focus is on:
- repetition through play
- confidence in action
- simple choices
- interaction with teammates and opponents
Supportive conditions (space, overloads, reduced pressure) are used to help success.
As players settle, opposition or constraints may increase gradually — but the activity must remain a game, not a drill or tactical exercise.
The coach shapes the environment and reinforces behaviours briefly, then steps away.
The game does the teaching.
Phase 3 – Football Game
The session finishes with a free or lightly conditioned game.
This phase allows players to:
- express themselves freely
- make decisions without guidance
- play with confidence and enjoyment
Coach intervention is minimal.
Players are encouraged to:
- self-organise
- experiment
- stay connected to the game
This phase confirms learning through application, not explanation.
Coaches should look for:
- bravery on the ball
- continued engagement
- enjoyment of the game
Not perfect execution.
Key Message for Coaches (U11)
- Keep the session flowing
- Protect confidence and enjoyment
- Let the game teach
- If time is limited, protect the Football Game
At U11, the opening phase may include ball-mastery elements either within the activity or briefly beforehand to support comfort and rhythm. Extra space, overloads, or reduced opposition are appropriate to help players settle early.
Coach Observation Focus – Phase 1 (Play with the Ball)
Watch for:
- Are players asking for the ball?
- Are they willing to turn or protect under pressure?
- Do they recover emotionally after mistakes?
Coach action:
- Encourage with simple praise
- Adjust space or numbers if confidence drops
- Avoid stopping the activity
Coach Observation Focus – Phase 2 (Learning Game)
Watch for:
- Are players trying to solve the problem themselves?
- Do they repeat actions (dribble, pass, move)?
- Are duels being engaged or avoided?
Coach action:
- Let the game run
- Reinforce effort and intent
- Brief individual prompts only if needed
Coach Observation Focus – Phase 3 (Football Game)
Watch for:
- Do players stay brave in free play?
- Are they involved without instruction?
- Does the game flow naturally?
Coach action:
- Stay mostly silent
- Allow self-organisation
- Praise behaviours after the game, not during
If players are active, involved, and enjoying the game, the session is working.

heading: 9. U11 Correction & Feedback Language
headingPath: COACH PACK 1 (U11) – TECHNICAL DEVELOPMENT PHASE > 9. U11 Correction & Feedback Language
content:
Purpose
To help coaches give clear, consistent, and age-appropriate feedback that supports learning without stopping the game.
At U11, correction is not about fixing football problems.
It is about guiding behaviour while play continues.
In Croatian methodology, correction is part of the rhythm of the session, not an interruption to it.
U11 Correction Philosophy (Croatian Foundation Model)
At this age:
- feedback is brief
- correction happens in flow
- learning comes from repetition, not explanation
Coaches correct only when players can immediately re-play the action.If a moment has passed, the coach observes and waits.
Types of Feedback at U11
(What Coaches Can Use — and When)
Type of Feedback | When Used | U11 Croatian Approach | Belco-Appropriate Language
Instant (“on the run”) | During live play | One-word cue to guide attention | “Go!” · “Help!” · “Protect!”
Pause & Replay | Only if play has fully broken | Brief reset, then restart quickly | “Hold — same game, go again.”
Guided Prompt | After repeated attempts | Ask simple awareness question | “What did you see?”
Reinforce the Idea | When intent is right | Praise behaviour, not outcome | “Good idea — keep trying.”
Group Reminder | When energy drops | Address team briefly, then play | “Team — stay brave. Play.”
Rule of thumb:If it takes longer than 10 seconds, it’s too much for U11.
U11 Correction Principles
1️⃣ Coach behaviour, not the player→ Focus on actions, not personalities
2️⃣ One message only→ Never stack corrections
3️⃣ Correct in rhythm→ Stop only if the action can be replayed immediately
4️⃣ Let success teach→ Praise intent and effort more than execution
Belco Common Coaching Language – U11 Safe Version
(Action cues only – no concepts, no structure)
Action Type | Short Cue | Positive Reinforcement
First Touch | “Go!” “Protect!” | “Good — you stayed on it.”
Passing | “Help!” “Again!” | “Nice — you stayed involved.”
Dribbling | “Be brave!” “Change!” | “Yes — you tried it.”
Support | “Option!” “Move!” | “Good help.”
Defending | “Get close!” “Stay with him!” | “Well done — you didn’t stop.”
Transition | “React!” “Go again!” | “Great reaction.”
Effort / Mentality | “Next action!” | “That’s Belco — keep going.”
Open body · scanning · spacing · five-second rule · structural language
Tone & Delivery at U11
- Before feedback: calm body, clear voice
- During feedback: 1–2 words maximum
- After feedback: let the game run and observe
Coaches should aim for this rhythm:
Prompt → Play → Observe
Not:
Explain → Correct → Control
Key Reminder for Coaches
If you are unsure whether to intervene:
Let the game continue and watch.
At U11, silence is often better than instruction.

heading: 10. U11 Game Day Guidance
headingPath: COACH PACK 1 (U11) – TECHNICAL DEVELOPMENT PHASE > 10. U11 Game Day Guidance
content:
(Croatian Foundation – Coach Support Version)
Purpose
At U11, the match is another opportunity to play and learn, not a test and not an evaluation.
The focus is not the scoreline.
The focus is how players engage with the game.
In Croatian methodology, match day reinforces confidence, bravery, and enjoyment, not outcomes.
Game Day Objectives (U11)
Element | Guidance
Game Intent | Encourage the same behaviours seen in training. If the week focused on dribbling or duels, the match question is simply: “Are players brave enough to try?”
Coach Voice | Keep sideline language very limited. Use 1–2 familiar, positive cues repeated calmly (e.g. “Be brave,” “Go again,” “Help your mate”).
Player Freedom | Allow players to find their own rhythm. Avoid directing play or positioning. Let them solve problems in real time.
Substitutions | Rotate evenly and early. All players experience different moments of the game. Learning always comes before results.
Half-Time Reflection | Keep it short and calm. Ask one or two simple questions: 1️⃣ “What went well for us?” 2️⃣ “Where can we be a bit braver?”
Coach Behaviour | Calm, encouraging, consistent. React to effort and intent, not the scoreline. Your tone sets the emotional safety of the game.
Croatian Match Day Principles (U11)
1️⃣ “Same words, same feeling.”→ Do not introduce new language on game day.
2️⃣ “Watch the behaviour, not the mistake.”→ Notice what players are trying, not what failed.
3️⃣ “Players play — coaches protect.”→ Protect confidence, rhythm, and enjoyment.
U11-Appropriate Sideline Language
(Encouragement, not instruction)
- “Be brave.”
- “Good effort.”
- “Go again.”
- “Help your mate.”
- “Nice idea.”
Back–mid–front · positional directions · tempo control · pressing commands

heading: U11 Coach Matchday Standards
headingPath: COACH PACK 1 (U11) – TECHNICAL DEVELOPMENT PHASE > 10. U11 Game Day Guidance > U11 Coach Matchday Standards
content:
Purpose
The coach sets the tone for the match environment.Professional detail builds credibility.Players mirror what they see — composure, preparation, and precision.
Coach Matchday Standards
APPEARANCE
Element | Standard
Belconnen Attire | Official Belconnen polo or approved top. Unified, consistent look across staff.
Footwear | Soccer footwear during warm-up and match. We look like football people, not spectators.
Sunglasses | Remove when speaking to players. Eye contact is essential for message absorption and connection.
Presentation | Neat, professional, deliberate. Our unified appearance signals control and precision.
GEAR
Item | Expectation
Balls | Pumped appropriately before arrival at pitch.
Bibs | Organised and accessible.
Cones | Blue and white matchday cones set and ready.
PREPAREDNESS
Element | Expectation
Substitution Plan | Rotation structure pre-planned. Know your intent before kick-off.
Team Talk | Clear theme linked to weekly focus. No improvisation.
Arrival Time | Minimum 45–60 minutes before kick-off. Field checked and organised.
Sideline Behaviour
Element | Standard
Coach Voice | Calm, clear, rhythmic.
Emotional Control | No visible frustration or emotional reactions.
Referee Interaction | Respectful at all times. Model behaviour.
Opposition Interaction | Professional greeting pre and post match.
Player Interaction | Eye contact, short cues, consistent language from training.
Parent Interaction | Friendly but brief. No tactical debate during game.
Belco Matchday Identity
1️⃣ Calm voice, clear cues.2️⃣ Detail before emotion.3️⃣ Teach relationships, not results.4️⃣ Represent the program with pride.

heading: U11 Pre-Match Warm-Up
headingPath: COACH PACK 1 (U11) – TECHNICAL DEVELOPMENT PHASE > 10. U11 Game Day Guidance > U11 Pre-Match Warm-Up
content:
(Croatian Foundation – Calm Readiness Model)
PRE-MATCH WARM-UP (20–25 MINUTES TOTAL)
Objective: Build rhythm, calm confidence, and connection with the ball — not intensity or instruction.
At U11, the pre-match warm-up is about feeling ready to play, not preparing a game plan.Its purpose is to help players:
- feel comfortable with the ball
- settle emotionally
- arrive at kick-off relaxed and confident
The warm-up should feel familiar, playful, and unforced.
U11 Warm-Up Principles
Warm-ups at this age should:
- include the ball early
- progress naturally from movement → ball → game
- avoid correction or instruction
- keep the coach’s voice calm and minimal
If players are smiling, moving freely, and enjoying the ball, the warm-up is doing its job.
U11 Pre-Match Warm-Up Structure
Block | Duration | Focus | Example Activity | Coach Tone & Cues
1️⃣ Movement & Feel | 4–5 mins | Rhythm, coordination, comfort | Free movement in small area: skips, turns, changes of direction, light accelerations | Calm, quiet“Light feet.”“Find your rhythm.”
2️⃣ Ball Comfort | 4–5 mins | Touch and confidence | One ball each: free touches, turns, feints, light 1v1 shadow play | “Enjoy the ball.”“Be brave.”
3️⃣ Play With a Mate | 4–5 mins | Connection and flow | Passing in pairs or small groups, movement after pass | “Pass and move.”“Help each other.”
4️⃣ Small Game | 5 mins | Awareness through play | 3v3 / 4v4 keep-ball or small possession game | Minimal voice“Keep it going.”
5️⃣ Direction to Goal | 4–5 mins | Confidence before kick-off | Small-sided game to goals or light finishing on the move | “Enjoy it.”“Play forward if you can.”
Important U11 Details
- The final 2–3 minutes are quiet
- Players hydrate and refocus
- No lines, no shouting, no urgency
- The coach’s body language sets the match tone
At U11, excitement comes from comfort, not hype.
Coach Reminder
Do not:
- correct technique
- stop activities
- introduce new language
Your role is to protect calmness and confidence.
If players feel good with the ball, they are ready.

heading: U11 Pre-Match Talk
headingPath: COACH PACK 1 (U11) – TECHNICAL DEVELOPMENT PHASE > 10. U11 Game Day Guidance > U11 Pre-Match Talk
content:
(Croatian Foundation – Emotional Anchoring)
PRE-MATCH TALK (2–3 MINUTES MAX)
Purpose
Set the emotional tone for the game.
At U11, the pre-match talk is not about instructions or reminders.
It is about helping players feel:
- calm
- confident
- ready to play
The game itself will take care of the rest.
Structure
Step | Coach Focus | Example Language (Belco tone, U11-safe)
1️⃣ Calm & Gather | Lower the energy, bring focus through stillness | “Eyes on me. Take a breath. We’re calm.”
2️⃣ Confidence First | Remind them they’re ready | “You’ve trained well. You’re ready to play.”
3️⃣ One Simple Anchor | ONE behaviour only | “Be brave on the ball.”
4️⃣ Team Feeling | Who are we today? | “Help each other. Stay involved.”
5️⃣ Finish Light | Release them to play | “Enjoy it. Go play.”
Key U11 Principles for Pre-Match Talks
1️⃣ Less is more→ One idea is enough
2️⃣ Emotion before information→ Calm players make better decisions
3️⃣ Familiar words only→ No new cues on match day
4️⃣ Silence matters→ Leave space after the last sentence
Coach Tone & Body Language
- Quiet voice
- Slow pace
- Short sentences
- Relaxed posture
At U11, how you speak matters more than what you say.

heading: U11 Half-Time Talk
headingPath: COACH PACK 1 (U11) – TECHNICAL DEVELOPMENT PHASE > 10. U11 Game Day Guidance > U11 Half-Time Talk
content:
(Croatian Foundation – Reset & Reassure)
HALF-TIME TALK (2–3 MINUTES MAX)
Purpose
Re-centre the team emotionally and mentally.
At U11, half-time is not a problem-solving meeting.It is a chance to:
- calm the group
- reinforce confidence
- gently refocus attention
Learning continues through the second half — not through explanation.
Structure
Step | Coach Focus | Example Language (Belco tone, U11-safe)
1️⃣ Calm the Group | Lower emotion, settle energy | “Sit down. Water. Take a breath.”
2️⃣ One Positive | Reinforce bravery or effort | “I like how you kept trying and stayed brave.”
3️⃣ One Gentle Adjustment | Behavioural nudge only | “When we lose it, let’s go again quicker.”
4️⃣ Light Question (Optional) | Invite awareness, not answers | “When did that work best?”
5️⃣ Release to Play | Reset confidence | “Same idea. Enjoy the second half.”
U11 Half-Time Rules
Rule | Meaning for Coach
One idea only | More than one creates confusion
Behaviour over outcome | Effort and intent matter most
Calm voice, calm body | You regulate the environment
Finish early | Silence helps messages land
Example U11 Half-Time Talk
“Good job. You stayed involved and kept trying.
When we lose the ball, let’s react quickly and help each other.
Same idea. Enjoy playing.”
Coach Behaviour Checklist (U11)
Aspect | Croatian Standard
Voice | Low, steady, unhurried
Body Language | Relaxed posture, at eye level
Focus | Confidence first, behaviour second
Timing | Never longer than 3 minutes
Consistency | Same tone, same words, every week
Key Takeaway for Coaches
At U11, half-time is about safety and rhythm — not correction.
If players return to the pitch:
- calm
- smiling
- willing to try
Then half-time has done its job.

heading: U11 Pre-Match Routine - Timeline
headingPath: COACH PACK 1 (U11) – TECHNICAL DEVELOPMENT PHASE > 10. U11 Game Day Guidance > U11 Pre-Match Routine - Timeline
content:
(Croatian Foundation – Calm Arrival Model)
Reference: All timings work backwards from KO (Kick-Off)
Context: No changerooms · players arrive changed
U11 Match-Day Timeline
Time (from KO) | Moment | Coach Focus | Coach Behaviour | Key Reminder
KO–50 | Arrival | Settle & connect | Greet players individually · light chat · no football talk | Human first
KO–40 | Free Ball Time | Comfort & confidence | Unstructured ball play · smiles encouraged | Ball removes nerves
KO–30 | Brief Gather | Emotional anchor | One calm message · short pause | One feeling only
KO–25 | Warm-Up | Activate through play | Ball-based · minimal voice | Warm up by playing
KO–10 | Final Cue | Confidence boost | One positive cue · quiet tone | Reminder, not instruction
KO–3 | Game Prep | Emotional calm | Encourage · smile · step back | Let them play
KO | Kick-Off | Observe & support | Coach less · watch more | Game teaches
Coach Warnings (U11)
Warning Sign | What It Means
Long speeches | Players switch off
Tactical detail | Too much thinking
Constant instructions | Confidence drops
Emotional intensity | Fear replaces freedom
U11 Match-Day Reminder
At U11, match day should feel just like training —the same tone, the same freedom, just with an opponent.
If the environment feels:
- calm
- familiar
- playful
then players will play their best football.
Key Croatian Principle (U11)
Arrival sets emotion.Emotion sets behaviour.
Your job is not to prepare football —it’s to protect the feeling that allows football to happen.

heading: U11 Pre-Match Routine - Detail
headingPath: COACH PACK 1 (U11) – TECHNICAL DEVELOPMENT PHASE > 10. U11 Game Day Guidance > U11 Pre-Match Routine - Detail
content:
🇭🇷 BELCO MATCHDAY BEHAVIOUR STANDARD (U11)Expanded Version – Coach Pack
Purpose (U11)
Matchday at U11 is about:
- emotional comfort
- confidence with the ball
- curiosity and willingness to try
- enjoyment of competition
It is not about:
- results
- systems or positions
- match management
- discipline through control
In Croatian methodology, U11 matches are learning environments, not performance tests.
Matchday Flow Explained (U11)
🕒 KO–50 | Arrival
Intent: Help players feel safe and settled.
Coach behaviour
- Greet every player by name
- Light, friendly conversation
- No football information
WhyYoung players cannot focus on football until they feel comfortable as people.
🕒 KO–40 | Free Ball Time
Intent: Remove nerves through the ball.
Coach behaviour
- Allow free ball play (juggling, 1v1s, small games)
- No organisation
- No correction unless safety is an issue
WhyIn Croatian environments, the ball is the primary emotional regulator.
🕒 KO–30 | Brief Group Gather (2 minutes max)
Intent: Create a simple emotional anchor.
Coach behaviour
- One idea only
- Calm voice
- Often framed as a question
U11-appropriate examples
- “When we have the ball, what helps us?”
- “How do we help our teammate?”
- “What do we do after we lose it?”
WhyQuestions invite awareness without instruction or pressure.
🕒 KO–25 | Warm-Up (Ball-Based)
Intent: Activate body and brain together.
Coach behaviour
- Ball involved immediately
- Small-sided, playful activities
- Minimal voice
WhyAt U11, warm-ups should look like football, not drills.
🕒 KO–10 | Final Reminder
Intent: Recall a familiar feeling.
Coach behaviour
- One short, positive cue
- Calm tone
U11-safe examples
- “Be brave.”
- “Help your mate.”
- “Enjoy the ball.”
WhyThis is a reminder, not instruction.
🕒 KO–3 | Game Prep
Intent: Emotional calm.
Coach behaviour
- Encourage
- Smile
- Step back
- No last instructions
WhySilence communicates trust.
🕒 KO | Kick-Off
Intent: Let the game teach.
Coach behaviour
- Observe more than instruct
- Intervene only for:
- safety
- emotional support
- very brief individual prompts
Croatian principleToo much coach voice = less player learning.
Coach Behaviour Standards (U11)
Do
- Stay calm and positive
- Use simple, familiar language
- Let players try and fail
- Reinforce effort and bravery
Avoid
- Sideline coaching
- Tactical explanations
- Emotional reactions to mistakes
- Over-stopping the game
Key Reminder for Coaches
If you coach a lot on matchday at U11,you are stealing learning moments.
Your job i
s not to manage the game.Your job is to protect the environment so the game can teach.

heading: U11 Game Framework
headingPath: COACH PACK 1 (U11) – TECHNICAL DEVELOPMENT PHASE > 10. U11 Game Day Guidance > U11 Game Framework
content:
(Replaces the 9v9 framework at U11)
U11 Game Framework
Game Reference – 9-a-Side
At U11, the game framework exists to:
- give players space and reference
- help coaches organise teams
- not define roles, patterns, or responsibilities
There is no fixed system at this age.
Team Organisation (U11)
- Players start in a balanced shape
- Positions are fluid
- Players are encouraged to:
- move freely
- explore different areas
- experience multiple moments of the game
Rotation is expected and encouraged across the season.
What Coaches Look For (Not What Players Are Told)
When we have the ball
- Are players asking for it?
- Are they brave enough to carry or pass?
- Are they helping the ball carrier?
When we lose the ball
- Do players react quickly?
- Do they try to get close and compete?
- Do they stay involved?
When the game flows
- Are players enjoying it?
- Is the ball moving?
- Is everyone engaged?
U11 Match-Day Principles (Game Behaviour)
Instead of instructions, we reinforce behaviours:
- Be brave on the ball
- Help your mate
- React when the ball changes
- Stay in the game
These behaviours matter more than shape.
Coach Use Only (Important)
Coaches may organise the starting shape for clarity and spacing.Coaches must not coach the shape during the game.
If the ball moves and players move with it, the framework is working.

heading: U11 Individual Competencies
headingPath: COACH PACK 1 (U11) – TECHNICAL DEVELOPMENT PHASE > 11. U11 Phase Outcomes > U11 Individual Competencies
content:
Theme: Comfort with the ball and bravery in individual actions.
By the end of the U11 phase, most players will increasingly show the following behaviours during games.These behaviours will appear often, not always, and will develop at different speeds for different players.
Emerging Individual Behaviours
- Comfort receiving and manipulating the ball with either foot in multiple directions.
- Willingness to receive the ball facing different directions and attempt to play forward when possible.
- Ability to change direction and speed while protecting the ball under pressure.
- Attempts to use feints, body movement, or changes of pace to unbalance an opponent.
- Use of short passing combinations under light pressure to stay involved in play.
- Engagement in 1v1 situations with the intent to compete, delay, or win the ball.
- Confidence striking the ball with different surfaces when opportunities arise.
- Attempts to choose between dribbling, passing, or shooting based on the moment.
- Visible courage and enjoyment in possession, even after mistakes.
- Immediate emotional reaction to losing possession (chasing, helping, recovering).
At U11, bravery with the ball matters more than clean execution.

heading: U11 Group Competencies
headingPath: COACH PACK 1 (U11) – TECHNICAL DEVELOPMENT PHASE > 11. U11 Phase Outcomes > U11 Group Competencies
content:
Theme: Beginning to play with teammates, not just around them.
The objective at this stage is small-group awareness, not team organisation.
Emerging Collective Habits
- Players naturally stay close enough to help each other around the ball.
- The team begins to move with the ball, not in fixed positions.
- Players offer simple support options to the ball carrier (early 2v1 behaviour).
- Quick emotional reaction when possession changes — attack to defend and defend to attack.
- Use of simple communication cues (“man on”, “help”, “turn”) when it feels natural.
- Celebration of effort in recovery and defending, not just goals.
- Informal triangle shapes appear around the ball without instruction.
- Players begin to stretch play with the ball and squeeze together without it.
- A habit of stepping forward to defend rather than dropping away.
- Goalkeeper begins to participate as a footballer, not just a shot-stopper.
At U11, the team idea starts with distance, effort, and shared rhythm — not structure.
Important Notes for Coaches
- These outcomes are observations, not expectations.
- Inconsistency is normal and healthy at this age.
- Absence of a behaviour does not mean lack of potential.
- Late developers often show these habits more clearly at U12–13.
The primary success measure at U11 is:
confidence, courage, and enjoyment of football.

heading: U11 sessions
headingPath: U11 sessions
content:
U11 – CYCLE 1Be Friends With the Ball (3 Weeks)
Week 1
Session 1 – Free Ball Play: Change & Protect
Session 2 – Keep the Ball in Chaos
Week 2
Session 1 – Direction Changes & Escapes
Session 2 – Shield, Turn & Go
Week 3
Session 1 – Ball Under Pressure Games
Session 2 – Dribble Survival Games

heading: U11 Cycle 1, week 1, session 1
headingPath: U11 sessions > U11 Cycle 1, week 1, session 1
content:
BELCONNEN UNITED FC – SESSION PLAN | CYCLE 1 | WEEK 1 | SESSION 1U11 | Be Friends With the Ball | Free Ball Play – Change & Protect
Session Outcome
Players feel more confident changing direction with the ball while protecting it from pressure.
Phase 1 – Introduction (10–15 mins)
Purpose
Let players freely explore changing direction and protecting the ball through play.
Setup
Area: 20x20m (adjust 18–24m)
Players: 10–12 (everyone with a ball)
Equipment: Cones, balls
Description
All players dribble freely inside the area. On their own timing, players change direction, speed, and surface while moving through traffic. Coaches introduce 2–3 passive “shadows” (no tackling) who move close and apply light pressure, forcing players to adjust, protect the ball, and move away. The activity should feel chaotic but playful, with lots of touches, collisions avoided through changes of direction, and players learning to keep the ball close under pressure.
Coach Behaviour
Observation cues:
- Do players keep the ball on the far foot when pressure arrives?
- Are direction changes helping players stay away from others?
Recognition comments:
- “Nice change — that kept the ball safe.”
- “Good protection — you stayed in control.”
- “I like how you moved away from pressure.”
Phase 2 – Main Part (25–30 mins)
Setup
Players: 3v3 or 4v4 (multiple games if needed)
Area: 20x25m (adjust 18x22 to 24x30)
Equipment: Balls, cones to mark end zones
Organisation: Continuous play with spare balls around the area
Description
Teams score by dribbling into the opponent’s end zone under control. Pressure is constant and close, encouraging players to change direction, slow down, protect the ball, and then move again. The game flows with frequent 1v1 moments and short bursts of possession. When it works well, players look calm on the ball, adjust their body, and use changes of direction to keep possession.
Rules & Success Conditions
Score by dribbling into the end zone with control
Immediate restart from the nearest ball
Single shaping rule: goals only count if the scorer has changed direction before entering the end zone
Managing Challenge (Same Game)
If the game is too easy:
- Reduce the width slightly
If the game is too hard:
- Increase the space
If energy or engagement drops:
- Change the direction of attack or swap end zones
Coach Behaviour – What to Watch For
Ball kept on the far foot when under pressure
Changes of direction to escape congestion
Calm protection before moving againObserve how these behaviours help players keep the ball, and adjust space or numbers without stopping play.
Phase 3 – Final Game (15–20 mins)
Purpose
Allow players to express free ball play in a realistic game.
Setup
Larger numbers: 5v5 or 6v6
Two goals
Normal football rules
Description
Free play with quick restarts and no conditions. Players are encouraged to enjoy having the ball, changing direction, and protecting it naturally as the game unfolds.
Transfer Emphasis (Optional)
Double goal if the scorer clearly changes direction away from pressure before scoring
Used only to highlight bravery and control on the ball
Coach Behaviour
Observation cues:
- Are players confident staying on the ball in tight spaces?
- Do they protect first before moving away?
Recognition phrases:
- “Brave on the ball.”
- “Nice change — you kept it.”
- “Good control under pressure.”
Belconnen United FC – Compact • Brave • Intelligent

heading: U11 Cycle 1, week 1, session 2

content:
BELCONNEN UNITED FC – SESSION PLAN | CYCLE 1 | WEEK 1 | SESSION 2U11 | Be Friends With the Ball | Keep the Ball in Chaos
Session Outcome
Players feel more confident keeping the ball under pressure in crowded, unpredictable situations.
Phase 1 – Introduction (10–15 mins)
Purpose
Expose players to chaos early and let them find control through the ball.
Setup
Area: 18x18m (adjust 16–22m)
Players: 10–12 (everyone with a ball)
Equipment: Cones, balls
Description
All players dribble freely inside a tight area. On the coach’s signal, players briefly pause, scan, then move again in any direction. Gradually, 2–3 players become “ball hunters” who try to knock other balls away (no tackling feet-to-feet). If a player loses their ball, they immediately recover any loose ball and rejoin. The game should feel messy, loud, and intense — lots of near-collisions, quick adjustments, and constant decision-making to protect the ball.
Coach Behaviour
Observation cues:
- Do players keep the ball close when space disappears?
- Are heads coming up before changing direction?
Recognition comments:
- “Good control — you stayed calm in traffic.”
- “Nice adjustment — you kept your ball alive.”
- “I like how you protected it under pressure.”
Phase 2 – Main Part (25–30 mins)
Setup
Players: 3v3 or 4v4 (multiple games if needed)
Area: 18x22m (adjust 16x20 to 22x26)
Equipment: Balls, 4 small gates per pitch
Organisation: Continuous play, spare balls around the outside
Description
Teams score by dribbling through any gate. Gates are spread unevenly, creating congestion and sudden pressure. Possession is often short, chaotic, and contested. Players must protect the ball, ride pressure, and move into new space to succeed. When the game is working well, players look composed despite traffic and show patience before escaping.
Rules & Success Conditions
Score by dribbling through any gate under control
Immediate restart from the nearest ball
Single shaping rule: players cannot score in the same gate twice in a row
Managing Challenge (Same Game)
If the game is too easy:
- Reduce space or remove one gate
If the game is too hard:
- Increase space or add an extra gate
If energy or engagement drops:
- Change gate positions to create a new picture
Coach Behaviour – What to Watch For
Close ball control in tight areas
Use of body to protect the ball
Calmness before changing directionObserve how these behaviours help players keep possession in chaos, and adjust space or gates without stopping play.
Phase 3 – Final Game (15–20 mins)
Purpose
Let players apply control and composure in a realistic game.
Setup
Larger numbers: 5v5 or 6v6
Two goals
Normal football rules
Description
Free play with quick restarts and no conditions. The game should feel open but still unpredictable, with many moments of pressure around the ball.
Transfer Emphasis (Optional)
Bonus goal if the scorer keeps the ball under pressure for a short moment before scoring
Used only to highlight calmness, not to force behaviour
Coach Behaviour
Observation cues:
- Do players stay composed when the game gets messy?
- Are they willing to hold the ball instead of rushing?
Recognition phrases:
- “Calm in chaos — well done.”
- “Good protection — you kept it.”
- “Brave to stay on the ball.”

heading: U11 Cycle 1, week 2, session 1

content:
BELCONNEN UNITED FC – SESSION PLAN | CYCLE 1 | WEEK 2 | SESSION 1U11 | Be Friends With the Ball | Direction Changes & Escapes
Session Outcome
Players feel more confident changing direction to escape pressure and keep the ball.
Phase 1 – Introduction (10–15 mins)
Purpose
Let players explore escaping pressure by changing direction with the ball.
Setup
Area: 18x18m (adjust 16–22m)
Players: 10–12 (everyone with a ball)
Equipment: Cones, balls
Description
All players dribble freely inside the area. The space is busy, with players constantly crossing paths. On their own timing, players change direction, speed, and surface to avoid others and keep control. After a few minutes, 2–3 players become light “chasers” who move close and apply pressure without tackling. Ball carriers respond by protecting the ball briefly, then changing direction to escape into new space. When it works well, the picture is full of sharp turns, sudden accelerations, and players staying calm as pressure arrives.
Coach Behaviour
Observation cues:
- Are players changing direction when pressure gets close?
- Do direction changes lead into movement away?
Recognition comments:
- “Nice turn — you escaped the pressure.”
- “Good change of direction, you kept the ball.”
- “I like how you moved away after the turn.”
Phase 2 – Main Part (25–30 mins)
Setup
Players: 2v2 or 3v3 (multiple games if needed)
Area: 15x20m (adjust 14x18 to 18x24)
Equipment: Balls, 4 small gates per pitch
Organisation: Continuous play, spare balls ready for quick restarts
Description
Teams score by dribbling through any gate. Gates are positioned so players are often forced toward pressure before escaping to score. Success comes from engaging an opponent, protecting the ball for a moment, then changing direction to break free. The game feels intense and full of short 1v1 battles. When it’s working well, players don’t rush — they draw pressure, turn away, and accelerate into space.
Rules & Success Conditions
Score by dribbling through any gate under control
Immediate restart from the nearest ball
Single shaping rule: players cannot score through the same gate twice in a row
Managing Challenge (Same Game)
If the game is too easy:
- Reduce space slightly or remove one gate
If the game is too hard:
- Increase space or add an extra gate
If energy or engagement drops:
- Rotate gate positions to change the picture
Coach Behaviour – What to Watch For
Use of turns to move away from pressure
First touch after the turn into free space
Willingness to engage an opponent before escapingObserve how these behaviours help players keep and progress with the ball, and adjust space or gates without stopping play.
Phase 3 – Final Game (15–20 mins)
Purpose
Allow players to apply direction changes and escapes in a free, realistic game.
Setup
Larger numbers: 5v5 or 6v6
Two goals
Normal football rules
Description
Free play with quick restarts and no added conditions. The game flows naturally, creating many moments of pressure around the ball. Players are free to use direction changes and escapes whenever they see the opportunity.
Transfer Emphasis (Optional)
Bonus goal if the scorer clearly changes direction to escape pressure before scoring
Used to highlight the behaviour, not to force it
Coach Behaviour
Observation cues:
- Do players stay brave when pressure arrives?
- Are escapes creating space to move forward?
Recognition phrases:
- “Great escape.”
- “Nice turn under pressure.”
- “Brave to stay on the ball.”
Belconnen United FC – Compact • Brave • Intelligent

heading: U11 Cycle 1, week 2, session 2
headingPath: U11 sessions > U11 Cycle 1, week 2, session 2
content:
BELCONNEN UNITED FC – SESSION PLAN | CYCLE 1 | WEEK 2 | SESSION 2U11 | Be Friends With the Ball | Shield, Turn & Go
Session Outcome
Players feel more confident protecting the ball, turning away from pressure, and accelerating into space.
Phase 1 – Introduction (10–15 mins)
Purpose
Introduce protecting the ball and escaping pressure through play.
Setup
Area: 20x20m (adjust 18–24m)
Players: 10–12 (everyone with a ball)
Equipment: Cones, balls
Description
All players move freely with a ball inside the area. A small number of players are designated as “chasers” without a ball. Chasers apply light pressure by getting close and forcing ball carriers to protect the ball. Ball carriers keep possession by shielding, using turns, and accelerating away into space. When a chaser wins the ball or forces it out, roles swap immediately. The game feels busy, noisy, and full of short battles where players constantly protect, turn, and move away.
Coach Behaviour
Observation cues:
- Are players placing their body between the ball and the pressure?
- Do turns lead immediately into movement away, not stopping?
Recognition comments:
- “Good body between ball and opponent — that bought you time.”
- “Nice turn — you escaped the pressure and found space.”
- “Strong shield — now go!”
Phase 2 – Main Part (25–30 mins)
Setup
Players: 2v2 or 3v3 (rotate pairs if needed)
Area: 15x20m (adjust 14x18 to 18x24)
Equipment: Balls, small goals or end zones
Organisation: Multiple games running side-by-side for high contact and quick restarts
Description
Small-sided games where players score by carrying the ball into an end zone or finishing in a small goal. Pressure is constant and close. Success comes from protecting the ball under contact, turning away from an opponent, and accelerating forward. The game works best when players are brave enough to engage defenders, shield first, then escape with purpose rather than rushing or panicking.
Rules & Success Conditions
Score by dribbling into the end zone or finishing in a small goal
Play restarts immediately from the coach or nearest ball
Single shaping rule: players must receive or collect the ball under pressure (no free starts)
Managing Challenge (Same Game)
If the game is too easy:
- Reduce space slightly
If the game is too hard:
- Increase space slightly
If energy drops:
- Change scoring direction or swap end zones for goals
Coach Behaviour – What to Watch For
Shielding with body shape and arm position
First touch into space after the turn
Willingness to engage an opponent before escapingObserve how these actions help players keep the ball and move forward, and adjust space or numbers without stopping play.
Phase 3 – Final Game (15–20 mins)
Purpose
Allow players to apply shielding, turning, and accelerating in a free game.
Setup
Larger numbers: 5v5 or 6v6
Two goals
Normal football rules
Description
Free play with quick restarts and no conditions. Players play naturally, with plenty of 1v1 moments. The focus is on bravery in possession — engaging pressure, protecting the ball, and escaping when the moment is right.
Transfer Emphasis (Optional)
Bonus goal for a player who shields and turns away from pressure before scoring
Used only to highlight the behaviour, not to force it
Coach Behaviour
Observation cues:
- Do players stay calm when pressure arrives?
- Are turns followed by forward movement?
Recognition phrases:
- “Strong shield — you stayed in control.”
- “Great turn, you broke away.”
- “Brave on the ball — keep going.”
Belconnen United FC – Compact • Brave • Intelligent

content:
BELCONNEN UNITED FC – SESSION PLAN | CYCLE 1 | WEEK 3 | SESSION 1U11 | Be Friends With the Ball | Ball Under Pressure Games
Session Outcome
Players feel more confident staying on the ball when an opponent is close and pressure is constant.
Phase 1 – Introduction (10–15 mins)
Purpose
Immerse players in constant ball pressure and let them discover ways to stay in control.
Setup
Area: 16x16m (adjust 14–20m)
Players: 10–12 (everyone with a ball)
Equipment: Cones, balls
Description
All players dribble freely inside a tight area. Space is crowded from the start. On their own timing, players adjust speed, direction, and body position to keep their ball. After a few minutes, 2–3 players become active pressure players who try to get close and force mistakes without tackling hard. If a ball is lost or knocked away, the player immediately recovers any free ball and continues. When it works well, the area feels intense and noisy, with players constantly adjusting their body, shielding briefly, and moving away under pressure.
Coach Behaviour
Observation cues:
- Do players stay balanced when pressure arrives?
- Are players protecting first before moving again?
Recognition comments:
- “Good balance — you stayed on the ball.”
- “Strong protection under pressure.”
- “I like how you didn’t panic.”
Phase 2 – Main Part (25–30 mins)
Setup
Players: 3v3 or 4v4 (multiple games if needed)
Area: 18x22m (adjust 16x20 to 22x26)
Equipment: Balls, small goals or end zones
Organisation: Continuous games with spare balls ready for instant restarts
Description
Small-sided games where pressure is immediate and constant. Teams score by dribbling into an end zone or finishing in a small goal. Defenders are encouraged to stay tight, creating frequent contact and contested moments. Success comes from players staying calm on the ball, using their body to protect it, and finding a way to move forward despite pressure. When the game is working well, players don’t rush passes or touches — they accept contact and keep the ball alive.
Rules & Success Conditions
Score by dribbling into the end zone or finishing in a small goal
Immediate restart from the coach or nearest ball
Single shaping rule: defenders must start close (within one arm’s length)
Managing Challenge (Same Game)
If the game is too easy:
- Reduce space slightly
If the game is too hard:
- Increase space slightly
If energy or engagement drops:
- Change scoring method (end zones ↔ small goals)
Coach Behaviour – What to Watch For
Use of body to protect the ball
Calm first touch under pressure
Willingness to stay engaged instead of clearing the ballObserve how these behaviours help players keep possession under pressure, and adjust space or scoring without stopping play.
Phase 3 – Final Game (15–20 mins)
Purpose
Allow players to apply ball-under-pressure behaviours freely in a realistic game.
Setup
Larger numbers: 5v5 or 6v6
Two goals
Normal football rules
Description
Free play with quick restarts and no conditions. Pressure emerges naturally from the game. Players are free to hold, protect, or move the ball as they choose.
Transfer Emphasis (Optional)
Bonus goal if the scorer stays on the ball under pressure before scoring
Used to highlight bravery and control, not to force decisions
Coach Behaviour
Observation cues:
- Do players stay composed when pressed?
- Are they willing to protect the ball instead of rushing?
Recognition phrases:
- “Strong under pressure.”
- “Calm on the ball.”
- “Brave to stay with it.”

heading: U11 Cycle 1, week 3, session 2
headingPath: U11 sessions > U11 Cycle 1, week 3, session 2
content:
BELCONNEN UNITED FC – SESSION PLAN | CYCLE 1 | WEEK 3 | SESSION 2U11 | Be Friends With the Ball | Dribble Survival Games
Session Outcome
Players feel more confident surviving with the ball under pressure by protecting, adjusting, and continuing to dribble.
Phase 1 – Introduction (10–15 mins)
Purpose
Drop players straight into dribble survival and let them discover how to stay alive on the ball.
Setup
Area: 16x16m (adjust 14–20m)
Players: 10–12 (everyone with a ball)
Equipment: Cones, balls
Description
All players dribble freely inside a tight area. From the start, 2–3 players are active “ball hunters” whose only job is to knock other balls away. There are no stoppages — if a player loses their ball, they immediately recover any loose ball and continue. The space feels chaotic and competitive, with constant pressure, quick reactions, and lots of small adjustments. When it works well, players slow down, protect the ball, change direction sharply, and survive instead of panicking.
Coach Behaviour
Observation cues:
- Do players keep the ball close when pressure is everywhere?
- Are they using their body to protect instead of turning away blindly?
Recognition comments:
- “You survived that — well done.”
- “Good protection, you kept your ball.”
- “I like how you stayed calm.”
Phase 2 – Main Part (25–30 mins)
Setup
Players: 2v2 or 3v3 (multiple games if needed)
Area: 15x20m (adjust 14x18 to 18x24)
Equipment: Balls, central survival zone marked with cones
Organisation: Continuous games, spare balls ready for instant restarts
Description
Teams play small-sided games where the ball is always contested. To score, a player must dribble into the central survival zone and keep the ball under control for a short moment before exiting. Defenders apply immediate pressure, forcing attackers to protect, adjust, and survive before escaping. The game feels intense, physical (within reason), and full of repeated 1v1 battles. When it’s working well, players don’t rush — they fight to keep the ball first, then find space.
Rules & Success Conditions
Score by dribbling into the survival zone, keeping control, then exiting
Immediate restart from the nearest ball
Single shaping rule: defenders must stay tight and engage (no dropping off)
Managing Challenge (Same Game)
If the game is too easy:
- Reduce the size of the survival zone
If the game is too hard:
- Increase the size of the survival zone
If energy or engagement drops:
- Move the survival zone to a different position
Coach Behaviour – What to Watch For
Ball protection using body and arm
Small touches to keep the ball alive
Willingness to engage pressure instead of avoiding itObserve how these behaviours help players survive with the ball, and adjust space or zone size without stopping play.
Phase 3 – Final Game (15–20 mins)
Purpose
Let players apply dribble survival naturally in a realistic game.
Setup
Larger numbers: 5v5 or 6v6
Two goals
Normal football rules
Description
Free play with quick restarts and no added conditions. The game flows naturally, creating repeated moments where players must survive pressure before progressing or scoring.
Transfer Emphasis (Optional)
Bonus goal if the scorer clearly protects the ball under pressure before scoring
Used to highlight survival, not to force decisions
Coach Behaviour
Observation cues:
- Do players stay with the ball when challenged?
- Are they protecting first before trying to escape?
Recognition phrases:
- “Strong survival.”
- “You stayed on the ball.”
- “Brave under pressure.”
Belconnen United FC – Compact • Brave • Intelligent

content:
BELCONNEN UNITED FC – SESSION PLAN | CYCLE 2 | WEEK 1 | SESSION 1U11 | Beat Your Player | 1v1 Any Direction
Session Outcome
Players feel more confident engaging an opponent and beating them with the ball in any direction.
Phase 1 – Introduction (10–15 mins)
Purpose
Invite players to attack space and opponents freely with the ball.
Setup
Area: 20x20m (adjust 18–24m)
Players: 10–12 (everyone with a ball)
Equipment: Cones, balls
Description
All players dribble freely inside the area. Space is shared and unpredictable. Players are encouraged to move in any direction, changing speed and angle to avoid collisions. After a few minutes, 2–3 players become light “chasers” who move close and try to touch another player’s shoulder (no tackling). Ball carriers respond by shifting direction, accelerating, or changing rhythm to escape. When it’s working well, players look adventurous — drawing pressure, cutting away sharply, and continuing their dribble.
Coach Behaviour
Observation cues:
- Are players changing direction when an opponent gets close?
- Do escapes lead into new space?
Recognition comments:
- “Nice change — you lost them.”
- “Good acceleration after the move.”
- “Brave to take them on.”
Phase 2 – Main Part (25–30 mins)
Setup
Players: 1v1 (multiple pitches running at once)
Area: 12x15m (adjust 10x14 to 14x18)
Equipment: Balls, 4 corner end zones or small gates
Organisation: Quick rotations; spare balls ready for immediate restarts
Description
Pure 1v1 games with no fixed direction. The attacker can score by dribbling into any end zone or through any gate. Defenders apply immediate pressure. Because scoring options exist in all directions, attackers must read the defender, shift the ball, and beat them using direction changes, feints, and acceleration. When it’s working well, attackers commit the defender, change direction sharply, and explode into space.
Rules & Success Conditions
Score by dribbling into any end zone or through any gate
Immediate restart with a new ball
Single shaping rule: attacker must engage the defender before scoring (no free runs)
Managing Challenge (Same Game)
If the game is too easy:
- Reduce the area slightly
If the game is too hard:
- Increase the area slightly
If energy or engagement drops:
- Change gate positions to create a new picture
Coach Behaviour – What to Watch For
Willingness to engage the defender
Direction changes to unbalance the opponent
Acceleration after the moveObserve how these actions help players beat their player, and adjust space or gate layout without stopping play.
Phase 3 – Final Game (15–20 mins)
Purpose
Let players apply 1v1 bravery freely in a realistic game.
Setup
Larger numbers: 5v5 or 6v6
Two goals
Normal football rules
Description
Free play with quick restarts and no added conditions. The game naturally creates many 1v1 moments in different areas of the pitch. Players are encouraged to take on opponents whenever the opportunity appears.
Transfer Emphasis (Optional)
Bonus goal if the scorer beats an opponent in a clear 1v1 before scoring
Used to highlight bravery, not to force decisions
Coach Behaviour
Observation cues:
- Are players willing to attack opponents with the ball?
- Do they change direction to create separation?
Recognition phrases:
- “Take them on.”
- “Great move.”
- “Brave in the 1v1.”
Belconnen United FC – Compact • Brave • Intelligent

heading: U11 Cycle 2, week 1, session 2
