content:
BELCONNEN UNITED FC – SESSION PLAN | CYCLE 2 | WEEK 1 | SESSION 2U11 | Beat Your Player | Attack the Space to Score
Session Outcome
Players feel more confident recognising space after beating a player and driving forward to score.
Phase 1 – Introduction (10–15 mins)
Purpose
Encourage players to notice space and attack it immediately with the ball.
Setup
Area: 20x20m (adjust 18–24m)
Players: 10–12 (everyone with a ball)
Equipment: Cones, balls
Description
All players dribble freely inside the area. Space is open and shared. Players are encouraged to move at different speeds, change direction, and carry the ball into open spaces. After a few minutes, 2–3 players become passive pressure players who move close and block space rather than tackle. Ball carriers respond by changing direction and accelerating into new space. When it’s working well, players lift their head, see space opening, and attack it with purpose.
Coach Behaviour
Observation cues:
- Do players recognise space opening in front of them?
- Are they accelerating into space rather than drifting?
Recognition comments:
- “Good drive into space.”
- “You saw it and went.”
- “Nice speed change forward.”
Phase 2 – Main Part (25–30 mins)
Setup
Players: 2v2 or 3v3 (multiple games if needed)
Area: 18x25m (adjust 16x22 to 22x30)
Equipment: Balls, small goals or end zones
Organisation: Continuous games with quick restarts and spare balls
Description
Small-sided games where teams score by driving into space and finishing in a small goal or end zone. Defenders start close, forcing attackers to engage, beat pressure, and then attack the space that appears. The game flows quickly with repeated moments where space opens briefly. When it’s working well, players take their first touch forward, accelerate decisively, and attack the goal rather than slowing down.
Rules & Success Conditions
Score by finishing in a small goal or dribbling into an end zone
Immediate restart from the nearest ball
Single shaping rule: goals only count if the scorer attacks forward space (no sideways finishes)
Managing Challenge (Same Game)
If the game is too easy:
- Reduce space slightly or narrow the pitch
If the game is too hard:
- Increase space slightly
If energy or engagement drops:
- Change goal positions or swap end zones for goals
Coach Behaviour – What to Watch For
First touch that goes forward into space
Acceleration once space is recognised
Willingness to drive at goal instead of slowing playObserve how these actions help players turn space into scoring chances, and adjust space or direction without stopping play.
Phase 3 – Final Game (15–20 mins)
Purpose
Allow players to apply attacking space freely in a realistic game.
Setup
Larger numbers: 5v5 or 6v6
Two goals
Normal football rules
Description
Free play with quick restarts and no added conditions. Space appears naturally through the game. Players are free to attack forward when they see the opportunity.
Transfer Emphasis (Optional)
Bonus goal if the scorer clearly drives into open space before scoring
Used to reward intent, not to force decisions
Coach Behaviour
Observation cues:
- Do players attack space with confidence?
- Are they positive once space opens?
Recognition phrases:
- “Attack that space.”
- “Good drive forward.”
- “You went when it opened.”
Belconnen United FC – Compact • Brave • Intelligent

heading: U11 Cycle 2, week 2, session 1
headingPath: U11 sessions > U11 Cycle 2, week 2, session 1
content:
BELCONNEN UNITED FC – SESSION PLAN | CYCLE 2 | WEEK 2 | SESSION 1U11 | Beat Your Player | Fake, Go & Accelerate
Session Outcome
Players feel more confident using a fake to unbalance an opponent, then accelerating away with the ball.
Phase 1 – Introduction (10–15 mins)
Purpose
Let players discover how a fake creates space to explode away with the ball.
Setup
Area: 20x20m (adjust 18–24m)
Players: 10–12 (everyone with a ball)
Equipment: Cones, balls
Description
All players dribble freely inside the area. Space is shared and busy. Players are encouraged to change speed and direction often. After a few minutes, 2–3 players become light “blockers” who step into passing or dribbling lines without tackling. Ball carriers respond by faking one way — with body, shoulder, or foot — then quickly going the other way and accelerating into new space. When it works well, the picture is full of sharp fakes, sudden bursts of speed, and players confidently escaping traffic.
Coach Behaviour
Observation cues:
- Do players use a fake before changing direction?
- Is there a clear speed change after the fake?
Recognition comments:
- “Nice fake — you shifted them.”
- “Good burst after the move.”
- “I like how you sold it, then went.”
Phase 2 – Main Part (25–30 mins)
Setup
Players: 1v1 or 2v2 (multiple games running)
Area: 12x18m (adjust 10x16 to 14x20)
Equipment: Balls, small goals or end zones
Organisation: Quick rotations, spare balls ready for immediate restarts
Description
Small-sided games focused on beating a direct opponent. Attackers start with the ball under pressure and score by dribbling into an end zone or finishing in a small goal. Defenders stay tight, encouraging attackers to use a fake to move them before exploding into space. The game flows quickly with repeated 1v1 moments. When it’s working well, attackers commit the defender, fake with intent, then accelerate decisively away.
Rules & Success Conditions
Score by dribbling into the end zone or finishing in the small goal
Immediate restart with a new ball
Single shaping rule: attacker must attempt a fake before scoring
Managing Challenge (Same Game)
If the game is too easy:
- Reduce space slightly
If the game is too hard:
- Increase space slightly
If energy or engagement drops:
- Change scoring direction or swap end zones and goals
Coach Behaviour – What to Watch For
Clear fake that unbalances the defender
Acceleration immediately after the fake
Willingness to engage the defender instead of avoiding themObserve how these behaviours help players beat their opponent, and adjust space or numbers without stopping play.
Phase 3 – Final Game (15–20 mins)
Purpose
Allow players to apply fakes and acceleration naturally in a realistic game.
Setup
Larger numbers: 5v5 or 6v6
Two goals
Normal football rules
Description
Free play with quick restarts and no added conditions. The game naturally creates moments where players face opponents directly and must decide whether to fake, go, or accelerate.
Transfer Emphasis (Optional)
Bonus goal if the scorer clearly uses a fake before accelerating past an opponent
Used to highlight the behaviour, not to force it
Coach Behaviour
Observation cues:
- Do players try to unbalance opponents before going?
- Is there real speed after the fake?
Recognition phrases:
- “Great fake.”
- “You shifted them, then went.”
- “Exploded away — well done.”
Belconnen United FC – Compact • Brave • Intelligent

heading: U11 Cycle 2, week 2, session 2
headingPath: U11 sessions > U11 Cycle 2, week 2, session 2
content:
BELCONNEN UNITED FC – SESSION PLAN | CYCLE 2 | WEEK 2 | SESSION 2U11 | Beat Your Player | Beat Them to Finish
Session Outcome
Players feel more confident beating an opponent and finishing the action with a shot or goal.
Phase 1 – Introduction (10–15 mins)
Purpose
Link beating a player directly to finishing with intent.
Setup
Area: 20x20m (adjust 18–24m)
Players: 10–12 (everyone with a ball)
Equipment: Cones, balls, 2–4 mini goals on the outside
Description
Players dribble freely inside the area with mini goals placed around the edge. On their own timing, players choose a goal to attack, but must beat a nearby player (through a fake, change of direction, or burst) before finishing into a goal. There is no set defender — pressure comes naturally from proximity and traffic. When it’s working well, players carry the ball with purpose, commit someone, then accelerate and finish quickly.
Coach Behaviour
Observation cues:
- Do players look to beat someone before shooting?
- Is there intent to finish immediately after escaping?
Recognition comments:
- “You beat them and finished — perfect.”
- “Nice move, straight to goal.”
- “Good decision to go and score.”
Phase 2 – Main Part (25–30 mins)
Setup
Players: 2v2 or 3v3 (multiple games if needed)
Area: 18x25m (adjust 16x22 to 22x30)
Equipment: Balls, small goals
Organisation: Continuous games, spare balls behind goals for fast restarts
Description
Small-sided games where the objective is to beat a direct opponent and finish. Goals are close enough to encourage dribbling at defenders rather than shooting early. Defenders start tight, creating frequent 1v1 moments near goal. When it’s working well, attackers engage the defender, shift them with a move, then drive forward and finish decisively instead of hesitating.
Rules & Success Conditions
Score by finishing in the small goal
Immediate restart from the nearest ball
Single shaping rule: goals only count if the attacker has beaten a defender just before the finish
Managing Challenge (Same Game)
If the game is too easy:
- Reduce space or move goals slightly closer together
If the game is too hard:
- Increase space or widen the pitch
If energy or engagement drops:
- Rotate goals or change attack direction
Coach Behaviour – What to Watch For
Commitment to the defender before finishing
Acceleration after the move
Positive, quick finishing actionsObserve how these behaviours lead to goals, and adjust space or goal position without stopping play.
Phase 3 – Final Game (15–20 mins)
Purpose
Let players freely connect beating a player with scoring in a real game.
Setup
Larger numbers: 5v5 or 6v6
Two goals
Normal football rules
Description
Free play with quick restarts and no added conditions. Natural 1v1 moments appear around the pitch, especially near goal. Players are encouraged to take responsibility to beat an opponent and finish when the chance appears.
Transfer Emphasis (Optional)
Double goal if the scorer clearly beats an opponent just before scoring
Used to reward bravery and decisiveness, not to force behaviour
Coach Behaviour
Observation cues:
- Do players attack defenders near goal?
- Are finishes decisive after beating someone?
Recognition phrases:
- “Beat them and score.”
- “Great take-on.”
- “You committed them and finished.”
Belconnen United FC – Compact • Brave • Intelligent

heading: U11 Cycle 2, week 3, session 1
headingPath: U11 sessions > U11 Cycle 2, week 3, session 1
content:
BELCONNEN UNITED FC – SESSION PLAN | CYCLE 2 | WEEK 3 | SESSION 1U11 | Beat Your Player | Duel Festival
Session Outcome
Players feel more confident engaging opponents in repeated 1v1 duels and competing to win the ball or beat their player.
Phase 1 – Introduction (10–15 mins)
Purpose
Drop players straight into constant duels and let them enjoy competing with the ball.
Setup
Area: 18x18m (adjust 16–22m)
Players: 10–12 (everyone with a ball)
Equipment: Cones, balls
Description
All players dribble freely inside the area. From the start, players are encouraged to challenge each other in light 1v1 duels — shoulder-to-shoulder moments, crossing paths, and protecting the ball when someone comes close. After a few minutes, players are encouraged to actively seek duels by moving toward others rather than avoiding them. The space feels busy and competitive, with lots of short battles, ball protection, fakes, and quick escapes. When it works well, players enjoy the challenge and stay brave on the ball.
Coach Behaviour
Observation cues:
- Are players willing to enter duels instead of avoiding them?
- Do they stay balanced and strong when contact arrives?
Recognition comments:
- “Good duel — you stayed strong.”
- “Brave to take them on.”
- “I like how you didn’t back out.”
Phase 2 – Main Part (25–30 mins)
Setup
Players: 1v1 or 2v2 (multiple pitches running at once)
Area: 12x15m (adjust 10x14 to 14x18)
Equipment: Balls, small goals or end zones
Organisation: Fast rotations; spare balls ready for instant restarts
Description
A series of rapid-fire duel games where every action is contested. In 1v1, attackers try to beat their opponent and score; defenders try to win the ball and counter immediately. In 2v2, duels happen constantly as players attack and defend in tight spaces. The intensity stays high, with short games and frequent restarts. When it’s working well, players actively hunt duels, engage opponents with confidence, and fight to win their moment.
Rules & Success Conditions
Score by finishing in a small goal or dribbling into an end zone
Immediate restart from the coach or nearest ball
Single shaping rule: all restarts begin with an immediate duel (defender starts close)
Managing Challenge (Same Game)
If the game is too easy:
- Reduce space slightly
If the game is too hard:
- Increase space slightly
If energy or engagement drops:
- Switch between 1v1 and 2v2 formats
Coach Behaviour – What to Watch For
Willingness to engage contact
Use of body to protect or win the ball
Quick reactions after winning or losing a duelObserve how these behaviours affect confidence and competitiveness, and adjust space or numbers without stopping play.
Phase 3 – Final Game (15–20 mins)
Purpose
Let players express duel mentality freely in a realistic game.
Setup
Larger numbers: 5v5 or 6v6
Two goals
Normal football rules
Description
Free play with quick restarts and no added conditions. Duels emerge naturally all over the pitch. Players are free to compete, take on opponents, and fight for the ball.
Transfer Emphasis (Optional)
Double goal if the scorer wins a clear 1v1 duel just before scoring
Used to highlight competitiveness, not to force behaviour
Coach Behaviour
Observation cues:
- Are players enjoying duels and competing repeatedly?
- Do they recover quickly after losing a duel?
Recognition phrases:
- “Good battle.”
- “You stayed in the duel.”
- “Brave and competitive.”
Belconnen United FC – Compact • Brave • Intelligent

heading: U11 Cycle 2, week 3, session 2
headingPath: U11 sessions > U11 Cycle 2, week 3, session 2
content:
BELCONNEN UNITED FC – SESSION PLAN | CYCLE 2 | WEEK 3 | SESSION 2U11 | Beat Your Player | 1v1 → 2v2 Transition Games
Session Outcome
Players feel more confident beating an opponent and reacting quickly when the game turns into a 2v2.
Phase 1 – Introduction (10–15 mins)
Purpose
Prepare players to engage a 1v1 and react immediately when another player joins.
Setup
Area: 18x18m (adjust 16–22m)
Players: 10–12 (everyone with a ball)
Equipment: Cones, balls
Description
All players dribble freely inside the area. Players are encouraged to move toward others and engage in short 1v1 moments — fakes, changes of direction, and shoulder-to-shoulder duels. On the coach’s call, players pair up with the nearest player and briefly play keep-ball together before separating and continuing to dribble alone. The feel is fluid and unpredictable: individual duels constantly appear, then quickly become shared moments before breaking apart again.
Coach Behaviour
Observation cues:
- Are players comfortable stepping into a 1v1?
- Do they react quickly when a teammate arrives?
Recognition comments:
- “Good duel — you stayed brave.”
- “Nice reaction when the picture changed.”
- “You adjusted quickly — well done.”
Phase 2 – Main Part (25–30 mins)
Setup
Players: 1v1 that naturally becomes 2v2
Area: 15x20m (adjust 14x18 to 18x24)
Equipment: Balls, small goals
Organisation: Two attackers start inside; two support players wait outside the pitch, one on each side
Description
Each game begins as a 1v1. As soon as the attacker beats the defender or the defender wins the ball, a second player from each team enters the pitch, turning the game into a 2v2. Play continues until a goal is scored or the ball goes out, then resets to 1v1 again. The game constantly shifts between individual battles and shared moments. When it’s working well, players commit fully to the 1v1, then immediately adapt — either supporting, combining, or defending once the second player arrives.
Rules & Success Conditions
Score by finishing in the small goal
Game always starts as 1v1 and becomes 2v2 on the trigger
Single shaping rule: the second player must enter immediately (no delay)
Managing Challenge (Same Game)
If the game is too easy:
- Reduce space slightly
If the game is too hard:
- Increase space slightly
If energy or engagement drops:
- Change the entry side of the second players
Coach Behaviour – What to Watch For
Commitment and bravery in the initial 1v1
Quick reactions when the second player enters
Ability to continue attacking or defending without hesitationObserve how players handle the shift from solo to shared play, and adjust space or entry timing without stopping the game.
Phase 3 – Final Game (15–20 mins)
Purpose
Allow players to apply 1v1 bravery and 2v2 reactions naturally in a realistic game.
Setup
Larger numbers: 5v5 or 6v6
Two goals
Normal football rules
Description
Free play with quick restarts and no added conditions. The game naturally shifts between individual duels and small group situations. Players are free to attack alone or combine as the moment demands.
Transfer Emphasis (Optional)
Double goal if a goal comes after a clear 1v1 that quickly becomes a 2v2
Used to highlight awareness and reaction, not to force behaviour
Coach Behaviour
Observation cues:
- Do players engage 1v1 moments confidently?
- Do they adjust quickly when numbers change?
Recognition phrases:
- “Great reaction.”
- “You stayed brave, then adapted.”
- “Good awareness when help arrived.”
Belconnen United FC – Compact • Brave • Intelligent

heading: U11 Cycle 3, week 1, session 1
headingPath: U11 sessions > U11 Cycle 3, week 1, session 1
content:
BELCONNEN UNITED FC – SESSION PLAN | CYCLE 3 | WEEK 1 | SESSION 1U11 | Play With a Mate | Pass & Move to Keep It
Session Outcome
Players feel more confident using a pass and movement to keep possession with a teammate.
Phase 1 – Introduction (10–15 mins)
Purpose
Introduce the idea that the ball can move faster and safer with a mate.
Setup
Area: 20x20m (adjust 18–24m)
Players: 10–12 (pairs sharing one ball)
Equipment: Cones, balls
Description
Players work in pairs, sharing one ball per pair, moving freely inside the area. Pairs pass while walking, jogging, and changing direction to avoid others. Pressure is natural from traffic rather than defenders. If pairs lose control, they simply reset and continue. The activity should feel light, social, and fluid. When it works well, players pass, move into new space, and keep the ball alive together without stopping.
Coach Behaviour
Observation cues:
- Do players move after passing to stay connected?
- Are pairs adjusting distance to keep the ball safe?
Recognition comments:
- “Nice pass — you moved to help again.”
- “Good support — you stayed available.”
- “I like how you kept it together.”
Phase 2 – Main Part (25–30 mins)
Setup
Players: 2v2 or 3v3 (multiple games if needed)
Area: 18x22m (adjust 16x20 to 22x26)
Equipment: Balls, end zones or small gates
Organisation: Continuous play with spare balls around the area
Description
Small-sided games focused on keeping possession. Teams score by completing a pass and then moving into space to receive again, before playing into an end zone or through a gate. Pressure is constant but manageable, encouraging players to use a teammate instead of forcing dribbles. When the game is working well, players pass, move immediately, and stay connected to keep the ball.
Rules & Success Conditions
Score by passing and then receiving again before entering an end zone or gate
Immediate restart from the nearest ball
Single shaping rule: the passer must move to a new space after passing
Managing Challenge (Same Game)
If the game is too easy:
- Reduce space slightly
If the game is too hard:
- Increase space slightly
If energy or engagement drops:
- Change scoring direction or move gates
Coach Behaviour – What to Watch For
Movement immediately after passing
Supporting angles and distances
Willingness to use a teammate to keep the ballObserve how pass-and-move actions help teams keep possession, and adjust space or numbers without stopping play.
Phase 3 – Final Game (15–20 mins)
Purpose
Allow players to apply pass-and-move naturally in a free game.
Setup
Larger numbers: 5v5 or 6v6
Two goals
Normal football rules
Description
Free play with quick restarts and no added conditions. Players are free to dribble or pass, but moments naturally appear where using a teammate helps keep the ball.
Transfer Emphasis (Optional)
Double goal if a goal comes after a clear pass and immediate movement to receive again
Used to highlight cooperation, not to force it
Coach Behaviour
Observation cues:
- Do players move after passing?
- Are teammates helping the ball carrier?
Recognition phrases:
- “Pass and move — well done.”
- “Good support.”
- “You kept it together.”
Belconnen United FC – Compact • Brave • Intelligent

heading: U11 Cycle 3, week 1, session 2
headingPath: U11 sessions > U11 Cycle 3, week 1, session 2
content:
BELCONNEN UNITED FC – SESSION PLAN | CYCLE 3 | WEEK 1 | SESSION 2U11 | Play With a Mate | Help the Ball Carrier
Session Outcome
Players feel more confident moving to help a teammate keep the ball under pressure.
Phase 1 – Introduction (10–15 mins)
Purpose
Help players recognise that movement without the ball supports the ball carrier.
Setup
Area: 20x20m (adjust 18–24m)
Players: 10–12 (pairs with one ball)
Equipment: Cones, balls
Description
Players work in pairs, sharing one ball. Pairs move freely inside the area, passing while walking or jogging. The ball carrier is encouraged to keep moving, while the off-ball player adjusts position to stay close and available. There are no defenders — pressure comes from traffic and limited space. When it works well, pairs naturally angle away from each other, stay connected, and keep the ball moving with short passes and constant support.
Coach Behaviour
Observation cues:
- Is the off-ball player moving to stay visible to the ball carrier?
- Does the distance between the pair help keep the ball safe?
Recognition comments:
- “Good help — you stayed available.”
- “Nice support for the ball.”
- “You made it easy for them.”
Phase 2 – Main Part (25–30 mins)
Setup
Players: 2v2 or 3v3 (multiple games if needed)
Area: 18x22m (adjust 16x20 to 22x26)
Equipment: Balls, end zones or small gates
Organisation: Continuous play with spare balls around the pitch
Description
Small-sided games where teams score by keeping the ball together and then entering an end zone or passing through a gate. Pressure is constant, encouraging the ball carrier to look for help. Success comes when teammates move early to support, offering simple passing options instead of hiding or standing still. When it’s working well, the ball carrier rarely feels alone — support arrives naturally and possession continues.
Rules & Success Conditions
Score by passing into an end zone or through a gate
Immediate restart from the nearest ball
Single shaping rule: a goal only counts if a teammate has offered support close to the ball carrier before the score
Managing Challenge (Same Game)
If the game is too easy:
- Reduce space slightly
If the game is too hard:
- Increase space slightly
If energy or engagement drops:
- Change the location or number of gates/end zones
Coach Behaviour – What to Watch For
Off-ball movement toward the ball carrier
Supporting angles that keep the ball safe
Willingness to help instead of waitingObserve how these behaviours help teams keep possession, and adjust space or scoring picture without stopping play.
Phase 3 – Final Game (15–20 mins)
Purpose
Allow players to apply supporting the ball carrier naturally in a free game.
Setup
Larger numbers: 5v5 or 6v6
Two goals
Normal football rules
Description
Free play with quick restarts and no added conditions. The game flows naturally, creating moments where the ball carrier is under pressure and needs help from teammates.
Transfer Emphasis (Optional)
Double goal if a goal comes after clear support to the ball carrier
Used to highlight cooperation, not to force behaviour
Coach Behaviour
Observation cues:
- Do players move to help when a teammate is under pressure?
- Is support arriving early enough to keep the ball?
Recognition phrases:
- “Good help.”
- “You supported the ball.”
- “Nice movement to help.”
Belconnen United FC – Compact • Brave • Intelligent

heading: U11 Cycle 3, week 2, session 1
headingPath: U11 sessions > U11 Cycle 3, week 2, session 1
content:
BELCONNEN UNITED FC – SESSION PLAN | CYCLE 3 | WEEK 2 | SESSION 1U11 | Play With a Mate | 2v1 – Use Your Mate
Session Outcome
Players feel more confident recognising a 2v1 and using a teammate to keep or progress the ball.
Phase 1 – Introduction (10–15 mins)
Purpose
Let players experience simple 2v1 moments through movement and sharing the ball.
Setup
Area: 20x20m (adjust 18–24m)
Players: 10–12 (groups of three with one ball)
Equipment: Cones, balls
Description
Players work in groups of three, moving freely inside the area with one ball. Two players share the ball while the third applies light, passive pressure by closing space without tackling. The pair keep the ball by moving, adjusting distance, and using simple passes. After a short time, roles rotate. The activity feels fluid and cooperative, with players discovering that staying connected and using a mate makes the ball safer.
Coach Behaviour
Observation cues:
- Are the two attackers staying close enough to help each other?
- Does the ball move when pressure arrives?
Recognition comments:
- “Good use of your mate.”
- “Nice support — the ball stayed safe.”
- “You shared it at the right time.”
Phase 2 – Main Part (25–30 mins)
Setup
Players: 2v1 (multiple games running)
Area: 15x20m (adjust 14x18 to 18x24)
Equipment: Balls, end zones or small goals
Organisation: Quick rotations; spare balls ready for immediate restarts
Description
Each game starts as a clear 2v1. The two attackers try to keep the ball and score by dribbling into an end zone or finishing in a small goal. The defender applies realistic pressure, forcing the attackers to decide whether to dribble or use their mate. When it’s working well, attackers draw the defender, share the ball at the right moment, and move again to stay involved.
Rules & Success Conditions
Score by dribbling into the end zone or finishing in the small goal
Immediate restart with a new ball
Single shaping rule: the defender must start close to the ball
Managing Challenge (Same Game)
If the game is too easy:
- Reduce space slightly
If the game is too hard:
- Increase space slightly
If energy or engagement drops:
- Swap scoring method (end zone ↔ small goal)
Coach Behaviour – What to Watch For
Ball carrier engaging the defender
Supporting player staying available and moving
Use of a simple pass to keep the advantageObserve how sharing the ball helps attackers succeed, and adjust space or defender distance without stopping play.
Phase 3 – Final Game (15–20 mins)
Purpose
Allow players to apply using a mate naturally in a free game.
Setup
Larger numbers: 5v5 or 6v6
Two goals
Normal football rules
Description
Free play with quick restarts and no added conditions. Natural 2v1 moments appear across the pitch. Players are free to dribble or pass as they choose.
Transfer Emphasis (Optional)
Double goal if a goal comes after a clear 2v1 where the ball is shared
Used to highlight cooperation, not to force behaviour
Coach Behaviour
Observation cues:
- Do players notice when they have a teammate free?
- Are they willing to share the ball to keep the advantage?
Recognition phrases:
- “Use your mate.”
- “Good 2v1.”
- “Nice decision to share it.”
Belconnen United FC – Compact • Brave • Intelligent

heading: U11 Cycle 3, week 2, session 2
headingPath: U11 sessions > U11 Cycle 3, week 2, session 2
content:
BELCONNEN UNITED FC – SESSION PLAN | CYCLE 3 | WEEK 2 | SESSION 2U11 | Play With a Mate | Pass or Drive Games
Session Outcome
Players feel more confident choosing whether to pass or drive with the ball to keep possession or move forward.
Phase 1 – Introduction (10–15 mins)
Purpose
Let players experience simple choices between passing and driving through free play.
Setup
Area: 20x20m (adjust 18–24m)
Players: 10–12 (pairs with one ball)
Equipment: Cones, balls
Description
Players work in pairs, sharing one ball, moving freely inside the area. The ball carrier can either drive with the ball or pass to their mate at any moment. The off-ball player adjusts position to stay available while also leaving space for a dribble. There are no defenders — pressure comes from traffic and limited space. When it works well, players naturally switch between carrying and passing without stopping, keeping the ball moving and protected.
Coach Behaviour
Observation cues:
- Do players recognise when there is space to drive?
- Are passes used when pressure closes space?
Recognition comments:
- “Good choice to drive.”
- “Nice pass — you kept it safe.”
- “I like how you changed your mind.”
Phase 2 – Main Part (25–30 mins)
Setup
Players: 2v2 or 3v3 (multiple games if needed)
Area: 18x22m (adjust 16x20 to 22x26)
Equipment: Balls, end zones or small gates
Organisation: Continuous play with spare balls ready for quick restarts
Description
Small-sided games where teams score by either driving into space or using a pass to access an end zone or gate. Defenders apply realistic pressure, forcing ball carriers to decide quickly: keep driving if space opens, or use a mate if pressure arrives. The game flows with frequent changes of possession and repeated decision moments. When it’s working well, players don’t force one option — they read the picture and act.
Rules & Success Conditions
Score by dribbling into an end zone or passing through a gate
Immediate restart from the nearest ball
Single shaping rule: the ball carrier must choose either a pass or a drive within a few seconds of receiving
Managing Challenge (Same Game)
If the game is too easy:
- Reduce space slightly or remove one gate
If the game is too hard:
- Increase space slightly or add an extra gate
If energy or engagement drops:
- Change the scoring picture (end zones ↔ gates)
Coach Behaviour – What to Watch For
Recognition of space to drive
Use of a simple pass when pressure closes
Smooth switching between passing and carryingObserve how these decisions help teams keep or progress the ball, and adjust space or scoring without stopping play.
Phase 3 – Final Game (15–20 mins)
Purpose
Allow players to freely decide between passing and driving in a realistic game.
Setup
Larger numbers: 5v5 or 6v6
Two goals
Normal football rules
Description
Free play with quick restarts and no added conditions. The game naturally presents moments where players must decide whether to drive forward or use a teammate.
Transfer Emphasis (Optional)
Double goal if a goal comes after a clear decision to pass or drive at the right moment
Used to highlight decision-making, not to force behaviour
Coach Behaviour
Observation cues:
- Do players recognise when to keep the ball themselves or share it?
- Are decisions made early and confidently?
Recognition phrases:
- “Good decision.”
- “You chose the right option.”
- “Nice read of the space.”
Belconnen United FC – Compact • Brave • Intelligent

heading: U11 Cycle 3, week 3, session 1
headingPath: U11 sessions > U11 Cycle 3, week 3, session 1
content:
BELCONNEN UNITED FC – SESSION PLAN | CYCLE 3 | WEEK 3 | SESSION 1U11 | Play With a Mate | Keep It Together
Session Outcome
Players feel more confident staying connected with a teammate to keep possession under pressure.
Phase 1 – Introduction (10–15 mins)
Purpose
Help players feel how staying close and connected makes the ball easier to keep.
Setup
Area: 20x20m (adjust 18–24m)
Players: 10–12 (pairs with one ball)
Equipment: Cones, balls
Description
Players work in pairs, sharing one ball, moving freely inside the area. Pairs pass, dribble, and adjust their distance to avoid traffic. There are no defenders — pressure comes from space, movement, and congestion. The focus is on pairs staying close enough to support each other without standing still. When it works well, the ball keeps moving, pairs look relaxed, and players naturally reposition to stay connected.
Coach Behaviour
Observation cues:
- Are pairs staying connected after each pass or dribble?
- Does distance help the ball stay safe?
Recognition comments:
- “You stayed together — nice.”
- “Good connection, the ball stayed alive.”
- “I like how you moved to help again.”
Phase 2 – Main Part (25–30 mins)
Setup
Players: 2v2 or 3v3 (multiple games if needed)
Area: 18x22m (adjust 16x20 to 22x26)
Equipment: Balls, end zones or small gates
Organisation: Continuous play with spare balls ready for fast restarts
Description
Small-sided games where teams score by keeping possession together and then entering an end zone or passing through a gate. Pressure is realistic and constant. Success comes from players staying close enough to help, moving after passes, and not leaving the ball carrier isolated. When the game is working well, the ball rarely stops, support arrives early, and teams feel calm even under pressure.
Rules & Success Conditions
Score by entering an end zone or passing through a gate
Immediate restart from the nearest ball
Single shaping rule: goals only count if two teammates are connected (within close support distance) just before the score
Managing Challenge (Same Game)
If the game is too easy:
- Reduce space slightly
If the game is too hard:
- Increase space slightly
If energy or engagement drops:
- Change gate or end-zone positions
Coach Behaviour – What to Watch For
Distance between teammates when under pressure
Movement to support the ball carrier
Willingness to stay connected instead of drifting awayObserve how connection helps teams keep the ball, and adjust space or scoring picture without stopping play.
Phase 3 – Final Game (15–20 mins)
Purpose
Allow players to apply staying connected naturally in a free game.
Setup
Larger numbers: 5v5 or 6v6
Two goals
Normal football rules
Description
Free play with quick restarts and no added conditions. The game naturally creates moments where players must decide whether to stay connected or separate. Players are free to dribble or pass as they choose.
Transfer Emphasis (Optional)
Double goal if a goal comes after clear connection between two teammates
Used to highlight togetherness, not to force behaviour
Coach Behaviour
Observation cues:
- Do players stay connected when pressure increases?
- Are they helping the ball carrier early?
Recognition phrases:
- “Keep it together.”
- “Good connection.”
- “You stayed close and helped.”
Belconnen United FC – Compact • Brave • Intelligent

heading: U11 Cycle 3, week 3, session 2
headingPath: U11 sessions > U11 Cycle 3, week 3, session 2
content:
BELCONNEN UNITED FC – SESSION PLAN | CYCLE 3 | WEEK 3 | SESSION 2U11 | Play With a Mate | Small Team Possession Games
Session Outcome
Players feel more confident keeping the ball together in small teams under pressure.
Phase 1 – Introduction (10–15 mins)
Purpose
Let players feel how sharing the ball in small groups helps them keep possession.
Setup
Area: 22x22m (adjust 20–26m)
Players: 10–12 (groups of 3 with one ball)
Equipment: Cones, balls
Description
Players work in groups of three, sharing one ball and moving freely inside the area. There are no defenders. Players pass, receive, and move to stay connected while avoiding other groups. The ball should keep moving, with players adjusting angles and distance naturally. When it works well, groups stay calm, the ball rarely stops, and players are constantly offering simple support options.
Coach Behaviour
Observation cues:
- Are all three players staying involved with the ball?
- Does movement help the group keep possession?
Recognition comments:
- “Nice support — everyone stayed connected.”
- “Good movement, the ball kept flowing.”
- “I like how you helped each other.”
Phase 2 – Main Part (25–30 mins)
Setup
Players: 3v3 or 4v4 (multiple games if needed)
Area: 18x24m (adjust 16x22 to 22x28)
Equipment: Balls, end zones or multiple small gates
Organisation: Continuous play with spare balls ready for immediate restarts
Description
Small team possession games where the main challenge is keeping the ball together under pressure. Teams score by completing a short sequence of possession and then entering an end zone or passing through a gate. Defenders apply realistic pressure, forcing attackers to move, support, and share the ball. When it’s working well, players pass and move instinctively, stay connected, and keep possession without forcing risky actions.
Rules & Success Conditions
Score by entering an end zone or passing through a gate after keeping possession
Immediate restart from the nearest ball
Single shaping rule: all players on the team must touch the ball before scoring
Managing Challenge (Same Game)
If the game is too easy:
- Reduce space slightly or remove a gate
If the game is too hard:
- Increase space slightly or add an extra gate
If energy or engagement drops:
- Change the scoring picture or rotate teams
Coach Behaviour – What to Watch For
Players offering support angles around the ball
Movement after passing to stay involved
Calmness when pressure increasesObserve how connection and movement help teams keep possession, and adjust space or scoring without stopping play.
Phase 3 – Final Game (15–20 mins)
Purpose
Allow players to apply small-team possession naturally in a free game.
Setup
Larger numbers: 5v5 or 6v6
Two goals
Normal football rules
Description
Free play with quick restarts and no added conditions. The game naturally creates moments where teams must keep the ball together to progress and score.
Transfer Emphasis (Optional)
Double goal if a goal comes after clear small-team possession involving multiple players
Used to highlight togetherness, not to force behaviour
Coach Behaviour
Observation cues:
- Do players stay connected as a team when under pressure?
- Are they using teammates to keep the ball alive?
Recognition phrases:
- “Kept it together.”
- “Good team possession.”
- “You shared it and stayed connected.”

heading: U11 Cycle 4, week 1, session 1
headingPath: U11 sessions > U11 Cycle 4, week 1, session 1
content:
BELCONNEN UNITED FC – SESSION PLAN | CYCLE 4 | WEEK 1 | SESSION 1U11 | Win It Back | Chase & Delay Games
Session Outcome
Players feel more confident reacting quickly to loss of the ball by chasing, slowing the opponent, and buying time.
Phase 1 – Introduction (10–15 mins)
Purpose
Introduce the habit of immediate reaction when the ball is lost.
Setup
Area: 20x20m (adjust 18–24m)
Players: 10–12 (everyone with a ball)
Equipment: Cones, balls
Description
All players dribble freely inside the area. On the coach’s call of “lose,” players gently leave their ball and immediately chase the nearest free ball carrier. The ball carrier’s job is to keep moving and protect the ball; the chaser’s job is to get close, slow them down, and stay between the ball and open space. After a few seconds, players return to their own ball and continue. The activity feels sharp and energetic, with constant transitions between attack and chase.
Coach Behaviour
Observation cues:
- Do players react instantly when they lose their ball?
- Are chasers slowing the ball carrier rather than diving in?
Recognition comments:
- “Great reaction — you chased straight away.”
- “Good delay — you slowed them down.”
- “Nice effort to get back quickly.”
Phase 2 – Main Part (25–30 mins)
Setup
Players: 2v2 or 3v3 (multiple games if needed)
Area: 18x22m (adjust 16x20 to 22x26)
Equipment: Balls, end zones or small goals
Organisation: Continuous games with spare balls ready for quick restarts
Description
Small-sided games where transitions happen constantly. When a team loses the ball, their immediate task is to chase and delay rather than win it straight away. Teams score by dribbling into an end zone or finishing in a small goal. The game flows quickly, with repeated moments of loss, chase, and recovery. When it’s working well, players sprint back, get goal-side, slow the attacker, and give teammates time to recover.
Rules & Success Conditions
Score by entering an end zone or finishing in a small goal
Immediate restart from the nearest ball
Single shaping rule: the nearest player must chase immediately on loss of possession
Managing Challenge (Same Game)
If the game is too easy:
- Reduce space slightly
If the game is too hard:
- Increase space slightly
If energy or engagement drops:
- Change scoring method (end zones ↔ goals)
Coach Behaviour – What to Watch For
Speed of reaction after losing the ball
Body position to slow and contain the attacker
Willingness to chase even after a mistakeObserve how chasing and delaying helps teams recover, and adjust space or scoring without stopping play.
Phase 3 – Final Game (15–20 mins)
Purpose
Allow players to apply chase-and-delay habits naturally in a free game.
Setup
Larger numbers: 5v5 or 6v6
Two goals
Normal football rules
Description
Free play with quick restarts and no added conditions. Transitions appear naturally, with frequent moments where teams lose the ball and must react quickly to defend.
Transfer Emphasis (Optional)
Double goal if a goal comes after a successful chase and delay that leads to winning the ball back
Used to highlight reaction and effort, not to force behaviour
Coach Behaviour
Observation cues:
- Do players react immediately after losing the ball?
- Are they slowing the attack instead of giving up?
Recognition phrases:
- “Great chase.”
- “You delayed them.”
- “Good effort to win it back.”

content:
BELCONNEN UNITED FC – SESSION PLAN | CYCLE 4 | WEEK 1 | SESSION 2U11 | Win It Back | Slow Them Down
Session Outcome
Players feel more confident getting back quickly to slow the opponent and stop them playing forward.
Phase 1 – Introduction (10–15 mins)
Purpose
Introduce the idea that defending starts by slowing the ball, not winning it immediately.
Setup
Area: 20x20m (adjust 18–24m)
Players: 10–12 (everyone with a ball)
Equipment: Cones, balls
Description
All players dribble freely inside the area. On the coach’s call of “switch,” players leave their ball and immediately move to slow the nearest ball carrier by getting in front, matching speed, and blocking space (no tackling). After a few seconds, players return to their own ball and continue dribbling. The activity feels sharp and reactive, with constant moments of stopping, turning, and re-engaging. When it works well, players recover quickly, stay balanced, and force the ball carrier to slow down or change direction.
Coach Behaviour
Observation cues:
- Do players get in front of the ball quickly?
- Are they staying low and balanced instead of rushing?
Recognition comments:
- “Good delay — you stopped them going forward.”
- “Nice body position — you slowed them.”
- “Quick reaction to get back.”
Phase 2 – Main Part (25–30 mins)
Setup
Players: 2v2 or 3v3 (multiple games if needed)
Area: 18x22m (adjust 16x20 to 22x26)
Equipment: Balls, end zones or small goals
Organisation: Continuous games with spare balls for fast restarts
Description
Small-sided games where the focus is on delaying the attack after losing the ball. Teams score by dribbling into an end zone or finishing in a small goal. When possession changes, the nearest defender’s first job is to slow the attacker by getting goal-side, blocking the direct path forward, and buying time for help to arrive. When the game is working well, attackers are forced sideways or backwards and defenders stay patient instead of diving in.
Rules & Success Conditions
Score by entering an end zone or finishing in a small goal
Immediate restart from the nearest ball
Single shaping rule: the first defender must slow the ball before attempting to win it
Managing Challenge (Same Game)
If the game is too easy:
- Reduce space slightly
If the game is too hard:
- Increase space slightly
If energy or engagement drops:
- Change scoring method (end zones ↔ goals)
Coach Behaviour – What to Watch For
Speed of recovery after losing the ball
Body shape that blocks forward space
Patience to delay rather than tackleObserve how slowing the attacker helps teammates recover, and adjust space or scoring without stopping play.
Phase 3 – Final Game (15–20 mins)
Purpose
Allow players to apply slowing-down behaviours naturally in a real game.
Setup
Larger numbers: 5v5 or 6v6
Two goals
Normal football rules
Description
Free play with quick restarts and no added conditions. Transition moments appear naturally, with frequent chances to slow the opponent after losing the ball.
Transfer Emphasis (Optional)
Double goal if a goal comes after a clear slow-down moment that leads to winning the ball back
Used to highlight defensive intent, not to force behaviour
Coach Behaviour
Observation cues:
- Do players slow the ball instead of diving in?
- Are attackers being forced to stop or change direction?
Recognition phrases:
- “You slowed them down.”
- “Good delay.”
- “You bought time for your team.”

content:
BELCONNEN UNITED FC – SESSION PLAN | CYCLE 4 | WEEK 2 | SESSION 1U11 | Win It Back | Steal It & Go Forward
Session Outcome
Players feel more confident winning the ball back and immediately driving forward with it.
Phase 1 – Introduction (10–15 mins)
Purpose
Introduce the habit of turning ball recovery into forward action.
Setup
Area: 20x20m (adjust 18–24m)
Players: 10–12 (everyone with a ball)
Equipment: Cones, balls
Description
All players dribble freely inside the area. On the coach’s call of “steal,” players leave their ball and immediately try to steal a nearby ball by getting close and poking it away (controlled contact only). Once a player wins a ball, their task is to drive forward into open space for a few seconds before rejoining free dribbling. The activity feels sharp and competitive, with quick reactions and short bursts forward after winning the ball.
Coach Behaviour
Observation cues:
- Do players react quickly to try to win the ball?
- After stealing it, do they move forward straight away?
Recognition comments:
- “Great steal — you went forward immediately.”
- “Nice reaction, then drive.”
- “I like how you didn’t stop after winning it.”
Phase 2 – Main Part (25–30 mins)
Setup
Players: 2v2 or 3v3 (multiple games if needed)
Area: 18x22m (adjust 16x20 to 22x26)
Equipment: Balls, end zones or small goals
Organisation: Continuous games with spare balls ready for quick restarts
Description
Small-sided games where the key moment is ball recovery. Teams score by dribbling into an end zone or finishing in a small goal. When a player wins the ball, the immediate focus is to go forward with speed rather than passing backwards or stopping. The game flows with frequent turnovers and transitions. When it’s working well, ball winners attack space quickly and force the opposition to react.
Rules & Success Conditions
Score by entering an end zone or finishing in a small goal
Immediate restart from the nearest ball
Single shaping rule: after winning the ball, the first action must be forward
Managing Challenge (Same Game)
If the game is too easy:
- Reduce space slightly
If the game is too hard:
- Increase space slightly
If energy or engagement drops:
- Change scoring method (end zones ↔ goals)
Coach Behaviour – What to Watch For
Timing and intent of the steal
First touch forward after winning the ball
Willingness to attack space immediatelyObserve how quick forward actions turn ball recovery into advantage, and adjust space or scoring without stopping play.
Phase 3 – Final Game (15–20 mins)
Purpose
Allow players to freely connect winning the ball with attacking forward.
Setup
Larger numbers: 5v5 or 6v6
Two goals
Normal football rules
Description
Free play with quick restarts and no added conditions. Natural moments appear where players win the ball and must decide what to do next.
Transfer Emphasis (Optional)
Double goal if a goal comes after a clear ball steal followed by a forward drive
Used to highlight intent, not to force behaviour
Coach Behaviour
Observation cues:
- Do players look forward immediately after winning the ball?
- Are recoveries turning into attacks?
Recognition phrases:
- “Steal and go.”
- “Great win — forward.”
- “You turned defence into attack.”
Belconnen United FC – Compact • Brave • Intelligent

content:
BELCONNEN UNITED FC – SESSION PLAN | CYCLE 4 | WEEK 2 | SESSION 2U11 | Win It Back | Win It Back to Score
Session Outcome
Players feel more confident winning the ball back and quickly turning it into a scoring chance.
Phase 1 – Introduction (10–15 mins)
Purpose
Create the link between winning the ball and attacking immediately.
Setup
- Area: 20x20m (adjust 18–24m)
- Players: 10–12 (everyone with a ball)
- Equipment: Cones, balls, 2–4 mini goals on the outside
Description
All players dribble freely inside the area. On the coach’s call of “win,” players leave their ball and try to win a nearby ball using controlled pressure and a poke tackle. Once a player wins a ball, they immediately attack one of the mini goals and finish. After scoring or missing, players return to free dribbling. The activity feels fast and competitive, with short bursts of defending followed by quick attacking intent.
Coach Behaviour
- Observation cues:
- Do players react instantly to try to win the ball?
- After winning it, do they look to score straight away?
- Recognition comments:
- “Great win — straight to goal.”
- “Nice reaction, then finish.”
- “You turned defence into attack.”
Phase 2 – Main Part (25–30 mins)
Setup
- Players: 2v2 or 3v3 (multiple games if needed)
- Area: 18x25m (adjust 16x22 to 22x30)
- Equipment: Balls, small goals
- Organisation: Continuous games with spare balls behind goals for quick restarts
Description
Small-sided games where the key moment is ball recovery. Teams score by finishing in a small goal. When a team wins the ball, the immediate focus is to attack the nearest goal with speed before the opponent can recover. Defenders are encouraged to press actively, creating frequent turnovers. When it’s working well, wins are followed by quick drives, shots come early, and opponents are caught disorganised.
Rules & Success Conditions
- Score by finishing in the small goal
- Immediate restart from the nearest ball
- Single shaping rule: after winning the ball, the team must attempt to score quickly (no retreating)
Managing Challenge (Same Game)
- If the game is too easy:
- Reduce space slightly or move goals closer
- If the game is too hard:
- Increase space slightly
- If energy or engagement drops:
- Change goal positions or swap attack direction
Coach Behaviour – What to Watch For
- Intensity and timing of the ball win
- First action forward after recovery
- Willingness to shoot or drive quicklyObserve how fast attacking after recovery leads to chances, and adjust space or goal position without stopping play.
Phase 3 – Final Game (15–20 mins)
Purpose
Allow players to freely connect winning the ball with scoring in a realistic game.
Setup
- Larger numbers: 5v5 or 6v6
- Two goals
- Normal football rules
Description
Free play with quick restarts and no added conditions. Transition moments appear naturally, with opportunities to win the ball and attack quickly.
Transfer Emphasis (Optional)
- Double goal if a goal comes within a few seconds of winning the ball back
- Used to highlight quick attacking intent, not to force behaviour
Coach Behaviour
- Observation cues:
- Do players attack immediately after winning the ball?
- Are recoveries leading to shots or goals?
- Recognition phrases:
- “Win it and score.”
- “Great reaction — straight to goal.”
- “You punished them after winning it.”

content:
BELCONNEN UNITED FC – SESSION PLAN | CYCLE 4 | WEEK 3 | SESSION 1U11 | Win It Back | Regain + Counter
Session Outcome
Players feel more confident winning the ball and attacking forward quickly before the opponent recovers.
Phase 1 – Introduction (10–15 mins)
Purpose
Create the habit of turning a regain into immediate forward movement.
Setup
- Area: 20x20m (adjust 18–24m)
- Players: 10–12 (everyone with a ball)
- Equipment: Cones, balls
Description
All players dribble freely inside the area. On the coach’s call of “regain,” players leave their ball and immediately try to win a nearby ball using controlled pressure and a poke tackle. As soon as a player regains a ball, they must drive forward into open space for a few seconds before rejoining free play. The activity feels sharp and reactive, with quick changes from defending to attacking. When it works well, players switch mindset instantly and explode forward after winning the ball.
Coach Behaviour
- Observation cues:
- Do players react instantly to the regain moment?
- Is the first movement after winning the ball forward?
- Recognition comments:
- “Great regain — straight forward.”
- “Nice reaction, you countered quickly.”
- “You won it and went.”
Phase 2 – Main Part (25–30 mins)
Setup
- Players: 3v3 or 4v4 (multiple games if needed)
- Area: 18x25m (adjust 16x22 to 22x30)
- Equipment: Balls, two small goals at each end
- Organisation: Continuous play with spare balls behind goals for fast restarts
Description
Small-sided games where the key moment is regaining possession. Teams score by finishing in a small goal. When a team regains the ball, they are encouraged to counter immediately by driving forward or attacking the nearest goal before the opposition can recover. The game is fast and transitional, with frequent turnovers and sudden attacks. When it’s working well, regains lead to quick forward movement, early shots, or decisive drives.
Rules & Success Conditions
- Score by finishing in the small goal
- Immediate restart from the nearest ball
- Single shaping rule: after regaining the ball, the team must attack forward immediately
Managing Challenge (Same Game)
- If the game is too easy:
- Reduce space slightly or bring goals closer
- If the game is too hard:
- Increase space slightly
- If energy or engagement drops:
- Change goal positions or attack direction
Coach Behaviour – What to Watch For
- Speed of reaction after the regain
- First touch or action going forward
- Willingness to counter before the defence is setObserve how quick counters create advantage, and adjust space or goal position without stopping play.
Phase 3 – Final Game (15–20 mins)
Purpose
Allow players to apply regain-and-counter behaviours naturally in a realistic game.
Setup
- Larger numbers: 5v5 or 6v6
- Two goals
- Normal football rules
Description
Free play with quick restarts and no added conditions. Regains and counter-attacks appear naturally as the game flows. Players are free to choose how quickly and directly they attack.
Transfer Emphasis (Optional)
- Double goal if a goal comes within a few seconds of regaining the ball
- Used to highlight counter-attacking intent, not to force behaviour
Coach Behaviour
- Observation cues:
- Do players look forward immediately after winning the ball?
- Are regains turning into quick attacks?
- Recognition phrases:
- “Regain and go.”
- “Great counter.”
- “You punished them after winning it.”

heading: U11 Cycle 4, week 3, session 2
headingPath: U11 sessions > U11 Cycle 4, week 3, session 2
content:
BELCONNEN UNITED FC – SESSION PLAN | CYCLE 4 | WEEK 3 | SESSION 2U11 | Win It Back | Transition Battle Games
Session Outcome
Players feel more confident reacting quickly in transition moments and competing to regain or protect the ball.
Phase 1 – Introduction (10–15 mins)
Purpose
Drop players into constant transition moments and let them experience the battle to react first.
Setup
- Area: 18x18m (adjust 16–22m)
- Players: 10–12 (everyone with a ball)
- Equipment: Cones, balls
Description
All players dribble freely inside the area. On the coach’s call of “switch,” players immediately leave their ball and attack the nearest loose ball, while the previous ball carrier must react to protect or recover it. After a few seconds, players return to free dribbling. The activity feels frantic and competitive, with frequent changes between attacking and defending roles. When it works well, players react instantly, change mindset quickly, and compete hard to either protect or win the ball.
Coach Behaviour
- Observation cues:
- Do players react immediately when the picture changes?
- Are players switching mindset quickly between attack and defence?
- Recognition comments:
- “Great reaction — you were first.”
- “Nice switch — you protected it.”
- “You reacted quicker than them.”
Phase 2 – Main Part (25–30 mins)
Setup
- Players: 3v3 or 4v4 (multiple games if needed)
- Area: 18x24m (adjust 16x22 to 22x28)
- Equipment: Balls, small goals
- Organisation: Continuous games with spare balls behind goals for fast restarts
Description
Small-sided games built around constant transitions. Teams score by finishing in a small goal. When possession changes, both teams must react instantly — the team that lost the ball tries to win it back, while the team that gained it tries to protect and move forward. The game feels intense, with repeated battles immediately after turnovers. When it’s working well, players sprint to react, compete fiercely for the next action, and never switch off after losing or winning the ball.
Rules & Success Conditions
- Score by finishing in the small goal
- Immediate restart from the nearest ball
- Single shaping rule: the first 3 seconds after a turnover are live battle time (no standing off)
Managing Challenge (Same Game)
- If the game is too easy:
- Reduce space slightly
- If the game is too hard:
- Increase space slightly
- If energy or engagement drops:
- Shorten game rounds and rotate teams quickly
Coach Behaviour – What to Watch For
- Speed of reaction at the moment of turnover
- Willingness to compete immediately after losing the ball
- Ability to protect the ball when possession is regainedObserve how reactions in the first moments after transition affect success, and adjust space or numbers without stopping play.
Phase 3 – Final Game (15–20 mins)
Purpose
Allow players to apply transition reactions freely in a realistic game.
Setup
- Larger numbers: 5v5 or 6v6
- Two goals
- Normal football rules
Description
Free play with quick restarts and no added conditions. Transition moments appear naturally throughout the game. Players are free to react, compete, and make decisions in real time.
Transfer Emphasis (Optional)
- Double goal if a goal comes after a quick win-back or successful protection immediately after transition
- Used to highlight reaction and competitiveness, not to force behaviour
Coach Behaviour
- Observation cues:
- Do players react instantly after losing or winning the ball?
- Are transitions being played with intensity?
- Recognition phrases:
- “Great reaction.”
- “You stayed switched on.”
- “Good battle after the turnover.”
Belconnen United FC – Compact • Brave • Intelligent

heading: U11 Cycle 5, week 1, session 1

content:
BELCONNEN UNITED FC – SESSION PLAN | CYCLE 5 | WEEK 1 | SESSION 1U11 | Score in Different Ways | Dribble to Finish
Session Outcome
Players feel more confident driving with the ball and finishing by themselves under pressure.
Phase 1 – Introduction (10–15 mins)
Purpose
Encourage players to see dribbling as a direct way to score.
Setup
- Area: 20x20m (adjust 18–24m)
- Players: 10–12 (everyone with a ball)
- Equipment: Cones, balls, 2–4 mini goals on the outside
Description
All players dribble freely inside the area with mini goals placed around the outside. Players choose when to attack a goal by driving out of the space and finishing. There are no defenders — pressure comes from traffic and decision-making. Players can attack any goal, change direction, pull out, or re-enter the space. When it works well, players carry the ball with intent, spot a chance, and finish confidently without hesitation.
Coach Behaviour
- Observation cues:
- Are players recognising moments to drive toward goal?
- Do they finish with confidence after the dribble?
- Recognition comments:
- “Nice drive to goal.”
- “You carried it and finished.”
- “I like how you went yourself.”
Phase 2 – Main Part (25–30 mins)
Setup
- Players: 2v2 or 3v3 (multiple games if needed)
- Area: 18x25m (adjust 16x22 to 22x30)
- Equipment: Balls, small goals
- Organisation: Continuous games with spare balls behind goals for quick restarts
Description
Small-sided games where players are encouraged to dribble to finish. Goals are positioned to invite driving runs rather than early shots. Defenders start close, creating realistic pressure. Players score by beating pressure with the ball and finishing themselves. When the game is working well, players attack defenders with intent, keep the ball close, and finish decisively instead of looking to pass first.
Rules & Success Conditions
- Score by finishing in the small goal
- Immediate restart from the nearest ball
- Single shaping rule: goals only count if the scorer has carried the ball forward before finishing
Managing Challenge (Same Game)
- If the game is too easy:
- Reduce space slightly or bring goals closer
- If the game is too hard:
- Increase space slightly
- If energy or engagement drops:
- Change goal positions or attack direction
Coach Behaviour – What to Watch For
- Willingness to drive with the ball toward goal
- Control of the ball at speed
- Confidence to finish without extra touchesObserve how dribbling creates scoring chances, and adjust space or goal position without stopping play.
Phase 3 – Final Game (15–20 mins)
Purpose
Allow players to apply dribble-to-finish actions freely in a real game.
Setup
- Larger numbers: 5v5 or 6v6
- Two goals
- Normal football rules
Description
Free play with quick restarts and no added conditions. Natural moments appear where players can drive forward and finish themselves.
Transfer Emphasis (Optional)
- Double goal if a goal comes from a clear dribble into the finishing area
- Used to highlight intent, not to force behaviour
Coach Behaviour
- Observation cues:
- Do players attack space toward goal with the ball?
- Are finishes decisive after the dribble?
- Recognition phrases:
- “Drive and finish.”
- “Great run to goal.”
- “You went yourself — well done.”
Belconnen United FC – Compact • Brave • Intelligent

heading: U11 Cycle 5, week 1, session 2
headingPath: U11 sessions > U11 Cycle 5, week 1, session 2
content:
BELCONNEN UNITED FC – SESSION PLAN | CYCLE 5 | WEEK 1 | SESSION 2U11 | Score in Different Ways | Finish After a Move
Session Outcome
Players feel more confident beating an opponent with a move and finishing straight after.
Phase 1 – Introduction (10–15 mins)
Purpose
Link a simple move directly to finishing with intent.
Setup
- Area: 20x20m (adjust 18–24m)
- Players: 10–12 (everyone with a ball)
- Equipment: Cones, balls, 2–4 mini goals around the outside
Description
Players dribble freely inside the area. On their own timing, players choose a moment to perform a move (fake, stop-start, change of direction), then immediately attack a mini goal and finish. There are no defenders — pressure comes from traffic and timing. Players can abort a finish and re-enter the space if it’s not on. When it works well, players show clear intent: move → accelerate → finish, without extra touches or hesitation.
Coach Behaviour
- Observation cues:
- Are players committing to a move before finishing?
- Is there acceleration immediately after the move?
- Recognition comments:
- “Nice move — straight to goal.”
- “You shifted it and finished.”
- “Good intent after the move.”
Phase 2 – Main Part (25–30 mins)
Setup
- Players: 1v1 or 2v2 (multiple games running)
- Area: 14x20m (adjust 12x18 to 18x24)
- Equipment: Balls, small goals
- Organisation: Quick restarts; spare balls behind goals
Description
Small-sided games where scoring comes from beating an opponent and finishing. Defenders start close to encourage engagement. Attackers must commit the defender with a move before going to goal. The game is intense and direct, with frequent chances to take on an opponent near goal. When it’s working well, players don’t rush shots — they create the moment with a move, then finish decisively.
Rules & Success Conditions
- Score by finishing in the small goal
- Immediate restart from the nearest ball
- Single shaping rule: goals only count if the attacker uses a move before finishing
Managing Challenge (Same Game)
- If the game is too easy:
- Reduce space or bring goals closer
- If the game is too hard:
- Increase space slightly
- If energy or engagement drops:
- Switch between 1v1 and 2v2 formats
Coach Behaviour – What to Watch For
- Willingness to engage the defender
- Clear move to unbalance the opponent
- Quick finish after the moveObserve how the move creates the chance to score, and adjust space or numbers without stopping play.
Phase 3 – Final Game (15–20 mins)
Purpose
Allow players to apply move-and-finish freely in a realistic game.
Setup
- Larger numbers: 5v5 or 6v6
- Two goals
- Normal football rules
Description
Free play with quick restarts and no added conditions. Natural moments appear where players can beat an opponent near goal and finish.
Transfer Emphasis (Optional)
- Double goal if a goal comes immediately after a clear move to beat an opponent
- Used to highlight the behaviour, not to force it
Coach Behaviour
- Observation cues:
- Do players try to beat an opponent before shooting?
- Are finishes quick after the move?
- Recognition phrases:
- “Move and finish.”
- “Great take-on.”
- “You created it and scored.”
Belconnen United FC – Compact • Brave • Intelligent

heading: U11 Cycle 5, week 2, session 1
headingPath: U11 sessions > U11 Cycle 5, week 2, session 1
content:
BELCONNEN UNITED FC – SESSION PLAN | CYCLE 5 | WEEK 2 | SESSION 1U11 | Score in Different Ways | Quick Finishes in the Box
Session Outcome
Players feel more confident finishing quickly inside the scoring area without extra touches or hesitation.
Phase 1 – Introduction (10–15 mins)
Purpose
Get players comfortable finishing fast when the chance appears.
Setup
- Area: 20x20m (adjust 18–24m)
- Players: 10–12 (everyone with a ball)
- Equipment: Cones, balls, 2–4 mini goals close to the edge of the area
Description
Players dribble freely inside the area. Mini goals are placed close and slightly angled to encourage quick shots. Players choose moments to step toward a goal and finish quickly, then return to free dribbling. There are no defenders — pressure comes from traffic and limited space near goal. When it works well, players take few touches, shoot early, and don’t overthink the moment.
Coach Behaviour
- Observation cues:
- Are players shooting as soon as the chance appears?
- Are extra touches disappearing near goal?
- Recognition comments:
- “Quick finish — well done.”
- “Nice decision to shoot early.”
- “You didn’t hesitate.”
Phase 2 – Main Part (25–30 mins)
Setup
- Players: 2v2 or 3v3 (multiple games if needed)
- Area: 18x22m (adjust 16x20 to 22x26)
- Equipment: Balls, small goals positioned close to the end line
- Organisation: Continuous play with spare balls behind goals for instant restarts
Description
Small-sided games played close to goal, encouraging fast finishes. Space is tight, pressure is immediate, and chances appear suddenly. Players score by finishing in the small goals. The key moment is inside the “box” area, where players must react quickly rather than dribble or set themselves too long. When it’s working well, shots come early, rebounds are attacked, and players stay alert for loose balls.
Rules & Success Conditions
- Score by finishing in the small goal
- Immediate restart from the nearest ball
- Single shaping rule: goals only count if the finish is taken within a short time after entering the scoring area
Managing Challenge (Same Game)
- If the game is too easy:
- Reduce space or move goals closer together
- If the game is too hard:
- Increase space slightly
- If energy or engagement drops:
- Add an extra ball to encourage rebounds and chaos
Coach Behaviour – What to Watch For
- Readiness to shoot quickly
- Body shape to finish without stopping the ball
- Reactions to rebounds or loose ballsObserve how speed of decision leads to goals, and adjust space or goal positions without stopping play.
Phase 3 – Final Game (15–20 mins)
Purpose
Allow players to apply quick finishing naturally in a realistic game.
Setup
- Larger numbers: 5v5 or 6v6
- Two goals
- Normal football rules
Description
Free play with quick restarts and no added conditions. Chances in and around goal appear naturally. Players are encouraged to finish early when space opens inside the scoring area.
Transfer Emphasis (Optional)
- Double goal if a goal is scored with a quick finish inside the box
- Used to highlight decisiveness, not to force behaviour
Coach Behaviour
- Observation cues:
- Do players recognise finishing moments early?
- Are shots taken without unnecessary touches?
- Recognition phrases:
- “Quick finish.”
- “You reacted first.”
- “No hesitation — great.”

content:
BELCONNEN UNITED FC – SESSION PLAN | CYCLE 5 | WEEK 2 | SESSION 2U11 | Score in Different Ways | One-Touch Scoring Games
Session Outcome
Players feel more confident finishing with one touch when the chance appears.
Phase 1 – Introduction (10–15 mins)
Purpose
Expose players to quick, instinctive finishing without extra touches.
Setup
- Area: 20x20m (adjust 18–24m)
- Players: 10–12 (pairs sharing one ball)
- Equipment: Cones, balls, 2–4 mini goals placed around the outside
Description
Players work in pairs, moving freely inside the area with one ball. One player dribbles while the other stays nearby. At any moment, the dribbler can play a short pass to their mate, who finishes first time into a nearby mini goal, then roles switch. There are no defenders — pressure comes from timing, movement, and traffic. When it works well, passes are short, movement is sharp, and finishes are instinctive.
Coach Behaviour
- Observation cues:
- Are players setting their body early to finish first time?
- Is the ball being played with intent into finishing space?
- Recognition comments:
- “Nice one-touch finish.”
- “Good pass — easy to finish.”
- “You were ready to shoot.”
Phase 2 – Main Part (25–30 mins)
Setup
- Players: 2v2 or 3v3 (multiple games if needed)
- Area: 18x22m (adjust 16x20 to 22x26)
- Equipment: Balls, small goals
- Organisation: Continuous play with spare balls behind goals for quick restarts
Description
Small-sided games played close to goal, encouraging quick combinations and one-touch finishes. Players score by finishing in the small goals. Space is tight, pressure is real, and chances appear suddenly. When it’s working well, the ball moves quickly, players arrive ready to shoot, and finishes are taken without stopping or extra control.
Rules & Success Conditions
- Score by finishing in the small goal
- Immediate restart from the nearest ball
- Single shaping rule: goals only count if the finish is one touch
Managing Challenge (Same Game)
- If the game is too easy:
- Reduce space slightly or move goals closer
- If the game is too hard:
- Allow one touch to set + one touch to finish
- If energy or engagement drops:
- Change goal positions or add an extra ball for rebounds
Coach Behaviour – What to Watch For
- Movement to arrive ready to finish
- Body shape before the ball arrives
- Confidence to shoot first timeObserve how readiness and timing lead to goals, and adjust space or rules without stopping play.
Phase 3 – Final Game (15–20 mins)
Purpose
Allow players to apply one-touch finishing naturally in a realistic game.
Setup
- Larger numbers: 5v5 or 6v6
- Two goals
- Normal football rules
Description
Free play with quick restarts and no added conditions. Natural moments appear where players can finish first time near goal.
Transfer Emphasis (Optional)
- Double goal if a goal is scored with a clear one-touch finish
- Used to highlight instinctive finishing, not to force behaviour
Coach Behaviour
- Observation cues:
- Are players arriving ready to finish?
- Do they trust the one-touch shot?
- Recognition phrases:
- “First time — well done.”
- “You were ready.”
- “Great quick finish.”

content:
BELCONNEN UNITED FC – SESSION PLAN | CYCLE 5 | WEEK 3 | SESSION 1U11 | Score in Different Ways | Finish Under Pressure
Session Outcome
Players feel more confident finishing when a defender is close and time is limited.
Phase 1 – Introduction (10–15 mins)
Purpose
Expose players to finishing while being chased or closed down.
Setup
- Area: 20x20m (adjust 18–24m)
- Players: 10–12 (everyone with a ball)
- Equipment: Cones, balls, 2–4 mini goals around the outside
Description
Players dribble freely inside the area. On their own timing, players attack a mini goal to finish. As they move toward goal, a nearby player applies light chase pressure from behind or the side (no tackling). The attacker must adjust body position and timing to finish while under pressure. After each finish, players rotate roles naturally. When it works well, players stay composed, protect the ball briefly, and finish without panicking.
Coach Behaviour
- Observation cues:
- Do players stay balanced when pressure arrives?
- Are finishes taken without rushing or freezing?
- Recognition comments:
- “Good composure under pressure.”
- “You stayed calm and finished.”
- “Nice protection before the shot.”
Phase 2 – Main Part (25–30 mins)
Setup
- Players: 2v2 or 3v3 (multiple games if needed)
- Area: 18x22m (adjust 16x20 to 22x26)
- Equipment: Balls, small goals
- Organisation: Continuous games with spare balls behind goals for quick restarts
Description
Small-sided games played close to goal with immediate pressure on the ball. Defenders start tight, forcing attackers to finish while being challenged. Goals are scored by finishing in the small goals. The game feels intense, with little time and space. When it’s working well, attackers protect the ball just enough, keep their head up, and finish decisively despite pressure.
Rules & Success Conditions
- Score by finishing in the small goal
- Immediate restart from the nearest ball
- Single shaping rule: defenders must start within close distance of the attacker
Managing Challenge (Same Game)
- If the game is too easy:
- Reduce space or move goals closer
- If the game is too hard:
- Increase space slightly
- If energy or engagement drops:
- Shorten games and rotate teams more frequently
Coach Behaviour – What to Watch For
- Body position to protect the ball before finishing
- Calm finishing actions despite contact
- Willingness to shoot under pressureObserve how composure leads to goals, and adjust space or defender distance without stopping play.
Phase 3 – Final Game (15–20 mins)
Purpose
Allow players to apply finishing under pressure naturally in a real game.
Setup
- Larger numbers: 5v5 or 6v6
- Two goals
- Normal football rules
Description
Free play with quick restarts and no added conditions. Pressure near goal appears naturally. Players are free to decide when to shoot, dribble, or protect the ball.
Transfer Emphasis (Optional)
- Double goal if a goal is scored while the shooter is under clear pressure
- Used to highlight composure, not to force behaviour
Coach Behaviour
- Observation cues:
- Do players stay composed when defenders are close?
- Are finishes taken confidently under pressure?
- Recognition phrases:
- “Calm finish.”
- “You handled the pressure.”
- “Strong under pressure.”

content:
BELCONNEN UNITED FC – SESSION PLAN | CYCLE 5 | WEEK 3 | SESSION 2U11 | Score in Different Ways | Rebound & Second-Chance Goals
Session Outcome
Players feel more confident reacting quickly to rebounds and scoring second-chance goals.
Phase 1 – Introduction (10–15 mins)
Purpose
Introduce the habit of staying alive after the first shot.
Setup
- Area: 20x20m (adjust 18–24m)
- Players: 10–12 (everyone with a ball)
- Equipment: Cones, balls, 2–4 mini goals or rebound targets
Description
Players dribble freely inside the area. On their own timing, players attack a mini goal and shoot. Immediately after the shot, players must stay active — reacting to rebounds off posts, cones, or missed shots by following in and finishing again. There are no defenders — pressure comes from timing, movement, and awareness. When it works well, players don’t admire shots; they stay switched on and hunt the next moment.
Coach Behaviour
- Observation cues:
- Do players keep moving after the first shot?
- Are eyes and body reacting quickly to rebounds?
- Recognition comments:
- “Great reaction — you followed it in.”
- “You stayed alive after the shot.”
- “Nice second effort.”
Phase 2 – Main Part (25–30 mins)
Setup
- Players: 2v2 or 3v3 (multiple games if needed)
- Area: 18x22m (adjust 16x20 to 22x26)
- Equipment: Balls, small goals
- Organisation: Continuous games with spare balls behind goals for fast restarts
Description
Small-sided games played close to goal where rebounds are common. Shots are encouraged, and play continues after saves, blocks, or rebounds. Goals are often scored on second or third actions rather than the first attempt. When it’s working well, players attack the goal area aggressively, react faster than opponents, and finish loose balls with confidence.
Rules & Success Conditions
- Score by finishing in the small goal
- Play continues after rebounds — no stoppages
- Single shaping rule: rebound goals count double
Managing Challenge (Same Game)
- If the game is too easy:
- Reduce space or move goals closer
- If the game is too hard:
- Increase space slightly
- If energy or engagement drops:
- Add an extra ball to increase chaos and rebounds
Coach Behaviour – What to Watch For
- Reaction speed after a shot
- Movement toward goal after finishing attempts
- Willingness to attack loose ballsObserve how second efforts create goals, and adjust space or goal position without stopping play.
Phase 3 – Final Game (15–20 mins)
Purpose
Allow players to apply rebound awareness naturally in a realistic game.
Setup
- Larger numbers: 5v5 or 6v6
- Two goals
- Normal football rules
Description
Free play with quick restarts and no added conditions. Rebounds and second chances appear naturally through saves, blocks, and deflections. Players are free to react and finish as the moment demands.
Transfer Emphasis (Optional)
- Double goal if a goal comes from a rebound or second action
- Used to highlight alertness, not to force behaviour
Coach Behaviour
- Observation cues:
- Do players stay active after shots?
- Are rebounds being attacked quickly?
- Recognition phrases:
- “Follow it in.”
- “Great second effort.”
- “You stayed alive.”
