content:
BELCONNEN UNITED FC – SESSION PLAN | CYCLE 6 | WEEK 1 | SESSION 1U11 | Win → Play → Defend | Fast Reaction Games
Session Outcome
Players feel more confident reacting quickly when the game changes — winning it, playing it, or defending immediately.
Phase 1 – Introduction (10–15 mins)
Purpose
Wake up fast reactions when the picture changes.
Setup
- Area: 18x18m (adjust 16–22m)
- Players: 10–12 (everyone with a ball)
- Equipment: Cones, balls
Description
All players dribble freely inside the area. On the coach’s call of different cues (“win”, “play”, “defend”), players must instantly react:
- “Win” – leave your ball and try to steal a nearby ball
- “Play” – find another player and pass once, then move
- “Defend” – get close to the nearest ball carrier and slow them downAfter a few seconds, players return to free dribbling. The activity feels sharp, unpredictable, and lively. When it works well, players switch mindset instantly without hesitation.
Coach Behaviour
- Observation cues:
- Are players reacting immediately to the cue?
- Do they switch roles without freezing or looking around?
- Recognition comments:
- “Quick reaction — you switched straight away.”
- “Good response, no hesitation.”
- “You stayed switched on.”
Phase 2 – Main Part (25–30 mins)
Setup
- Players: 3v3 or 4v4 (multiple games if needed)
- Area: 18x24m (adjust 16x22 to 22x28)
- Equipment: Balls, two small goals
- Organisation: Continuous play with spare balls behind goals for instant restarts
Description
Small-sided games built around constant change. Teams score by finishing in the small goals. When possession changes, the team that wins the ball must try to play forward, while the team that loses it must immediately react to defend or delay. There are frequent turnovers, quick decisions, and short bursts of attacking and defending. When it’s working well, players react faster than the opponent and gain advantage through speed of response rather than structure.
Rules & Success Conditions
- Score by finishing in the small goal
- Immediate restart from the nearest ball
- Single shaping rule: the first 3 seconds after any turnover are live — everyone must react immediately
Managing Challenge (Same Game)
- If the game is too easy:
- Reduce space slightly
- If the game is too hard:
- Increase space slightly
- If energy or engagement drops:
- Shorten games and rotate teams more frequently
Coach Behaviour – What to Watch For
- Speed of reaction after winning or losing the ball
- Willingness to play forward quickly after a win
- Immediate effort to defend after losing itObserve how faster reactions create advantage, and adjust space or numbers without stopping play.
Phase 3 – Final Game (15–20 mins)
Purpose
Allow players to apply fast reactions naturally in a realistic game.
Setup
- Larger numbers: 5v5 or 6v6
- Two goals
- Normal football rules
Description
Free play with quick restarts and no added conditions. The game naturally includes moments of winning, playing, and defending in quick succession. Players are free to react and decide as the game demands.
Transfer Emphasis (Optional)
- Double goal if a goal comes after a quick reaction to a turnover
- Used to highlight alertness and speed of response, not to force behaviour
Coach Behaviour
- Observation cues:
- Do players react instantly when the ball changes teams?
- Are reactions turning into advantage or recovery?
- Recognition phrases:
- “Quick reaction.”
- “You switched on first.”
- “Fast response — well done.”

content:
BELCONNEN UNITED FC – SESSION PLAN | CYCLE 6 | WEEK 1 | SESSION 2U11 | Win → Play → Defend | Win & Play Forward
Session Outcome
Players feel more confident playing forward immediately after winning the ball.
Phase 1 – Introduction (10–15 mins)
Purpose
Create the habit of looking forward first after winning possession.
Setup
- Area: 18x18m (adjust 16–22m)
- Players: 10–12 (everyone with a ball)
- Equipment: Cones, balls
Description
All players dribble freely inside the area. On the coach’s call of “win,” players leave their ball and try to steal a nearby ball using controlled pressure. As soon as a player wins a ball, their task is to drive forward into space for a few seconds before rejoining free dribbling. The activity feels sharp and competitive, with constant switches from defending to attacking. When it works well, players don’t pause after winning the ball — they move forward immediately.
Coach Behaviour
- Observation cues:
- Do players react instantly to win the ball?
- Is the first action after winning the ball forward?
- Recognition comments:
- “Great win — forward straight away.”
- “You didn’t stop, you went.”
- “Nice reaction after the win.”
Phase 2 – Main Part (25–30 mins)
Setup
- Players: 3v3 or 4v4 (multiple games if needed)
- Area: 18x24m (adjust 16x22 to 22x28)
- Equipment: Balls, two small goals
- Organisation: Continuous games with spare balls behind goals for fast restarts
Description
Small-sided games where the key moment is ball recovery. Teams score by finishing in the small goals. When a team wins the ball, the first intention is to play forward — either by driving with the ball or making a forward action toward goal. The opposition reacts by trying to recover and defend. When the game is working well, regains quickly turn into attacks and defenders are forced to scramble.
Rules & Success Conditions
- Score by finishing in the small goal
- Immediate restart from the nearest ball
- Single shaping rule: after winning the ball, the first action must go forward
Managing Challenge (Same Game)
- If the game is too easy:
- Reduce space slightly
- If the game is too hard:
- Increase space slightly
- If energy or engagement drops:
- Shorten rounds and rotate teams quickly
Coach Behaviour – What to Watch For
- Speed of reaction after regaining possession
- First touch or movement going forward
- Willingness to attack before the defence is setObserve how forward actions after regains create advantage, and adjust space or numbers without stopping play.
Phase 3 – Final Game (15–20 mins)
Purpose
Allow players to freely apply win-and-play-forward habits in a realistic game.
Setup
- Larger numbers: 5v5 or 6v6
- Two goals
- Normal football rules
Description
Free play with quick restarts and no added conditions. Natural moments appear where teams win the ball and must decide whether to go forward or not. Players are free to make their own decisions, with an emphasis on positive forward intent.
Transfer Emphasis (Optional)
- Double goal if a goal comes after a clear forward action immediately following a regain
- Used to highlight intent, not to force behaviour
Coach Behaviour
- Observation cues:
- Do players look forward immediately after winning the ball?
- Are regains turning into attacking moments?
- Recognition phrases:
- “Win it and go.”
- “Forward straight away.”
- “Great reaction after the regain.”

heading: U11 Cycle 6, week 2, session 1

content:
BELCONNEN UNITED FC – SESSION PLAN | CYCLE 6 | WEEK 2 | SESSION 1U11 | Win → Play → Defend | Counter or Recover
Session Outcome
Players feel more confident deciding quickly whether to counter forward or recover to defend after winning or losing the ball.
Phase 1 – Introduction (10–15 mins)
Purpose
Expose players to fast decisions between attacking forward and recovering to defend.
Setup
- Area: 18x18m (adjust 16–22m)
- Players: 10–12 (everyone with a ball)
- Equipment: Cones, balls
Description
All players dribble freely inside the area. On the coach’s call of “counter,” players leave their ball and try to steal a nearby ball, then immediately drive forward into open space. On the call of “recover,” players leave their ball and sprint to get goal-side of the nearest ball carrier, slowing them down. Calls are mixed unpredictably. The activity feels sharp and demanding, with constant mindset changes. When it works well, players react instantly and choose the right behaviour without hesitation.
Coach Behaviour
- Observation cues:
- Do players react immediately to the cue?
- Are movements decisive rather than half-speed?
- Recognition comments:
- “Good decision — you countered straight away.”
- “Nice recovery — you got goal-side quickly.”
- “You switched on fast.”
Phase 2 – Main Part (25–30 mins)
Setup
- Players: 3v3 or 4v4 (multiple games if needed)
- Area: 18x24m (adjust 16x22 to 22x28)
- Equipment: Balls, two small goals
- Organisation: Continuous play with spare balls behind goals for quick restarts
Description
Small-sided games with constant transitions. Teams score by finishing in the small goals. When a team wins the ball, players must decide instantly: if there is space, counter forward; if not, the opposition recovers and defends. Likewise, the team that loses the ball must either slow the counter or recover quickly to protect the goal. When the game is working well, players read the picture fast and react with full commitment.
Rules & Success Conditions
- Score by finishing in the small goal
- Immediate restart from the nearest ball
- Single shaping rule: the first 3 seconds after a turnover define the action (counter or recover)
Managing Challenge (Same Game)
- If the game is too easy:
- Reduce space slightly
- If the game is too hard:
- Increase space slightly
- If energy or engagement drops:
- Shorten game rounds and rotate teams more often
Coach Behaviour – What to Watch For
- Speed of reaction at the moment of turnover
- Forward intent when space is available
- Immediate recovery runs when defending is neededObserve how quick decisions affect success, and adjust space or numbers without stopping play.
Phase 3 – Final Game (15–20 mins)
Purpose
Allow players to apply counter-or-recover decisions freely in a realistic game.
Setup
- Larger numbers: 5v5 or 6v6
- Two goals
- Normal football rules
Description
Free play with quick restarts and no added conditions. Transition moments appear naturally. Players are free to counter quickly or recover to defend based on what they see.
Transfer Emphasis (Optional)
- Double goal if a goal comes from a clear counter
- Public recognition for clear recovery runs that prevent a chance
- Used to highlight decision-making, not to force behaviour
Coach Behaviour
- Observation cues:
- Do players read whether to go forward or recover?
- Are reactions immediate and committed?
- Recognition phrases:
- “Good read — you countered.”
- “Great recovery run.”
- “You chose the right action.”
Belconnen United FC – Compact • Brave • Intelligent

heading: U11 Cycle 6, week 2, session 2

content:
BELCONNEN UNITED FC – SESSION PLAN | CYCLE 6 | WEEK 2 | SESSION 2U11 | Win → Play → Defend | Help Behind the Ball
Session Outcome
Players feel more confident recovering quickly behind the ball to help their team defend together.
Phase 1 – Introduction (10–15 mins)
Purpose
Create the habit of helping behind the ball immediately after losing possession.
Setup
- Area: 18x18m (adjust 16–22m)
- Players: 10–12 (everyone with a ball)
- Equipment: Cones, balls
Description
All players dribble freely inside the area. On the coach’s call of “help,” players leave their ball and sprint to get goal-side of the nearest ball carrier, slowing them down and blocking forward space. After a few seconds, players return to their ball and continue dribbling. The activity feels sharp and repetitive, with lots of short recovery runs. When it works well, players react instantly, sprint back with intent, and naturally form a line behind the ball.
Coach Behaviour
- Observation cues:
- Do players react immediately to help behind the ball?
- Are recovery runs direct and committed?
- Recognition comments:
- “Great help — you got behind the ball fast.”
- “Nice recovery run.”
- “You helped your team straight away.”
Phase 2 – Main Part (25–30 mins)
Setup
- Players: 3v3 or 4v4 (multiple games if needed)
- Area: 18x24m (adjust 16x22 to 22x28)
- Equipment: Balls, two small goals
- Organisation: Continuous play with spare balls behind goals for quick restarts
Description
Small-sided games where transitions are constant. Teams score by finishing in the small goals. When a team loses the ball, the immediate task for players away from the ball is to sprint back and get behind it, helping the first defender. The aim is to delay, block space, and make the opponent play around them. When it’s working well, players recover quickly, compact the space, and defend as a group rather than chasing individually.
Rules & Success Conditions
- Score by finishing in the small goal
- Immediate restart from the nearest ball
- Single shaping rule: after losing the ball, all players must attempt to recover behind the ball before pressing again
Managing Challenge (Same Game)
- If the game is too easy:
- Reduce space slightly
- If the game is too hard:
- Increase space slightly
- If energy or engagement drops:
- Shorten game rounds and rotate teams quickly
Coach Behaviour – What to Watch For
- Speed and direction of recovery runs
- Willingness to sprint back instead of watching play
- Players positioning themselves goal-side to help defendObserve how collective recovery helps stop attacks, and adjust space or numbers without stopping play.
Phase 3 – Final Game (15–20 mins)
Purpose
Allow players to apply helping behind the ball naturally in a realistic game.
Setup
- Larger numbers: 5v5 or 6v6
- Two goals
- Normal football rules
Description
Free play with quick restarts and no added conditions. Transition moments appear naturally. Players are free to make decisions, with an emphasis on reacting quickly to help the team when possession is lost.
Transfer Emphasis (Optional)
- Double goal if a team scores after a clear collective recovery behind the ball and then counter-attacks
- Used to highlight teamwork and responsibility, not to force behaviour
Coach Behaviour
- Observation cues:
- Do players recover behind the ball quickly after losing it?
- Are they helping teammates rather than defending alone?
- Recognition phrases:
- “Good help behind the ball.”
- “Everyone recovered — well done.”
- “You helped your team defend.”
Belconnen United FC – Compact • Brave • Intelligent

heading: U11 Cycle 6, week 3, session 1

content:
BELCONNEN UNITED FC – SESSION PLAN | CYCLE 6 | WEEK 3 | SESSION 1U11 | Win → Play → Defend | Chaos Transition Games
Session Outcome
Players feel more confident reacting and competing effectively when the game becomes chaotic after turnovers.
Phase 1 – Introduction (10–15 mins)
Purpose
Expose players to constant, unpredictable transitions and demand instant reactions.
Setup
- Area: 18x18m (adjust 16–22m)
- Players: 10–12 (everyone with a ball)
- Equipment: Cones, balls
Description
All players dribble freely inside the area. On the coach’s call of “chaos,” players immediately leave their ball and react to the nearest situation — steal a ball, protect a ball, or slow a ball carrier. There is no single correct action; players must read what’s closest and react instantly. After a few seconds, players return to free dribbling. The activity feels messy, fast, and competitive. When it works well, players don’t freeze — they act first and adjust on the move.
Coach Behaviour
- Observation cues:
- Do players react instantly without waiting for instruction?
- Are players fully committing to their first action?
- Recognition comments:
- “Good reaction — you went straight away.”
- “You acted first.”
- “Nice switch when it got messy.”
Phase 2 – Main Part (25–30 mins)
Setup
- Players: 3v3 or 4v4 (multiple games if needed)
- Area: 18x24m (adjust 16x22 to 22x28)
- Equipment: Balls, two small goals
- Organisation: Continuous play with spare balls behind goals for fast restarts
Description
Small-sided games designed to stay chaotic. Teams score by finishing in the small goals. Extra balls are fed in immediately when the ball goes out, creating overlapping transition moments. Possession changes often, pressure is constant, and players must continuously decide whether to win it, play forward, or defend. When it’s working well, players react faster than opponents, recover quickly after mistakes, and stay engaged through the chaos.
Rules & Success Conditions
- Score by finishing in the small goal
- Immediate restart with a new ball
- Single shaping rule: no pauses after turnovers — play continues immediately
Managing Challenge (Same Game)
- If the game is too easy:
- Reduce space slightly
- If the game is too hard:
- Increase space slightly
- If energy or engagement drops:
- Add a second ball briefly to increase chaos
Coach Behaviour – What to Watch For
- Speed of reaction at every turnover
- Willingness to compete again after losing the ball
- Ability to switch between attacking and defending rolesObserve how players cope with disorder, and adjust space or ball supply without stopping play.
Phase 3 – Final Game (15–20 mins)
Purpose
Allow players to manage chaotic transitions naturally in a realistic game.
Setup
- Larger numbers: 5v5 or 6v6
- Two goals
- Normal football rules
Description
Free play with quick restarts and no added conditions. Transitions occur constantly and unpredictably. Players are free to make decisions, recover, and compete as the game unfolds.
Transfer Emphasis (Optional)
- Double goal if a goal comes after a chaotic turnover sequence
- Used to highlight reaction and resilience, not to force behaviour
Coach Behaviour
- Observation cues:
- Do players stay engaged when the game becomes messy?
- Are reactions immediate after mistakes or turnovers?
- Recognition phrases:
- “You stayed in it.”
- “Great reaction in the chaos.”
- “You kept competing.”
Belconnen United FC – Compact • Brave • Intelligent

heading: U11 Cycle 6, week 3, session 2
headingPath: U11 sessions > U11 Cycle 6, week 3, session 2
content:
BELCONNEN UNITED FC – SESSION PLAN | CYCLE 6 | WEEK 3 | SESSION 2U11 | Win → Play → Defend | Transition Tournament
Session Outcome
Players feel more confident competing through repeated transitions and reacting faster than opponents across short games.
Phase 1 – Introduction (10–15 mins)
Purpose
Prepare players for rapid transitions and repeated game restarts.
Setup
- Area: 18x18m (adjust 16–22m)
- Players: 10–12 (everyone with a ball)
- Equipment: Cones, balls
Description
All players dribble freely inside the area. On the coach’s call of “change,” players immediately leave their ball and react to the nearest situation — win a ball, protect a ball, or slow a ball carrier. After a few seconds, players return to free dribbling. Calls come frequently and unpredictably. The activity feels sharp and competitive, with players constantly switching roles. When it works well, players react instantly and stay mentally ready for the next moment.
Coach Behaviour
- Observation cues:
- Are players reacting immediately to each change?
- Do they commit fully to their first action?
- Recognition comments:
- “Quick reaction.”
- “You switched on straight away.”
- “Good intent when it changed.”
Phase 2 – Main Part (25–30 mins)
Setup
- Players: 3v3 or 4v4 (mini tournament format)
- Area: 18x24m (adjust 16x22 to 22x28)
- Equipment: Balls, two small goals per pitch
- Organisation:
- Short games (2–3 minutes)
- Winners stay on or rotate clockwise
- Spare balls behind goals for instant restarts
Description
A mini tournament of short, intense games. Teams score by finishing in the small goals. Games restart immediately after goals or when the ball goes out, keeping transitions constant. With short rounds and frequent changes of opponent, players are repeatedly exposed to win → play → defend moments. When it’s working well, intensity stays high, reactions are sharp, and players compete hard every round.
Rules & Success Conditions
- Score by finishing in the small goal
- Immediate restart with a new ball
- Single shaping rule: the first 3 seconds after every restart or turnover are live — react immediately
Managing Challenge (Same Game)
- If the tournament is too easy:
- Reduce space slightly
- If it is too hard:
- Increase space slightly
- If energy or engagement drops:
- Shorten game times and increase rotations
Coach Behaviour – What to Watch For
- Speed of reaction after goals, restarts, and turnovers
- Willingness to compete immediately every round
- Ability to reset quickly for the next gameObserve how repeated transitions affect focus and intensity, and adjust space or rotation timing without stopping play.
Phase 3 – Final Game (15–20 mins)
Purpose
Allow players to express everything learned across the cycle in one free game.
Setup
- Larger numbers: 5v5 or 6v6
- Two goals
- Normal football rules
Description
Free play with quick restarts and no added conditions. All moments — winning it, playing forward, and defending — appear naturally. Players manage the game themselves after a tournament-style load.
Transfer Emphasis (Optional)
- Public recognition for teams or players who react fastest after turnovers
- Used to highlight mentality and readiness, not to force behaviour
Coach Behaviour
- Observation cues:
- Do players stay sharp despite fatigue?
- Are transitions still being played with intent?
- Recognition phrases:
- “You stayed ready.”
- “Great reaction again.”
- “You competed every round.”
Belconnen United FC – Compact • Brave • Intelligent

Week 1
- Session 1 – Defend Your Zone Together
- Session 2 – Stay Close & Protect
Week 2
- Session 1 – Help the First Defender
- Session 2 – Nearest 3 Defend
Week 3
- Session 1 – Protect the Middle
- Session 2 – Stay Connected Game

content:
BELCONNEN UNITED FC – SESSION PLAN | CYCLE 7 | WEEK 1 | SESSION 1U11 | Stay Close Together | Defend Your Zone Together
Session Outcome
Players feel more confident staying close together to protect space and defend as a group.
Phase 1 – Introduction (10–15 mins)
Purpose
Help players feel how staying close makes defending easier.
Setup
- Area: 18x18m (adjust 16–22m)
- Players: 10–12 (everyone with a ball)
- Equipment: Cones, balls
Description
All players dribble freely inside the area. On the coach’s call of “defend,” players leave their ball and group together in a small cluster, moving as one to block space and slow nearby ball carriers (no tackling). After a few seconds, players return to their own ball and continue dribbling. The activity feels simple and repetitive. When it works well, players naturally close gaps, move together, and make the space feel tight for the attacker.
Coach Behaviour
- Observation cues:
- Do players move closer together when defending?
- Are gaps between players shrinking quickly?
- Recognition comments:
- “Nice — you stayed close together.”
- “Good job closing the space.”
- “You defended as a group.”
Phase 2 – Main Part (25–30 mins)
Setup
- Players: 3v3 or 4v4 (multiple games if needed)
- Area: 18x24m (adjust 16x22 to 22x28)
- Equipment: Balls, end zones or small goals
- Organisation: Continuous games with spare balls ready for fast restarts
Description
Small-sided games where teams defend a zone together. Teams score by entering an end zone or finishing in a small goal. When defending, players are encouraged to protect space as a unit rather than chasing individually. Attackers try to move the ball to create gaps; defenders try to stay connected and block forward progress. When it’s working well, defenders slide together, close space early, and make it hard to play through them.
Rules & Success Conditions
- Score by entering the end zone or finishing in the small goal
- Immediate restart from the nearest ball
- Single shaping rule: defenders must stay within close distance of each other when out of possession
Managing Challenge (Same Game)
- If the game is too easy:
- Increase space slightly
- If the game is too hard:
- Reduce space slightly
- If energy or engagement drops:
- Change scoring method (end zones ↔ goals)
Coach Behaviour – What to Watch For
- Distance between defenders
- Movement together toward the ball
- Willingness to hold space instead of chasingObserve how staying close helps teams defend space, and adjust pitch size without stopping play.
Phase 3 – Final Game (15–20 mins)
Purpose
Allow players to apply group defending naturally in a realistic game.
Setup
- Larger numbers: 5v5 or 6v6
- Two goals
- Normal football rules
Description
Free play with quick restarts and no added conditions. Defensive moments appear naturally. Players are free to defend as they choose, with an emphasis on staying connected and protecting space together.
Transfer Emphasis (Optional)
- Double goal if a team wins the ball back after clearly staying compact and connected
- Used to highlight togetherness, not to force behaviour
Coach Behaviour
- Observation cues:
- Do players stay close when defending?
- Are spaces between defenders being protected?
- Recognition phrases:
- “Stay close together.”
- “Good team defending.”
- “You protected the space.”
Belconnen United FC – Compact • Brave • Intelligent

content:
BELCONNEN UNITED FC – SESSION PLAN | CYCLE 7 | WEEK 1 | SESSION 2U11 | Stay Close Together | Stay Close & Protect
Session Outcome
Players feel more confident staying close to teammates to protect space and help each other defend.
Phase 1 – Introduction (10–15 mins)
Purpose
Help players feel how closeness and support make defending safer.
Setup
- Area: 18x18m (adjust 16–22m)
- Players: 10–12 (everyone with a ball)
- Equipment: Cones, balls
Description
All players dribble freely inside the area. On the coach’s call of “protect,” players leave their ball and move close to two or three nearby teammates, forming a small group that shifts together to block space and slow ball carriers (no tackling). After a few seconds, players return to their own ball and continue dribbling. The picture should feel calm and organised. When it works well, players close gaps quickly and move as a group rather than spreading out.
Coach Behaviour
- Observation cues:
- Do players move closer together when protecting space?
- Are gaps between teammates disappearing quickly?
- Recognition comments:
- “Good job staying close.”
- “You protected that space together.”
- “Nice support for each other.”
Phase 2 – Main Part (25–30 mins)
Setup
- Players: 3v3 or 4v4 (multiple games if needed)
- Area: 18x24m (adjust 16x22 to 22x28)
- Equipment: Balls, end zones or small goals
- Organisation: Continuous play with spare balls ready for quick restarts
Description
Small-sided games where defending teams are encouraged to stay close and protect space together. Teams score by entering an end zone or finishing in a small goal. Attackers try to move the ball to pull defenders apart; defenders try to slide, recover, and stay connected. When it’s working well, defenders arrive together, block central space, and make attackers slow down or go wide.
Rules & Success Conditions
- Score by entering an end zone or finishing in the small goal
- Immediate restart from the nearest ball
- Single shaping rule: defenders must stay within close support distance of a teammate when out of possession
Managing Challenge (Same Game)
- If the game is too easy:
- Increase space slightly
- If the game is too hard:
- Reduce space slightly
- If energy or engagement drops:
- Change scoring method (end zones ↔ goals)
Coach Behaviour – What to Watch For
- Distance between defenders when the opponent has the ball
- Movement together toward danger areas
- Willingness to protect space instead of chasing aloneObserve how closeness helps teams defend, and adjust pitch size without stopping play.
Phase 3 – Final Game (15–20 mins)
Purpose
Allow players to apply staying close and protecting space naturally in a real game.
Setup
- Larger numbers: 5v5 or 6v6
- Two goals
- Normal football rules
Description
Free play with quick restarts and no added conditions. Defensive moments appear naturally. Players are free to make decisions, with an emphasis on helping each other by staying close and protecting space.
Transfer Emphasis (Optional)
- Double goal if a team wins the ball back after clearly staying close and protecting space together
- Used to highlight togetherness, not to force behaviour
Coach Behaviour
- Observation cues:
- Do players stay connected when defending?
- Are dangerous spaces being protected by more than one player?
- Recognition phrases:
- “Stay close and protect.”
- “Good help for your teammate.”
- “You defended that space together.”
Belconnen United FC – Compact • Brave • Intelligent

content:
BELCONNEN UNITED FC – SESSION PLAN | CYCLE 7 | WEEK 2 | SESSION 1U11 | Stay Close Together | Help the First Defender
Session Outcome
Players feel more confident moving quickly to support the first defender and protect space together.
Phase 1 – Introduction (10–15 mins)
Purpose
Help players recognise that defending works better when the first defender is not alone.
Setup
- Area: 18x18m (adjust 16–22m)
- Players: 10–12 (everyone with a ball)
- Equipment: Cones, balls
Description
All players dribble freely inside the area. On the coach’s call of “press,” one player becomes the first defender by slowing a nearby ball carrier. Immediately, 1–2 nearby players leave their ball and move in to help by getting close, blocking space, and staying goal-side (no tackling). After a few seconds, players return to free dribbling. When it works well, the first defender is quickly supported and the ball carrier feels surrounded rather than free.
Coach Behaviour
- Observation cues:
- Does help arrive quickly for the first defender?
- Are players closing space together instead of standing off?
- Recognition comments:
- “Good help — you backed them up.”
- “Nice support for the first defender.”
- “You arrived quickly to help.”
Phase 2 – Main Part (25–30 mins)
Setup
- Players: 3v3 or 4v4 (multiple games if needed)
- Area: 18x24m (adjust 16x22 to 22x28)
- Equipment: Balls, end zones or small goals
- Organisation: Continuous play with spare balls ready for fast restarts
Description
Small-sided games where defending success depends on supporting the first defender. Teams score by entering an end zone or finishing in a small goal. When a defender presses the ball, teammates are encouraged to move in quickly to help — blocking passing lanes, closing space, and staying compact. When it’s working well, attackers are slowed, forced sideways, or stopped because the first defender is backed up immediately.
Rules & Success Conditions
- Score by entering the end zone or finishing in the small goal
- Immediate restart from the nearest ball
- Single shaping rule: when one defender presses the ball, at least one teammate must move to support
Managing Challenge (Same Game)
- If the game is too easy:
- Increase space slightly
- If the game is too hard:
- Reduce space slightly
- If energy or engagement drops:
- Change scoring method (end zones ↔ goals)
Coach Behaviour – What to Watch For
- Speed of support for the first defender
- Distances between defenders when pressing
- Willingness to help instead of watchingObserve how quick support helps win or slow the ball, and adjust space without stopping play.
Phase 3 – Final Game (15–20 mins)
Purpose
Allow players to apply helping the first defender naturally in a realistic game.
Setup
- Larger numbers: 5v5 or 6v6
- Two goals
- Normal football rules
Description
Free play with quick restarts and no added conditions. Pressing moments appear naturally. Players are free to decide when to press and when to help, with an emphasis on not leaving the first defender isolated.
Transfer Emphasis (Optional)
- Double goal if a team wins the ball back after clear support arrives for the first defender
- Used to highlight teamwork, not to force behaviour
Coach Behaviour
- Observation cues:
- Does the first defender receive help quickly?
- Are players defending together instead of alone?
- Recognition phrases:
- “Good help for your teammate.”
- “You didn’t leave them alone.”
- “Nice team defending.”
Belconnen United FC – Compact • Brave • Intelligent

content:
BELCONNEN UNITED FC – SESSION PLAN | CYCLE 7 | WEEK 2 | SESSION 2U11 | Stay Close Together | Nearest 3 Defend
Session Outcome
Players feel more confident reacting together as the nearest three to protect space and defend the ball.
Phase 1 – Introduction (10–15 mins)
Purpose
Introduce the idea that the nearest three players must react together to defend.
Setup
- Area: 18x18m (adjust 16–22m)
- Players: 10–12 (everyone with a ball)
- Equipment: Cones, balls
Description
All players dribble freely inside the area. On the coach’s call of “three,” the nearest three players to any ball carrier immediately leave their balls and move together to slow, block, and protect space around that ball (no tackling). The ball carrier keeps moving, trying to escape. After a few seconds, players return to free dribbling. When it works well, reactions are instant, distances are short, and the ball carrier feels pressure from more than one angle.
Coach Behaviour
- Observation cues:
- Do the nearest three react immediately?
- Are they arriving together, not one by one?
- Recognition comments:
- “Nice — the three of you went together.”
- “Good reaction as a group.”
- “You closed that space fast.”
Phase 2 – Main Part (25–30 mins)
Setup
- Players: 3v3 or 4v4 (multiple games if needed)
- Area: 18x24m (adjust 16x22 to 22x28)
- Equipment: Balls, end zones or small goals
- Organisation: Continuous play with spare balls ready for fast restarts
Description
Small-sided games where defending success depends on the nearest three players reacting together. Teams score by entering an end zone or finishing in a small goal. When a team loses the ball, the nearest three players are responsible for defending — slowing the ball, blocking forward space, and protecting central areas. When it’s working well, pressure is immediate, support is close, and attackers are forced to stop or play sideways.
Rules & Success Conditions
- Score by entering an end zone or finishing in the small goal
- Immediate restart from the nearest ball
- Single shaping rule: on loss of possession, the nearest three must react to defend before anyone else engages
Managing Challenge (Same Game)
- If the game is too easy:
- Increase space slightly
- If the game is too hard:
- Reduce space slightly
- If energy or engagement drops:
- Change scoring method (end zones ↔ goals)
Coach Behaviour – What to Watch For
- Speed of reaction from the nearest three
- Distances between those three when defending
- Willingness to protect space together rather than chasing aloneObserve how group reactions limit space, and adjust pitch size without stopping play.
Phase 3 – Final Game (15–20 mins)
Purpose
Allow players to apply nearest-three defending naturally in a realistic game.
Setup
- Larger numbers: 5v5 or 6v6
- Two goals
- Normal football rules
Description
Free play with quick restarts and no added conditions. Defensive moments appear naturally. Players are free to react, but emphasis is on nearby players helping immediately when the ball is lost.
Transfer Emphasis (Optional)
- Double goal if a team wins the ball back after the nearest three clearly react together
- Used to highlight collective defending, not to force behaviour
Coach Behaviour
- Observation cues:
- Do the nearest three react quickly on loss?
- Are players defending in small groups rather than individually?
- Recognition phrases:
- “Nearest three — well done.”
- “You defended that together.”
- “Good group reaction.”
Belconnen United FC – Compact • Brave • Intelligent

heading: U11 Cycle 7, week 3, session 1

content:
BELCONNEN UNITED FC – SESSION PLAN | CYCLE 7 | WEEK 3 | SESSION 1U11 | Stay Close Together | Protect the Middle
Session Outcome
Players feel more confident defending the central space together and stopping opponents playing through the middle.
Phase 1 – Introduction (10–15 mins)
Purpose
Help players feel why the middle of the pitch is worth protecting together.
Setup
- Area: 18x18m (adjust 16–22m)
- Players: 10–12 (everyone with a ball)
- Equipment: Cones (mark a small central square), balls
Description
All players dribble freely inside the area. A small central square is marked as the “middle.” On the coach’s call of “protect,” players leave their ball and move together to protect the central square by standing close, blocking access, and shifting side to side as ball carriers move around it (no tackling). After a few seconds, players return to free dribbling. When it works well, the middle feels crowded, gaps disappear quickly, and attackers are forced to go around rather than through.
Coach Behaviour
- Observation cues:
- Do players close the middle quickly when called?
- Are they staying close together while protecting it?
- Recognition comments:
- “Good job protecting the middle.”
- “Nice — you closed the centre together.”
- “You made it hard to play through.”
Phase 2 – Main Part (25–30 mins)
Setup
- Players: 3v3 or 4v4 (multiple games if needed)
- Area: 18x24m (adjust 16x22 to 22x28)
- Equipment: Balls, small goals or end zones, cones marking a central channel
- Organisation: Continuous play with spare balls ready for quick restarts
Description
Small-sided games where the key defensive task is protecting the middle. Teams score by entering an end zone or finishing in a small goal. A narrow central channel is marked to represent the most dangerous space. Defending teams are encouraged to stay compact and block this central area, forcing attackers to play around the outside. When it’s working well, defenders slide together, deny central passes, and slow attacks by protecting the most valuable space.
Rules & Success Conditions
- Score by entering an end zone or finishing in the small goal
- Immediate restart from the nearest ball
- Single shaping rule: goals count double if scored through the central channel
Managing Challenge (Same Game)
- If the game is too easy:
- Widen the pitch slightly
- If the game is too hard:
- Narrow the pitch or central channel
- If energy or engagement drops:
- Swap scoring method (goals ↔ end zones)
Coach Behaviour – What to Watch For
- Defender positioning in relation to the middle
- Distance between defenders when blocking central space
- Willingness to let the ball go wide while protecting the centreObserve how compactness protects the middle, and adjust space without stopping play.
Phase 3 – Final Game (15–20 mins)
Purpose
Allow players to apply protecting the middle naturally in a realistic game.
Setup
- Larger numbers: 5v5 or 6v6
- Two goals
- Normal football rules
Description
Free play with quick restarts and no added conditions. Attacking and defending moments appear naturally. Players are free to make decisions, with emphasis on staying compact and protecting central areas when defending.
Transfer Emphasis (Optional)
- Double goal if a team wins the ball back after clearly protecting the middle together
- Used to highlight defensive priorities, not to force behaviour
Coach Behaviour
- Observation cues:
- Are players staying compact around the middle when defending?
- Is central space being protected before wide areas?
- Recognition phrases:
- “Protect the middle.”
- “Good compact defending.”
- “You blocked the centre together.”
Belconnen United FC – Compact • Brave • Intelligent

content:
BELCONNEN UNITED FC – SESSION PLAN | CYCLE 7 | WEEK 3 | SESSION 2U11 | Stay Close Together | Stay Connected Game
Session Outcome
Players feel more confident staying connected to teammates while defending and moving together as the game shifts.
Phase 1 – Introduction (10–15 mins)
Purpose
Let players feel how staying connected helps them react and defend more easily.
Setup
- Area: 18x18m (adjust 16–22m)
- Players: 10–12 (everyone with a ball)
- Equipment: Cones, balls
Description
All players dribble freely inside the area. On the coach’s call of “connect,” players leave their ball and link up with two nearby teammates, moving together as a small group to slow and block a ball carrier (no tackling). Groups shift side to side, staying close and connected. After a few seconds, players return to free dribbling. When it works well, players close gaps quickly, communicate naturally, and move as one unit.
Coach Behaviour
- Observation cues:
- Do players stay close while moving together?
- Are they adjusting together as the ball moves?
- Recognition comments:
- “Nice connection — you moved together.”
- “Good support for each other.”
- “You stayed connected.”
Phase 2 – Main Part (25–30 mins)
Setup
- Players: 3v3 or 4v4 (multiple games if needed)
- Area: 18x24m (adjust 16x22 to 22x28)
- Equipment: Balls, small goals or end zones
- Organisation: Continuous play with spare balls ready for fast restarts
Description
Small-sided games where success depends on staying connected while defending. Teams score by entering an end zone or finishing in a small goal. When defending, players are encouraged to move together, close space as a group, and avoid being stretched apart. Attackers try to pull defenders away from each other; defenders try to stay compact and slide together. When it’s working well, defenders arrive in pairs or threes, protect space, and make attacking progress difficult.
Rules & Success Conditions
- Score by entering an end zone or finishing in the small goal
- Immediate restart from the nearest ball
- Single shaping rule: when defending, players must stay within close support distance of a teammate
Managing Challenge (Same Game)
- If the game is too easy:
- Increase space slightly
- If the game is too hard:
- Reduce space slightly
- If energy or engagement drops:
- Change scoring method (end zones ↔ goals)
Coach Behaviour – What to Watch For
- Distances between defenders
- Movement together toward the ball
- Willingness to protect space as a group rather than chase aloneObserve how connection helps teams defend effectively, and adjust pitch size without stopping play.
Phase 3 – Final Game (15–20 mins)
Purpose
Allow players to apply staying connected naturally in a realistic game.
Setup
- Larger numbers: 5v5 or 6v6
- Two goals
- Normal football rules
Description
Free play with quick restarts and no added conditions. Defensive moments appear naturally. Players are free to make decisions, with emphasis on staying connected and helping nearby teammates.
Transfer Emphasis (Optional)
- Double goal if a team wins the ball back after clearly staying connected as a group
- Used to highlight togetherness, not to force behaviour
Coach Behaviour
- Observation cues:
- Do players stay connected when defending?
- Are teammates helping each other quickly?
- Recognition phrases:
- “Stay connected.”
- “Good team shape.”
- “You defended that together.”

heading: U11 Cycle 8, week 1, session 1

content:
BELCONNEN UNITED FC – SESSION PLAN | CYCLE 8 | WEEK 1 | SESSION 1U11 | Find the Free Player | Look Before You Get It
Session Outcome
Players feel more confident looking around before receiving the ball to find space or a free teammate.
Phase 1 – Introduction (10–15 mins)
Purpose
Create the habit of scanning before the ball arrives.
Setup
- Area: 18x18m (adjust 16–22m)
- Players: 10–12 (everyone with a ball)
- Equipment: Cones, balls
Description
All players dribble freely inside the area. While moving, players are encouraged to lift their head and notice who is nearby and where space is. On the coach’s call of a number or colour, players must quickly point to it while still controlling the ball, then continue dribbling. The task encourages head movement without stopping the ball. When it works well, players are dribbling smoothly, scanning often, and staying aware of what’s around them.
Coach Behaviour
- Observation cues:
- Are players lifting their head while the ball is moving?
- Can they react to information without losing control?
- Recognition comments:
- “Nice look up — you kept the ball moving.”
- “Good awareness.”
- “You saw it early.”
Phase 2 – Main Part (25–30 mins)
Setup
- Players: 3v3 or 4v4 (multiple games if needed)
- Area: 18x24m (adjust 16x22 to 22x28)
- Equipment: Balls, end zones or small goals
- Organisation: Continuous play with spare balls ready for quick restarts
Description
Small-sided games where success depends on awareness before receiving. Teams score by entering an end zone or finishing in a small goal. Pressure is realistic, but space opens quickly if players look early and choose well. When the game is working well, players receive the ball already knowing where space or teammates are, allowing quicker decisions and smoother play.
Rules & Success Conditions
- Score by entering an end zone or finishing in a small goal
- Immediate restart from the nearest ball
- Single shaping rule: players must look up before receiving the ball (coach reinforces through recognition, not stoppage)
Managing Challenge (Same Game)
- If the game is too easy:
- Reduce space slightly
- If the game is too hard:
- Increase space slightly
- If energy or engagement drops:
- Change scoring method (end zones ↔ goals)
Coach Behaviour – What to Watch For
- Head movement before receiving
- First touch away from pressure
- Decisions made quickly after the first touchObserve how scanning improves decision-making, and adjust space without stopping play.
Phase 3 – Final Game (15–20 mins)
Purpose
Allow players to apply scanning naturally in a realistic game.
Setup
- Larger numbers: 5v5 or 6v6
- Two goals
- Normal football rules
Description
Free play with quick restarts and no added conditions. Players are free to dribble or pass, but moments naturally appear where looking early helps them find space or a free teammate.
Transfer Emphasis (Optional)
- Double goal if a goal comes after a clear look before receiving
- Used to highlight awareness, not to force behaviour
Coach Behaviour
- Observation cues:
- Do players scan before the ball arrives?
- Are decisions quicker after receiving?
- Recognition phrases:
- “Good look before you got it.”
- “You saw the space early.”
- “Nice awareness.”
Belconnen United FC – Compact • Brave • Intelligent

heading: U11 Cycle 8, week 1, session 2
headingPath: U11 sessions > U11 Cycle 8, week 1, session 2
content:
BELCONNEN UNITED FC – SESSION PLAN | CYCLE 8 | WEEK 1 | SESSION 2U11 | Find the Free Player | Find Space to Play
Session Outcome
Players feel more confident moving into space to receive the ball and keep play going.
Phase 1 – Introduction (10–15 mins)
Purpose
Help players feel how moving away from others creates space to play.
Setup
- Area: 18x18m (adjust 16–22m)
- Players: 10–12 (pairs with one ball)
- Equipment: Cones, balls
Description
Players work in pairs, sharing one ball, moving freely inside the area. The ball carrier dribbles slowly while the partner moves to find space to receive. Short passes are played whenever space appears. There are no defenders — pressure comes from congestion and timing. When it’s working well, the off-ball player keeps drifting into new spaces, the ball moves smoothly, and pairs rarely stop.
Coach Behaviour
- Observation cues:
- Is the off-ball player moving away from traffic?
- Does movement make passing easier?
- Recognition comments:
- “Nice movement into space.”
- “You made yourself free.”
- “Good support — easy pass.”
Phase 2 – Main Part (25–30 mins)
Setup
- Players: 3v3 or 4v4 (multiple games if needed)
- Area: 18x24m (adjust 16x22 to 22x28)
- Equipment: Balls, end zones or small gates
- Organisation: Continuous play with spare balls ready for quick restarts
Description
Small-sided games where success comes from finding space to receive. Teams score by passing into an end zone or through a gate after a teammate has moved into space to receive the ball. Defenders apply realistic pressure, forcing players to move, adjust, and stay aware. When it’s working well, players don’t hide behind defenders — they drift, check away, and show clearly for the ball.
Rules & Success Conditions
- Score by passing into an end zone or through a gate
- Immediate restart from the nearest ball
- Single shaping rule: a goal only counts if the receiver has moved into space before receiving
Managing Challenge (Same Game)
- If the game is too easy:
- Reduce space slightly or remove a gate
- If the game is too hard:
- Increase space slightly or add an extra gate
- If energy or engagement drops:
- Change gate positions or scoring direction
Coach Behaviour – What to Watch For
- Movement off the ball to find space
- Clear body shape when receiving
- Willingness to move again after passingObserve how movement creates passing options, and adjust space or scoring without stopping play.
Phase 3 – Final Game (15–20 mins)
Purpose
Allow players to apply finding space naturally in a realistic game.
Setup
- Larger numbers: 5v5 or 6v6
- Two goals
- Normal football rules
Description
Free play with quick restarts and no added conditions. Players are free to dribble or pass, but space constantly opens and closes. Those who move well away from pressure find more time on the ball.
Transfer Emphasis (Optional)
- Double goal if a goal comes after a clear movement into space to receive
- Used to highlight off-ball movement, not to force behaviour
Coach Behaviour
- Observation cues:
- Do players move to make themselves free?
- Are passes finding players in space?
- Recognition phrases:
- “You found space.”
- “Good movement off the ball.”
- “You made it easy to play.”

heading: U11 Cycle 8, week 2, session 1

content:
BELCONNEN UNITED FC – SESSION PLAN | CYCLE 8 | WEEK 2 | SESSION 1U11 | Find the Free Player | Switch the Game
Session Outcome
Players feel more confident recognising when one side is crowded and switching play to a free teammate.
Phase 1 – Introduction (10–15 mins)
Purpose
Help players feel how space appears when the ball moves away from pressure.
Setup
- Area: 18x18m, split visually into two halves (adjust 16–22m)
- Players: 10–12 (pairs with one ball)
- Equipment: Cones, balls
Description
Players work in pairs, sharing one ball. Pairs move freely, but the coach encourages them to spend a few seconds in one half before switching to the other half with a pass or carry. There are no defenders — pressure comes from congestion. When one side feels busy, players naturally move the ball to the quieter side. When it’s working well, players notice crowding early and change sides smoothly without stopping.
Coach Behaviour
- Observation cues:
- Do players notice when one side is crowded?
- Are switches calm and controlled?
- Recognition comments:
- “Nice switch — you found space.”
- “Good decision to move it away from pressure.”
- “You saw the free side early.”
Phase 2 – Main Part (25–30 mins)
Setup
- Players: 3v3 or 4v4
- Area: 18x24m, clearly divided into two wide channels (adjust 16x22 to 22x28)
- Equipment: Balls, end zones or small goals
- Organisation: Continuous play with spare balls for fast restarts
Description
Small-sided games where space is created by switching the ball. Teams score by entering an end zone or finishing in a small goal, but play often becomes crowded on one side. Success comes when players recognise pressure, move the ball across the pitch, and attack the free side. When it’s working well, defenders get drawn to the ball, and attackers use width to find time and space.
Rules & Success Conditions
- Score by entering the end zone or finishing in the small goal
- Immediate restart from the nearest ball
- Single shaping rule: a goal counts double if it comes after a clear switch from one side to the other
Managing Challenge (Same Game)
- If the game is too easy:
- Narrow the pitch slightly
- If the game is too hard:
- Widen the pitch slightly
- If energy or engagement drops:
- Change scoring direction or swap channels
Coach Behaviour – What to Watch For
- Awareness of pressure on the ball
- Passes or carries that move play away from congestion
- Movement of teammates into free space on the far sideObserve how switching creates time and space, and adjust pitch width without stopping play.
Phase 3 – Final Game (15–20 mins)
Purpose
Allow players to apply switching play naturally in a realistic game.
Setup
- Larger numbers: 5v5 or 6v6
- Two goals
- Normal football rules
Description
Free play with quick restarts and no added conditions. Crowded moments appear naturally. Players are free to dribble or pass, with emphasis on finding the free side when pressure builds.
Transfer Emphasis (Optional)
- Double goal if a goal comes after a clear switch of play
- Used to highlight awareness and patience, not to force behaviour
Coach Behaviour
- Observation cues:
- Do players recognise when to move the ball away from pressure?
- Are teammates spreading out to offer a switch option?
- Recognition phrases:
- “Good switch.”
- “You found the free side.”
- “Nice decision to change the game.”

heading: U11 Cycle 8, week 2, session 2

content:
BELCONNEN UNITED FC – SESSION PLAN | CYCLE 8 | WEEK 2 | SESSION 2U11 | Find the Free Player | Use the Whole Pitch
Session Outcome
Players feel more confident using the full width and depth of the pitch to find free teammates.
Phase 1 – Introduction (10–15 mins)
Purpose
Help players feel how space appears when they spread out.
Setup
- Area: 22x18m (wider than long, adjust 20–26m width)
- Players: 10–12 (pairs with one ball)
- Equipment: Cones, balls
Description
Players work in pairs, sharing one ball, moving freely across the wide area. The ball carrier dribbles while the partner moves into different spaces — wide, high, or behind — to receive. Pairs are encouraged to travel across the full width, not staying in one lane. There are no defenders — pressure comes from space awareness and timing. When it works well, players naturally spread out, pass across space, and avoid bunching in the middle.
Coach Behaviour
- Observation cues:
- Are pairs using the full width of the area?
- Is the ball being moved away from traffic?
- Recognition comments:
- “Nice — you used the whole space.”
- “Good decision to go wide.”
- “You made the pitch big.”
Phase 2 – Main Part (25–30 mins)
Setup
- Players: 3v3 or 4v4
- Area: 22x24m (wide pitch, adjust 20x22 to 26x28)
- Equipment: Balls, wide end zones or wide gates
- Organisation: Continuous play with spare balls ready for fast restarts
Description
Small-sided games where success comes from using width and depth. Teams score by passing or dribbling into wide end zones or through wide gates. Central areas become crowded quickly, so players must move the ball and themselves to different parts of the pitch. When it’s working well, players spread out naturally, switch play, and find free teammates by using all available space.
Rules & Success Conditions
- Score by entering a wide end zone or passing through a wide gate
- Immediate restart from the nearest ball
- Single shaping rule: goals only count if the ball has travelled through two different areas of the pitch before scoring
Managing Challenge (Same Game)
- If the game is too easy:
- Narrow the pitch slightly
- If the game is too hard:
- Widen the pitch slightly
- If energy or engagement drops:
- Change gate positions or scoring direction
Coach Behaviour – What to Watch For
- Player spacing across the pitch
- Willingness to move wide or drop away from pressure
- Passes that travel into free spaceObserve how pitch use creates time and options, and adjust width without stopping play.
Phase 3 – Final Game (15–20 mins)
Purpose
Allow players to apply using the whole pitch naturally in a realistic game.
Setup
- Larger numbers: 5v5 or 6v6
- Two goals
- Normal football rules
Description
Free play with quick restarts and no added conditions. The full pitch is available. Space appears wide, deep, and central depending on movement. Players are free to dribble or pass, with encouragement to stretch the game.
Transfer Emphasis (Optional)
- Double goal if a goal comes after clear use of width or a switch to a free side
- Used to highlight awareness and spacing, not to force behaviour
Coach Behaviour
- Observation cues:
- Are players spreading out to use the pitch?
- Is the ball moving away from congestion?
- Recognition phrases:
- “Good width.”
- “You used the whole pitch.”
- “Nice find in space.”

content:
BELCONNEN UNITED FC – SESSION PLAN | CYCLE 8 | WEEK 3 | SESSION 1U11 | Find the Free Player | Wide to Score
Session Outcome
Players feel more confident using wide space to create a chance and score.
Phase 1 – Introduction (10–15 mins)
Purpose
Help players feel how going wide can open a path to goal.
Setup
- Area: 22x18m (wider than long, adjust 20–26m width)
- Players: 10–12 (pairs with one ball)
- Equipment: Cones, balls, 2 mini goals placed wide
Description
Players work in pairs, sharing one ball. Pairs move freely across the wide area. The ball carrier dribbles while the partner moves into wide space to receive. When a pair reaches a wide goal, they can finish and then re-enter play. There are no defenders — pressure comes from congestion in central areas. When it works well, players naturally drift wide, receive facing forward, and attack goal from wide positions.
Coach Behaviour
- Observation cues:
- Are players recognising space out wide?
- Does moving wide make finishing easier?
- Recognition comments:
- “Nice — you found space wide.”
- “Good decision to go wide and score.”
- “You made the pitch big.”
Phase 2 – Main Part (25–30 mins)
Setup
- Players: 3v3 or 4v4 (multiple games if needed)
- Area: 22x24m (wide pitch, adjust 20x22 to 26x28)
- Equipment: Balls, two goals, wide channels marked with cones
- Organisation: Continuous play with spare balls ready for fast restarts
Description
Small-sided games where goals are easiest to score after using wide space. Central areas quickly become crowded, encouraging players to move the ball wide to attack. Teams try to stretch the game, find a free player in the wide channel, and then attack goal. When it’s working well, defenders get pulled across, space opens near goal, and wide players attack decisively.
Rules & Success Conditions
- Score by finishing in the main goals
- Immediate restart from the nearest ball
- Single shaping rule: goals count double if the attack includes a pass or dribble in a wide channel before scoring
Managing Challenge (Same Game)
- If the game is too easy:
- Narrow the pitch slightly
- If the game is too hard:
- Widen the pitch or channels
- If energy or engagement drops:
- Flip goals or attack direction
Coach Behaviour – What to Watch For
- Movement into wide space to receive
- Timing of passes to wide players
- Attacking intent after receiving wideObserve how width creates chances, and adjust pitch width without stopping play.
Phase 3 – Final Game (15–20 mins)
Purpose
Allow players to apply using width to score naturally in a real game.
Setup
- Larger numbers: 5v5 or 6v6
- Two goals
- Normal football rules
Description
Free play with quick restarts and no added conditions. Space appears naturally wide and central. Players are free to dribble or pass, with encouragement to use wide areas to create scoring chances.
Transfer Emphasis (Optional)
- Double goal if a goal comes after clear use of wide space
- Used to highlight decision-making, not to force behaviour
Coach Behaviour
- Observation cues:
- Do players look wide when the middle is crowded?
- Are wide players attacking positively?
- Recognition phrases:
- “Good use of width.”
- “You went wide to score.”
- “Nice decision — wide and finish.”

heading: U11 Cycle 8, week 3, session 2

content:
BELCONNEN UNITED FC – SESSION PLAN | CYCLE 8 | WEEK 3 | SESSION 2U11 | Find the Free Player | Change the Picture
Session Outcome
Players feel more confident changing the picture by moving the ball or themselves to find a free option.
Phase 1 – Introduction (10–15 mins)
Purpose
Help players feel that when the picture is blocked, it can be changed.
Setup
- Area: 18x18m (adjust 16–22m)
- Players: 10–12 (pairs with one ball)
- Equipment: Cones, balls
Description
Players work in pairs, sharing one ball, moving freely inside the area. The ball carrier dribbles while the partner moves to offer a passing option. When space feels blocked or crowded, pairs are encouraged to change the picture by turning away, moving the ball to a new area, or switching roles. There are no defenders — pressure comes from congestion and timing. When it works well, players don’t force passes; they reset, move, and create a new picture to play from.
Coach Behaviour
- Observation cues:
- Do players move when the picture is blocked?
- Are they comfortable resetting and trying again?
- Recognition comments:
- “Good reset — you changed the picture.”
- “Nice turn away from pressure.”
- “You created a new option.”
Phase 2 – Main Part (25–30 mins)
Setup
- Players: 3v3 or 4v4 (multiple games if needed)
- Area: 18x24m (adjust 16x22 to 22x28)
- Equipment: Balls, end zones or small goals
- Organisation: Continuous play with spare balls ready for fast restarts
Description
Small-sided games where the ball is often blocked or pressured. Teams score by entering an end zone or finishing in a small goal. When forward options are closed, players must change the picture by turning out, passing away from pressure, or moving to a new space to receive again. When it’s working well, the ball moves away from danger, teammates reposition quickly, and new passing lanes appear.
Rules & Success Conditions
- Score by entering an end zone or finishing in the small goal
- Immediate restart from the nearest ball
- Single shaping rule: if forward play is blocked, the ball must be moved or carried to a new space before attacking again
Managing Challenge (Same Game)
- If the game is too easy:
- Reduce space slightly
- If the game is too hard:
- Increase space slightly
- If energy or engagement drops:
- Change scoring method (end zones ↔ goals)
Coach Behaviour – What to Watch For
- Willingness to turn away from pressure
- Passes that move the ball to a new area
- Movement off the ball to create a new pictureObserve how changing the picture creates new options, and adjust space without stopping play.
Phase 3 – Final Game (15–20 mins)
Purpose
Allow players to apply changing the picture naturally in a realistic game.
Setup
- Larger numbers: 5v5 or 6v6
- Two goals
- Normal football rules
Description
Free play with quick restarts and no added conditions. Pressure and blocked options appear naturally. Players are free to dribble or pass, with emphasis on not forcing play when the picture isn’t right.
Transfer Emphasis (Optional)
- Double goal if a goal comes after a clear reset or change of picture
- Used to highlight decision-making, not to force behaviour
Coach Behaviour
- Observation cues:
- Do players reset when options are closed?
- Are new pictures being created through movement or ball travel?
- Recognition phrases:
- “Good change of picture.”
- “You reset and found space.”
- “Nice decision to go again.”
