heading: U11 Cycle 9, week 1, session 1
headingPath: U11 sessions > U11 Cycle 9, week 1, session 1
content:
BELCONNEN UNITED FC – SESSION PLAN | CYCLE 9 | WEEK 1 | SESSION 1U11 | Play Smart | When to Go Fast
Session Outcome
Players feel more confident recognising moments to speed up play and attack quickly.
Phase 1 – Introduction (10–15 mins)
Purpose
Help players feel the difference between calm play and moments to accelerate.
Setup
- Area: 18x18m (adjust 16–22m)
- Players: 10–12 (everyone with a ball)
- Equipment: Cones, balls
Description
All players dribble freely inside the area at a relaxed pace. On the coach’s call of “fast,” players immediately accelerate with the ball for a few seconds into open space, then return to a calmer dribble. The calls come unpredictably. The activity should feel rhythmic — calm, then sudden bursts. When it works well, players change speed decisively and recognise open space quickly.
Coach Behaviour
- Observation cues:
- Do players react instantly to the moment to go fast?
- Is there a clear change of speed?
- Recognition comments:
- “Nice — you went fast at the right moment.”
- “Good acceleration.”
- “You recognised the space.”
Phase 2 – Main Part (25–30 mins)
Setup
- Players: 3v3 or 4v4 (multiple games if needed)
- Area: 18x24m (adjust 16x22 to 22x28)
- Equipment: Balls, small goals or end zones
- Organisation: Continuous play with spare balls ready for quick restarts
Description
Small-sided games where players choose when to speed up play. Teams score by entering an end zone or finishing in a small goal. Most of the game is calm and contested, but when space opens — after a turn, regain, or pass — players are encouraged to attack quickly. When it’s working well, players don’t rush all the time; they wait, then explode when the picture is right.
Rules & Success Conditions
- Score by entering an end zone or finishing in the small goal
- Immediate restart from the nearest ball
- Single shaping rule: goals count double if the attack includes a clear moment of acceleration into space
Managing Challenge (Same Game)
- If the game is too easy:
- Reduce space slightly
- If the game is too hard:
- Increase space slightly
- If energy or engagement drops:
- Change scoring direction or swap end zones/goals
Coach Behaviour – What to Watch For
- Recognition of space to attack quickly
- Clear speed change with the ball
- Willingness to slow down again if the moment isn’t onObserve how timing of speed creates advantage, and adjust space without stopping play.
Phase 3 – Final Game (15–20 mins)
Purpose
Allow players to apply speed decisions naturally in a realistic game.
Setup
- Larger numbers: 5v5 or 6v6
- Two goals
- Normal football rules
Description
Free play with quick restarts and no added conditions. Players are free to dribble or pass, choosing when to play fast or slow based on what they see.
Transfer Emphasis (Optional)
- Double goal if a goal comes after a well-timed fast attack
- Used to highlight decision-making, not to force constant speed
Coach Behaviour
- Observation cues:
- Do players recognise when to accelerate?
- Are fast moments purposeful rather than rushed?
- Recognition phrases:
- “Good moment to go fast.”
- “You waited, then exploded.”
- “Smart decision to attack.”
Belconnen United FC – Compact • Brave • Intelligent

heading: U11 Cycle 9, week 1, session 2
headingPath: U11 sessions > U11 Cycle 9, week 1, session 2
content:
BELCONNEN UNITED FC – SESSION PLAN | CYCLE 9 | WEEK 1 | SESSION 2U11 | Play Smart | When to Keep the Ball
Session Outcome
Players feel more confident keeping the ball when there is no space to go forward.
Phase 1 – Introduction (10–15 mins)
Purpose
Help players feel that keeping the ball can be the best option when space is closed.
Setup
- Area: 18x18m (adjust 16–22m)
- Players: 10–12 (everyone with a ball)
- Equipment: Cones, balls
Description
All players dribble freely inside the area. The space is intentionally tight. Players are encouraged to move calmly, protect the ball, turn away from pressure, and keep control rather than trying to escape quickly. On the coach’s call of “keep,” players slow down, use their body to protect the ball, and stay in control for a few seconds before moving again. When it works well, the picture looks calm — players are balanced, turning away from others, and keeping the ball safely despite pressure.
Coach Behaviour
- Observation cues:
- Do players stay calm when space is tight?
- Are they protecting the ball instead of forcing forward runs?
- Recognition comments:
- “Good decision to keep it.”
- “Nice protection — you stayed calm.”
- “You waited instead of rushing.”
Phase 2 – Main Part (25–30 mins)
Setup
- Players: 3v3 or 4v4 (multiple games if needed)
- Area: 16x20m (slightly tighter than usual, adjust 14x18 to 18x22)
- Equipment: Balls, end zones or small goals
- Organisation: Continuous play with spare balls ready for fast restarts
Description
Small-sided games where space is limited and pressure is constant. Teams score by entering an end zone or finishing in a small goal, but opportunities to go forward are not always available. Players must decide when to keep the ball by protecting it, turning away, or using a teammate rather than forcing play. When it’s working well, players show patience, recycle possession naturally, and wait for the right moment to attack.
Rules & Success Conditions
- Score by entering an end zone or finishing in the small goal
- Immediate restart from the nearest ball
- Single shaping rule: goals count double if the team keeps possession for a short period under pressure before scoring
Managing Challenge (Same Game)
- If the game is too easy:
- Reduce space slightly
- If the game is too hard:
- Increase space slightly
- If energy or engagement drops:
- Switch scoring method (end zones ↔ goals)
Coach Behaviour – What to Watch For
- Willingness to protect the ball under pressure
- Turning away instead of forcing forward
- Use of teammates to keep possessionObserve how patience helps teams stay in control, and adjust space without stopping play.
Phase 3 – Final Game (15–20 mins)
Purpose
Allow players to apply decisions about keeping the ball naturally in a realistic game.
Setup
- Larger numbers: 5v5 or 6v6
- Two goals
- Normal football rules
Description
Free play with quick restarts and no added conditions. The game naturally presents moments where going forward is on and moments where keeping the ball is smarter. Players are free to decide.
Transfer Emphasis (Optional)
- Double goal if a goal comes after a clear moment of patience and keeping the ball
- Used to highlight smart decisions, not slow play
Coach Behaviour
- Observation cues:
- Do players recognise when there is no space to go forward?
- Are they comfortable keeping the ball instead of forcing it?
- Recognition phrases:
- “Good decision to keep it.”
- “You stayed calm under pressure.”
- “Smart choice — you waited.”
Belconnen United FC – Compact • Brave • Intelligent

heading: U11 Cycle 9, week 2, session 1
headingPath: U11 sessions > U11 Cycle 9, week 2, session 1
content:
BELCONNEN UNITED FC – SESSION PLAN | CYCLE 9 | WEEK 2 | SESSION 1U11 | Play Smart | Score or Keep It
Session Outcome
Players feel more confident deciding whether to attack and score or keep the ball when the chance isn’t on.
Phase 1 – Introduction (10–15 mins)
Purpose
Help players feel the difference between moments to score and moments to keep the ball.
Setup
- Area: 18x18m (adjust 16–22m)
- Players: 10–12 (everyone with a ball)
- Equipment: Cones, balls
Description
All players dribble freely inside the area. When space opens in front of them, players are encouraged to drive forward quickly. When space closes, players slow down, turn away, and protect the ball. The coach occasionally calls “score” or “keep” to sharpen awareness, but players are encouraged to read the picture themselves. When it works well, players change speed naturally and don’t force forward actions when the space isn’t there.
Coach Behaviour
- Observation cues:
- Do players recognise when space is open or closed?
- Are speed changes clear and purposeful?
- Recognition comments:
- “Good choice — you kept it.”
- “Nice decision to go and score.”
- “You read the picture well.”
Phase 2 – Main Part (25–30 mins)
Setup
- Players: 3v3 or 4v4 (multiple games if needed)
- Area: 18x24m (adjust 16x22 to 22x28)
- Equipment: Balls, small goals or end zones
- Organisation: Continuous play with spare balls ready for quick restarts
Description
Small-sided games where players constantly decide whether to attack or keep possession. Teams score by finishing in the small goals or entering an end zone. Some moments invite quick attacks; others require patience and ball protection. When it’s working well, players don’t rush every attack — they keep the ball when pressure is high and accelerate when space appears.
Rules & Success Conditions
- Score by entering an end zone or finishing in the small goal
- Immediate restart from the nearest ball
- Single shaping rule: goals count double if the team makes a clear decision to keep the ball under pressure before attacking
Managing Challenge (Same Game)
- If the game is too easy:
- Reduce space slightly
- If the game is too hard:
- Increase space slightly
- If energy or engagement drops:
- Change scoring method (end zones ↔ goals)
Coach Behaviour – What to Watch For
- Recognition of scoring chances versus closed space
- Willingness to protect and recycle the ball
- Calmness before accelerating to attackObserve how decision-making affects control and chances, and adjust space without stopping play.
Phase 3 – Final Game (15–20 mins)
Purpose
Allow players to freely choose between scoring and keeping the ball in a real game.
Setup
- Larger numbers: 5v5 or 6v6
- Two goals
- Normal football rules
Description
Free play with quick restarts and no added conditions. The game naturally presents moments to attack quickly and moments where patience is needed. Players manage these decisions themselves.
Transfer Emphasis (Optional)
- Double goal if a goal comes after a clear decision to keep the ball before attacking
- Used to highlight smart choices, not slow play
Coach Behaviour
- Observation cues:
- Are players choosing wisely between attacking and keeping possession?
- Do they stay calm under pressure?
- Recognition phrases:
- “Smart decision.”
- “You waited, then attacked.”
- “Good choice to keep it.”
Belconnen United FC – Compact • Brave • Intelligent

heading: U11 Cycle 9, week 2, session 2
headingPath: U11 sessions > U11 Cycle 9, week 2, session 2
content:
BELCONNEN UNITED FC – SESSION PLAN | CYCLE 9 | WEEK 2 | SESSION 2U11 | Play Smart | Read the Moment Games
Session Outcome
Players feel more confident reading the moment and choosing the best action — go forward, keep it, or use a teammate.
Phase 1 – Introduction (10–15 mins)
Purpose
Expose players to changing pictures and encourage them to act based on what they see.
Setup
- Area: 18x18m (adjust 16–22m)
- Players: 10–12 (everyone with a ball)
- Equipment: Cones, balls
Description
All players dribble freely inside the area. The space naturally becomes busy and then opens again. Players are encouraged to react to what they see: accelerate when space appears, slow and protect when it closes, or change direction to find a new picture. The coach occasionally calls “change” to prompt players to re-read the situation and adjust. When it works well, players are constantly scanning, changing speed, and adapting without stopping.
Coach Behaviour
- Observation cues:
- Are players reacting to space opening and closing?
- Do they adjust speed and direction naturally?
- Recognition comments:
- “Good read — you changed your action.”
- “Nice decision in that moment.”
- “You adjusted quickly.”
Phase 2 – Main Part (25–30 mins)
Setup
- Players: 3v3 or 4v4 (multiple games if needed)
- Area: 18x24m (adjust 16x22 to 22x28)
- Equipment: Balls, small goals or end zones
- Organisation: Continuous play with spare balls ready for quick restarts
Description
Small-sided games where the picture is constantly changing. Teams score by entering an end zone or finishing in a small goal. Pressure, space, and numbers shift quickly, forcing players to read each moment. Sometimes the best option is to attack fast; other times it’s to keep the ball or reset. When it’s working well, players don’t pre-decide — they adapt their action to what’s in front of them.
Rules & Success Conditions
- Score by entering an end zone or finishing in the small goal
- Immediate restart from the nearest ball
- Single shaping rule: goals count double if the action before scoring clearly matches the moment (fast when space is on, calm when it’s not)
Managing Challenge (Same Game)
- If the game is too easy:
- Reduce space slightly
- If the game is too hard:
- Increase space slightly
- If energy or engagement drops:
- Change scoring direction or goal type
Coach Behaviour – What to Watch For
- Speed changes based on space
- Decisions to go forward, keep, or reset
- Willingness to abandon one idea and choose anotherObserve how reading the moment improves control and chances, and adjust space without stopping play.
Phase 3 – Final Game (15–20 mins)
Purpose
Allow players to freely read and manage game moments in a realistic setting.
Setup
- Larger numbers: 5v5 or 6v6
- Two goals
- Normal football rules
Description
Free play with quick restarts and no added conditions. The game presents constant decisions. Players are free to choose how they play each moment based on what they see.
Transfer Emphasis (Optional)
- Double goal if a goal comes after a clearly smart decision that fits the moment
- Used to highlight game understanding, not specific actions
Coach Behaviour
- Observation cues:
- Are players adapting their decisions as the game changes?
- Do they avoid forcing actions when the moment isn’t right?
- Recognition phrases:
- “Good read of the moment.”
- “You chose the right action.”
- “Smart football.”
Belconnen United FC – Compact • Brave • Intelligent

heading: U11 Cycle 9, week 3, session 1
headingPath: U11 sessions > U11 Cycle 9, week 3, session 1
content:
BELCONNEN UNITED FC – SESSION PLAN | CYCLE 9 | WEEK 3 | SESSION 1U11 | Play Smart | Simple Game Control
Session Outcome
Players feel more confident controlling the game by choosing calm actions under pressure.
Phase 1 – Introduction (10–15 mins)
Purpose
Help players feel how calm actions slow the game and bring control.
Setup
- Area: 18x18m (adjust 16–22m)
- Players: 10–12 (everyone with a ball)
- Equipment: Cones, balls
Description
All players dribble freely inside the area. The focus is on calm movement, soft touches, and turning away from traffic. When the coach calls “control,” players slow the game: protect the ball, turn out of pressure, and keep possession for a few seconds before moving again. There is no rush to escape. When it works well, the area looks composed — fewer collisions, more balance, and players choosing safety over speed.
Coach Behaviour
- Observation cues:
- Do players stay calm when space is tight?
- Are they turning away instead of forcing forward?
- Recognition comments:
- “Nice control — you slowed it down.”
- “Good decision to protect.”
- “You stayed calm there.”
Phase 2 – Main Part (25–30 mins)
Setup
- Players: 3v3 or 4v4 (multiple games if needed)
- Area: 16x22m (slightly compact, adjust 14x20 to 18x24)
- Equipment: Balls, small goals or end zones
- Organisation: Continuous play with spare balls ready for quick restarts
Description
Small-sided games where pressure is constant and space is limited. Teams score by entering an end zone or finishing in a small goal. The emphasis is on controlling moments rather than rushing attacks. Players are encouraged to keep the ball, reset play, and wait for clearer chances. When it’s working well, the game feels steady: fewer forced passes, better spacing, and attacks built from patience.
Rules & Success Conditions
- Score by entering an end zone or finishing in the small goal
- Immediate restart from the nearest ball
- Single shaping rule: goals count double if the team shows a clear calm phase (keeping the ball under pressure) before attacking
Managing Challenge (Same Game)
- If the game is too easy:
- Reduce space slightly
- If the game is too hard:
- Increase space slightly
- If energy or engagement drops:
- Switch scoring method (end zones ↔ goals)
Coach Behaviour – What to Watch For
- Ball protection under pressure
- Decisions to recycle rather than force play
- Calm body shape and balance on the ballObserve how patience leads to control, and adjust space without stopping play.
Phase 3 – Final Game (15–20 mins)
Purpose
Allow players to apply simple control naturally in a realistic game.
Setup
- Larger numbers: 5v5 or 6v6
- Two goals
- Normal football rules
Description
Free play with quick restarts and no added conditions. The game naturally swings between fast and slow moments. Players are free to decide when to calm the game and when to attack.
Transfer Emphasis (Optional)
- Double goal if a goal comes after a clear period of calm possession
- Used to highlight control, not to slow the game artificially
Coach Behaviour
- Observation cues:
- Do players recognise when to slow the game?
- Are they comfortable keeping the ball under pressure?
- Recognition phrases:
- “Good control.”
- “You calmed the game down.”
- “Smart football.”
Belconnen United FC – Compact • Brave • Intelligent

heading: U11 Cycle 9, week 3, session 2
headingPath: U11 sessions > U11 Cycle 9, week 3, session 2
content:
BELCONNEN UNITED FC – SESSION PLAN | CYCLE 9 | WEEK 3 | SESSION 2U11 | Play Smart | Smart Decision Matches
Session Outcome
Players feel more confident making smart decisions consistently across a full game — when to go, when to keep it, and when to reset.
Phase 1 – Introduction (10–15 mins)
Purpose
Prepare players to recognise and adapt to different game moments.
Setup
- Area: 18x18m (adjust 16–22m)
- Players: 10–12 (everyone with a ball)
- Equipment: Cones, balls
Description
All players dribble freely inside the area. The space naturally tightens and opens. Players are encouraged to constantly adjust: accelerate into open space, slow and protect when crowded, or turn away to find a new picture. The coach occasionally calls “decide” to prompt players to quickly choose the best action based on what they see. When it works well, players look comfortable changing decisions without panic or hesitation.
Coach Behaviour
- Observation cues:
- Are players changing actions based on space and pressure?
- Do they stay calm when the picture changes?
- Recognition comments:
- “Good decision.”
- “You chose the right option there.”
- “Nice adjustment.”
Phase 2 – Main Part (25–30 mins)
Setup
- Players: 3v3 or 4v4 (match format, multiple pitches if needed)
- Area: 18x24m (adjust 16x22 to 22x28)
- Equipment: Balls, small goals
- Organisation:
- Short matches (3–4 minutes)
- Quick rotations
- Spare balls ready for immediate restarts
Description
A series of short matches where players are challenged to manage the game themselves. Teams score by finishing in the small goals. Each match naturally presents different problems: tight pressure, open space, fatigue, and momentum swings. Players must decide when to attack quickly, when to keep possession, and when to reset the play. When it’s working well, the game feels balanced — not rushed, not slow — with players adapting to each moment.
Rules & Success Conditions
- Score by finishing in the small goal
- Immediate restart from the nearest ball
- Single shaping rule: no extra rules — success is judged by quality of decisions, not outcomes
Managing Challenge (Same Game)
- If matches are too easy:
- Reduce space slightly
- If matches are too hard:
- Increase space slightly
- If energy or engagement drops:
- Shorten matches and increase rotation speed
Coach Behaviour – What to Watch For
- Decision-making under pressure
- Changes in tempo based on the moment
- Willingness to reset rather than force playObserve how players manage the game themselves, and adjust space or match length without stopping play.
Phase 3 – Final Game (15–20 mins)
Purpose
Let players express full game intelligence without intervention.
Setup
- Larger numbers: 5v5 or 6v6
- Two goals
- Normal football rules
Description
Free play with quick restarts and no added conditions. This is a true match environment. Players manage tempo, decisions, and game flow independently, drawing on everything developed across the cycle.
Transfer Emphasis (Optional)
- Public recognition for players or teams showing smart decision-making across the game
- Used to value thinking, not just scoring
Coach Behaviour
- Observation cues:
- Are players making better decisions as the game unfolds?
- Do they adjust play based on score, space, and pressure?
- Recognition phrases:
- “Smart football.”
- “You managed that moment well.”
- “Good game awareness.”

content:
BELCONNEN UNITED FC – SESSION PLAN | CYCLE 10 | WEEK 1 | SESSION 1U11 | Festival of Football | Mixed Games – All Themes
Session Outcome
Players feel more confident playing freely, adapting to different game problems, and enjoying football in many forms.
Phase 1 – Introduction (10–15 mins)
Purpose
Reignite joy, freedom, and readiness through varied ball contact and game pictures.
Setup
- Area: 20x20m (adjust 18–24m)
- Players: 10–12 (everyone with a ball)
- Equipment: Cones, balls, 2–4 mini goals
Description
Players dribble freely in the area. At random moments, the coach introduces simple changes: add a mini goal to score in, ask players to find a partner and pass once, or encourage a quick 1v1 duel before returning to free play. There is no single objective — the focus is freedom, variety, and responsiveness. When it works well, the space feels alive: players are smiling, engaged, and constantly adjusting to new pictures.
Coach Behaviour
- Observation cues:
- Are players comfortable switching between actions?
- Is the energy positive and playful?
- Recognition comments:
- “Nice adjustment — you solved that quickly.”
- “Good creativity.”
- “You stayed brave with the ball.”
Phase 2 – Main Part (25–30 mins)
Setup
- Players: Multiple small games running (1v1, 2v2, 3v3)
- Area: Several small pitches (12x18 to 18x24m)
- Equipment: Balls, cones, small goals, gates
- Organisation:
- Rotate games every 4–6 minutes
- Quick transitions between games
- Spare balls everywhere
Description
A carousel of mixed small-sided games, each offering a different football problem:
- One pitch encourages dribbling and 1v1s
- One pitch rewards passing or finding a free player
- One pitch creates chaos with quick transitionsPlayers rotate through the games, adapting quickly to new rules and pictures. There is no coaching of solutions. When it’s working well, players jump into each game fast, read the rules naturally, and compete with enthusiasm.
Rules & Success Conditions
- Each pitch has one simple way to score (goal, gate, end zone)
- Immediate restarts
- Single shaping rule per pitch only (kept simple and visible)
Managing Challenge (Same Games)
- If games are too easy:
- Reduce space or add an opponent
- If games are too hard:
- Increase space or numbers
- If energy drops:
- Shorten rotations and increase variety
Coach Behaviour – What to Watch For
- Willingness to engage with new problems
- Confidence on the ball across different games
- Quick adaptation to changing rules and picturesObserve which games bring out creativity, bravery, and connection, and adjust space or rotations without stopping play.
Phase 3 – Final Game (15–20 mins)
Purpose
Celebrate football with free play that includes everything.
Setup
- Larger numbers: 5v5 or 6v6
- Two goals
- Normal football rules
Description
Free play with quick restarts and no conditions. All themes from the season appear naturally: dribbling, passing, pressing, switching, scoring, and defending together. Players manage the game themselves. The feeling should be joyful, competitive, and expressive.
Transfer Emphasis (Optional)
- Public recognition for brave play, creativity, teamwork, or enjoyment
- No bonus rules — value behaviours, not outcomes
Coach Behaviour
- Observation cues:
- Are players enjoying themselves while competing?
- Are they expressing different parts of their game?
- Recognition phrases:
- “That’s football.”
- “Brave play.”
- “You enjoyed that moment.”

content:
BELCONNEN UNITED FC – SESSION PLAN | CYCLE 10 | WEEK 1 | SESSION 2U11 | Festival of Football | Team Challenges
Session Outcome
Players feel more confident working together to solve game challenges and compete as a team.
Phase 1 – Introduction (10–15 mins)
Purpose
Create connection, communication, and shared problem-solving with the ball.
Setup
- Area: 20x20m (adjust 18–24m)
- Players: 10–12 (small teams of 3–4, one ball per team)
- Equipment: Cones, balls, 2–4 mini goals or gates
Description
Players form small teams, each with one ball. Teams move freely in the area, completing simple collective challenges: everyone must touch the ball before scoring in a mini goal, or the team must dribble together to a gate and finish. Challenges change every few minutes but remain simple and game-like. When it works well, players talk, adjust to each other, and solve problems together without stopping.
Coach Behaviour
- Observation cues:
- Are teams communicating and moving together?
- Do players include everyone in the challenge?
- Recognition comments:
- “Good teamwork.”
- “You solved that together.”
- “Nice communication.”
Phase 2 – Main Part (25–30 mins)
Setup
- Players: 3v3 or 4v4 team challenge games
- Area: Multiple small pitches (14x18 to 18x24m)
- Equipment: Balls, small goals, gates, cones
- Organisation:
- Short games (3–5 minutes)
- Teams rotate between different challenges
- Immediate restarts
Description
A series of team-based challenge games. Each pitch presents a different collective problem, for example:
- Score after everyone has touched the ball
- Score through two different goals
- Win it back as a group before scoringTeams rotate through challenges, adapting quickly. The emphasis is on cooperation, shared responsibility, and enjoying competition together. When it’s working well, players encourage each other, adjust roles naturally, and celebrate team success.
Rules & Success Conditions
- Each pitch has one clear team challenge
- Score by completing the challenge and finishing
- Immediate restarts to keep momentum
Managing Challenge (Same Games)
- If challenges are too easy:
- Reduce space or add a defender
- If challenges are too hard:
- Increase space or simplify the task
- If energy drops:
- Shorten games and rotate faster
Coach Behaviour – What to Watch For
- Communication between teammates
- Willingness to involve everyone
- Collective reactions after losing or winning the ballObserve how teams work together, and adjust space or challenge difficulty without stopping play.
Phase 3 – Final Game (15–20 mins)
Purpose
Celebrate teamwork in a free, competitive game.
Setup
- Larger numbers: 5v5 or 6v6
- Two goals
- Normal football rules
Description
Free play with quick restarts and no added conditions. Teamwork appears naturally through defending together, combining to attack, and supporting each other. Players manage the game themselves.
Transfer Emphasis (Optional)
- Public recognition for teams showing strong cooperation, communication, or support
- No bonus goals — value the team behaviours, not just scoring
Coach Behaviour
- Observation cues:
- Are players supporting and encouraging each other?
- Is the team staying connected in attack and defence?
- Recognition phrases:
- “Great team effort.”
- “You worked together.”
- “That was a team goal.”

content:
BELCONNEN UNITED FC – SESSION PLAN | CYCLE 10 | WEEK 2 | SESSION 1U11 | Festival of Football | Express Yourself
Session Outcome
Players feel more confident expressing themselves with the ball — trying ideas, taking risks, and enjoying freedom.
Phase 1 – Introduction (10–15 mins)
Purpose
Reopen freedom and creativity without fear of mistakes.
Setup
- Area: 20x20m (adjust 18–24m)
- Players: 10–12 (everyone with a ball)
- Equipment: Cones, balls, 2–4 mini goals
Description
Players dribble freely in the area with complete freedom. Mini goals are scattered so players can finish whenever they feel like it. There are no instructions on how to move, turn, or finish — players choose. Some will dribble fast, some slow, some will try tricks, some will shoot early. When it’s working well, the area looks unpredictable and alive, with players experimenting and enjoying the ball.
Coach Behaviour
- Observation cues:
- Are players trying different actions with the ball?
- Are they comfortable making mistakes?
- Recognition comments:
- “Love the confidence.”
- “Nice idea — keep trying.”
- “That was brave.”
Phase 2 – Main Part (25–30 mins)
Setup
- Players: 2v2 or 3v3 (multiple games running)
- Area: 14x20 to 18x24m
- Equipment: Balls, small goals or gates
- Organisation:
- Short free games
- Immediate restarts
- Rotate opponents regularly
Description
Free small-sided games with minimal structure. Teams score by finishing in the goals or dribbling through gates. There are no restrictions on touches, movement, or solutions. Players are encouraged to solve problems their own way — dribble, pass, combine, or shoot. When it’s working well, games look different from pitch to pitch, with players expressing personality and confidence.
Rules & Success Conditions
- Score by goal or gate
- Immediate restart
- Single shaping rule: none — freedom is the rule
Managing Challenge (Same Games)
- If games are too easy:
- Reduce space slightly
- If games are too hard:
- Increase space slightly
- If energy drops:
- Change opponents or scoring method
Coach Behaviour – What to Watch For
- Willingness to take players on
- Variety of actions with the ball
- Confidence after mistakesObserve who expresses themselves freely and who holds back, and adjust space or numbers without stopping play.
Phase 3 – Final Game (15–20 mins)
Purpose
Let players fully express themselves in a real game.
Setup
- Larger numbers: 5v5 or 6v6
- Two goals
- Normal football rules
Description
Free play with quick restarts and no conditions. Players are encouraged to enjoy the game, try things, and play with personality. All actions are allowed — dribbles, passes, shots, risks. The game should feel joyful and competitive.
Transfer Emphasis (Optional)
- Public recognition for brave attempts, creativity, and confidence
- No bonus goals — value expression, not outcomes
Coach Behaviour
- Observation cues:
- Are players confident to try things in the game?
- Do they recover quickly from mistakes?
- Recognition phrases:
- “That’s you — keep playing.”
- “Brave idea.”
- “Love the confidence.”

Session Outcome
Players feel more confident playing with Belco values — brave on the ball, helping teammates, and competing together.
Phase 1 – Introduction (10–15 mins)
Purpose
Reconnect players with how Belco football feels on the ball.
Setup
- Area: 20x20m (adjust 18–24m)
- Players: 10–12 (everyone with a ball)
- Equipment: Cones, balls, 2–4 mini goals
Description
Players dribble freely in the area. At different moments, the coach encourages simple Belco behaviours to appear naturally: take a player on, protect the ball, use a teammate, or finish when it feels right. Mini goals allow spontaneous scoring. There is no set order or task — players are free to express themselves while staying brave and connected. When it’s working well, players look confident, active, and willing to engage with the ball and each other.
Coach Behaviour
- Observation cues:
- Are players playing with confidence and intent?
- Are they involving teammates naturally?
- Recognition comments:
- “That’s brave football.”
- “Good use of your mate.”
- “You stayed confident on the ball.”
Phase 2 – Main Part (25–30 mins)
Setup
- Players: 3v3 or 4v4 (multiple games if needed)
- Area: 18x24m (adjust 16x22 to 22x28)
- Equipment: Balls, small goals
- Organisation:
- Continuous games
- Quick restarts
- Rotate teams every 4–6 minutes
Description
Free-flowing small-sided games with minimal structure. Players are encouraged to play our way: be brave in 1v1s, help teammates when under pressure, and compete when the ball is lost. There are no tactical instructions — behaviours emerge through the game itself. When it’s working well, players take responsibility on the ball, support each other, and react together in transitions.
Rules & Success Conditions
- Score by finishing in the small goals
- Immediate restart from the nearest ball
- Single shaping rule: none — Belco behaviours are recognised, not forced
Managing Challenge (Same Games)
- If games are too easy:
- Reduce space slightly or add a neutral defender
- If games are too hard:
- Increase space slightly
- If energy drops:
- Change teams or shorten games
Coach Behaviour – What to Watch For
- Bravery on the ball (dribble, protect, engage)
- Support for teammates in possession and defence
- Collective reaction when the ball is lostObserve which Belco values appear most clearly, and adjust space or numbers without stopping play.
Phase 3 – Final Game (15–20 mins)
Purpose
Let players express Belco football freely in a full game.
Setup
- Larger numbers: 5v5 or 6v6
- Two goals
- Normal football rules
Description
Free play with quick restarts and no conditions. Players manage the game themselves. All Belco behaviours are welcomed: bravery, teamwork, effort, and enjoyment. The focus is on identity, not outcome.
Transfer Emphasis (Optional)
- Public recognition for actions that reflect Belco values(bravery, helping a teammate, reacting together)
- No bonus goals — value how the game is played
Coach Behaviour
- Observation cues:
- Do players look confident and connected?
- Are Belco behaviours visible without instruction?
- Recognition phrases:
- “That’s our way.”
- “Brave and together.”
- “You played the Belco way.”

heading: U12 Coach Pack
content:
BELCONNEN UNITED FC
TECHNICAL DEPARTMENT
——————————————
COACH PACK YEAR 2
TECHNICAL DEVELOPMENT PHASE (U12)
——————————————
Control of the Belco Player
——————————————
Prepared by:
Scott Conlon – Technical Director
Belconnen United FC
V2025.03

heading: 1. U12 Message to the Coaches
headingPath: COACH PACK 1 (U12) – TECHNICAL DEVELOPMENT PHASE > 1. U12 Message to the Coaches
content:
This phase is where Croatian football culture begins.
Your job isn’t to control the session — it’s to shape habits. The players must fall in love with having the ball, with risk, and with duels. Mistakes are learning fuel; don’t protect them from failure.
Keep your voice simple and consistent — repeat key words: “brave,” “first touch forward,” “find your mate.” Every cue you use now will echo through the pathway.
Let the game teach. Every activity must include decision, direction, and opposition. That’s the Croatian way — real football from day one.
By the end of this phase, players should recognise the rhythm of win it, play it, move again. That’s the seed of our Belco identity: bravery in duels and confidence to build the play.
“Teach them to love the ball, win the duel, and move it with purpose.”

heading: 2. U12 Player Characteristics
headingPath: COACH PACK 1 (U12) – TECHNICAL DEVELOPMENT PHASE > 2. U12 Player Characteristics
content:
U12 players are energetic, curious, and highly impressionable. Their coordination and perception are developing rapidly, making this the ideal window for technical acquisition and movement intelligence.
Typical traits:
- High enthusiasm and short concentration spans — thrive on variety and competition.
- Eager to dribble, run, and “show” their skills; less aware of teammates early in the year.
- Starting to grasp cause–effect in football (space, pressure, options).
- Can manage short tactical concepts if shown visually or through guided questions.
- Mentally resilient when encouraged — confidence grows through success, not correction.
Coach takeaway: keep the game alive, challenging, and rewarding. Over-coaching breaks the rhythm; guided discovery keeps engagement high.

heading: 3. U12 Phase Overview
headingPath: COACH PACK 1 (U12) – TECHNICAL DEVELOPMENT PHASE > 3. U12 Phase Overview
content:
Area | Key Points
Age Phase | U12 – Technical Foundation
Development Focus | Ball mastery under pressure, early decision-making, coordination, and confidence.
Session Rhythm | 2 sessions per week. 70% technical repetition, 30% game-related application. Short intervals, high intensity.
Training Ratio | Technical dominance with tactical hints (support, spacing, timing).
Game Focus | Encourage players to express — don’t freeze play for tactical talk. Reward brave touches, smart passes, and recovery runs.
Belco Identity Seeds | 🔹 Build & Control the Tempo (through ball comfort) 🔹 Duel Mentality (through 1v1 focus) 🔹 Create Superiority (through small overloads).
Coach Role | Positive guide. Short corrections, lots of reinforcement. Be the rhythm, not the referee.

heading: 4. U12 Coaching Emphasis
headingPath: COACH PACK 1 (U12) – TECHNICAL DEVELOPMENT PHASE > 4. U12 Coaching Emphasis
content:
Coaching Dimension | Emphasis for This Phase | Practical Examples / Notes
Session Rhythm | High-tempo, frequent touches, rest through rotation. | Short 4–6 minute blocks. Avoid long instruction.
Coaching Voice | Energetic and supportive. Use short cue language. | “Be brave.” / “Open body.” / “Find your mate.”
Correction Style | Correct through activity, not stoppage. Demonstrate if needed. | “Show me again but faster.” / “Where was your first touch?”
Technical Detail | First touch direction, body shape, ball protection, passing weight. | Always connect technique to a game situation.
Tactical Habits | Support angle, simple passing triangles, spacing in small groups. | Early introduction of “support behind and to the side.”
Psychological Theme | Build courage and creativity. No fear of losing the ball. | Praise ideas, not only outcomes.
Language Focus | Action-based & habit cues. | “Pass–move–get it back.” / “Back–Mid–Front.” / “Win your duel.”
Coach Behaviours to Reward | Players who: 1️⃣ stay brave, 2️⃣ use both feet, 3️⃣ look up before touching. | “Good — you saw it before you touched!”
Croatian Guideline | The ball is always the teacher — not the cones. | Keep them moving, scanning, reacting.

heading: 5. U12 Technical Focus List
headingPath: COACH PACK 1 (U12) – TECHNICAL DEVELOPMENT PHASE > 5. U12 Technical Focus List
content:
Purpose
At U12, technical work is taught through football reality – pressure, direction, opposition.Each action is repeated across 5×6-week cycles through repetition with variation: same technique, new decision.Coaches guide execution quality inside realistic games – not separate drills.This list defines the year’s technical learning goals.
1. Ball Mastery Under Pressure
Focus | What It Looks Like | How It’s Trained
1v1 control & protection | Player can shield and escape using body & first touch. | 1v1 duels, small goals, transition games.
Turning both directions | Turns away from pressure to protect or face forward. | 3v3 directional games; “receive to face” work.
Feints & changes of pace | Uses small touches, shifts tempo to beat opponent. | 1v1 and 2v2 drive zones.
First touch to create time | Cushion touch to move away from pressure. | Rondo / possession games with limited space.
2. Passing & Receiving for Team Connection
Focus | What It Looks Like | How It’s Trained
Open body to receive | Player sees both ball and next pass. | 4v2 / 5v3 rondos, positional games.
Play with far foot | First touch preps next action. | Technical warm-ups, bounce passes.
Accurate short passing | Pass weight & angle allow tempo control. | Small directional games; “find the friend.”
Support & move after pass | Always offering new passing line. | 3v3–5v5 circulation games.
3. Combination & Cooperation Techniques
Focus | What It Looks Like | How It’s Trained
Wall pass (bounce) | Quick give-and-go to escape pressure. | 2v2 + neutral; bounce gate games.
Third-man run | Player runs beyond to receive next ball. | 4v3 / 5v4 combination games.
Underlap / overlap movement | Timed run to support wide play. | Wide channel positional games.
Timing of release | Knows when to pass vs carry. | Drive–Draw–Play setups, 3v2 overloads.
4. Ball Carrying & Driving
Focus | What It Looks Like | How It’s Trained
Drive into open space | Confident first steps forward on open ball. | 3v3–4v4 directional games.
Draw opponent before passing | Lures pressure to create extra man. | Drive–Draw–Play situations.
Carry between lines | Moves ball past first defender. | Zone-based 5v5 games.
5. Defensive & Transition Techniques
Focus | What It Looks Like | How It’s Trained
Defend side-on | Body shape sees both ball and opponent. | 1v1 defending & shadow press.
Delay before tackle | Slows opponent until cover arrives. | 2v2 compact defending games.
Immediate press after loss | First step forward on transition. | 4v4 transition mini-games.
Compact support distance | Close enough for cover & counter-press. | 5v5 with spacing constraints.
6. Finishing & Final-Third Execution
Focus | What It Looks Like | How It’s Trained
Arrive for cut-back | Second-line run timed behind ball. | Assist-zone games & 4v4+GK.
Controlled side-foot finish | Accuracy over power. | Small-goal finishing games.
Decision on shot vs pass | Chooses best final action. | Positional 5v5 games to goal.
7. Cognitive / Perceptual Habits
Focus | What It Looks Like | How It’s Trained
Scanning before receiving | Checks both sides, recognises pressure. | Rondos, reaction games.
Recognising open-ball cue | Drives forward when no pressure. | 4v4 directional games.
Reaction to loss/gain | Transition mindset. | 3-zone continuous games.
Communication & leadership | Talks, points, supports. | Every SSG – verbal cue coaching.

heading: 6. U12 Language Bank
headingPath: COACH PACK 1 (U12) – TECHNICAL DEVELOPMENT PHASE > 6. U12 Language Bank
content:
(Coach Communication Library – To Be Used Consistently Across All Coaches)
This chapter introduces the shared football language used at Belco to help young players understand the game through simple concepts and cooperation. The language is organised around the thirds of the pitch and the key moments of the game, but at this age it is used as a guide for awareness, not a set of instructions or tactical rules.
For U12–13 players, the purpose of language is to support decision-making, encourage connection with teammates, and build confidence with the ball. Cues are short, familiar, and often reinforced through questions, allowing players to recognise situations and explore solutions through play. Coaches use this language to prompt thinking and reinforce positive behaviours, not to control actions or outcomes.
The focus at this stage is on learning football through the ball and the game itself. The language bank helps create consistency across teams while protecting creativity, enjoyment, and curiosity. As players approach the tactical phase, these same concepts will remain, but the responsibility to recognise and act on them will gradually shift from the coach to the player.
U12
U12 Build-Up and Ball Circulation
Belco Identity Link → Build & Control the Tempo / Create Superiority
Coaching Intention | Phrases / Language Cues
Body orientation & scanning | “Open your body.” “See before you receive.” “Half-turn early.” “Face the field.”
Decision & rhythm | “Play through, not around.” “Be calm before quick.” “Keep the rhythm.”
Support play | “Find the middle man.” “Back → Mid → Front.” “Help the man on the ball.”
Recognition of open ball | “Open ball — go forward!” “Now the game speeds up.”
Possession control | “Keep it clean.” “Play what you see.” “Don’t chase the ball, move for it.”
U12 Attacking and Creating
Belco Identity Link → Create Superiority / Finishing Mentality
Coaching Intention | Phrases / Language Cues
Penetration mindset | “Play forward when you can.” “Drive to draw.” “Commit the defender.”
Combination play | “Pass, move, get it back.” “Find a friend.” “Third-man runs!” “Bounce and go.”
Finishing mentality | “Attack with intent.” “Arrive late to score.” “Follow your pass.” “Hit the target — quality first.”
Use of width | “Stretch them.” “See the far side.” “Winger wide, inside runs.”
Decision before action | “See it, say it, play it.” “Don’t guess — read the play.”
U12 Defending & Compactness
Belco Identity Link → Collective Compactness / Duel Mentality
Coaching Intention | Phrases / Language Cues
First defender habits | “Close the gap fast.” “Show him one way.” “Stay on your feet.”
Collective defending | “Stay connected.” “Talk, shift, move as one.” “Protect the middle.”
Press triggers | “Step on the closed ball!” “React on the back pass.”
Compactness | “Don’t stretch the line.” “Stay tight, stay smart.” “Compact and ready.”
Duel mentality | “Win your 1v1.” “Be brave, don’t wait.” “Strong body — clean tackle.”
U12 Transition Moments
Belco Identity Link → Aggressive Recovery / Tempo Control
Coaching Intention | Phrases / Language Cues
On regain | “Win → play forward.” “First pass counts.” “Support the regain.”
On loss | “Lose it? Win it back!” “Five-second rule!” “React first.”
Team balance | “If we go, we all go.” “Recover behind the ball.” “Stay compact after pass.”
Tempo switch | “Slow the game, breathe.” “Change rhythm — fast then calm.”
U12 Mentality & Communication
Belco Identity Link → Duel Mentality / Control the Tempo
Coaching Intention | Phrases / Language Cues
Competitive mindset | “Every duel matters.” “Win your battle.” “Never give it away easy.”
Composure | “Stay calm — control the tempo.” “Breathe and see the next action.”
Voice & leadership | “Talk early, help each other.” “Clear call = clear action.”
Effort recognition | “That’s Belco courage.” “Good recovery run.” “Great reaction.”

heading: 7. U12 Annual Cycle Progression
headingPath: COACH PACK 1 (U12) – TECHNICAL DEVELOPMENT PHASE > 7. U12 Annual Cycle Progression
content:
Structure: The U12 season consists of 10 cycles (3-Week Cycles, 2 Sessions per Week ≈30 weeks, 60 sessions)
Method: Game-based technical learning, repetition inside decision, identity through coaching language
Belco Seeds: “Open ball”, “Create the overload”, “Stay connected”, “Play through, not around”, “Win your duel”.
U12 cycles are numbered Cycle 1 to Cycle 10
Cycle | Theme | Week 1 | Week 2 | Week 3
1 | Dribbling & Ball Control | Owning the Ball 1v1 Mastery & Body Feints Shield & Turn | Attack the Defender – Directional 1v1 → 2v2 Press Resistance Dribbling | Drive to Commit Mini Tournament – Duels & Transitions
2 | Passing & Receiving | Short Passing Triangles Support Angles – Open Body Shape | Passing Under Pressure – 4v2 Rondos Receive to Play Forward | Build from the GK 3v3 + Targets Back → Mid → Front Game
3 | 1v1 Defending & Regain | First Defender Habits – Delay & Control Reaction to Loss | 2v2 Defending – Cover & Support Closed Ball Press | Regain & Counter – Win it Back Together Transition Reaction Game
4 | Finishing & Shooting | Finishing from Dribble – Attack with Intent 1-Touch Finishes | Combination to Finish – Wall Pass Release Cut-Back Zone | Finishing Under Pressure 2v2 + Keeper Arrive Late to Score
5 | Cooperation (2v1–3v2) | Create the Overload – Drive–Draw–Play Support Timing | Bounce & Break Lines Third-Man Runs – Connect Units | Positional Game – Find the Free Man Mini Game – Cooperate to Score
6 | Transition (Win–Play–React) | Immediate Regain – 5-Second Rule Compact Recovery | First Pass Forward – Counter Moments Support on Transition | React & Recover – Protect Middle Small-Sided Transition Tournament
7 | Small Group Defending | Compact 3v3 Block – Protect Zone 14 Defend as a Unit | Press Together – Nearest 3 Go Communication & Line Coordination | Defend Depth – Through-Ball Control Game – Stay Connected
8 | Build to Score | Build Out of Pressure – Open Ball Decisions Back Support | Progress Through Midfield – Find the Pivot Play Forward When You Can | Switch of Play – Use Width & Depth Final Third Entry – Break Lines
9 | Game Intelligence | Scanning – See Before You Receive Spatial Awareness | Decision Speed – Tempo Changes Play Fast or Slow | Managing Possession – Control Rhythm Playmaker Game – Read the Moment
10 | Mini Tournament / Review | Identity Games – All Themes Mixed Belco Cues Refresher | Express Your Game – Belco Cup Festival Coach–Player Reflection | Team Identity Games – Play with Purpose Season Review Challenge
How to Use It
- Each session theme = 60-70min session when we build content later.
- Week 1: teach the action (technique under pressure).
- Week 2: link the action (decision & cooperation).
- Week 3: apply the action (game integration & transition).
- Keep using Belco language cues in every block so identity builds naturally.

heading: 8. U12 Training Sessions
headingPath: COACH PACK 1 (U12) – TECHNICAL DEVELOPMENT PHASE > 8. U12 Training Sessions
content:
Training Chapter – Coach Standard (Coach Pack Version)
Belco training sessions are not copied from Croatian drills.
At this age, session structure supports learning — it must never restrict play or creativity.
They are built on the same philosophy and session structure, adapted to our players, coaches, and environment.
Every session follows a clear learning sequence:
- Prepare the player (uvodni)
- Develop the main idea (glavni)
- Transfer learning into the game (završni)
These phases describe purpose, not drills.
Coaches are not expected to run fixed exercises. They are expected to:
- respect the session flow
- connect activities to the game
- increase realism and pressure as the session progresses
- use shared language consistently
The session examples that follow show one way to apply this methodology. Activities can be changed, adapted, or replaced, as long as the learning intent, progression, and behavioural standards remain.
We don’t copy exercises.We copy how learning is structured.

heading: U12 Session Structure
headingPath: COACH PACK 1 (U12) – TECHNICAL DEVELOPMENT PHASE > 8. U12 Training Sessions > U12 Session Structure
content:
How Belco Training Sessions Are Structured (U12)
Belco training sessions for U12 players follow a Croatian learning sequence, where players learn football through the ball, repetition, and the game.
The session is deliberately simple. It is designed to:
- build confidence and enjoyment
- maximise ball contact and repetition
- support decision-making through play
- avoid tactical organisation or instruction
As the session progresses:
- early activity supports success through space or numbers
- pressure increases gradually through opposition and realism
- coach input reduces, not increases
The session always follows three connected phases.
Phase 1 – Play with the Ball
This phase helps players arrive ready to play, both physically and emotionally.
The priority is:
- comfort on the ball
- enjoyment and engagement
- rhythm and confidence
Players should experience:
- lots of ball contact
- freedom of movement
- early success
Overloads, space, and assisted situations are encouraged to support confidence.This phase is not about teaching or correcting — it is about helping players feel good with the ball and ready to engage.
Coaches should see:
- smiles
- energy building naturally
- willingness to participate
Phase 2 – Learning Game
This is the main learning phase of the session.
Learning happens through small-sided, game-based activities that present a simple football problem (e.g. 1v1, 2v1, 2v2).
The focus is on:
- repetition with freedom
- confidence in action
- simple decision-making
- engagement with teammates and opponents
Supportive conditions (space, overloads, reduced pressure) are used to help success.As players settle, opposition or constraints may increase gradually, but the activity must remain a game — not a drill or tactical exercise.
Coaches guide the environment and reinforce behaviours, but avoid over-instruction.The game itself does the teaching.
Phase 3 – Football Game
The session finishes with a free or lightly conditioned game.
This phase allows players to:
- express learning naturally
- make decisions without guidance
- play with confidence and enjoyment
Coach intervention is minimal.Players are encouraged to self-organise, experiment, and play.
This phase confirms learning through application, not explanation.
Coaches should look for:
- bravery on the ball
- continued engagement
- enjoyment of the game
Not perfect execution.
Key Message for Coaches (U12)
- Keep the session flowing
- Protect confidence and enjoyment
- Let the game teach
- If time is limited, protect the Football Game
At U12, the Introduction phase may include a ball mastery component, either as part of the activity or immediately beforehand, to support comfort and rhythm. Overloads, extra space, or reduced opposition are appropriate to help players settle and succeed early.
If players are active, involved, and enjoying the game, the session is working.

heading: C1|W1|S1 – Owning the Ball: 1v1 Mastery & Body Feints (part 1)
headingPath: COACH PACK 1 (U12) – TECHNICAL DEVELOPMENT PHASE > 8. U12 Training Sessions > U12 Session Structure > C1|W1|S1 – Owning the Ball: 1v1 Mastery & Body Feints
content:
Session Outcomes
Players gain confidence to attack 1v1 | Beat opponents | Keep possession under pressure.
Activation/Intro (8-10mins)
Setup & Description | Coaching Focus | 
4 cones per player in one line. Mirror Moves – pairs face each other; one leads feints, the other mirrors. Swap every 30 s. Players have balls, move sideways along the line, left and right. If not enough cones use 2 or 3, and ask the players to stop after 1m | • Stay balanced, light on feet • Eyes up – read the body | 
Technical/Positional (15-20mins)
Setup & Description | Coaching Focus | 
10x10m area 1v1 lanes (8 × 15 m). Player A attacks small gate vs defender B. Quick rotation. Can have players in pairs and they take turns defending. Split across two areas. | • First touch forward • Feint then explode • Protect the ball | 
Tactical/Main (20-25mins)
Setup & Description | Coaching Focus | 
30 × 25 m. Field divided in thirds. 4v4 + 4 neutrals Score by dribbling through mini-goals. Encourage isolated 1v1s. 2 neutrals on the sides at halfway, and 2 at the end of the field between mini-goals. | • Recognise 1v1 moments • Be brave to face forward • Use body disguise | 
Transfer/End Game (10-15mins)
Setup & Description | Coaching Focus | 
40 x 30 m, goals at each end. 6v6 free game – remove zones every 5 min to increase freedom. Look for: • Facing forward on first touch. • Using feint to break line. • | • Observe use of feints • Encourage confidence and creativity | 
Element | Detail
Objective / Outcomes | Improve confidence in 1v1 situations and ability to beat defenders with controlled body movement and quick changes of direction.
Technical Detail | • Forward touch to attack space • Low centre of gravity • Explosive cut after feint • Protect ball with arm and body angle.
Coaching Messages | “Face them – don’t hide.” “Beat the player, then protect the ball.”
Common Mistakes & Fixes | • Too close to defender → create space first. • No explosion → shorter touch then burst. • Eyes down → head up before move.
Praise Language | “Nice disguise – froze the defender.” “Great burst – took space with purpose.”
Behaviours to Reward | • Facing forward under pressure • Using feints with intent • Immediate reaction after losing the ball.
Reflection Question | “What makes a defender move first – your touch or your eyes?”
Extended Coach Pack Version
BELCONNEN UNITED FC – COACH PACK (U12)
Cycle 1 – Dribbling & Ball Control
Week 1 – Owning the Ball | Session 1 – 1v1 Mastery & Body Feints
Session Overview
Objective:Develop players who are confident carrying the ball under pressure, can unbalance opponents with body feints, and use controlled touches to protect possession.
Development Logic:
- Croatian Foundation: “Learning football through the ball.” U10–11 emphasis on 1v1 mastery, ball coordination, and decision-making through play.
- Belco Application: Builds bravery and control — key steps toward Tempo Control and Compactness in future phases.
- Session Flow: Individual control → duels → directional decision → transfer into free play.
Practice 1 – Intro / Activation: “Mirror Moves”
Setup:
- Area: 12 × 12 m grid (or half penalty box).
- Organisation: players work in pairs facing each other, 2–3 m apart.
- Equipment: cones on grid edges, 1 ball per pair.
Description:Player A leads — performs light body feints, side shifts, stop–starts, or scissors; Player B mirrors. After 30 s, swap roles.
Coaching Detail:
- Stay on toes, knees bent, eyes up.
- Exaggerate body movement for rhythm and disguise.
- Add ball after 2–3 rounds: mirror moves now done with dribbles.
Common Mistakes & Fixes:
- Players flat-footed → remind them to bounce on toes.
- Tiny feints → exaggerate the shoulder and hip.
Progression:Turn it into a reaction race — leader feints, then drives 5 m; mirror must react and chase.
Practice 2 – Technical / Positional: “1v1 Lanes”
Setup:
- Area: 8 × 15 m lanes (mark 4–6 side-by-side).
- 2 players per lane: one attacker (A) and one defender (B).
- Small 1 m gate goals at each end.
- Rotate every 60–90 sec.
Description:A attacks B to score by dribbling through opposite gate; if B wins, counter to their own gate. Restart immediately.
Coaching Detail:
- First touch forward and controlled.
- Use feint (scissors, step-over, shoulder drop).
- After beating opponent, accelerate 2–3 fast touches.
Croatian Logic: repetition and rhythm — constant duels, short recovery.Belco Focus: “Win your duel. Own your space.”
Common Mistakes / Fixes:
- Approach too slow → “Attack the defender’s feet, not the space.”
- Over-dribbling → “One move, one burst.”
Progression:Allow a teammate behind to serve and support — introduces timing for overloads.
Practice 3 – Main Game / Tactical Application: “4v4 + 4 Neutrals”
Setup:
- Area: 30 × 25 m rectangle, divided subtly into three horizontal zones (defensive–middle–attacking thirds) with flat markers.
- Teams: 4v4 inside; 4 neutrals support play.
- 2 wide neutrals on touchlines (halfway).
- 2 target neutrals behind each end line.
- Goals: 1 m mini-goals at both ends, just inside the end line.
Description:Normal play inside the area. Teams score by:1️⃣ Dribbling through a mini-goal, or2️⃣ Passing into a target neutral who sets a teammate to dribble through.Rotate neutrals every 3–4 min.
Coaching Detail:
- Recognise 1v1 isolation → attack quickly.
- Use wide neutrals to escape pressure; target neutrals to reward forward intent.
- Stay compact behind the ball after losing possession.
Decision Triggers:

heading: C1|W1|S1 – Owning the Ball: 1v1 Mastery & Body Feints (part 2)
headingPath: COACH PACK 1 (U12) – TECHNICAL DEVELOPMENT PHASE > 8. U12 Training Sessions > U12 Session Structure > C1|W1|S1 – Owning the Ball: 1v1 Mastery & Body Feints
content:
- Defender closes space → feint + burst.
- Defender delays → use neutral to create overload.
- Neutral receives facing forward → immediate forward run.
Belco Cue:
“Use the wide neutral to escape — the target neutral to punish.”
Common Mistakes & Fixes:
- Standing still when neutral receives → “React to the open ball cue.”
- Players hiding behind defenders → “Find line of sight before ball travels.”
Progressions:
- Limit wide neutrals to two touches → speeds decision tempo.
- Add scoring bonus for 1v1 goal (2 points) → reinforces bravery.
Croatian Method Connection:Game-based repetition — realistic width, numerical cues, directional play through decision.
Practice 4 – End Game / Transfer: “6v6 Free Play”
Setup:
- Area: 40 × 30 m, 6v6 including goalkeepers or target goals.
- Remove zones every 5 min to promote freedom.
Objective:Observe if players attempt 1v1s and body feints in realistic play.
Coaching Focus:
- Encourage players to apply their moves naturally.
- Minimal intervention — only reinforce bravery and tempo of decisions.
Observation Notes:Look for:
- Facing forward on first touch.
- Using feint to break line.
- Immediate counter-press after losing the ball.
Summary Notes
Belco Behaviour Links:
- Compact: recover quickly after duels.
- Brave: take responsibility to attack 1v1.
- Intelligent: recognise when to attack vs combine.
Key Takeaways:
- 1v1 mastery is the base for all future Belco principles (Overloads, Tempo Control).
- Confidence and repetition through realistic duels.
- Coaches must reward intent, not only outcome.
End of Coach Pack – C1 | W1 | S1Belconnen United FC – The Belco Way: Compact • Brave • Intelligent

heading: C1|W1|S2 – Shield & Turn: Protecting the Ball & Changing Direction
headingPath: COACH PACK 1 (U12) – TECHNICAL DEVELOPMENT PHASE > 8. U12 Training Sessions > U12 Session Structure > C1|W1|S2 – Shield & Turn: Protecting the Ball & Changing Direction
content:
PART 1 – COACH-LITE SESSION PLAN (PRINT VERSION)
BELCONNEN UNITED FC – SESSION PLAN | CYCLE 1 | WEEK 1 | SESSION 2U12 | Cycle 1 – Dribbling & Ball Control | Week 1 – Owning the Ball | Session 2 – Shield & Turn: Protecting the Ball & Changing Direction
Session Outcomes
Players learn to protect the ball under pressure, use their body to maintain control, and change direction to retain possession.
Activation / Intro (8–10 mins)
Setup & DescriptionPairs inside 15 × 15 m grid. One ball per pair. Player A shields the ball while B tries to touch it. After 10 s, swap. Progress: A shields → turns → passes back.Coaching Focus• Side-on stance, arm extended• Low balance, feel defender before turning• Protect first – then play
Technical / Positional (15–20 mins)
Setup & Description3 corridors 12 × 8 m. One attacker, one defender. Attacker shields for 2 s then turns and dribbles to start line. Rotate.Coaching Focus• Use sole/inside turn to escape• Scan shoulder → turn to space• Explode 2–3 touches after turn
Tactical / Main (20–25 mins)
Setup & Description25 × 20 m field divided in halves. 3v3 + 2 wide neutrals. Mini-goals on end lines. Teams score by dribbling through gate after successful turn or shield. Rotate neutrals every 3 min.Coaching Focus• Recognise pressure – shield then turn to escape• Use neutral to reset when trapped• Compact reaction after loss• Belco cue: “Protect to play.”
Transfer / End Game (10–15 mins)
Setup & Description6v6 on 40 × 30 m field with mini-goals. Rule: goal only counts if possession included a turn under pressure.Coaching Focus• Observe shielding technique under contact• Reward turns that change direction of play• Encourage possession patience
Coaching Information
Element | Detail
Objective / Outcomes | Improve players’ ability to protect the ball, recognise pressure, and turn away to keep possession.
Technical Detail | • Side-on stance • Arm bar • Use sole or inside turn • Scan • Explosive exit after turn
Coaching Messages | “Protect first – then play.” / “Feel them before you face them.”
Common Mistakes & Fixes | Standing square → show side-on hips
Praise Language | “Great shield – you owned that space.” / “Smart turn – escaped the trap.”
Behaviours to Reward | Using body to protect
Reflection Question | “When should you shield and when should you turn?”
Footer:Belconnen United FC – Compact • Brave • Intelligent
PART 2 – EXTENDED COACH PACK VERSION
Session Header
Cycle 1 – Dribbling & Ball Control | Week 1 – Owning the Ball | Session 2 – Shield & Turn: Protecting the Ball & Changing Direction
Session Overview
Objective: Teach players to protect the ball under contact and change direction to retain possession and build composure.Development Logic: Aligned with Croatian U10–11 principle of learning football through the ball — focus on balance, contact awareness, and rhythm of turns. Belco overlay emphasises bravery and intelligent use of body position.
Practice 1 – Intro / Activation: “Mirror Shield”
Setup:
- Area: 15 × 15 m grid
- Organisation: pairs with 1 ball
- Equipment: cones marking grid edges
Description:
Player A shields ball while B tries to touch it. After 10 s, swap. Add progression – A must turn and pass to B after command.
Coaching Detail:
- Side-on stance – arm extended, knees bent
- Feel pressure first then turn
- Scan and use nearest foot to roll away
Common Mistakes & Fixes:
- Turning blind → scan before spin
- Standing square → side-on hips
Progression:
Turn then dribble 5 m to cone – adds direction and acceleration.
Practice 2 – Technical / Positional: “Shield & Turn Corridors”
Setup:
- Three corridors 12 × 8 m
- Pairs per lane – A (attacker) B (defender)
- Mini-goals or cone gates at each end
Description:
A shields from B for 2 s then turns and dribbles to start. Rotate roles. Encourage variety of turns.
Coaching Detail:
- Use body between ball and defender
- Roll with front foot or inside turn
- Scan for space before turn
- Explode after release
Common Mistakes & Fixes:
- Dribbling too early → hold pressure first
- Not checking shoulder → blind turns
Progression:
Add teammate ahead for pass after turn → introduces timing and support.
Practice 3 – Main Game / Tactical Application: “Protect & Play 3v3 + 2 Neutrals”
Setup:
- Area: 25 × 20 m, divided into two halves
- Players: 3v3 inside + 2 wide neutrals (mid-touchlines)
- Goals: 1 m cone gates on each end line
Description:
Normal possession play with direction. Teams score by dribbling through gate after successful turn or shield. Rotate neutrals every 3 min.
Tactical Purpose:
Develop timing and decision of when to protect vs when to release. Encourages composure and body positioning before penetration.Coaching Detail:
- Recognise pressure → shield and secure
- Use neutral to reset then receive side-on
- Turn toward space when pressure delays
- Compact reaction when ball lost
Variations / Adjustments:
- Neutrals 2-touch max → tempo increase
- 1 “joker” inside to create 3v3 + 3 → support angles
Belco Cue:
“Protect to play – turn to escape.”
Practice 4 – End Game / Transfer: “Free Play with Turn Bonus”
Setup:
- 6v6 on 40 × 30 m field with mini-goals
- Scoring bonus = 1 pt for successful shield + turn leading to retained possession
Description:
Normal free game with light coaching. Track successful turns that retain possession.Observation Focus:
- Players using body for protection
- Changing direction to open new lanes
- Team compactness after transition
Summary Notes
Key Takeaways: Protect before you play; scan and turn to space; control pressure with balance.Behaviours to Reward: Body positioning, awareness, confidence under contact.Session Progression Logic: Individual technique → decision under pressure → game application.
