Common Mistakes & Fixes | Nervous play → remind players it’s a celebration
Praise Language | “Look how far you’ve come.” / “That’s growth.”
Behaviours to Reward | Bravery, effort, reflection
Reflection Question | “What improved most for you this season?”
Session Design Note | Review format reinforces confidence and ownership
Language Note | Belco identity language used positively and simply
Belconnen United FC – Compact • Brave • Intelligent

heading: U13 Coach Pack
content:
BELCONNEN UNITED FC
TECHNICAL DEPARTMENT
——————————————
COACH PACK 3
TECHNICAL DEVELOPMENT PHASE – U13
——————————————
Connection of the Belco Player
——————————————
Prepared by:
Scott Conlon – Technical Director
Belconnen United FC
V2025.02

heading: 1. U13 Message to the Coaches
headingPath: COACH PACK 2 (U13) – TECHNICAL DEVELOPMENT PHASE > 1. U13 Message to the Coaches
content:
This phase connects everything.
Players must now link actions to teammates — not just control and pass, but understand why and when.
Croatian football treats this age as the bridge between learning technique and applying intelligence.
Your job is to help them see pictures before the ball arrives.
Keep sessions relational: always with partners, always with purpose.
Encourage scanning, rhythm, and movement. The biggest mistake is over-talking — let tempo and repetition build their understanding.
The Croatian phrase is “Mirno, pa brzo” — “Calm, then fast.”
That sums up the work here: calm body, clear head, quick play.
Reward connection: the pass that supports, the run that helps, the voice that organises.
Those small moments are the start of our Belco identity — Build & Control the Tempo and Create Superiority through intelligence, not force.
“Help them see before they act, and play faster by thinking earlier.”

heading: 2. U13 Player Characteristics
headingPath: COACH PACK 2 (U13) – TECHNICAL DEVELOPMENT PHASE > 2. U13 Player Characteristics
content:
U12–13 players are entering a pre-tactical, cognitive growth stage — they begin to understand football as a shared puzzle, not just an individual challenge.
They are more analytical, more aware of others, and increasingly capable of deliberate action.
Typical traits:
- Improved coordination and body control — able to execute techniques with more precision.
- Developing perception — can scan and process more information before acting.
- Growing competitiveness — want success but can handle constructive challenge.
- Begin to understand cause and consequence — when pressing, spacing, or timing affect team outcome.
- Emotionally volatile at times — need consistency, clear feedback, and ownership of their actions.
Coach takeaway: This is the age to refine technique through responsibility. Players must learn to play the right action, not just the clever one.
Encourage decision-making and reflection — “What did you see? Why did you choose that?”

heading: 3. U13 Phase Overview
headingPath: COACH PACK 2 (U13) – TECHNICAL DEVELOPMENT PHASE > 3. U13 Phase Overview
content:
Area | Key Points
Age Phase | U12–13 – Linking Phase
Development Focus | Transition from individual technique to cooperative play. Introduce tempo control and group rhythm.
Session Rhythm | 2 sessions per week. 60% technical repetition inside cooperation, 40% tactical (support, width, tempo).
Training Ratio | 3:2 – Technical to Tactical. Build combinations inside realistic game shapes (3v3s, 4v2s, 6v4s).
Game Focus | Encourage possession with meaning. Use direction, create overloads, and connect lines. Mistakes in tempo are part of learning.
Belco Identity Seeds | 🔹 Build & Control the Tempo (through rhythm and scanning) 🔹 Create Superiority (through Drive–Draw–Play and third-man runs) 🔹 Collective Compactness (through early shape awareness).
Coach Role | Connect detail to behaviour. Ask questions that make players think: “Why was that pass late?” or “What did you see?”

heading: 4. U13 Coaching Emphasis
headingPath: COACH PACK 2 (U13) – TECHNICAL DEVELOPMENT PHASE > 4. U13 Coaching Emphasis
content:
Coaching Dimension | Emphasis for This Phase | Practical Examples / Notes
Session Rhythm | Alternating tempos. Work calm → fast → calm. | Rondo → directional game → small-sided match.
Coaching Voice | Move from commands to cues. Use relational terms. | “Support him.” “Open your body.” “Find the extra man.”
Correction Style | Guided discovery — ask, don’t tell. | “What did you see?” “Could you open earlier?”
Technical Detail | First touch to next action; body orientation; passing weight; scanning before control. | Correct before bad habits stick.
Tactical Habits | Support triangle principles; tempo variation; third-man runs; compact rest-shape. | Teach “stay connected even when we attack.”
Psychological Theme | Confidence in rhythm; patience in build-up; trust teammates. | Praise calm play under pressure.
Language Focus | Introduce rhythm and connection words. | “Drive–Draw–Play.” “Open ball.” “Calm then fast.”
Coach Behaviours to Reward | Communication and early movement. | “You checked early — that’s why you had time.”
Croatian Guideline | The ball moves faster when the mind is calm. | Teach rhythm, not rush.

heading: 5. U13 Technical Focus List
headingPath: COACH PACK 2 (U13) – TECHNICAL DEVELOPMENT PHASE > 5. U13 Technical Focus List
content:
Purpose
At U12–13, players connect their technique to tactical understanding.Training introduces structure, triggers, and coordinated team actions — but the focus remains game-based and perceptual.
The Croatian model defines this as “tactical thinking through technical execution.”Belco maintains the same approach: teach football through football, embedding the 8 principles in every context.
1. Advanced Ball Mastery – Technique Under Tactical Pressure
Focus | What It Looks Like | How It’s Trained
1v1 under positional pressure | Wins duel to progress, not just survive. | 1v1–2v2 in directional zones (progression or retention).
Turning to escape compact spaces | Turns to face forward or reset intelligently. | Central grid games; “pivot turns.”
Use of body feint to open lanes | Creates passing angle or overload option. | Drive–Draw–Play exercises, 3v3 half-space games.
First touch into tactical space | Receives to attack or build connection. | Rondo → positional progression (4v2 → 6v3).
2. Passing & Receiving Within Team Shape
Focus | What It Looks Like | How It’s Trained
Receiving between lines | Finds and uses pockets behind midfield. | 6v6 directional games with target players.
Third-player orientation | Receives aware of next movement. | Triangular build-up grids, bounce + third-man.
Vertical–horizontal pass rhythm | Changes ball path to unbalance opponent. | 5v3–6v4 positional games, direction constraint.
Longer-range passing accuracy | Controlled driven passes to switch play. | Medium-sized build-up games, tempo emphasis.
3. Combination & Progression Techniques
Focus | What It Looks Like | How It’s Trained
Bounce + third-man in real tempo | Quick triangular patterns under time pressure. | 5v4, 6v5 overloads; progression through thirds.
Give–go vs give–keep recognition | Chooses the right combination for context. | Directional possession with decision gates.
Positional rotation | Two players exchange zones while maintaining shape. | Midfield trio rotations, small pitch pattern games.
Creating overloads by movement | Runs attract pressure to free teammate. | 4v3 → 5v4 overload + switch games.
4. Ball Carrying & Penetration
Focus | What It Looks Like | How It’s Trained
Carry between lines with head up | Drives through gap to advance. | Half-pitch directional 6v6–8v8.
Drive to commit & release | Draws defender to create open-ball. | Drive–Draw–Play structured games.
Tempo change through dribble | Slow to attract, accelerate to break. | Rondo → breakline dribble constraint.
Recognising open-ball moments | Identifies time/space trigger for penetration. | 6v6 directional with open-ball scoring rule.
5. Defensive Technique in Collective Context
Focus | What It Looks Like | How It’s Trained
Press–cover balance | 1st defender engages, 2nd covers. | 3v3, 5v5 defending games.
Compactness across units | Distances between lines remain tight. | 7v7 team defending; width/height rules.
Counter-press angles | Immediate reaction from nearest 3 players. | 5v5 transition games.
Tracking recovery runs | Reacts to loss by defending goal first. | Directional transition drills.
6. Finishing & Creative End Actions
Focus | What It Looks Like | How It’s Trained
Assist zone cut-backs | Arrives in space behind defensive line. | Wide overload → cut-back games.
Second-line finishing | Late arrival from midfield to score. | 5v5 + neutrals, timing runs.
Finishing under pressure | Chooses best option (shoot/pass). | 7v7 attacking transition games.
Combination to finish | Final pass from bounce/third-man pattern. | 4v3 + GK; half-space finishing games.
7. Tactical–Cognitive Habits
Focus | What It Looks Like | How It’s Trained
Scanning before every touch | Reads pressure and space early. | All rondos, repetition focus.
Recognising overload creation | Moves or passes to form 2v1/3v2. | Overload constraint games.
Tempo awareness | Chooses to slow, recycle, or accelerate. | Build-up games with time limit to penetrate.
Transition anticipation | Prepares position before turnover. | Continuous 5v5/6v6 transition circuits.

heading: 6. U13 Language Bank
headingPath: COACH PACK 2 (U13) – TECHNICAL DEVELOPMENT PHASE > 6. U13 Language Bank
content:
(Coach Communication Library – To Be Used Consistently Across All Coaches)
This chapter introduces the shared football language used at Belco to help young players understand the game through simple concepts and cooperation. The language is organised around the thirds of the pitch and the key moments of the game, but at this age it is used as a guide for awareness, not a set of instructions or tactical rules.
For U11–13 players, the purpose of language is to support decision-making, encourage connection with teammates, and build confidence with the ball. Cues are short, familiar, and often reinforced through questions, allowing players to recognise situations and explore solutions through play. Coaches use this language to prompt thinking and reinforce positive behaviours, not to control actions or outcomes.
The focus at this stage is on learning football through the ball and the game itself. The language bank helps create consistency across teams while protecting creativity, enjoyment, and curiosity. As players approach the tactical phase, these same concepts will remain, but the responsibility to recognise and act on them will gradually shift from the coach to the player.
U13 Build-Up and Support Play
Belco Identity Link → Build & Control the Tempo / Create Superiority
Coaching Intention | Phrases / Language Cues
Possession rhythm | “Build with calm.” “Control the rhythm.” “Speed comes from calm.”
Body shape & awareness | “Scan early.” “Half-turn to see the field.” “Play what you see.”
Using the pivot | “Find the pivot to connect.” “Middle man controls the game.”
Decision to progress | “See the open ball — go!” “Back → Mid → Front with purpose.”
Composure | “Don’t rush the build — own the ball.” “Keep it clean before you go.”
U13 Combination & Progression
Belco Identity Link → Create Superiority / Build & Control the Tempo
Coaching Intention | Phrases / Language Cues
Support & rotation | “Create the triangle.” “Support the support.” “Rotate to stay free.”
Third-man play | “Third-man opens the door.” “Use the bounce to break lines.”
Timing & tempo | “Wait for the cue.” “One touch to draw, next to break.”
Overload awareness | “Find the extra man.” “Play to superiority.”
Forward intent | “Pass forward when it’s on.” “Progress, don’t force.”
U13 Pressing & Regain
Belco Identity Link → Aggressive Recovery / Collective Compactness
Coaching Intention | Phrases / Language Cues
Press triggers | “Press on the return pass.” “Closed ball — step in.”
Compactness | “Squeeze the lines.” “Move together.” “Stay tight, press brave.”
Collective reaction | “Nearest three go.” “React before they breathe.”
Recovery run habits | “Sprint to shape.” “Recover behind the line.”
Mentality | “Win it high.” “Don’t wait — we hunt.”
U13 Width, Switch, and Tempo
Belco Identity Link → Build & Control the Tempo / Create Superiority
Coaching Intention | Phrases / Language Cues
Field awareness | “Use the full pitch.” “Stretch to control.”
Switch triggers | “Change sides to break lines.” “Play one way, finish the other.”
Rhythm management | “Slow to draw, quick to break.” “Tempo through circulation.”
Width management | “Wingers wide, fullbacks time your run.”
Collective patience | “Don’t chase — move them.”
U13 Finishing & Decision Speed
Belco Identity Link → Duel Mentality / Create Superiority
Coaching Intention | Phrases / Language Cues
Finishing mentality | “Arrive late to score.” “Be calm in the box.”
Decision speed | “First look, then move.” “Touch to finish, not to think.”
Link play | “Combine to create.” “One touch sets up the next.”
Quality emphasis | “Precision beats power.” “Make it count.”
Reaction | “Shoot when the picture’s clear.” “Stay composed.”
U13 Transition & Compactness
Belco Identity Link → Aggressive Recovery / Collective Compactness
Coaching Intention | Phrases / Language Cues
After regain | “Play forward early.” “Find support fast.”
After loss | “Close the middle.” “Compact fast — protect the core.”
Tempo awareness | “Switch rhythm now.” “Fast then calm.”
Recovery cue | “Five-second fight, then drop.”
Team mentality | “React first — that’s ours.”
U13 Managing Possession & Game Rhythm
Belco Identity Link → Build & Control the Tempo
Coaching Intention | Phrases / Language Cues
Tempo control | “Set the pace.” “Control the rhythm.”
Change of speed | “Calm the ball, then go.” “Speed up through control.”
Collective control | “We manage the game.” “Don’t chase — make them chase.”
Patience | “Wait for the open ball.” “Keep pressure through possession.”
Intelligence | “Think two passes ahead.”
U13 Mentality & Communication
Belco Identity Link → Duel Mentality / Build & Control the Tempo
Coaching Intention | Phrases / Language Cues
Emotional control | “Play the moment, not the mood.” “Stay calm, stay clear.”
Leadership | “Organise early.” “Talk your teammate through it.”
Bravery | “Play brave.” “Don’t hide from the ball.”
Reflection | “What did you see?” “Good decision — tell him why.”
Competitive mentality | “React before they do.” “That’s Belco intensity.”
U13 Croatian Communication Logic (Age 12–13)
Coaching Shift | How It Sounds
From: technical corrections | “Lock your ankle.” “Play through him.”
From: isolated cues | “Pass faster.” “Change the tempo.”
To: relational and rhythm cues | “Create space for him.” “Slow to draw, fast to play.”
From: command → guidance | “Do this.” → “See it?” “What’s open?”

heading: 7. U13 Annual Cycle Progression
headingPath: COACH PACK 2 (U13) – TECHNICAL DEVELOPMENT PHASE > 7. U13 Annual Cycle Progression
content:
Structure: The U13 season consists of 9 cycles (3-Week Cycles, 2 Sessions per Week ≈27-30 weeks, 60 sessions)
Focus: Group tactical cooperation, tempo awareness, control through rhythm and collective mentality
Belco Seeds: “Open ball”, “Create overload”, “Compact together”, “Tempo is ours”, “Stay connected”.
U13 cycles are numbered Cycle 1 to Cycle 9
Cycle | Theme | Week 1 | Week 2 | Week 3
1 | Build-Up & Support | Building with GK – Calm Under Pressure Support Angles to Play Forward | Open Ball Decisions – Recognise Triggers Progress Through First Line | Build Through Midfield – Connect Back → Mid → Front Tempo Control in Build-Up
2 | Combination Play | Wall Pass to Break Line Third-Man Support | Drive–Draw–Play – Create the Extra Player Timing of Movement | Rotations & Overloads – Create Superiority Combination to Finish
3 | Press & Regain | Compact Press – Nearest 3 Go Reaction to Loss | Counter-Press on Closed Ball Recover Shape Fast | Transition Games – Win, Play, React Collective Pressing Challenge
4 | Switch of Play & Width | Width Awareness – Stretch the Field Open Body to Find Far Side | Build One Way, Finish the Other Use the Pivot Switch | Switch + Penetrate – From Wide to Central Full-Pitch Decision Game
5 | Finishing Under Pressure | Finishing from Build-Up Arrive Late in the Box | Combination to Finish Cut-Back & Second Line | Decision Finishing – Calm in the Box Make It Count Tournament
6 | Transition Moments | Fast Reaction Game – Win → Play Play → Press | Counter-Attack Structure – First Pass Forward | Manage Transition – Balance Attack & Defence Tempo Shift Challenge
7 | Compactness & Balance | Line Distances – Stay Connected Protect the Core | Defensive Cooperation – Cover & Support | Compact Block Game – Move as One Press Triggers from Mid-Block
8 | Managing Possession | Keep the Ball to Control Circulate with Purpose | Rhythm Game – Speed Up, Slow Down Maintain Pressure | Game Management Scenarios – Control Tempo End-Game Decisions
9 | Tournament / Game Application | All-Theme SSGs – Build to Finish Compact to Counter | Mixed Principles Game – Identity Under Pressure | Belco Cup Phase – Play Our Way Team Reflection Review
Coach Guidance
- Each cycle = 3 weeks, two sessions per week (6 total).
- Week 1: isolate and teach the football behaviour (Croatian “learning phase”).
- Week 2: link to cooperation and tempo (Croatian “connection phase”).
- Week 3: apply in real game or integrated transitions (Croatian “game phase”).
- Identity is seeded through language — “open ball”, “tempo”, “compact together”, “create overload”.
- Keep short, intense Croatian work-rest tempo (≈70–90 sec games, 30 sec rest).
- Every 3rd week should finish with a “Croatian Game Day” — competitive small-sided formats for learning through stress.

heading: 8. U13 Training Sessions
headingPath: COACH PACK 2 (U13) – TECHNICAL DEVELOPMENT PHASE > 8. U13 Training Sessions
content:
Training Chapter – Coach Standard (Coach Pack Version)
Belco training sessions are not copied from Croatian drills.
At U16 and above, this structure is no longer a teaching guide.
It is a performance standard. Activities must be game-realistic, intensity must be honest, and players are expected to self-correct using shared language.
They are built on the same philosophy and session structure, adapted to our players, coaches, and environment.
Every session follows a clear learning sequence:
- Prepare the player (uvodni)
- Develop the main idea (glavni)
- Transfer learning into the game (završni)
These phases describe purpose, not drills.
Coaches are not expected to run fixed exercises. They are expected to:
- respect the session flow
- connect activities to the game
- increase realism and pressure as the session progresses
- use shared language consistently
The session examples that follow show one way to apply this methodology. Activities can be changed, adapted, or replaced, as long as the learning intent, progression, and behavioural standards remain.
We don’t copy exercises.We copy how learning is structured.

heading: U13 Session Structure
headingPath: COACH PACK 2 (U13) – TECHNICAL DEVELOPMENT PHASE > 8. U13 Training Sessions > U13 Session Structure
content:
How Belco Training Sessions Are Structured (U13)
At U13, Belco training sessions continue to prioritise technical development, while beginning to place greater emphasis on how players connect with each other.
Players are learning that:
- their decisions affect teammates
- space and timing matter
- cooperation creates advantage
However, they still require:
- freedom to explore
- repetition through play
- confidence with the ball
The session structure reflects this balance. It supports individual expression while gradually introducing collective understanding — without moving into tactics or team organisation.
Sessions follow a Croatian learning sequence, where:
- players arrive through comfort and repetition
- learning is shaped through situations, not instruction
- early success is supported through space or overloads
- realism increases gradually across the session
- the game remains the primary teacher
Coach language is used to guide awareness, not to provide solutions.
How the Session Progresses at U13
Across the session, learning develops through continuity rather than distinct blocks:
- Early phases help players reconnect with the ball and each other
- Small-sided games create repeated situations to solve
- Pressure increases through opposition, time, or realism
- Players are encouraged to recognise and act together
Coach involvement remains light and supportive.The aim is to help players see relationships, not follow instructions.
The session always follows three connected phases, which appear in the same order.
Phase 1 – Play with the Ball
This phase prepares players physically and mentally while reinforcing comfort and confidence on the ball.
At U13, this phase:
- may include ball mastery elements
- may involve light interaction with teammates
- begins to encourage awareness beyond the ball
The priority remains:
- rhythm and coordination
- freedom of movement
- early success
Overloads, extra space, or reduced opposition are appropriate to help players settle quickly and feel capable.
This phase is not about teaching cooperation explicitly.It is about helping players arrive confident, connected, and ready to engage.
Coaches should:
- allow play to flow
- encourage quality and intent
- avoid stopping to explain
Phase 2 – Learning Game
This is the core learning phase of the session.
Here, players are placed in small-sided, game-based situations that require them to solve football problems together, such as:
- when to support
- how far to stand from a teammate
- when to carry the ball or combine
The focus is on relationships, not isolated technique:
- distances
- angles
- timing of movement
Constraints are used to invite behaviour, not force it.Pressure may increase gradually through space, time, or opposition, but the activity must always remain a game.
The coach’s role is to:
- observe how players connect
- reinforce positive behaviours
- guide attention with brief cues
Direct instruction and solutions are avoided.Players learn by experiencing the situation repeatedly.
Phase 3 – Football Game
The session finishes with a free or lightly conditioned game.
This phase allows players to:
- apply learning in a realistic context
- make shared decisions
- manage transitions naturally
As assistance is removed and numbers move closer to even, realism increases.
Coach intervention is minimal.The emphasis is on:
- how players recognise situations
- how they support each other
- how they adjust without instruction
This phase confirms learning through application, not explanation.
Coach Guidance (U13)
At U13, effective coaching means:
- allowing players to connect actions together
- using structure to support understanding, not control
- supporting success early through space or overloads
- gradually increasing realism across the session
If players are:
- making decisions together
- adjusting to teammates
- staying engaged and confident
then the session is doing its job.

heading: 9. U13 Correction & Feedback Language
headingPath: COACH PACK 2 (U13) – TECHNICAL DEVELOPMENT PHASE > 9. U13 Correction & Feedback Language
content:
Purpose
At this age, correction becomes relational and conceptual.
The coach moves from fixing individual technique to adjusting collective understanding: shape, support, and tempo.
Croatians use the principle:
“Correct the connection, not the player.”
Correction Philosophy (Croatian Model)
Type of Feedback | When Used | Croatian Approach | Belco Example Language
Instant (micro cue) | During play, to manage rhythm. | Use voice tempo to regulate pace. | “Calm.” “Speed now.” “Support!”
Freeze & Recreate | To fix team spacing or wrong tempo. | Stop short, show line connection, replay. | “Hold — check spacing.” “Now go again, calm first.”
Guided Question | To build awareness. | Connect to rhythm and vision. | “Who was open?” “Was our tempo right?”
Reinforce Success | After correct timing or connection. | Highlight team cooperation. | “Perfect rhythm — calm then fast.”
Collective Reminder | To re-establish shape. | Speak to whole line or unit. | “Midfield line — connect sooner.” “Stay closer between lines.”
Croatian Correction Principles (U13)
1️⃣ Correct relationships before technique.2️⃣ Keep flow — rhythm is learning.3️⃣ Ask before telling.4️⃣ Coach tempo using tone — voice becomes metronome.
Belco Common Coaching Language (U13 Level)
Action Type | Correction Cue | Positive Reinforcement
Support & Shape | “Stay connected.” “Find the triangle.” | “Good — you gave him rhythm.”
Tempo Control | “Calm, then fast.” “Set rhythm.” | “Excellent — you slowed to see it.”
Passing & Combinations | “Create the extra man.” “Bounce to play through.” | “Yes — third man open.”
Scanning & Awareness | “See before touch.” “Open to the field.” | “You saw it early — brilliant.”
Transition | “React!” “Fast five!” | “We won it and played — perfect.”
Mentality | “Stay brave in rhythm.” “Don’t rush.” | “Loved that — calm under pressure.”
Tone & Delivery
- During play: soft tone for calm, sharp for trigger.
- Correction: 1–2 seconds max, return to flow.
- After action: replay positive rhythm before freezing for negatives.
“In Croatia, correction keeps the music — you don’t stop the song to fix one note.”
That’s your U12–13 coaching voice: rhythm first, correction through connection.

heading: 10. U13 Game Day Guidance
headingPath: COACH PACK 2 (U13) – TECHNICAL DEVELOPMENT PHASE > 10. U13 Game Day Guidance
content:
Purpose
The match extends the training theme into a full-tempo context.
Players must now link their technical actions with rhythm, spacing, and awareness.
Croatian coaches at this stage judge progress by how connected the team looks, not by result.
Game Day Objectives
Element | Guidance
Game Intent | Apply the weekly theme — e.g., if the training focus was Combination Play, the match objective is “Can we find the extra man and connect lines under pressure?”
Coach Voice | Use tempo and connection language: “Drive–Draw–Play.”, “Calm then fast.”, “See the open ball.” Keep tone calm, confident, rhythmic.
Player Guidance | Encourage scanning and communication: “Who can see the open ball?” / “Where’s the extra man?”
Substitution Focus | Players should rotate within lines — centre to side, side to middle — to learn relationships.
Half-Time Reflection | Use 2 guided questions:
 | 1️⃣ “How was our rhythm?”
 | 2️⃣ “When did we connect best?”
Coach Behaviour | Supportive but firm. Use voice to guide tempo — slow tone to calm them, fast tone to re-energise.
Croatian Matchday Principles (U13)
1️⃣ “Game flow reveals understanding.” – let the match show what they learned.
2️⃣ “Coach the relationships.” – feedback about spacing and timing, not errors.
3️⃣ “Tempo is control.” – avoid emotional sideline energy; teach rhythm.
Example Match Dialogue
“Drive him — now play!”
“Good — calm before fast!”
“Stay connected, help the ball!”
“Open ball — forward now!”
These phrases keep consistency between the week’s training and the match picture — same cues, faster tempo.

heading: U13 Coach Matchday Standards
headingPath: COACH PACK 2 (U13) – TECHNICAL DEVELOPMENT PHASE > 10. U13 Game Day Guidance > U13 Coach Matchday Standards
content:
Purpose
The coach sets the tone for the match environment.Professional detail builds credibility.Players mirror what they see — composure, preparation, and precision.
Coach Matchday Standards
APPEARANCE
Element | Standard
Belconnen Attire | Official Belconnen polo or approved top. Unified, consistent look across staff.
Footwear | Soccer footwear during warm-up and match. We look like football people, not spectators.
Sunglasses | Remove when speaking to players. Eye contact is essential for message absorption and connection.
Presentation | Neat, professional, deliberate. Our unified appearance signals control and precision.
GEAR
Item | Expectation
Balls | Pumped appropriately before arrival at pitch.
Bibs | Organised and accessible.
Cones | Blue and white matchday cones set and ready.
PREPAREDNESS
Element | Expectation
Substitution Plan | Rotation structure pre-planned. Know your intent before kick-off.
Team Talk | Clear theme linked to weekly focus. No improvisation.
Arrival Time | Minimum 45–60 minutes before kick-off. Field checked and organised.
Sideline Behaviour
Element | Standard
Coach Voice | Calm, clear, rhythmic.
Emotional Control | No visible frustration or emotional reactions.
Referee Interaction | Respectful at all times. Model behaviour.
Opposition Interaction | Professional greeting pre and post match.
Player Interaction | Eye contact, short cues, consistent language from training.
Parent Interaction | Friendly but brief. No tactical debate during game.
Belco Matchday Identity
1️⃣ Calm voice, clear cues.2️⃣ Detail before emotion.3️⃣ Teach relationships, not results.4️⃣ Represent the program with pride.

heading: U13 Pre-Match Warm-Up
headingPath: COACH PACK 2 (U13) – TECHNICAL DEVELOPMENT PHASE > 10. U13 Game Day Guidance > U13 Pre-Match Warm-Up
content:
PRE-MATCH WARM-UP (20-25 MINUTES TOTAL)
Objective: Connect players through rhythm, scanning, and early tactical cues.
At U11–13, the pre-match warm-up is about comfort, confidence, and readiness to play, not structure or tactics.
Its purpose is to help players feel good with the ball, settle emotionally, and arrive at kick-off relaxed and curious.
Warm-ups at this age should:
- include the ball early
- feel playful and familiar
- progress naturally from free movement into simple game actions
- keep coach voice low and positive
Coaches should guide, not control. Use short prompts or questions if needed, but avoid instruction, correction, or pressure. If players are smiling, moving freely, and engaged with the ball, the warm-up is doing its job.
Block | Duration | Focus | Example Activity | Coach Cues / Tone
1️⃣ Dynamic Movement & Activation | 4-5 mins | Mobility, rhythm, body readiness. | Dynamic movement circuit (skips, side shuffles, lunges, 10m accelerations). | “Light and sharp.” “Build rhythm.”
2️⃣ Technical Rhythm (Ball Each) | 4 mins | Clean touches, control, balance. | Ball manipulation with direction changes, feints, quick turns. | “Keep tempo.” “Change speed, keep control.”
3️⃣ Partner Passing with Scanning | 4 mins | Movement and visual connection. | Pairs 6–8m apart; one-touch passes, bounce-pass variations, both feet. | “Look before pass.” “Find your friend’s rhythm.”
4️⃣ Positional Rondos / 4v2 | 5–6 mins | Awareness, angles, rhythm of support. | 4v2 rondos (one-touch limit), rotate defenders often. | “Calm → fast.” “Support early.”
5️⃣ Directional Possession / Mini Game | 5–6 mins | Activation of cooperation. | 4v4 or 5v5 directional possession with mini goals. | “Play through, not around.” “Move as one.”
Detail:
- Emphasis on calm entry, sharp exit — start relaxed, finish with tempo.
- The coach uses rhythm language — calm voice to start, slightly faster tone near the end.
- No running lines or hype; everything includes the ball.
“Warm-up rhythm = game rhythm.”

heading: U13 Pre-Match Talk
headingPath: COACH PACK 2 (U13) – TECHNICAL DEVELOPMENT PHASE > 10. U13 Game Day Guidance > U13 Pre-Match Talk
content:
PRE-MATCH TALK (less than 5 MINUTES)
Purpose
Shift the focus from individual bravery to team connection.
The coach’s role is to calm energy and remind players of their cooperative cues — tempo, support, and scanning.
Structure
Step | Coach Focus | Example Language (Croatian simplicity, Belco tone)
1️⃣ Calm Gathering | Bring team in quietly; create focus. | “Eyes up — one deep breath. We start calm.”
2️⃣ Connect to Week’s Theme | Reinforce one clear tactical idea. | “All week we worked on helping the man on the ball — same today.”
3️⃣ Two or Three Group Reminders | Build rhythm and awareness. | “Support early.” / “Find triangles.” / “See the open ball.”
4️⃣ Team Behaviour Standard | Link to identity and control. | “Stay connected — we control tempo.”
5️⃣ Finish Soft & Positive | Send them out calm and clear. | “Calm start, play through, enjoy the rhythm.”
Pre-Match Principles (U12–13)
1️⃣ “Calm start, sharp brain.”2️⃣ “Coach rhythm, not hype.”3️⃣ “Remind, don’t instruct.”
Coach tone: low, confident, rhythmic — tempo of voice sets tempo of play.

heading: U13 Half-Time Talk
headingPath: COACH PACK 2 (U13) – TECHNICAL DEVELOPMENT PHASE > 10. U13 Game Day Guidance > U13 Half-Time Talk
content:
HALF-TIME TALK (less than 2 MINUTES)
Purpose
Guide reflection on rhythm and cooperation.
At this phase, players are learning to “see the team picture” — your job is to help them recognise it.
Structure
Step | Coach Focus | Example Language (Croatian realism)
1️⃣ Reset Energy | Sit them, water, short silence. | “Take a breath. Let’s slow the game down.”
2️⃣ One Positive Linked to Theme | Reinforce what connected. | “Good — we supported early and kept rhythm.”
3️⃣ One Correction | Keep it tactical but simple. | “When we lose it, we must connect quicker — help the ball.”
4️⃣ Guided Question | Encourage group ownership. | “When did we play best — calm or rushed?”
5️⃣ Finish Calm & Confident | Leave with clear intention. | “Calm start again — play through, not around.”
Half-Time Rules (U13)
Rule | Meaning for Coach
1️⃣ Stay rhythmic. | Use calm voice to rebuild control.
2️⃣ Focus on connection. | Mention support, distance, rhythm — not individuals.
3️⃣ Ask, don’t tell. | Players start owning the solution.
4️⃣ End with intention. | Give one clear focus for restart.
Example Complete U13 Half-Time Talk
“Good half — we supported well and found rhythm.
When we lost it, we waited a bit — next half, connect faster.
Stay calm, control tempo, and help the man on the ball.
Enjoy the rhythm — we play our way.”
Coach Behaviour Summary
Aspect | Croatian Standard for U13
Voice | Calm, steady, rhythmic; not emotional.
Body Language | Stand tall but composed; use calm gestures for tempo.
Focus | Group cooperation, rhythm, scanning.
Timing | 2–3 minutes pre-match, 3 minutes half-time.
Consistency | Repeat key phrases across week and match: “Calm → fast,” “See before play.”
Key Takeaway:
“At U12–13, coaching the match is about rhythm and connection —you teach players to feel the tempo, not to chase it.”

heading: U13 Pre-Match Routine - Timeline
headingPath: COACH PACK 2 (U13) – TECHNICAL DEVELOPMENT PHASE > 10. U13 Game Day Guidance > U13 Pre-Match Routine - Timeline
content:
Reference: All timings work backwards from KO (Kick-Off)Context: No changerooms · players arrive changed
Time (from KO) | Moment | Coach Focus | Coach Behaviour | Key Reminder
KO–50 | Arrival | Settle & connect | Greet players · light chat · no tactics | Human first
KO–40 | Free Ball Time | Comfort & confidence | Unstructured ball play · fun | Ball removes nerves
KO–30 | Brief Gather | Simple focus | One idea · question-led | One picture only
KO–25 | Warm-Up | Activate through play | Ball-based · minimal voice | Warm up by playing
KO–10 | Final Reminder | Cue recall | One short cue · positive tone | Reminder, not instruction
KO–3 | Game Prep | Emotional calm | Encourage · smile · no talk | Let them play
KO | Kick-Off | Observe & support | Coach less · watch more | Game teaches
⚠️ COACH WARNINGS (U11–13)
Warning Sign | What It Means
Long speeches | Overloading young players
Tactical detail | Age-inappropriate
Constant instructions | Players stop thinking
Emotional intensity | Confidence drops
REMINDER
At U11–13, matchday should feel like training — just with an opponent.

heading: U13 Pre-Match Routine - Detail
headingPath: COACH PACK 2 (U13) – TECHNICAL DEVELOPMENT PHASE > 10. U13 Game Day Guidance > U13 Pre-Match Routine - Detail
content:
🇭🇷 BELCO MATCHDAY BEHAVIOUR STANDARD (U11–13)
EXPANDED VERSION – COACH PACK
PURPOSE (U11–13)
Matchday at this age is about:
- emotional comfort
- confidence with the ball
- curiosity and decision-making
- enjoyment of competition
It is not about:
- results
- systems
- match management
- discipline through control
Croatian methodology treats matches at this age as learning environments, not performance tests.
MATCHDAY FLOW EXPLAINED
🕒 KO–50 | Arrival
Intent: Settle players emotionally
Coach behaviour
- Greet every player
- Casual conversation
- No football information
WhyYoung players cannot receive football detail until they feel safe and settled.
🕒 KO–40 | Free Ball Time
Intent: Remove nerves through the ball
Coach behaviour
- Allow juggling, 1v1s, small games
- No organisation
- No correction unless unsafe
WhyCroatian programs use the ball as the primary emotional regulator.
🕒 KO–30 | Brief Group Gather (2–3 minutes)
Intent: Create a simple focus
Coach behaviour
- One/two ideas only
- Often phrased as a question
Examples
- “When we can face forward, what do we do?”
- “How do we help the player on the ball?”
- “What do we do when we lose it?”
WhyQuestions activate thinking without pressure.
🕒 KO–25 | Warm-Up (Ball-Based)
Intent: Activate body and brain together
Coach behaviour
- Ball involved immediately
- Small-sided, playful activities
- Minimal instruction
WhyWarm-up should look like football, not drills.
🕒 KO–10 | Final Reminder
Intent: Recall a familiar cue
Coach behaviour
- One short reminder
- Calm, positive tone
Examples
- “Can we help the player on the ball?”
- “Remember our spacing.”
- “Be brave.”
WhyThis is a reminder, not instruction.
🕒 KO–3 | Game Prep
Intent: Emotional calm
Coach behaviour
- Encourage
- Smile
- No last instructions
WhySilence signals trust.
🕒 KO | Kick-Off
Intent: Let the game teach
Coach behaviour
- Observe more than instruct
- Intervene only for:
- safety
- emotional support
- very brief correction
Croatian principle
Too much coach voice = less player learning.
COACH BEHAVIOUR STANDARDS (U11–13)
Do
- Be calm and positive
- Use simple language
- Let players try and fail
- Reinforce effort, not outcome
Avoid
- Sideline coaching
- Tactical explanations
- Emotional reactions to mistakes
- Overuse of stoppages
“If you coach a lot on matchday at U11–13, you are stealing learning moments.”

heading: U13 Game Framework
headingPath: COACH PACK 2 (U13) – TECHNICAL DEVELOPMENT PHASE > 10. U13 Game Day Guidance > U13 Game Framework
content:
Game Framework – (9-a-Side)
- Base Shape: 1-3-3-2
- Key Roles:
- Back 3: Build calmly, stay compact when we lose it.
- Middle 3: Create triangles, find the pivot, control tempo.
- Front 2: Stretch vertically, link and finish.
- Team Instructions:
- Build through thirds, not over.
- Press together — nearest three go.
- Stay compact in transition.
- Tempo: calm → quick → calm.

heading: U13 Individual Competencies
headingPath: COACH PACK 2 (U13) – TECHNICAL DEVELOPMENT PHASE > 11. U13 Phase Outcomes > U13 Individual Competencies
content:
Theme: Connection, cooperation, and decision-making at tempo.
By the end of this phase, the player can:
- Orient body shape to play forward or switch sides.
- Adjust first touch based on visual cues (“open” or “closed” ball).
- Combine with teammates using wall passes and third-man runs.
- Maintain correct support angles and distances in small groups.
- Scan before receiving and make decisions earlier.
- Recognise and execute fast–slow tempo changes during play.
- Win back possession collectively (first 5 seconds mentality).
- Defend and attack as part of a connected unit (2v1–3v2).
- Show controlled technical quality in medium space and speed.
- Display rhythm and confidence — control before speed.
“Technique now lives inside the cooperation.”

heading: U13 Group Competencies
headingPath: COACH PACK 2 (U13) – TECHNICAL DEVELOPMENT PHASE > 11. U13 Phase Outcomes > U13 Group Competencies
content:
Group Cooperation & Rhythm
Objective: Build team understanding — how movement, support, and tempo create structure.
Team Competencies:
- Maintain correct team width and depth in 6v6–9v9 formats.
- Rotate positions and understand simple zones (left / centre / right).
- Move as a line in pressing or defending.
- Support behind, beside, and beyond the ball.
- Recognise open-ball and closed-ball cues collectively.
- Build from the goalkeeper through the pivot (back → mid → front).
- Manage transitions through immediate regrouping.
- Circulate the ball to find space, not to waste time.
- Encourage communication for tempo (“calm,” “fast,” “switch”).
- Demonstrate team rhythm — all players move and rest together.
“The ball’s rhythm becomes the team’s rhythm.”

heading: U13 sessions
headingPath: U13 sessions
content:
U13 – CYCLE 1Build-Up & Support (3 Weeks)
Week 1• Session 1 – Building with the GK: Calm Under Pressure• Session 2 – Support Angles to Play Forward
Week 2• Session 1 – Open Ball Decisions: Recognise Triggers• Session 2 – Progress Through the First Line
Week 3• Session 1 – Build Through Midfield: Connect Back → Mid → Front• Session 2 – Tempo Control in Build-Up

heading: U13 Cycle 1, week 1, session 1
headingPath: U13 sessions > U13 Cycle 1, week 1, session 1
content:
BELCONNEN UNITED FC – SESSION PLAN | CYCLE 1 | WEEK 1 | SESSION 1(Segoe UI 9 pt grey text, Belco-blue underline)
U13 | Build-Up & Support | Week 1 | Building with GK: Calm Under Pressure
Session Outcomes
Build calmly with the goalkeeper by creating clear support angles and playing with controlled rhythm under light pressure.
Ball Mastery Block (Optional – 5–10 mins)
Rotation: A – Controlled Touch Rhythm
Setup
- Area: 20x20m square (adjust to numbers)
- Players: All players inside the grid
- Equipment: 1 ball per player
Description
Players move freely with the ball, using inside/outside touches, rolls, and gentle direction changes. Rhythm builds as players find their own tempo, occasionally accelerating then settling back into control. Movements stay continuous, with players changing direction to avoid others. The block naturally increases tempo before easing into calm control, setting the tone for playing composed football.
Coaching Focus
- High ball contact and continuous movement
- Freedom to explore touches and direction changes
- Build rhythm and tempo without stopping play
- Prepare players mentally for calm decision-making in the first practice
Activation / Intro (10–12 mins)
Setup
- Area: 30x25m
- Players: 5v2 + GK (rotate defenders)
- Equipment: Balls, cones
Description
Play starts with the GK, who combines with five outfield players to keep possession against two pressing defenders. Players score a point for completing five passes that include the GK. If defenders win the ball, they switch with the players who lost possession. Play restarts quickly from the GK.
Coaching Focus
- Supporting early and on clear passing angles to the GK
- Scanning before receiving
- Playing with rhythm rather than rushing the first pass
Progression
- Reduce the space slightly or allow defenders to press more aggressively.
Technical – Solving the Situation (15–20 mins)
Setup
- Area: 40x30m, divided into two halves
- Players: GK + 4v3 in the build-up half, 2 targets in the opposite half
- Equipment: Goals or target zones, balls
Description
Play begins with the GK and back unit building against three opponents. The objective is to connect through the GK and progress the ball into the opposite half by passing into either target. Players must stay connected, adjusting distances and angles to support the ball. If defenders win it, they attempt to score quickly on the main goal.
Coaching Focus
- Body shape to see multiple options when receiving
- Timing of movement to support the GK
- Choosing when to play quickly and when to circulate to reset
Progression
- Add a fourth defender or limit touches for attackers to increase pressure.
Situational Game (25–30 mins)
Setup
- Area: 60x40m, directional
- Players: 6v6 + GK (or 7v7 if numbers allow)
- Equipment: Full goals, balls
Description
Normal game with play always restarting from the GK. Teams are encouraged to build short first. Goals count double if the attack includes a calm build involving the GK. Transitions are live and immediate after turnovers.
Coaching Focus
- Recognising when to connect short versus playing away from pressure
- Supporting play on the far side
- Maintaining rhythm while staying connected
Progression
- Remove double-goal incentive and allow full realism.
Transfer / End Game (10–15 mins)
Setup
- Area: Match-sized for numbers
- Players: Normal teams
- Direction: Full directional play
- Equipment: Goals, balls
Description
Free game with normal rules. Coach observes without stopping play, allowing players to apply learning naturally.
Coaching Focus
- Observe calm build-up choices
- Look for spontaneous support and composed decisions without prompts
Coaching Information (U13)
Element | Detail
Objective / Outcomes | Players consistently offer support to the GK and build play with composure
Technical Detail | First touch away from pressure, open body shape, appropriate tempo choice
Coaching Messages (Belco Voice) | “Stay calm, stay connected.” / “Use the GK to reset.”
Common Mistakes & Fixes | Rushing forward → encourage recycle through GK; flat support → adjust angle early
Praise Language | “Good decision to reset.” / “Nice calm support.”
Behaviours to Reward | Scanning, composure, willingness to support behind the ball
Reflection Question | “What helped us stay calm when they pressed?”
Session Design Note | Early activities use numerical advantage to support success
Language Note | Belco vocabulary used to support recognition, not explanation
Belconnen United FC – Compact • Brave • Intelligent

heading: U13 Cycle 1, week 1, session 2
headingPath: U13 sessions > U13 Cycle 1, week 1, session 2
content:
BELCONNEN UNITED FC – SESSION PLAN | CYCLE 1 | WEEK 1 | SESSION 2(Segoe UI 9 pt grey text, Belco-blue underline)
U13 | Build-Up & Support | Week 1 | Support Angles to Play Forward
Session Outcomes
Create clear support angles to help the ball carrier play forward with control and intent.
Ball Mastery Block (Optional – 5–10 mins)
Rotation: A – Controlled Touch Rhythm
Setup
- Area: 20x20m square
- Players: All players inside the grid
- Equipment: 1 ball per player
Description
Players move freely with the ball, using inside/outside touches and rolls while changing direction to avoid traffic. Tempo rises and settles naturally as players accelerate into space, then slow to regain control. Players adjust their paths based on others’ movement, creating constant rhythm and awareness before transitioning into the first game.
Coaching Focus
- High ball contact and continuous movement
- Freedom to explore touches and direction changes
- Build rhythm and tempo without stopping play
- Prepare players for quick support movements in possession
Activation / Intro (10–12 mins)
Setup
- Area: 30x25m, rectangular
- Players: 4v2 + 2 outside support players
- Equipment: Balls, cones
Description
Four players keep possession against two defenders inside the grid. Two support players are positioned on opposite sides and play one-touch only. The objective is to use angled support to play through or around defenders. If defenders win the ball, they switch with the player who lost it. Play restarts quickly.
Coaching Focus
- Supporting early and on an angle that faces forward
- Scanning before receiving
- Playing with rhythm rather than standing flat
Progression
- Limit inside players to two touches or reduce the space slightly.
Technical – Solving the Situation (15–20 mins)
Setup
- Area: 40x30m, directional
- Players: 4v4 + 2 neutrals (neutrals play with the team in possession)
- Equipment: Balls, cones, mini-goals or target zones
Description
Teams build possession using the two neutrals to create angles and play forward into target zones. Players must adjust distance and angle to the ball carrier to receive facing forward. If possession is lost, immediate transition applies as the other team attacks the opposite targets.
Coaching Focus
- Body shape to receive on the half-turn
- Timing of movement to arrive, not stand
- Choosing when to play forward and when to reset
Progression
- Remove one neutral or restrict touches to increase pressure.
Situational Game (25–30 mins)
Setup
- Area: 60x40m, directional
- Players: 6v6 + GK
- Equipment: Full goals, balls
Description
Normal game with a condition that goals count only if the attacking move includes a forward pass played from an angled support position. Play always restarts from the GK to encourage structured build-up and connection.
Coaching Focus
- Recognising angles that allow forward play
- Supporting on different lines and heights
- Maintaining rhythm while staying connected
Progression
- Remove scoring condition to increase realism.
Transfer / End Game (10–15 mins)
Setup
- Area: Match-sized for numbers
- Players: Normal teams
- Direction: Full directional play
- Equipment: Goals, balls
Description
Free game with minimal conditions. Coach observes application of support angles without intervention.
Coaching Focus
- Observe spontaneous angled support
- Look for forward play created through movement, not instruction
Coaching Information (U13)
Element | Detail
Objective / Outcomes | Players consistently offer angled support to help play forward
Technical Detail | Support angle, open body shape, first touch into space
Coaching Messages (Belco Voice) | “Angle first, then play.” / “Help the ball face forward.”
Common Mistakes & Fixes | Flat support → arrive on an angle; rushing forward pass → recycle and move again
Praise Language | “Great angle.” / “Nice support to play forward.”
Behaviours to Reward | Scanning, movement off the ball, willingness to support
Reflection Question | “Which angle helped us play forward most often?”
Session Design Note | Early activities include numerical support to promote success
Language Note | Belco vocabulary used to support recognition, not explanation
Belconnen United FC – Compact • Brave • Intelligent

heading: U13 Cycle 1, week 2, session 1
headingPath: U13 sessions > U13 Cycle 1, week 2, session 1
content:
BELCONNEN UNITED FC – SESSION PLAN | CYCLE 1 | WEEK 2 | SESSION 1(Segoe UI 9 pt grey text, Belco-blue underline)
U13 | Build-Up & Support | Week 2 | Open Ball Decisions: Recognise Triggers
Session Outcomes
Recognise open-ball moments and choose when to play forward, carry, or connect to keep rhythm.
Ball Mastery Block (Optional – 5–10 mins)
Rotation: B – Turning Under Contact
Setup
- Area: 20x20m square
- Players: All players inside the grid
- Equipment: 1 ball per player
Description
Players move with the ball using shielding turns, pivots, and escape touches. Contact is simulated by passing close to others, forcing players to protect first, then turn into space. Rhythm builds as players alternate between protection and release, accelerating after each successful turn before settling again.
Coaching Focus
- High ball contact and continuous movement
- Freedom to explore turns and escapes
- Build rhythm through protect → release moments
- Prepare players to recognise when the ball is open
Activation / Intro (10–12 mins)
Setup
- Area: 30x25m
- Players: 4v2 inside + 2 outside bounce players
- Equipment: Balls, cones
Description
Four players keep possession against two defenders. Outside players act as bounce options with one touch. When a player receives facing open space, they are encouraged to play forward through the grid or connect out and re-enter. If defenders win the ball, roles rotate quickly.
Coaching Focus
- Scanning before receiving
- Recognising open body shape as a trigger
- Playing with rhythm, not forcing forward play
Progression
- Reduce space or allow defenders to press more aggressively.
Technical – Solving the Situation (15–20 mins)
Setup
- Area: 40x30m, directional
- Players: 5v5 + 2 neutrals (neutrals play with possession)
- Equipment: Balls, cones, target zones
Description
Teams look to progress the ball into end zones. Neutrals help create overloads and time on the ball. The key condition: players can score only after a forward action following an open-body receive (pass, carry, or split). Transitions are immediate on loss.
Coaching Focus
- Body shape to receive facing forward
- Identifying triggers: time, space, opponent distance
- Choosing between play forward, carry, or connect
Progression
- Remove one neutral or add a touch limit to increase pressure.
Situational Game (25–30 mins)
Setup
- Area: 60x40m, directional
- Players: 6v6 + GK
- Equipment: Full goals, balls
Description
Normal game with restarts from the GK. Teams are encouraged to recognise open-ball moments to attack quickly. Goals count double if the action starts from an open receive in the build-up phase.
Coaching Focus
- Recognising when the ball is open
- Connecting quickly to exploit space
- Maintaining rhythm while staying compact
Progression
- Remove double-goal incentive for full realism.
Transfer / End Game (10–15 mins)
Setup
- Area: Match-sized for numbers
- Players: Normal teams
- Direction: Full directional play
- Equipment: Goals, balls
Description
Free game with minimal conditions. Coach observes decision-making without intervention.
Coaching Focus
- Observe spontaneous recognition of triggers
- Look for calm, confident forward decisions
Coaching Information (U13)
Element | Detail
Objective / Outcomes | Players consistently recognise open-ball moments and act with intent
Technical Detail | Open body shape, first touch into space, tempo choice
Coaching Messages (Belco Voice) | “See it early.” / “Open ball, go.”
Common Mistakes & Fixes | Forcing forward play → reset and move again; closed body → adjust angle before receive
Praise Language | “Great recognition.” / “Good choice on the open ball.”
Behaviours to Reward | Scanning, composure, brave forward decisions
Reflection Question | “What told you the ball was open?”
Session Design Note | Numerical support used early to support recognition and success
Language Note | Belco vocabulary supports recognition, not explanation
Belconnen United FC – Compact • Brave • Intelligent

heading: U13 Cycle 1, week 2, session 2
headingPath: U13 sessions > U13 Cycle 1, week 2, session 2
content:
BELCONNEN UNITED FC – SESSION PLAN | CYCLE 1 | WEEK 2 | SESSION 2(Segoe UI 9 pt grey text, Belco-blue underline)
U13 | Build-Up & Support | Week 2 | Progress Through First Line
Session Outcomes
Progress the ball through the opponent’s first line using support angles, timing, and calm decisions.
Ball Mastery Block (Optional – 5–10 mins)
Rotation: B – Turning Under Contact
Setup
- Area: 20x20m square
- Players: All players inside the grid
- Equipment: 1 ball per player
Description
Players move freely with the ball, using shielding touches, pivots, and turns to escape nearby pressure. As players pass close to others, they protect the ball first, then turn sharply into space and accelerate away. Rhythm builds through repeated protect → turn → release actions, preparing players to escape the first line.
Coaching Focus
- High ball contact and continuous movement
- Freedom to explore turns and escapes
- Build rhythm through contact and release
- Prepare players to turn away from pressure
Activation / Intro (10–12 mins)
Setup
- Area: 30x25m, split into two horizontal zones
- Players: 4v2 in the build zone + 2 targets in the end zone
- Equipment: Balls, cones
Description
Four players keep possession against two defenders in the build zone. The aim is to play through the defenders into either target player in the end zone. Once connected, play resets quickly back to the build zone. Defenders rotate after interceptions.
Coaching Focus
- Supporting on different angles and heights
- Scanning to recognise gaps in the first line
- Playing with patience before penetrating
Progression
- Allow a defender to recover into the end zone after the pass.
Technical – Solving the Situation (15–20 mins)
Setup
- Area: 40x30m, directional
- Players: 5v4 (attacking overload)
- Equipment: Balls, cones, mini-goals or target zones
Description
Play starts with the attacking team building against four defenders set as the first line. The objective is to progress through that line into the attacking zone by passing, carrying, or combining. Attacking players must adjust distance and timing to create a clear lane. If defenders win the ball, they counter quickly.
Coaching Focus
- Body shape to play forward
- Timing of movement to break the line
- Choosing between pass, carry, or reset
Progression
- Add a recovering defender or reduce space to increase pressure.
Situational Game (25–30 mins)
Setup
- Area: 60x40m, directional
- Players: 6v6 + GK
- Equipment: Full goals, balls
Description
Normal game with restarts from the GK. The opposition is encouraged to hold a compact first line. Goals count double if the attacking team progresses through the first line with control before scoring.
Coaching Focus
- Recognising when the first line is set or broken
- Supporting underneath and beyond the ball
- Maintaining rhythm while playing forward
Progression
- Remove double-goal incentive for full realism.
Transfer / End Game (10–15 mins)
Setup
- Area: Match-sized for numbers
- Players: Normal teams
- Direction: Full directional play
- Equipment: Goals, balls
Description
Free game with minimal conditions. Coach observes how players attempt to break the first line without prompts.
Coaching Focus
- Observe calm progression through pressure
- Look for spontaneous support and movement
Coaching Information (U13)
Element | Detail
Objective / Outcomes | Players consistently progress the ball through the first line
Technical Detail | Support angle, open body shape, first touch into space
Coaching Messages (Belco Voice) | “Fix them, then play.” / “Break the first line.”
Common Mistakes & Fixes | Forcing passes → reset and move; standing flat → adjust angle and timing
Praise Language | “Great patience.” / “Nice line break.”
Behaviours to Reward | Scanning, composure, brave forward intent
Reflection Question | “What helped us get through the first line?”
Session Design Note | Early overloads support success and recognition
Language Note | Belco vocabulary supports recognition, not explanation
Belconnen United FC – Compact • Brave • Intelligent

heading: U13 Cycle 1, week 3, session 1
headingPath: U13 sessions > U13 Cycle 1, week 3, session 1
content:
BELCONNEN UNITED FC – SESSION PLAN | CYCLE 1 | WEEK 3 | SESSION 1(Segoe UI 9 pt grey text, Belco-blue underline)
U13 | Build-Up & Support | Week 3 | Build Through Midfield: Connect Back → Mid → Front
Session Outcomes
Connect play from the back through midfield to the front with clear support angles, timing, and controlled rhythm.
Ball Mastery Block (Optional – 5–10 mins)
Rotation: C – Mirror Feints
Setup
- Area: 20x20m square
- Players: Pair players up; one ball per pair
- Equipment: Balls, cones
Description
Players work in pairs, facing each other. One player leads with the ball using body feints, changes of pace, and direction; the partner mirrors the movement without a ball. Roles switch regularly. Rhythm builds as players exaggerate disguise, then explode into short bursts before settling again. The block flows directly into the first practice without stopping.
Coaching Focus
- High ball contact and continuous movement
- Freedom to explore disguise and reaction
- Build rhythm through show → fake → explode
- Prepare players to create separation before connecting
Activation / Intro (10–12 mins)
Setup
- Area: 30x25m, split into three vertical lanes (back / mid / front)
- Players: 4v2 in the central area + 2 target players in the front lane
- Equipment: Balls, cones
Description
Play starts with the back players keeping possession against two defenders. The objective is to connect into the midfield lane, then play forward into either front target. Once the front target receives, play resets quickly back to the start. Players rotate roles regularly to experience all lines.
Coaching Focus
- Supporting underneath the ball to connect forward
- Scanning to see the next line before receiving
- Playing with rhythm rather than forcing the forward pass
Progression
- Allow one defender to recover into the midfield lane after the first pass.
Technical – Solving the Situation (15–20 mins)
Setup
- Area: 45x30m, divided into three horizontal zones (back / mid / front)
- Players: 5v4 (attacking overload)
- Equipment: Balls, cones, mini-goals or target zones
Description
Play begins in the back zone. The attacking team must connect through the midfield zone before accessing the front zone to score. Players must adjust distances and angles to support progression between lines. If defenders win the ball, they counter quickly toward the back zone goal.
Coaching Focus
- Body shape to receive facing the next line
- Timing of midfield movement to arrive between lines
- Choosing when to play through, around, or reset
Progression
- Add a recovering defender into the midfield zone or reduce space.
Situational Game (25–30 mins)
Setup
- Area: 60x40m, directional
- Players: 6v6 + GK
- Equipment: Full goals, balls
Description
Normal game with all restarts from the GK. Goals count double if the attack clearly connects back → midfield → front before finishing. Transitions are live and immediate.
Coaching Focus
- Recognising midfield as the link, not a stopping point

BELCONNEN UNITED FC – SESSION PLAN | CYCLE 1 | WEEK 3 | SESSION 2(Segoe UI 9 pt grey text, Belco-blue underline)
U13 | Build-Up & Support | Week 3 | Tempo Control in Build-Up
Session Outcomes
Control tempo in build-up by recognising when to slow the game, reset, or accelerate forward.
Ball Mastery Block (Optional – 5–10 mins)
Rotation: C – Mirror Feints
Setup
- Area: 20x20m square
- Players: Paired up, one ball per pair
- Equipment: Balls, cones
Description
Players face each other. One player leads with the ball using feints, pauses, and short bursts; the partner mirrors the movement without a ball. The leader varies tempo—slow touches, stop-start moments, then quick acceleration. Roles rotate regularly. The flow builds awareness of changing speed before moving into game-based work.
Coaching Focus
- High ball contact and continuous movement
- Freedom to disguise, pause, and accelerate
- Build rhythm through change of tempo
- Prepare players to control speed before passing
Activation / Intro (10–12 mins)
Setup
- Area: 30x25m
- Players: 5v2 rondo + GK (GK always involved)
- Equipment: Balls, cones
Description
Five players keep possession against two defenders, with the GK acting as a reset option. Teams score a point for switching tempo—three calm passes followed by a quick forward or split pass. If defenders win the ball, they swap with the player who lost possession. Play restarts immediately from the GK.
Coaching Focus
- Supporting underneath to allow reset
- Scanning to recognise pressure and time
- Playing with rhythm, not constant speed
Progression
- Reduce space or allow defenders to press more aggressively.
Technical – Solving the Situation (15–20 mins)
Setup
- Area: 45x30m, directional
- Players: 5v4 (attacking overload)
- Equipment: Balls, cones, target goals or zones
Description
Play starts with the attacking team building from the back. To score, teams must show a clear tempo change: circulate calmly, then accelerate to progress forward. Defenders aim to disrupt rhythm and counter if they win possession. Attacking players must adjust distances and angles to control speed.
Coaching Focus
- Body shape to receive with options
- Recognising triggers to slow or speed up
- Choosing when to reset through the back line
Progression
- Add a recovering defender or limit touches in attacking moments.
Situational Game (25–30 mins)
Setup
- Area: 60x40m, directional
- Players: 6v6 + GK
- Equipment: Full goals, balls
Description
Normal game with all restarts from the GK. Goals count double if the attack shows clear tempo control—calm circulation followed by purposeful acceleration. Transitions are live and immediate.
Coaching Focus
- Recognising when to slow the game
- Accelerating once space or advantage appears
- Staying connected while changing tempo
Progression
- Remove double-goal condition for full realism.
Transfer / End Game (10–15 mins)
Setup
- Area: Match-sized for numbers
- Players: Normal teams
- Direction: Full directional play
- Equipment: Goals, balls
Description
Free game with minimal conditions. Coach observes how players manage tempo naturally in build-up and transition.
Coaching Focus
- Observe spontaneous tempo changes
- Look for calm resets and decisive acceleration
Coaching Information (U13)
Element | Detail
Objective / Outcomes | Players consistently control tempo in build-up situations
Technical Detail | First touch quality, body orientation, speed of pass
Coaching Messages (Belco Voice) | “Slow to control, fast to hurt.” / “Reset, then go.”
Common Mistakes & Fixes | Rushing every pass → recycle and settle; overplaying slow → recognise trigger to accelerate
Praise Language | “Great patience.” / “Perfect moment to speed it up.”
Behaviours to Reward | Composure, scanning, intelligent tempo choices
Reflection Question | “What told you it was time to slow down or speed up?”
Session Design Note | Early overloads support success and tempo awareness
Language Note | Belco vocabulary supports recognition, not explanation

BELCONNEN UNITED FC – SESSION PLAN | CYCLE 2 | WEEK 1 | SESSION 1(Segoe UI 9 pt grey text, Belco-blue underline)
U13 | Combination Play | Week 1 | Wall Pass to Break Line
Session Outcomes
Use wall passes to break a defensive line and play forward with timing and rhythm.
Ball Mastery Block (Optional – 5–10 mins)
Rotation: D – 1v0 → 1v1 Activation
Setup
- Area: 20x15m, split into two channels
- Players: Pairs; one ball per pair
- Equipment: Balls, cones
Description
Players start 1v0, carrying the ball across the channel with a turn or change of pace at halfway. On the return run, a passive defender is introduced to create a 1v1 moment. The attacker drives, turns, or slips past before accelerating away. Rhythm builds as players move from repetition into light opposition, then rotate roles smoothly.
Coaching Focus
- High ball contact and continuous movement
- Freedom to carry, turn, and attack space
- Build rhythm through drive → decision → release
- Prepare players for combination play after engagement
Activation / Intro (10–12 mins)
Setup
- Area: 30x25m
- Players: 3v3 + 2 neutral bounce players on the outside
- Equipment: Balls, cones
Description
Teams keep possession with help from two neutral bounce players who play one-touch only. A point is scored by completing a wall pass (pass → set → play through) to break the central space and keep possession. If the ball is lost, immediate transition applies. Play restarts quickly from the coach.
Coaching Focus
- Supporting close and on an angle
- Passing with intent to invite the return
- Playing with rhythm rather than forcing the combination
Progression
- Limit touches for inside players or reduce space.
Technical – Solving the Situation (15–20 mins)
Setup
- Area: 40x30m, directional
- Players: 4v4 + 2 neutrals (neutrals play with possession)
- Equipment: Balls, cones, mini-goals or target zones
Description
Teams attack directionally toward target goals. The key condition: goals count only after a successful wall pass breaks a defender or line. Players must adjust distance and timing to combine at speed. Neutrals support angles and rhythm. Transitions are immediate on loss.
Coaching Focus
- Timing of the pass and movement after passing
- Body shape to receive and set cleanly
- Choosing the right moment to play the wall pass
Progression
- Remove one neutral or add a recovering defender to increase pressure.
Situational Game (25–30 mins)
Setup
- Area: 60x40m, directional
- Players: 6v6 + GK
- Equipment: Full goals, balls
Description
Normal game with all restarts from the GK. Teams are encouraged to break lines through combinations. Goals count double if a wall pass is used to break the first or midfield line before scoring.
Coaching Focus
- Recognising when a wall pass is on
- Supporting close to enable quick combinations
- Maintaining rhythm while staying connected
Progression
- Remove double-goal condition for full realism.
Transfer / End Game (10–15 mins)
Setup
- Area: Match-sized for numbers
- Players: Normal teams
- Direction: Full directional play
- Equipment: Goals, balls
Description
Free game with minimal conditions. Coach observes spontaneous use of wall passes without prompts.
Coaching Focus
- Observe natural combination play
- Look for timing and intent rather than forced patterns
Coaching Information (U13)
Element | Detail
Objective / Outcomes | Players consistently use wall passes to break defensive lines
Technical Detail | Pass weight, supporting distance, timing of movement
Coaching Messages (Belco Voice) | “Pass and move.” / “Play it back to go through.”
Common Mistakes & Fixes | Standing after pass → move immediately; forced wall pass → recycle and try again
Praise Language | “Great timing.” / “Nice combination to break the line.”
Behaviours to Reward | Willingness to support close, quick movement after pass
Reflection Question | “What made the wall pass work that time?”
Session Design Note | Early numerical support helps players recognise combination moments
Language Note | Belco vocabulary supports recognition, not explanation

U13 | Combination Play | Week 1 | Third-Man Support
Session Outcomes

Setup

- Equipment: Balls, cones
Description
Players work in threes. Player A carries the ball 1v0 into space and releases to Player B. Player B sets the ball first time into the path of Player C, who runs onto it and accelerates away. On the return, a passive defender is added to create a light 1v1 before the release. Roles rotate smoothly. Rhythm builds through carry → set → run actions.

- High ball contact and continuous movement
- Freedom to carry and release with timing
- Build rhythm through pass → set → run
- Prepare players for third-man movement patterns

- Players: 3v3 + 3 neutral players (neutrals play with possession)
- Equipment: Balls, cones

Teams keep possession using neutrals to create overloads. A point is scored by playing through a neutral into a third-man run that breaks pressure. Neutrals are limited to one touch to encourage movement off the ball. If possession is lost, immediate transition applies. Play restarts quickly.

- Remove double-goal condition for full realism.

- Area: Match-sized for numbers

U13 | Combination Play | Week 2 | Drive–Draw–Play: Create the Extra Player
Session Outcomes
Create an extra player by driving with the ball, drawing pressure, and releasing at the right moment.
Ball Mastery Block (Optional – 5–10 mins)
Rotation: A – Controlled Touch Rhythm
Setup
Area: 20x20m square
Players: All players inside the grid
Equipment: 1 ball per player
Description
Players move freely with the ball using inside/outside touches, rolls, and directional changes. Tempo varies naturally as players accelerate into space, then slow to regain control. Players adjust movement to avoid others, constantly changing direction and rhythm. The block flows directly into the first practice without stopping.
Coaching Focus
High ball contact and continuous movement
Freedom to explore tempo changes
Build rhythm and awareness through movement
Prepare players to carry the ball with control
Activation / Intro (10–12 mins)
Setup
Area: 30x25m
Players: 4v2 inside + 2 outside support players
Equipment: Balls, cones
Description
Four players keep possession against two defenders. Outside support players stay wide and play one-touch. A point is scored when a central player drives with the ball, draws a defender, and releases to an outside player. If defenders win the ball, they rotate with the player who lost possession. Play restarts quickly.
Coaching Focus
Driving with intent toward pressure
Recognising when a defender commits
Releasing the ball at the right moment
Progression
Reduce space or allow defenders to press more aggressively.
Technical – Solving the Situation (15–20 mins)
Setup
Area: 40x30m, directional
Players: 4v4 + 2 neutrals (neutrals play with possession)
Equipment: Balls, cones, mini-goals or target zones
Description
Teams attack directionally using the neutrals to create overloads. The key condition: progression or goals must come after a drive that draws a defender before the pass. Players must coordinate distances and timing so the ball carrier can attack space and create a free teammate. Transitions are immediate on loss.
Coaching Focus
Body shape and touch to attack space
Timing of the release pass
Supporting ahead and beyond the ball
Progression
Remove one neutral or add a recovering defender to increase pressure.
Situational Game (25–30 mins)
Setup
Area: 60x40m, directional
Players: 6v6 + GK
Equipment: Full goals, balls
Description
Normal game with all restarts from the GK. Goals count double if the attack includes a clear drive–draw–play action that creates a free player before scoring. Play flows with live transitions.
Coaching Focus
Recognising when to drive at the defender
Creating superiority through movement with the ball
Maintaining rhythm while combining forward
Progression
Remove double-goal condition for full realism.
Transfer / End Game (10–15 mins)
Setup
Area: Match-sized for numbers
Players: Normal teams
Direction: Full directional play
Equipment: Goals, balls
Description
Free game with minimal conditions. Coach observes natural use of drive–draw–play moments without prompts.
Coaching Focus
Observe brave carries into pressure
Look for calm releases that free a teammate
Coaching Information (U13)
Element | Detail
Objective / Outcomes | Players consistently create an extra player by driving and releasing
Technical Detail | First touch forward, close control, timing of pass
Coaching Messages (Belco Voice) | “Drive to draw.” / “Commit them, then play.”
Common Mistakes & Fixes | Passing too early → attack space first; overdribbling → release once defender commits
Praise Language | “Great drive.” / “Perfect release.”
Behaviours to Reward | Bravery on the ball, scanning, intent to create advantage
Reflection Question | “What told you the defender was committed?”

Language Note | Belco vocabulary supports recognition, not explanation

Equipment: 1 ball per player
Description
Players move freely with the ball using inside/outside touches, rolls, and directional changes. Players alternate between slow control and short accelerations, adjusting movement to avoid others. Rhythm builds through repeated changes of pace, then settles back into calm control before flowing into the first practice.
Coaching Focus
High ball contact and continuous movement
Freedom to vary tempo and direction
Build rhythm and awareness
Prepare players to move at the right moment, not too early
Activation / Intro (10–12 mins)
Setup
Area: 30x25m
Players: 4v2 + 2 outside support players
Equipment: Balls, cones
Description
Four players keep possession against two defenders. Outside support players stay on the sides and play one-touch. Inside players must time their movement to receive after the ball is released—arriving late rather than waiting. A point is scored for a clean receive that allows the next pass to be played forward. Quick restarts keep rhythm high.

Moving after the pass, not before
Arriving on the move rather than standing
Scanning to judge when to support

Reduce space or allow defenders to press more aggressively.

Players: 4v4 + 2 neutrals (neutrals play with possession)
Equipment: Balls, cones, mini-goals or target zones

Teams attack directionally. The key condition: progression or goals count only if the receiving player arrives into space after the pass is played. Neutrals help maintain rhythm and create passing lanes. Players must coordinate distance and timing so movement happens at the right moment to beat pressure.
Coaching Focus
Timing runs to arrive, not wait
Body shape to receive on the move
Recognising when to hold position and when to go

Remove one neutral or add a recovering defender to increase pressure.

Setup
Area: 60x40m, directional
Players: 6v6 + GK
Equipment: Full goals, balls

Maintaining rhythm while staying connected

Remove double-goal condition for full realism.

Setup
Area: Match-sized for numbers
Players: Normal teams
Direction: Full directional play
Equipment: Goals, balls

Coaching Focus
Observe players arriving on the move
Look for improved connection through better timing
Coaching Information (U13)
Element | Detail
Objective / Outcomes | Players consistently time their movement to support and combine effectively
Technical Detail | Movement timing, first touch on the move, body orientation
Coaching Messages (Belco Voice) | “Arrive, don’t wait.” / “Move after the pass.”
Common Mistakes & Fixes | Moving too early → delay and arrive late; standing flat → reset and time run
Praise Language | “Perfect timing.” / “Great arrival.”
Behaviours to Reward | Scanning, patience, intelligent off-ball movement
