heading: U14 Coach Pack
content:
BELCONNEN UNITED FC
TECHNICAL DEPARTMENT
——————————————
COACH PACK 4
TACTICAL FOUNDATION PHASE – U14
——————————————
Beginning to act as one unit
——————————————
Prepared by:
Scott Conlon – Technical Director
Belconnen United FC
V2026.03

heading: 1. Message to the U14 Coaches
headingPath: COACH PACK 3 (U14) – TACTICAL FOUNDATION PHASE > 1. Message to the U14 Coaches
content:
This is the phase where football becomes tactical.
Your players now need structure — not restriction, but shape that helps them see and act together.
The Croatians describe it as “learning cooperation” — players understanding that their position gives the team balance, and their timing gives the team rhythm.
Your job is to teach the game within the game: how build-up, pressing, and compactness link into one flow.
The biggest shift from the younger years — the coach must now connect the exercises.
Every drill should feed the match idea, and your feedback should link the parts together.
Don’t overcomplicate: two or three key ideas per week, repeated through the month.
Use our shared language to create clarity — “Open ball, play through.”, “Closed, compact.”, “Return to reset.”
At this stage, we’re teaching collective intelligence — the tactical foundation for Build & Control the Tempo, Collective Compactness, and Aggressive Recovery.
“Now they don’t just play football — they understand it.”

heading: 2. U14 Player Characteristics
headingPath: COACH PACK 3 (U14) – TACTICAL FOUNDATION PHASE > 2. U14 Player Characteristics
content:
U14 players are emerging from the technical phase with stronger physical and cognitive abilities.
They are ready to think more strategically, yet still need confidence and guidance in how to act as part of a coordinated team.
Typical traits:
- Increasing physical strength and endurance, but inconsistent coordination due to growth.
- Beginning to understand tactical cause–effect — “if I move here, he can move there.”
- Need constant reminders about compact distances and unit shape.
- Still emotionally reactive — coaches should reinforce decision-making, not emotion.
- Thrive on challenge and structure; lose focus when play feels random.
Coach takeaway:
Lead through tactical purpose, not control.
Show them what the team shape means, not just where to stand.
This year, the coach becomes an architect — connecting individual actions to collective balance.

heading: 3. U14 Phase Overview
headingPath: COACH PACK 3 (U14) – TACTICAL FOUNDATION PHASE > 3. U14 Phase Overview
content:
Area | Key Points
Age Phase | U14 – Tactical Foundation
Development Focus | Team coordination: build-up organisation, compact defending, collective transitions.
Session Rhythm | 2 sessions per week. 50:50 technical–tactical. Use realistic shapes (4v4 to 7v7).
Training Ratio | Tactical principle → technical support → game repetition. 1:1 ratio within session blocks.
Game Focus | Build-up and mid-block defending. Play through pressure, defend compact, react as a unit.
Belco Identity Seeds | 🔹 Build & Control the Tempo (possession and rhythm). 🔹 Collective Compactness (team distances). 🔹 Aggressive Recovery (first reaction after loss).
Coach Role | Organiser and connector. Plan transitions between exercises with intent. Reinforce positional discipline and reaction speed.

heading: 4. U14 Coaching Emphasis
headingPath: COACH PACK 3 (U14) – TACTICAL FOUNDATION PHASE > 4. U14 Coaching Emphasis
content:
Coaching Dimension | Emphasis for This Phase | Practical Examples / Notes
Session Rhythm | Balance control and intensity. Tactical tempo = “pause to see, fast to act.” | Build-up game → transition drill → press practice.
Coaching Voice | Fewer words, stronger triggers. Introduce team cues. | “Compact!” “Step!” “Return!” “Play through!”
Correction Style | Mix freeze with replay. Correct group distances, body angles, and tempo shifts. | Stop only when the team shape breaks.
Technical Detail | First touch facing play; pass timing; support angles between lines. | Build-up rhythm: one touch too soon = lost tempo.
Tactical Habits | Compactness, pressing coordination, recognition of open/closed ball cues. | “Nearest three go.” “Open ball — stretch.”
Psychological Theme | Patience and responsibility. Mental maturity begins here. | “Wait for the moment.” “Stay connected.”
Language Focus | Trigger and collective cues. | “Closed = compact.” “Open = go.” “Return ball = reset.”
Coach Behaviours to Reward | Players who scan before pressing or play between lines with control. | “Good — calm before fast.”
Croatian Guideline | “The game organises the players; the coach organises the game.” | Plan exercises that mirror real team shapes.

heading: 5. U14 Technical Focus List
headingPath: COACH PACK 3 (U14) – TACTICAL FOUNDATION PHASE > 5. U14 Technical Focus List
content:
Purpose
At U14, the player begins to see football as a system of coordinated actions.
The goal is to connect individual technique to team intelligence — learning how personal habits (first touch, scanning, timing) shape collective movement (compactness, tempo, and overloads).
Technique now serves tactical function — quality of execution under pressure, rhythm, and awareness.
Croatian model: Taktičko-tehničko učenje kroz igru — “tactical–technical learning through play.”
Belco identity: compact, brave, intelligent — now expressed through controlled cooperation.
1. Technique Under Tactical Pressure
Focus | What It Looks Like | How It’s Trained
First touch direction & control | Player adjusts first touch away from pressure, opens body to see the next line. | 4v4 + 3 positional games with pressing defenders.
Timing of release (after drawing pressure) | Holds or releases the ball based on teammate support and opponent cues. | Drive–Draw–Play games; must fix before passing.
Play with far foot under pressure | Opens up body to pass in tempo or escape opponent. | 3v3 rondos with tight zones; cue = “show far foot.”
Decision under time constraint | Recognises when to go forward, circulate, or protect. | 5v5 tempo game – 5 seconds to find forward pass.
Belco links: Build Through Thirds, Overloads, Tempo Control. |  | 
2. Positional Awareness & Support
Focus | What It Looks Like | How It’s Trained
Support angles in compact shape | Stays connected, always one passing lane open. | 6v3–7v4 build-up grids – must show side-on.
Distance and timing to support | Steps in or out based on pressure on the ball. | Triangles with pressure: inside/outside movement.
Receiving between lines | Finds and stays in pocket, not too deep. | 5v5 directional zones – must receive on the half-turn.
Playing in relation to next action | First touch prepares the next line. | Bounce game: must play one line forward after bounce.
Belco links: Build Through Thirds, Bounce & Third-Man, Compactness. |  | 
3. Combination & Movement to Create Advantage
Focus | What It Looks Like | How It’s Trained
Bounce pass to escape pressure | Quick 1-2 to change direction or find third man. | 3v3 + bounce player; must exit on second touch.
Third-man coordination | One checks short, one runs behind. | 4v3 build-up triangles; bonus for third-man play.
Timing of movement to receive | Arrives as ball travels, not before. | 5v4 + GK; delayed runs rewarded.
Rotation between lines (under / beyond) | Midfielder and forward rotate to open lane. | 6v6 positional grid – rotation rule every switch.
Belco links: Drive–Draw–Play, Bounce & Third-Man, Overloads. |  | 
4. Ball Carrying & Drive Decisions
Focus | What It Looks Like | How It’s Trained
Recognising open-ball cues | When no pressure, drives forward with control. | 4v4 directional – open-ball = must advance.
Driving to fix defenders | Carries at opponent to create extra player. | 3v2 transition games; score if you draw and play.
Carrying into pressure to release | Uses drive to attract and free teammate. | 5v5 + neutral; drive then bounce.
Change of direction to protect | Turns to maintain rhythm and spacing. | 2v2 wide channels; turn and exit gate.
Belco links: Open Ball, Overloads, Tempo Control. |  | 
5. Defensive Technique in Compact Structure
Focus | What It Looks Like | How It’s Trained
Compact pressing distances | Defenders stay within 6–8m between lines. | 6v6 half-pitch block; recover compactness rule.
Press–cover–balance coordination | Nearest engages, others adjust to protect. | 4v4 defensive rotation; cover shadow zones.
Transition press (5-second rule) | First step forward after loss, unit reacts. | 5v5 transition mini-games.
Body shape and control in duels | Low, side-on, delay or tackle. | 1v1–2v2 wide channel defending.
Belco links: Compactness, Counter-Pressing, Duel Mentality. |  | 
6. Finishing & Final-Third Habits
Focus | What It Looks Like | How It’s Trained
Arriving in assist zone | Wide or inside player times run into cross zone. | 4v4 + GK; goal after cut-back = 2 points.
Second-line support | Midfielder follows attack for rebound or pass. | 5v5 directional; extra point for trailing run.
Finishing from compact spaces | Calm, side-foot accuracy in traffic. | 6v6 penalty-box game; 2-touch limit.
Decision: cross or cut-back | Reads space, not habit. | 3v2 wide overloads; finish only from cut-back.
Belco links: Assist Zone, Overloads, Tempo Control. |  | 
7. Cognitive & Tactical Habits
Focus | What It Looks Like | How It’s Trained
Scanning and orientation | Checks both sides before receiving or pressing. | 4v2–5v3 rondos; call “open” side aloud.
Recognising tempo moments | Knows when to slow circulation or drive forward. | 7v7 + GK build-up with tempo triggers.
Communication & leadership | Constant information exchange within line. | 6v6 compact games; silent vs loud variations.
Transition reaction speed | Immediate shape change after gain/loss. | Continuous 5v5 transitions – “3 seconds to shape.”
Belco links: Tempo Control, Compactness, Open Ball. |  |

heading: 6. U14 Language Bank (part 1)
headingPath: COACH PACK 3 (U14) – TACTICAL FOUNDATION PHASE > 6. U14 Language Bank
content:
This chapter defines the shared tactical language used across the Belco program to describe the game by moments and by thirds: in possession, out of possession, and transition. Rather than prescribing patterns or movements, the language bank provides collective concept cues that allow teams to recognise situations, act together, and control tempo under match pressure.
The language is deliberately short, visual, and repeatable, enabling players to self-correct in real time and coaches to intervene briefly without over-coaching. Organising the game through the back, middle, and front thirds reflects how decisions actually occur on the pitch, while transition language connects these moments into one continuous game flow. This approach mirrors Croatian and European methodology, where tactical understanding is built through shared terminology, repetition, and realism, not constant instruction.
From U14 onward, the language remains consistent across age groups, while the expectation of execution increases. What begins as recognition at U14–15 becomes a performance standard at U16 and above. This continuity ensures players are not relearning football concepts each season, but instead developing the ability to read the game, manage moments, and take ownership as they prepare for senior football.
U14 BUILD-UP FROM THE BACK
Belco Identity: Build & Control the Tempo · Create Superiority
Concept Focus | Collective Language Cue | Purpose
Structure First | “Set the shape.” “Back four ready.” | Shape before speed. Players recognise build-up positions automatically.
Create the Free Man | “Who’s spare?” “Find the free man.” | Numerical superiority is the foundation of progression.
Support on Different Lines | “Different lines.” “Two options.” | Prevents flat play; creates forward and diagonal solutions.
Attract to Progress | “Invite them.” “Bring him, then play.” | Draw pressure deliberately to open space elsewhere.
Progress with Intent | “Step in if free.” “Break a line.” | Forward play only when advantage is created — not forced.
U14 PROGRESSING THROUGH MIDFIELD
Belco Identity: Create Superiority · Build & Control the Tempo
Concept Focus | Language Cues | Purpose / Croatian Logic
Open Ball Recognition | “Open ball — trigger run!” “Now we go!” | Read and react collectively to forward-facing player.
Third-Man Combinations | “Bounce and go.” “Third man opens the lane.” | Classic Croatian progression habit.
Drive–Draw–Play | “Drive inside — draw him — play outside.” | Central rhythm for overloads and rhythm control.
Level & Outside | “Stay level — make the blind run.” | Used in rotations and wide triangle play.
Pivot Use | “Middle man controls tempo.” “Play through the pivot.” | Encourages midfield rhythm management.
Tempo Control | “Slow → quick → slow.” “We set the rhythm.” | Players manage flow instead of reacting to opponents.
U14 FINISHING FROM POSITIONAL PLAY
Belco Identity: Create Superiority · Duel Mentality
Concept Focus | Language Cues | Purpose / Croatian Logic
Assist Zone | “Go to the assist zone!” “Deliver back — not over!” | Attacking pattern: penetrate to byline and cut back.
Cut-Back Mentality | “Drive the line — cut back with purpose.” | High-probability finish model; second-line arrival.
Drive–Draw–Play | “Draw him wide — play inside.” | Overload and isolate wide duels.
Level & Outside | “Stay outside — inside opens.” | Wide positioning creates channel for the third man.
Finishing Focus | “Arrive late — attack the cut-back.” “Hit the target, don’t blast.” | Finishing efficiency and timing.
U14 PRESSING ORGANISATION
Belco Identity: Collective Compactness · Aggressive Recovery
Concept Focus | Language Cues | Purpose / Croatian Logic
Press Triggers | “Closed ball — press!” “Return ball — step in!” | Triggers pressing cues based on visual signals.
Nearest Three Go | “Nearest three go together!” | Collective, compact pressing shape.
Compactness | “Stay tight — squeeze the line.” “Protect the core.” | Croatian hallmark; central compactness before aggression.
Compact to Attack | “Win it — play forward!” “Press to play!” | Pressing becomes a way to create attacking rhythm.
Recovery Voice | “Shape first, then step.” “Don’t chase alone.” | Emphasises collective patience.
U14 DEFENDING THE MIDDLE THIRD
Belco Identity: Collective Compactness · Build & Control the Tempo
Concept Focus | Language Cues | Purpose / Croatian Logic
Defensive Shape | “Compact block — control distance.” | Mid-block control and discipline.
Reading Cues | “Closed ball — stay.” “Open ball — drop.” | Players read visual signals, not coach voice.
Communication | “Talk the line.” “Move as one.” | Ownership of shape.
Delay Discipline | “Hold — delay — then step.” | Teaches timing of defensive pressure.
Transition Ready | “Compact to break.” | Always ready to shift into attack.
U14 TRANSITION TO ATTACK
Belco Identity: Build & Control the Tempo · Aggressive Recovery
Concept Focus | Language Cues | Purpose / Croatian Logic
First Pass After Regain | “Find open ball.” “Win → play forward.” | Prioritise tempo and direction on regain.
Support Runs | “Run to help the regain.” “Sprint to support.” | Creates fast, connected transition.
Drive–Draw–Play | “Drive to draw — release fast.” | Controlled acceleration after regain.
Tempo Control | “Fast brain, calm feet.” | Keeps quality while increasing tempo.
U14 TRANSITION TO DEFENCE
Belco Identity: Aggressive Recovery · Collective Compactness
Concept Focus | Language Cues | Purpose / Croatian Logic

heading: 6. U14 Language Bank (part 2)
headingPath: COACH PACK 3 (U14) – TACTICAL FOUNDATION PHASE > 6. U14 Language Bank
content:
Immediate Reaction | “Lose it? Close it!” “Nearest three press.” | 5-second regain principle.
Compact Recovery | “Protect the middle.” “Get narrow fast.” | Prevent central progression.
Tempo Delay | “Slow them down.” “Buy us seconds.” | Intelligent delay for regrouping.
Team Reaction | “Everyone reacts — together.” | No passengers mentality.
U14 SWITCH OF PLAY & WIDTH MANAGEMENT
Belco Identity: Build & Control the Tempo · Create Superiority
Concept Focus | Language Cues | Purpose / Croatian Logic
Recognition | “Play one way — finish the other.” “Far side free.” | Recognise imbalance.
Return Ball Use | “Reset — change side!” | Teaches circulation to break structure.
Width | “Stretch to control.” “Outside line ready.” | Creates space to advance inside.
Level & Outside | “Stay level — open the angle.” | Shape management cue for wide players.
Tempo Rhythm | “Slow → quick → slow.” | Change rhythm to move opponent.
U14 TEAM MENTALITY & GAME INTELLIGENCE
Belco Identity: Build & Control the Tempo · Duel Mentality
Concept Focus | Language Cues | Purpose / Croatian Logic
Tempo Ownership | “We control the rhythm.” “Don’t chase the game.” | Reinforces control mindset.
Resilience | “Next ball.” “React, reset, repeat.” | Croatian hallmark: composure + continuity.
Confidence | “Play brave.” “Trust the structure.” | Encourages self-coaching.
Communication | “One voice, one shape.” | Unity and leadership.
Reflection | “What picture did you see?” | Encourages autonomy and decision reflection.

heading: 7. U14 Annual Cycle Progression
headingPath: COACH PACK 3 (U14) – TACTICAL FOUNDATION PHASE > 7. U14 Annual Cycle Progression
content:
Structure: The U14 season consists of 8 Cycles · 3–4 weeks each · 2 sessions/week (≈28–30 weeks)Focus: Team tactical cooperation, unit connection, tempo control, pressing organisation.Belco Language Layer: “Open ball early.” / “Play through, not around.” / “Compact together.” / “React first.” / “Stretch to control.”
U14 cycles are numbered Cycle 1 to Cycle 8
Cycle | Theme | Duration | Week 1 | Week 2 | Week 3 | Week 4
1 | Build-Up from the Back | 4 weeks | GK + Backline Triangles – Calm Start Under Press Open Ball Recognition | Support & Depth – Midfielder Drops In Pivot Links Play | Press Resistance Games – Play Through First Line Back → Mid Connection | Full-Team Build-Up Game – Build to Beat the Press Rhythm and Control
2 | Progressing Through Midfield | 3 weeks | Midfield Rotation Basics – Triangles and Third-Man Timing to Support | Build Through Overload – Find the Free Midfielder Create Superiority | Tempo Game – Calm to Accelerate Through Ball Finishing | 
3 | Finishing from Positional Play | 4 weeks | Positional Attack – Occupy 5 Lanes Create the Final Pass | Arrive Late – Second Line Finishing Attack the Cut-Back | Combination to Finish – Wall Pass in Zone 14 3rd-Man Timing | Collective Attack Game – From Build-Up to Finish Rhythm to Purpose
4 | Pressing Organisation | 4 weeks | Press Triggers – Closed Ball and Return Pass Angles of Approach | Compact Front Line – Nearest 3 Go Cover and Balance | Team Press – Forward + Mid Unit Connection Win it High | Pressing Game – Regain in 5 Seconds Transition to Finish
5 | Defending the Middle Third | 3 weeks | Compact Mid-Block – Protect Central Zone Line Distances | Control Depth – Track Runners Move as a Unit | Delay and Regain – Defend Forward When Possible Counter Shape | 
6 | Transition to Attack | 3 weeks | Reaction After Regain – First Pass Forward Support to Counter | Break with Speed – Exploit Space Compact Recovery if Lost | Counter-Attack Game – Win → Play → Support Finishing the Break | 
7 | Transition to Defence | 3 weeks | Reaction After Loss – Nearest 3 Press Delay Counter | Counter-Press Triggers – Closed or Return Ball Compact Behind | Compact Recovery – Regain Shape Fast Protect the Middle | 
8 | Switch of Play & Width Management | 3 weeks | Recognise When to Switch – Use Pivot Open Ball Scan | Width and Depth – Stretch to Control Circulate to Create | Change Sides to Break Lines – Tempo Through Circulation Final Third Entry |

heading: 8 Training Sessions
headingPath: COACH PACK 3 (U14) – TACTICAL FOUNDATION PHASE > 8 Training Sessions
content:
Training Chapter – Coach Standard (Coach Pack Version)
Belco training sessions are not copied from Croatian drills.
They are built on the same philosophy and session structure, adapted to our players, coaches, and environment.
Every session follows a clear learning sequence:
- Prepare the player (uvodni)
- Develop the main idea (glavni)
- Transfer learning into the game (završni)
These phases describe purpose, not drills.
Coaches are not expected to run fixed exercises. They are expected to:
- respect the session flow
- connect activities to the game
- increase realism and pressure as the session progresses
- use shared language consistently
The session examples that follow show one way to apply this methodology. Activities can be changed, adapted, or replaced, as long as the learning intent, progression, and behavioural standards remain.
We don’t copy exercises.We copy how learning is structured.

heading: U14 Session Structure
headingPath: COACH PACK 3 (U14) – TACTICAL FOUNDATION PHASE > 8 Training Sessions > U14 Session Structure
content:
How Belco Training Sessions Are Structured (U14)
At U14, Belco training sessions mark the beginning of the tactical foundation phase. Players are now expected to move beyond purely individual or paired actions and begin to recognise situations, coordinate decisions, and take responsibility within the game.
This does not mean teaching team structure, systems, or patterns.Instead, players learn how to:
- read the game
- recognise advantage or danger
- act together with teammates
- adapt their behaviour under pressure
Learning still happens through the game, not through instruction.
Croatian Learning Logic at U14
Sessions follow a Croatian learning sequence, where:
- understanding emerges through directional game situations
- players learn by experiencing problems repeatedly
- early conditions may support recognition and success
- realism increases gradually through pressure and numbers
- coach language shifts from encouragement toward expectation and accountability
The aim is not perfect execution, but shared understanding and reliable behaviour inside game moments.
How the Session Progresses at U14
Across the session:
- players move from comfort toward responsibility
- decisions become faster and more consequential
- assistance is reduced progressively
- players are expected to adjust without constant guidance
The coach still designs the environment, but now also:
- demands attention
- reinforces standards
- holds players accountable for decisions
The session always follows three connected phases, which remain consistent across the pathway.
Phase 1 – Play with the Ball
This phase prepares players physically and cognitively while setting expectations for focus, intensity, and awareness.
At U14, this phase:
- is still ball-based and continuous
- includes interaction with teammates
- introduces direction and purpose
- demands higher concentration than U13
The focus is on:
- quality of movement
- awareness of others
- readiness to engage
Early advantages (space, time, or numbers) may be used to help players recognise situations, not to organise structure.
This phase is not about positioning or tactics.It is about preparing players to think and act within the game.
Coaches should:
- set standards early
- reinforce focus and intent
- keep communication short and clear
Phase 2 – Learning Game
This is the core learning phase of the session.
Players are placed in directional, game-based situations that require them to:
- coordinate actions with teammates
- recognise advantage or pressure
- manage distances, angles, and timing
Typical interactions involve:
- small groups (2–4 players)
- realistic spacing
- natural transitions
Overloads may still be used to support understanding, but they are reduced progressively.Pressure increases through opposition, time, or consequence.
The focus is on:
- shared decision-making
- responsibility for actions
- maintaining quality as realism increases
The coach’s role is to:
- observe behaviours
- reinforce responsibility
- correct attention and intent, not solutions
Players are expected to solve problems together.
Phase 3 – Football Game
The session finishes with a free or lightly conditioned football game.
This phase tests whether learning transfers into:
- realistic pressure
- natural transitions
- spontaneous decision-making
Numbers move closer to even, and conditions are simplified to reflect real football.
Coach intervention is minimal.The coach observes:
- how players recognise situations
- how they adjust collectively
- how consistently behaviours appear
This phase confirms learning through behaviour, not explanation.
Coach Guidance (U14)
At U14, effective coaching means:
- shifting responsibility onto the players
- expecting attention and effort
- allowing mistakes, but not disengagement
- valuing recognition and coordination over outcomes
The coach no longer leads learning —the game does.

heading: 9. U14 Correction & Feedback Language
headingPath: COACH PACK 3 (U14) – TACTICAL FOUNDATION PHASE > 9. U14 Correction & Feedback Language
content:
Purpose
Correction now focuses on collective understanding — spacing, coordination, and timing.
Croatians see this phase as “teaching the lines to speak.”
The coach’s goal is to ensure the team moves as one unit and reads cues in rhythm.
U14 Correction Philosophy (Croatian Model)
Type of Feedback | When Used | Croatian Approach | Belco Example Language
Instant (Trigger) | During match flow; maintain collective awareness. | One-word cues guide shape. | “Compact!” “Step!” “Reset!”
Freeze & Correct Shape | When lines lose spacing or connection. | Freeze whole unit, reset distances, replay. | “Hold — look at lines. Compact now. Go again.”
Guided Reflection | Half-time or after phase of play. | Ask lines how they could control rhythm better. | “Where was our open ball?” “Were we too high?”
Reinforce Success | When compactness or press timing works. | Name the trigger and the success. | “Perfect — open ball, you pressed as three.”
Collective Reminder | After repeated spacing or tempo issue. | Address all lines. | “Team — control our distances. Compact means together.”
Croatian Correction Principles (U14)
1️⃣ Correct the group, not the individual.
2️⃣ Language = trigger. One word, clear meaning.
3️⃣ Recreate with rhythm. Always replay after correction.
4️⃣ Coach positionally. Stand behind team to see spacing.
Belco–Croatian Common Coaching Language (U14 Level)
Action Type | Correction Cue | Positive Reinforcement
Build-Up | “Open!” “Play through!” | “Good — calm under pressure.”
Compactness | “Hold!” “Tighter!” | “Nice — line stayed together.”
Pressing | “Closed — step!” “Fast five!” | “Excellent trigger reaction.”
Tempo Control | “Return ball — calm!” “Circulate!” | “Good reset — we controlled it.”
Transition | “React!” “Compact to attack!” | “Love that — quick regain.”
Mentality | “Stay patient.” “Read it.” | “Perfect — calm before fast.”
Tone & Delivery
- Voice: calm, directive, neutral tone.
- Timing: use silence as a tool — let players solve before speaking.
- Correction: prioritise group tempo over individual mistake.
- Reinforcement: name the trigger that worked — it builds pattern recognition.
“At this age, coaching is no longer energy — it’s control.”
That’s the Croatian coaching identity from U14 upward: clarity, tempo, and tactical calm.

heading: 10. U14 Game Day Guidance
headingPath: COACH PACK 3 (U14) – TACTICAL FOUNDATION PHASE > 10. U14 Game Day Guidance
content:
Purpose
The match becomes the tactical laboratory.
Players must now apply what they trained — positional structure, compactness, pressing cues, and tempo management.
Croatian coaches treat the match as the test of organisation, not the test of emotion.
U14 Game Day Objectives
Element | Guidance
Game Intent | Apply tactical principles: build with structure, defend compact, press with purpose. If the week’s focus was Build-Up from the Back, your match focus is “Can we find our open-ball triggers and progress through the thirds with shape?”
Coach Voice | Use collective trigger words: “Open!”, “Compact!”, “Press!”, “Return!”. Avoid long sentences. Let triggers replace shouting.
Player Guidance | Encourage communication across lines. Midfield leads tempo, back line leads compactness.
Substitution Focus | Tactical rotation — players learn positional relationships, not just minutes.
Half-Time Reflection | Ask 2 questions:
 | 1️⃣ “Did we stay compact?”
 | 2️⃣ “When did we control the tempo?”
Coach Behaviour | Calm, directive, minimal emotion. Correct structure, not result. Use voice to maintain rhythm and shape.
U14 Croatian Matchday Principles
1️⃣ “Organisation before emotion.” – compactness and tempo first.2️⃣ “Fewer words, stronger triggers.” – one trigger replaces a sentence.3️⃣ “Coach from the game, not above it.” – stand where you can read structure, not near the ball.
Example Match Dialogue
“Compact — step!”
“Open ball — stretch!”
“Closed — hold!”
“Return — reset rhythm!”
“Fast five — go press!”
The voice becomes short, rhythmic, and collective — this is the language of tactical football.

heading: U14 Coach Matchday Standards
headingPath: COACH PACK 3 (U14) – TACTICAL FOUNDATION PHASE > 10. U14 Game Day Guidance > U14 Coach Matchday Standards
content:
Purpose
The coach sets the tone for the match environment.Professional detail builds credibility.Players mirror what they see — composure, preparation, and precision.
Coach Matchday Standards
APPEARANCE
Element | Standard
Belconnen Attire | Official Belconnen polo or approved top. Unified, consistent look across staff.
Footwear | Soccer footwear during warm-up and match. We look like football people, not spectators.
Sunglasses | Remove when speaking to players. Eye contact is essential for message absorption and connection.
Presentation | Neat, professional, deliberate. Our unified appearance signals control and precision.
GEAR
Item | Expectation
Balls | Pumped appropriately before arrival at pitch.
Bibs | Organised and accessible.
Cones | Blue and white matchday cones set and ready.
PREPAREDNESS
Element | Expectation
Substitution Plan | Rotation structure pre-planned. Know your intent before kick-off.
Team Talk | Clear theme linked to weekly focus. No improvisation.
Arrival Time | Minimum 45–60 minutes before kick-off. Field checked and organised.
Sideline Behaviour
Element | Standard
Coach Voice | Calm, clear, rhythmic.
Emotional Control | No visible frustration or emotional reactions.
Referee Interaction | Respectful at all times. Model behaviour.
Opposition Interaction | Professional greeting pre and post match.
Player Interaction | Eye contact, short cues, consistent language from training.
Parent Interaction | Friendly but brief. No tactical debate during game.
Belco Matchday Identity
1️⃣ Calm voice, clear cues.2️⃣ Detail before emotion.3️⃣ Teach relationships, not results.4️⃣ Represent the program with pride.

heading: U14 Pre-Match Warm-Up
headingPath: COACH PACK 3 (U14) – TACTICAL FOUNDATION PHASE > 10. U14 Game Day Guidance > U14 Pre-Match Warm-Up
content:
PRE-MATCH WARM-UP (25–28 MINUTES TOTAL)
Objective: Prepare physically and mentally for tactical execution — compactness, build-up rhythm, pressing cues.
The pre-match warm-up is not fitness, not motivation, and not teaching.
Its job is to prepare players to execute what they already know with calm, clarity, and control.
Warm-ups should:
- progress from individual activation to collective organisation
- reflect how the team intends to play
- introduce the ball early
- become quieter and more player-led as kick-off approaches
Coaches use the warm-up to observe emotional tempo and spacing, not to run drills or give speeches.If the warm-up is loud, rushed, or emotional, it has missed its purpose.
Block | Duration | Focus | Example Activity | Coach Cues / Tone
1️⃣ Dynamic Movement & Activation | 5 mins | Mobility and control of pace. | Jog–skip–acceleration flow; lateral work, hip mobility, short sprints. | “Set tempo early.” “Move together.”
2️⃣ Technical Activation (Ball Each) | 4-5 mins | Touch, body orientation, scanning. | 1 ball each: turns, inside–outside control, quick first touch and play forward. | “Clean touch.” “Eyes up.”
3️⃣ Positional Rondos (by line) | 5-6 mins | Compact support and triggers. | Two 5v2 rondos — one for defenders + mids, one for mids + forwards. | “Compact shape.” “Calm → fast.”
4️⃣ Pattern or Build-Up Sequence | 5–6 mins | Positional connection and open-ball triggers. | 7v4 or 6v3 build-up rehearsal to target player. | “Open ball — stretch!” “Return — calm!”
5️⃣ Directional Game or Press Trigger Drill | 5 mins | Press shape, first five seconds intensity. | 5v5 to mini goals; when ball lost, 5second press rule. | “Fast five!” “Compact!” “Press together.”
Detail:
- Coach tone = quiet, steady, precise.
- Warm-up ends with tactical rhythm: a few repetitions of the press trigger or build-up pattern.
- Assistant leads physical prep; head coach observes team organisation — already coaching compactness visually.
“The warm-up is the first rehearsal of the match structure.”

heading: U14 Pre-Match Talk
headingPath: COACH PACK 3 (U14) – TACTICAL FOUNDATION PHASE > 10. U14 Game Day Guidance > U14 Pre-Match Talk
content:
PRE-MATCH TALK (5–6 MINUTES)
Purpose
Set tactical clarity and calm focus.
At U14+, Croatian coaches expect players to act on shared triggers rather than receive instruction. Players must leave the talk knowing when to act and as a unit.
Structure
Step | Coach Focus | Example Language (Croatian tactical tone)
1️⃣ Calm Gather | Stand behind formation board, low tone. | “Eyes up — calm start. One picture only today.”
2️⃣ State Tactical Focus | Name the collective principle for this match. | “Today we build through, stay compact, and press on the closed ball.”
3️⃣ Give 2–3 Unit Triggers | Clear triggers or positional links. | “Open ball — we stretch.” “Closed — step.” “Return ball — calm again.”
4️⃣ Behaviour Standard | Discipline and collective action. | “We stay connected — every metre matters.”
5️⃣ End with Rhythm, Not Emotion | Calm finish and reset. | “Calm start, press together, trust our rhythm.”
Observed Croatian Pre-Match Tendencies (U14+)
1️⃣ “Clarity replaces motivation.”2️⃣ “Trigger language replaces shouting.”3️⃣ “Coach from rhythm, not emotion.”
Coach tone: calm, serious, controlled — like a conductor before a performance.

heading: U14 Half-Time Talk
headingPath: COACH PACK 3 (U14) – TACTICAL FOUNDATION PHASE > 10. U14 Game Day Guidance > U14 Half-Time Talk
content:
HALF-TIME TALK (3–5 MINUTES)
Purpose
Evaluate compactness, spacing, and pressing behaviour — not results.
Croatian U14 coaches see half-time as a tactical reset where players reflect on their positional work.
Structure
Step | Coach Focus | Example Language (Croatian tactical tone)
1️⃣ Calm Entry | Sit, short silence — let energy settle. | “Breathe. Calm — look at me.”
2️⃣ One Positive Linked to Tactical Plan | Reinforce organisation or trigger use. | “Good first half — we built calmly and pressed well on the closed ball.”
3️⃣ One Correction (Collective) | Focus on line distances or timing. | “Our midfield was too far from defence — close faster when we lose it.”
4️⃣ Guided Reflection | Encourage recognition of cues. | “When was our best press — open ball or closed?”
5️⃣ Finish Calm & Confident | Reset collective focus. | “Stay compact, press together, trust tempo — calm start again.”
Half-Time Rules (U14)
Rule | Meaning for Coach
1️⃣ Correct structure, not emotion. | Focus on spacing, not mistakes.
2️⃣ Use trigger language. | “Open,” “Closed,” “Return,” “Compact.”
3️⃣ Let silence do half the work. | Calm tone regulates team energy.
4️⃣ End with one tactical focus. | Players leave knowing exactly what behaviour matters.
Example Complete U14 Half-Time Talk
“Good half — we built calmly and stayed compact.
When we lost it, our lines opened — next half, we close quicker.
Remember, closed ball = press, open ball = stretch.
Calm start, control the game, stay connected.”
Coach Behaviour Summary
Aspect | Croatian Standard for U14
Voice | Calm, low, directive; no hype.
Body Language | Upright, minimal gestures; use hand for compactness cues.
Focus | Organisation, spacing, triggers.
Timing | 3–4 mins pre-match, 3–5 mins half-time.
Consistency | Same language across week and game.
Key Takeaway:
“At U14, correction and communication move from technique to tactics.
The coach no longer creates energy — they regulate it through rhythm, tone, and triggers.”

heading: U14 Pre-Match Routine - Timeline
headingPath: COACH PACK 3 (U14) – TACTICAL FOUNDATION PHASE > 10. U14 Game Day Guidance > U14 Pre-Match Routine - Timeline
content:
Reference: All timings work backwards from KO
Time (from KO) | Moment | Coach Focus | Coach Behaviour Standard | Key Reminder
KO–60 | Arrival | Settle & organise | Calm presence · logistics only · no football talk | Emotional state first
KO–50 | Change / Prep | Individual routine | Minimal voice · observe only | Not a coaching window
KO–45 | Main Team Talk | Tactical clarity | One picture · shared language · calm tone | Clarity > motivation Less than 6mins
KO–40 | Warm-Up Begins | Activate & reinforce | Reduce voice · use constraints · players take over | Movement reinforces ideas
KO–10 | Final Reminder | Trigger recall | One sentence · one trigger · then silence | Cue, not a talk
KO–4 | Leave Change Room | Emotional control | No talking · calm body language | Restraint = authority
KO | Kick-Off | Observe & manage | Correct standards only · no constant instruction | Players control the game
NON-NEGOTIABLE WARNINGS (FOR COACHES)
Warning Sign | What It Means
Repeating yourself | You’re talking too long
Adding late information | It should have been coached earlier
Emotional build-up | Clarity is being lost
Coaching constantly after KO | Players aren’t owning the game
CROATIAN MATCHDAY PRINCIPLES (REMEMBER)
- Calm beats intensity
- Structure beats speeches
- Triggers beat shouting
- Restraint signals authority
Pre-match talks don’t add ideas — they remove doubt.

heading: U14 Pre-Match Routine - Detail
headingPath: COACH PACK 3 (U14) – TACTICAL FOUNDATION PHASE > 10. U14 Game Day Guidance > U14 Pre-Match Routine - Detail
content:
PURPOSE OF MATCHDAY ROUTINE
- Create calm, clarity, and readiness
- Reinforce shared tactical language
- Protect players from over-coaching
- Ensure the match is a performance moment, not a teaching session
Training does the teaching.Matchday protects execution.
MATCHDAY FLOW (KO-BASED)
🕒 KO–60 MINUTES | ARRIVAL
Focus: Settle, organise, decompress
Coach Behaviour
- Calm presence
- No tactical talk
- Logistics only (kit, hydration, admin)
Croatian Logic
- Players must arrive emotionally neutral
- No football information before they are ready to receive it
🕒 KO–50 MINUTES | CHANGE / PREP
Focus: Individual routine
Coach Behaviour
- Minimal voice
- Observe, don’t instruct
- Allow players to settle into their own rhythm
Standard
- This is not a coaching window
🕒 KO–45 MINUTES | MAIN TEAM TALK (BRIEF)
Focus: Tactical clarity, not motivation
Coach Behaviour
- Calm tone
- One tactical picture only
- Use shared language (already trained)
- No emotional build-up
Content Rules
- 1 collective focus
- 2–3 known triggers (maximum)
- No new information
- No individuals singled out
Croatian Principle
Clarity replaces motivation.
🕒 KO–40 MINUTES | WARM-UP BEGINS (≈25 MIN)
Focus: Physical + cognitive activation
Coach Behaviour
- Minimal instruction
- Reinforce ideas through constraints, not voice
- Gradually hand control to players
Standard
- Coaching volume decreases as KO approaches
- Final phase of warm-up is player-led
🕒 KO–10 MINUTES | FINAL REMINDER (≈1 MIN)
Focus: Trigger recall only
Coach Behaviour
- One sentence
- One reminder
- Calm delivery
Example
“Closed ball — we go together.”
Then silence.
Croatian Logic
- This is a cue, not a talk
🕒 KO–4 MINUTES | LEAVE CHANGE ROOM
Focus: Emotional control
Coach Behaviour
- No talking
- Calm body language
- Observe only
🕒 KO | KICK-OFF
Coach Role
- Observe
- Correct only when standards drop
- No constant instruction
If the coach is controlling the game verbally, the players are not.
MATCHDAY WARNINGS (NON-NEGOTIABLE)
For Coaches
- If you repeat yourself, you are talking too long
- If you add information late, it should not have been added at all
- If emotion rises, clarity drops
Red Flags
- Long pre-match speeches
- Tactical changes introduced on matchday
- Emotional hype before KO
These are anti-development behaviours.
CROATIAN MATCHDAY PHILOSOPHY (SUMMARY)
- Calm beats intensity
- Structure beats speeches
- Triggers beat shouting
- Restraint signals authority
Pre-match talks don’t add ideas — they remove doubt.

heading: U14 Game Framework
headingPath: COACH PACK 3 (U14) – TACTICAL FOUNDATION PHASE > 10. U14 Game Day Guidance > U14 Game Framework
content:
Game Framework – U14+ (11-a-Side)
- Base Shape: 1-4-3-3
- Key Roles:
- Pivot: Control rhythm, connect lines.
- 8s/10s: Create overloads, run beyond.
- Wingers: Stretch, attack assist zone.
- Back Four: Build triangles, defend compact.
- Team Instructions:
- Play through, not around.
- Open-ball = go forward.
- Compact when we lose it.
- Tempo control = identity.

heading: Individual Competencies
headingPath: COACH PACK 3 (U14) – TACTICAL FOUNDATION PHASE > 11. U14 Phase Outcomes > Individual Competencies
content:
Theme: Applying technique to team structure and tactical roles.
By the end of this phase, the player can:
- Build from the back using correct positioning and body shape.
- Find and use the pivot under pressure.
- Read pressing cues (open / closed / return ball).
- Execute third-man combinations with awareness of timing.
- Maintain positional discipline and compact team shape.
- Defend with awareness of line distances and unit movement.
- Switch play with controlled tempo and precision.
- Finish from structured team attacks (cut-backs, second-line runs).
- Communicate and adjust based on teammates’ positions.
- Demonstrate calm execution and patience under pressure.
“Quality of technique now proves understanding of structure.”

heading: Group Competencies
headingPath: COACH PACK 3 (U14) – TACTICAL FOUNDATION PHASE > 11. U14 Phase Outcomes > Group Competencies
content:
Collective Habits
Objective: Establish team shape and coordinated unit work.
Team Competencies:
- Maintain compact distances between lines (8–12m vertically).
- Coordinate pressing triggers (closed ball, return ball).
- Understand team build-up structure (3+2 or 2+3 base).
- Recognise overload zones and exploit them.
- Switch play to opposite side under control.
- Counter-press immediately after loss (5-second rule).
- Recover into mid-block compactness as default defensive posture.
- Manage line balance — one steps, others cover.
- Combine fullback–winger–midfield rotations naturally.
- Defend transitions collectively, not individually.
“Organisation builds confidence — confidence builds rhythm.”

heading: 12. U14 Session plans – (examples/template)
headingPath: COACH PACK 3 (U14) – TACTICAL FOUNDATION PHASE > 12. U14 Session plans – (examples/template)
content:
U11 examples below.
Session Outcomes
Players gain confidence to attack 1v1 | Beat opponents | Keep possession under pressure.
Activation/Intro (8-10mins)
Setup & Description | Coaching Focus | 
4 cones per player in one line. Mirror Moves – pairs face each other; one leads feints, the other mirrors. Swap every 30 s. Players have balls, move sideways along the line, left and right. If you don’t have enough cones use 2 or 3, and ask the players to stop after 1m | • Stay balanced, light on feet • Eyes up – read the body | 
Technical/Positional (15-20mins)
Setup & Description | Coaching Focus | 
10x10m area 1v1 lanes (8 × 15 m). Player A attacks small gate vs defender B. Quick rotation. Can have players in pairs and they take turns defending. Split across two areas. | • First touch forward • Feint then explode • Protect the ball | 
Tactical/Main (20-25mins)
Setup & Description | Coaching Focus | 
30 × 25 m. Field divided in thirds. 4v4 + 4 neutrals Score by dribbling through mini-goals. Encourage isolated 1v1s. 2 neutrals on the sides at halfway, and 2 at the end of the field between mini-goals. | • Recognise 1v1 moments • Be brave to face forward • Use body disguise | 
Transfer/End Game (10-15mins)
Setup & Description | Coaching Focus | 
40 x 30 m, goals at each end. 6v6 free game – remove zones every 5 min to increase freedom. Look for: • Facing forward on first touch. • Using feint to break line. • | • Observe use of feints • Encourage confidence and creativity | 
Element | Detail
Objective / Outcomes | Improve confidence in 1v1 situations and ability to beat defenders with controlled body movement and quick changes of direction.
Technical Detail | • Forward touch to attack space • Low centre of gravity • Explosive cut after feint • Protect ball with arm and body angle.
Coaching Messages | “Face them – don’t hide.” “Beat the player, then protect the ball.”
Common Mistakes & Fixes | • Too close to defender → create space first. • No explosion → shorter touch then burst. • Eyes down → head up before move.
Praise Language | “Nice disguise – froze the defender.” “Great burst – took space with purpose.”
Behaviours to Reward | • Facing forward under pressure • Using feints with intent • Immediate reaction after losing the ball.
Reflection Question | “What makes a defender move first – your touch or your eyes?”
Session Outcomes
Activation/Intro (8-10mins)
Setup & Description | Coaching Focus | 
 | • • | 
Technical/Positional (15-20mins)
Setup & Description | Coaching Focus | 
 | • | 
Tactical/Main (20-25mins)
Setup & Description | Coaching Focus | 
 | • |

Setup & Description | Coaching Focus | 
 | • | 
Element | Detail
Objective / Outcomes | Improve confidence in 1v1 situations and ability to beat defenders with controlled body movement and quick changes of direction.
Technical Detail | • Forward touch to attack space • Low centre of gravity • Explosive cut after feint • Protect ball with arm and body angle.
Coaching Messages | “Face them – don’t hide.” “Beat the player, then protect the ball.”
Common Mistakes & Fixes | • Too close to defender → create space first. • No explosion → shorter touch then burst. • Eyes down → head up before move.

heading: U14 sessions
headingPath: U14 sessions
content:
U14 – CYCLE 1Build-Up from the Back (4 Weeks)
Week 1• Session 1 – GK + Backline Triangles: Calm Start Under Press• Session 2 – Open Ball Recognition
Week 2• Session 1 – Support & Depth: Midfielder Drops In• Session 2 – Pivot Links Play
Week 3• Session 1 – Press-Resistance Games: Play Through the First Line• Session 2 – Back → Mid Connection
Week 4• Session 1 – Full-Team Build-Up Game: Build to Beat the Press• Session 2 – Rhythm and Control

heading: U14 Cycle 1, week 1, session 1
headingPath: U14 sessions > U14 Cycle 1, week 1, session 1
content:
BELCONNEN UNITED FC – SESSION PLAN | CYCLE 1 | WEEK 1 | SESSION 1
(Segoe UI 9 pt grey text, Belco-blue underline)
U14 | Build-Up from the Back | Week 1 | GK + Backline Triangles: Calm Start Under Press
Session Outcomes
Build play calmly from the back by connecting GK and backline in triangles under controlled pressure.
Activation / Intro (10–15 mins)
Setup
- Area: 30x25m, starting zone near one goal
- Players: GK + 3 defenders vs 2 pressers
- Equipment: Balls, cones, 2 mini goals positioned wide
Description
- Play always starts with the GK.
- Backline + GK circulate the ball to keep possession.
- Pressers apply controlled pressure.
- Defenders score by playing into either wide mini goal.
- Key rule: GK must be involved in every sequence.
Coaching Focus
- Calm first touch and body shape
- Creating angles to support the GK
- Decision-making under light pressure
Progression
- Add a third presser
- Limit defenders to 2 touches
Technical / Small-Group (15–25 mins)
Setup
- Area: 40x30m, directional
- Players: GK + 3v3
- Targets: Two mini goals wide or end zones
Description
- Play starts with the GK.
- Backline must create triangles to play out under pressure.
- Pressing team looks to win and score quickly.
- On success, defenders progress the ball into a wide target.
- Rotate roles regularly.
Coaching Focus
- Distances between GK and defenders
- Angles of support (behind, side, forward)
- Playing away from pressure when possible
Progression
- Add a central target player beyond the press
- Increase pressing intensity
Tactical / Group Game (20–30 mins)
Setup
- Area: 55x40m, full width
- Players: 7v7 + GK
- Goals: Normal goals
Description
- All play starts with the GK.
- Team scores double if they build out through the backline and progress into midfield under control.
- Opposition presses realistically but without constant high press.
Coaching Focus
- Team structure in build-up
- GK as an extra player
- Patience vs forcing play
Progression
- Remove scoring incentive
- Allow free pressing
Transfer / End Game (15–25 mins)
Setup
- Directional game, maximum 7v7
- Full width, appropriate length
- Normal rules
Description
- Free play with minimal intervention.
- Coach observes build-up behaviour from the GK under pressure.
Coaching Focus
- Players offering clear angles early
- Calm decisions in the first phase
- Collective confidence to play out
Coaching Information (U14)
Element | Detail
Objective / Outcomes | Team builds calmly from the back using GK-backline triangles
Technical / Tactical Detail | Body shape, passing angles, spacing, decision-making under press
Coaching Messages (Belco Voice) | “Stay calm.” • “Give an angle.” • “Use the GK.”
Common Mistakes & Fixes | Hiding from the ball → “Show early.” • Forced passes → “Reset and recycle.”
Praise Language | “Good calm build.” • “That’s brave football.”
Behaviours to Reward | Composure, communication, bravery, support play
Reflection Question | “How did we help the GK stay calm?”
Session Design Note | Early overloads support confidence before realism increases

content:
BELCONNEN UNITED FC – SESSION PLAN | CYCLE 1 | WEEK 1 | SESSION 2
(Segoe UI 9 pt grey text, Belco-blue underline)
U14 | Build-Up from the Back | Week 1 | Open Ball Recognition
Session Outcomes
Recognise the open ball in the first phase and use body shape and scanning to play forward under pressure.
Activation / Intro (10–15 mins)
Setup
- Area: 30x25m, build-up zone near one goal
- Players: GK + 3 defenders vs 2 pressers
- Equipment: Balls, cones, 2 mini goals positioned wide
Description
- Play starts with the GK every time.
- Backline circulates under light pressure.
- Defenders score by playing into a wide mini goal when the receiver is on the open ball.
- Key rule: Receiver must scan before receiving; first touch must face away from pressure.
Coaching Focus
- Scanning before the ball arrives
- Open body shape to see both sides
- Calm first touch under pressure
Progression
- Add a third presser
- Limit defenders to 2 touches
Technical / Small-Group (15–25 mins)
Setup
- Area: 40x30m, directional
- Players: GK + 3v3
- Targets: Two wide mini goals or end zones
Description
- Play starts with the GK.
- Backline must recognise when a defender is free on the open side.
- Objective is to receive on the open ball and play forward or wide.
- Pressing team tries to lock one side.
- Rotate roles regularly.
Coaching Focus
- Checking shoulders before receiving
- First touch away from pressure
- Decision: play forward, wide, or reset
Progression
- Add a central target beyond the press
- Increase pressing intensity
Tactical / Group Game (20–30 mins)
Setup
- Area: 55x40m, full width
- Players: 7v7 + GK
- Goals: Normal goals
Description
- All play starts with the GK.
- Team scores double if they:
- Build out from the back
- Find a player receiving on the open ball
- Progress into midfield under control
- Opposition presses realistically.
Coaching Focus
- Team shape to create open-ball options
- GK positioning to support switches
- Patience to wait for the open side
Progression
- Remove scoring incentive
- Free pressing
Transfer / End Game (15–25 mins)
Setup
- Directional game, maximum 7v7
- Full width, appropriate length
- Normal rules
Description
- Free play with minimal intervention.
- Coach observes whether players naturally scan and receive on the open ball in build-up.
Coaching Focus
- Defenders opening body shape
- GK supporting circulation
- Confidence to play forward when open
Coaching Information (U14)
Element | Detail
Objective / Outcomes | Team recognises and uses open-ball moments in build-up
Technical / Tactical Detail | Scanning, body shape, first touch direction, decision speed
Coaching Messages (Belco Voice) | “Scan early.” • “Open up.” • “Play what you see.”
Common Mistakes & Fixes | Closed body → “Open your hips.” • Rushed pass → “Take the touch.”
Praise Language | “Great scan.” • “That open touch helped us play.”
Behaviours to Reward | Awareness, bravery to receive, calm decisions
Reflection Question | “What did you see before the ball arrived?”
Session Design Note | Reduced pressure supports scanning habits before full realism
Language Note | Reinforce Belco terms: open ball, scan, build-up

content:
BELCONNEN UNITED FC – SESSION PLAN | CYCLE 1 | WEEK 2 | SESSION 1
(Segoe UI 9 pt grey text, Belco-blue underline)
U14 | Build-Up from the Back | Week 2 | Support & Depth: Midfielder Drops In
Session Outcomes
Build out from the back with calm support and depth by using a midfielder dropping in to connect and progress under pressure.
Activation / Intro (10–15 mins)
Setup
- Area: 30x25m build-up zone near one goal
- Players: GK + back three + 1 midfielder vs 3 pressers
- Equipment: Balls, cones, 2 mini goals positioned wide
Description
- Play always starts with the GK.
- Midfielder begins higher and can drop in between or beside defenders to support.
- Defenders score by playing into either wide mini goal.
- Key rule: A goal only counts if the midfielder drops to receive during the build-up.
Coaching Focus
- Timing of the midfielder’s drop
- Creating depth in front of the GK
- Calm circulation under light pressure
Progression
- Add an extra presser
- Limit players to 2 touches
Technical / Small-Group (15–25 mins)
Setup
- Area: 40x30m, directional
- Players: GK + 3 defenders + 1 midfielder vs 4 pressers
- Targets: Two wide mini goals or end zones
Description
- Build-up starts with the GK.
- Midfielder must:
- Drop to offer a forward option
- Or stay high to stretch the press
- Objective is to play through or around the press using depth support.
- Rotate the midfielder role regularly.
Coaching Focus
- Recognising when to drop vs stay
- Body shape to receive facing forward
- Distances between GK, defenders, and midfielder
Progression
- Add a central target beyond the press
- Increase pressing intensity
Tactical / Group Game (20–30 mins)
Setup
- Area: 55x40m, full width
- Players: 7v7 + GK
- Goals: Normal goals
Description
- All play starts with the GK.
- Team scores double if they:
- Build out using a midfielder dropping in
- Progress the ball into midfield under control
- Opposition presses realistically.
Coaching Focus
- Team structure in first phase
- Midfielder creating depth and connection
- Patience to recycle when forward play isn’t on
Progression
- Remove scoring incentive
- Free pressing
Transfer / End Game (15–25 mins)
Setup
- Directional game, maximum 7v7
- Full width, appropriate length
- Normal rules
Description
- Free play with minimal intervention.
- Coach observes whether midfielders naturally drop to support build-up.
Coaching Focus
- Midfielder’s awareness of space and timing
- GK and defenders recognising support options
- Collective calmness under pressure
Coaching Information (U14)
Element | Detail
Objective / Outcomes | Team builds with support and depth through a dropping midfielder
Technical / Tactical Detail | Timing of drop, spacing, body shape, connection between lines
Coaching Messages (Belco Voice) | “Give depth.” • “Drop to help.” • “Connect us.”
Common Mistakes & Fixes | Midfielder dropping too early → “Wait for the moment.” • Flat build-up → “Create depth.”
Praise Language | “Great support.” • “That drop opened the game.”
Behaviours to Reward | Awareness, bravery to receive, communication, composure
Reflection Question | “When did dropping in help us play forward?”
Session Design Note | Structured support builds confidence before full freedom
Language Note | Reinforce Belco terms: support, depth, build-up

content:
BELCONNEN UNITED FC – SESSION PLAN | CYCLE 1 | WEEK 2 | SESSION 2
(Segoe UI 9 pt grey text, Belco-blue underline)
U14 | Build-Up from the Back | Week 2 | Pivot Links Play
Session Outcomes
Build out from the back by using the pivot to connect defence to midfield and play forward under pressure.
Activation / Intro (10–15 mins)
Setup
- Area: 30x25m build-up zone near one goal
- Players: GK + 3 defenders + 1 pivot vs 3 pressers
- Equipment: Balls, cones, 2 mini goals positioned wide
Description
- All play starts with the GK.
- Pivot starts ahead of the backline and can move freely to receive.
- Defenders score by playing into either wide mini goal via the pivot.
- Key rule: The pivot must receive at least one pass in every build-up sequence.
Coaching Focus
- Pivot’s movement to find space between lines
- Body shape to receive and play forward
- Calm circulation to draw pressure before connecting inside
Progression
- Add a fourth presser
- Limit the pivot to 2 touches
Technical / Small-Group (15–25 mins)
Setup
- Area: 40x30m, directional
- Players: GK + 3 defenders + 1 pivot vs 4 pressers
- Targets: Two wide mini goals or end zones
Description
- Build-up begins with the GK.
- Defenders circulate to create a passing lane into the pivot.
- Pivot must:
- Receive on the half-turn when possible
- Play forward, wide, or reset depending on pressure
- Pressing team looks to block central access.
- Rotate the pivot role regularly.
Coaching Focus
- Timing of the pass into the pivot
- Pivot scanning before receiving
- Distances and angles supporting the pivot after the first pass
Progression
- Add a central target beyond the press
- Increase pressing intensity and realism
Tactical / Group Game (20–30 mins)
Setup
- Area: 55x40m, full width
- Players: 7v7 + GK
- Goals: Normal goals
Description
- All restarts begin with the GK.
- Team scores double if they:
- Build through the pivot
- Progress the ball into midfield under control
- Opposition presses realistically, looking to lock central spaces.
Coaching Focus
- Team shape to support the pivot
- Pivot decision-making under pressure
- Patience to recycle when the pivot is marked
Progression
- Remove scoring incentive
- Allow full free pressing
Transfer / End Game (15–25 mins)
Setup
- Directional game, maximum 7v7
- Full width, appropriate length
- Normal rules
Description
- Free play with minimal intervention.
- Coach observes whether players naturally look to connect through the pivot in build-up.
Coaching Focus
- Pivot constantly checking shoulders
- Defenders recognising when to play inside vs wide
- Collective confidence to play through pressure
Coaching Information (U14)
Element | Detail
Objective / Outcomes | Team connects build-up to midfield consistently through the pivot
Technical / Tactical Detail | Scanning, body shape, pass timing, support angles
Coaching Messages (Belco Voice) | “Find the pivot.” • “Play through us.” • “Open up.”
Common Mistakes & Fixes | Forced central pass → “Move it first.” • Pivot marked → “Recycle and re-open.”
Praise Language | “Great connection.” • “That pivot pass broke pressure.”
Behaviours to Reward | Awareness, bravery to receive, calm decisions, support play
Reflection Question | “When was the right moment to play into the pivot?”
Session Design Note | Using the pivot builds connection before expanding the game model
Language Note | Reinforce Belco terms: pivot, connect, build-up

content:
BELCONNEN UNITED FC – SESSION PLAN | CYCLE 1 | WEEK 3 | SESSION 1
(Segoe UI 9 pt grey text, Belco-blue underline)
U14 | Build-Up from the Back | Week 3 | Press Resistance Games: Play Through First Line
Session Outcomes
Play through the opponent’s first pressing line with calm decisions, strong support angles, and collective bravery.
Activation / Intro (10–15 mins)
Setup
- Area: 30x25m build-up zone near one goal
- Players: GK + 4 outfield vs 3 pressers
- Equipment: Balls, cones, 2 mini goals positioned beyond the press
Description
- Play always starts with the GK.
- Possession team aims to play through the first line of pressure.
- Defenders score by finding a teammate beyond the press into either mini goal.
- Key rule: At least one pass must be played through the central lane before scoring.
Coaching Focus
- Calmness under pressure
- Creating angles behind and beside the ball
- Recognising when to play through vs reset
Progression
- Add a fourth presser
- Limit build-up players to 2 touches
Technical / Small-Group (15–25 mins)
Setup
- Area: 40x30m, directional
- Players: GK + 4v4
- Targets: End zone or mini goals beyond the press
Description
- Build-up begins with the GK.
- Pressing team actively looks to lock the ball side.
- Possession team must:
- Move the press
- Create a free player
- Play through the first line into the target zone
- Rotate pressing and possession roles regularly.
Coaching Focus
- Body shape to receive under pressure
- Timing of support movements
- Speed of decision once the press is beaten
Progression
- Reduce space between lines
- Allow pressing team to counter immediately on regain
Tactical / Group Game (20–30 mins)
Setup
- Area: 55x40m, full width
- Players: 7v7 + GK
- Goals: Normal goals
Description
- All restarts begin with the GK.
- Team scores double if they:
- Play through the first pressing line
- Progress into midfield under control
- Opposition presses with game-realistic intensity.
Coaching Focus
- Team shape in first phase
- Collective bravery to play through pressure
- Support arriving quickly after the line is broken
Progression
- Remove scoring incentive
- Free pressing and free build-up
Transfer / End Game (15–25 mins)
Setup
- Directional game, maximum 7v7
- Full width, appropriate length
- Normal rules
Description
- Free play with minimal intervention.
- Coach observes whether players naturally look to break the first line rather than go long.
Coaching Focus
- Confidence to receive under pressure
- Teammates offering clear support angles
- Calm reactions after mistakes
Coaching Information (U14)
Element | Detail
Objective / Outcomes | Team consistently plays through the first pressing line
Technical / Tactical Detail | Body shape, pass timing, support angles, decision speed
Coaching Messages (Belco Voice) | “Play through.” • “Give an angle.” • “Be brave.”
Common Mistakes & Fixes | Panic clearance → “Reset and support.” • Flat support → “Change your angle.”
Praise Language | “That broke the press.” • “Brave to play.”
Behaviours to Reward | Composure, bravery, communication, support
Reflection Question | “What helped us play through the press?”
Session Design Note | Directional games create realistic pressure and decision-making
Language Note | Reinforce Belco terms: press resistance, support, play through

Session Outcomes
Connect the backline to midfield under pressure, progressing play through central connections rather than bypassing lines.

Setup

- Players: GK + 3 defenders + 2 midfielders vs 3 pressers

Description

- Backline circulates to draw pressure.
- Midfielders must move to receive between or just beyond the first pressing line.
- Defenders score by playing into a midfielder who then finds a mini goal.
- Key rule: A goal only counts if the ball travels back → mid through a central connection.
Coaching Focus
- Midfielders scanning and adjusting position
- Timing of the pass from the back
- Body shape to receive facing forward
Progression
- Add an extra presser
- Limit midfielders to 2 touches

Setup

- Players: GK + 3 defenders + 2 midfielders vs 4 pressers
- Targets: End zones or mini goals beyond midfield line

- Pressing team locks one side or central lane.

- Move the press
- Find the right moment to connect into midfield
- Midfielders can receive:
- On the half-turn
- Or bounce and re-position to receive again
- Rotate midfield roles regularly.
Coaching Focus
- Midfielder positioning between lines
- Pass weight and timing from the back
- Support around the receiving midfielder
Progression
- Reduce space between lines
- Allow pressing team to counter immediately on regain

Setup

- Goals: Normal goals

- All restarts begin with the GK.
- Team scores double if they:
- Build from the back
- Connect into midfield under control
- Progress play forward rather than going long
- Opposition presses with realistic intensity.
Coaching Focus
- Team shape to support back → mid connections
- Midfielders offering angles at different heights
- Patience to recycle if midfield access is blocked
Progression
- Remove scoring incentive
- Fully free play

Setup
- Directional game, maximum 7v7
- Full width, appropriate length
- Normal rules
Description
- Free play with minimal intervention.
- Coach observes whether players naturally look to connect through midfield during build-up.
Coaching Focus
- Midfielders constantly checking shoulders
- Defenders recognising the right moment to play inside
- Collective confidence to play through pressure

Element | Detail
Objective / Outcomes | Team consistently connects build-up play from back into midfield
Technical / Tactical Detail | Midfielder positioning, scanning, pass timing, support angles

Common Mistakes & Fixes | Bypassing midfield → “Can we connect first?” • Static mids → “Move to be seen.”
Praise Language | “Great connection.” • “That broke the press.”
Behaviours to Reward | Bravery to receive, awareness, composure, communication

Build out from the back as a full team, calmly beating the press through collective structure, support, and intelligent decisions.

Description
- All play starts with the GK.
- Possession team builds out under controlled pressure.
- Pressing team aims to win and score quickly.
- Build-up team scores by playing into either mini goal beyond the press.
- Key rule: At least three players must be involved before progressing forward.
Coaching Focus

- Calm ball circulation under pressure
- Recognising when to play through, around, or reset

- Limit build-up players to 2 touches

Setup
- Area: 45x35m, directional
- Players: GK + 6v6
- Targets: End zones or mini goals beyond midfield line

- Opposition presses with realistic intensity.
- Possession team must use:
- GK support
- Backline width
- Midfield depth
- Objective is to progress through the press into the target zone.
- Rotate pressing and build-up roles.
Coaching Focus
- Creating overloads near the ball
- Using the free player
- Support arriving immediately after beating the press
Progression
- Reduce space between units
- Allow pressing team to counter immediately on regain

- Collective calmness and bravery

- Directional game, maximum 7v7

- Players recognising pressing cues
- Team maintaining spacing and support
- Confidence to build even after mistakes

Common Mistakes & Fixes | Panic clearance → “Reset and support.” • Static shape → “Move to help.”
Praise Language | “That was brave build-up.” • “Great team connection.”

content:
BELCONNEN UNITED FC – SESSION PLAN | CYCLE 1 | WEEK 4 | SESSION 2
(Segoe UI 9 pt grey text, Belco-blue underline)
U14 | Build-Up from the Back | Week 4 | Rhythm and Control
Session Outcomes
Control the tempo of build-up play by recognising when to slow, when to speed up, and how to stay connected under pressure.
Activation / Intro (10–15 mins)
Setup
- Area: 30x25m build-up zone near one goal
- Players: GK + 4 outfield vs 3 pressers
- Equipment: Balls, cones, 2 mini goals positioned beyond the press
Description
- All play starts with the GK.
- Possession team circulates the ball under controlled pressure.
- Defenders score by playing into either mini goal.
- Key rule: Team must complete at least 4 passes before progressing forward.
- Emphasis on calm circulation before accelerating the play.
Coaching Focus
- Calm first touch and pass quality
- Awareness of pressure cues
- Collective patience before playing forward
Progression
- Reduce required passes to 3
- Add an extra presser to increase pressure
Technical / Small-Group (15–25 mins)
Setup
- Area: 40x30m, directional
- Players: GK + 5v4
- Targets: End zone or mini goals beyond midfield line
Description
- Build-up begins with the GK.
- Possession team must control the rhythm:
- Circulate to move the press
- Speed up once a free player appears
- Pressing team works realistically to disrupt tempo.
- Rotate pressing roles regularly.
Coaching Focus
- Recognising slow vs fast moments
- Pass weight and timing
- Support positioning to maintain rhythm
Progression
- Limit possession players to 2 touches
- Allow pressing team to counter immediately on regain
Tactical / Group Game (20–30 mins)
Setup
- Area: 60x45m, near full width
- Players: 7v7 + GK
- Goals: Normal goals
Description
- Normal game flow; all restarts begin with the GK.
- Team scores double if they:
- Build with patience
- Change tempo to beat the press
- Progress into the attacking half under control
- Opposition presses freely and realistically.
Coaching Focus
- Full-team rhythm in build-up
- Collective understanding of tempo change
- Decision-making under pressure
Progression
- Remove scoring incentive
- Fully free game
Transfer / End Game (15–25 mins)
Setup
- Directional game, maximum 7v7
- Full width, appropriate length
- Normal rules
Description
- Free play with minimal intervention.
- Coach observes whether players naturally manage rhythm and control in build-up.
Coaching Focus
- Players recognising when to slow or speed up
- Team staying connected during circulation
- Confidence to reset and re-build
Coaching Information (U14)
Element | Detail
Objective / Outcomes | Team consistently controls build-up rhythm under pressure
Technical / Tactical Detail | Pass quality, scanning, tempo recognition, support angles
Coaching Messages (Belco Voice) | “Slow it.” • “Now speed up.” • “Control the game.”
Common Mistakes & Fixes | Rushing play → “Take a breath.” • Over-circulating → “Can we go now?”
Praise Language | “Great control.” • “Perfect tempo change.”
Behaviours to Reward | Composure, awareness, communication, bravery
Reflection Question | “What told you to slow down or speed up?”
Session Design Note | Rhythm control consolidates all build-up principles from the cycle

headingPath: U14 sessions > U14 Cycle 2, week 1, session 1
content:
BELCONNEN UNITED FC – SESSION PLAN | CYCLE 2 | WEEK 1 | SESSION 1
(Segoe UI 9 pt grey text, Belco-blue underline)
U14 | Progressing Through Midfield | Week 1 | Midfield Rotation Basics: Triangles and Third-Man
Session Outcomes
Progress through midfield by forming triangles, rotating positions, and using the third-man to play forward under pressure.
Activation / Intro (10–15 mins)
Setup
- Area: 30x25m central midfield zone
- Players: 5v3 inside
- Equipment: Balls, cones, 2 mini goals on opposite sides
Description
- Possession team keeps the ball using midfield triangles.
- Players must constantly rotate positions after passing.
- A point is scored by playing through a third-man combination to reach a mini goal.
- Key rule: Passer cannot receive the next pass directly — third-man required.
Coaching Focus
- Creating clear triangle angles
- Movement immediately after passing
- Awareness of the third-man option
Progression
- Add a fourth defender
- Limit possession players to 2 touches
Technical / Small-Group (15–25 mins)
Setup
- Area: 40x30m, directional
- Players: 4v4 + 1 neutral (neutral plays as central midfielder)
- Targets: End zones behind each team
Description
- Attacking team looks to progress through midfield using rotations.
- Neutral helps create triangles and third-man options.
- Defenders apply realistic pressure.
- To score, the ball must be played forward via a third-man.
- On regain, defenders counter into the opposite end zone.
Coaching Focus
- Timing of rotations (don’t rotate too early)
- Body shape to receive on the half-turn
- Weight and angle of the third-man pass
Progression
- Remove the neutral (4v4)
- Allow defenders to press aggressively from the start
Tactical / Group Game (20–30 mins)
Setup
- Area: 55x40m, central emphasis
- Players: 7v7 + GK
- Goals: Normal goals
Description
- Normal game flow with quick restarts.
- Team scores double if they:
- Progress through midfield using a triangle
- Break a line via a third-man action
- Coach allows play to flow to encourage natural rotations.
Coaching Focus
- Midfield unit staying connected
- Recognising when to rotate vs hold position
- Supporting the ball from different heights
Progression
- Remove scoring incentive
- Play fully normal rules
Transfer / End Game (15–25 mins)
Setup
- Directional game, maximum 7v7
- Full width, appropriate length
- Normal rules
Description
- Free play with minimal intervention.
- Coach observes whether midfield rotations and third-man actions appear naturally.
Coaching Focus
- Players offering angles around the ball
- Third-man movements recognised without prompts
- Team progressing centrally rather than forcing wide or long
Coaching Information (U14)
Element | Detail
Objective / Outcomes | Team progresses through midfield using triangles and third-man combinations
Technical / Tactical Detail | Angles of support, rotation timing, body shape, third-man pass quality
Coaching Messages (Belco Voice) | “Make the triangle.” • “Move after you pass.” • “Find the third.”
Common Mistakes & Fixes | Standing after pass → “Rotate out.” • Forcing forward pass → “Use the third-man.”
Praise Language | “Great rotation.” • “That third-man broke the line.”
Behaviours to Reward | Awareness, movement, connection, bravery to play inside
Reflection Question | “How did the third-man help us play forward?”
Session Design Note | Simple rotation rules create repetition before full freedom
Language Note | Reinforce Belco terms: triangle, third-man, connect

heading: U14 Cycle 2, week 1, session 2
headingPath: U14 sessions > U14 Cycle 2, week 1, session 2
content:
BELCONNEN UNITED FC – SESSION PLAN | CYCLE 2 | WEEK 1 | SESSION 2
(Segoe UI 9 pt grey text, Belco-blue underline)
U14 | Progressing Through Midfield | Week 1 | Timing to Support
Session Outcomes
Support the ball at the right moment and height to help teammates play forward through midfield under pressure.
Activation / Intro (10–15 mins)
Setup
- Area: 30x25m central midfield zone
- Players: 5v3 inside
- Equipment: Balls, cones, 2 mini goals on opposite sides
Description
- Possession team circulates the ball.
- Supporting players must adjust their position after the ball moves, not before.
- A point is scored when the ball carrier plays forward into a mini goal with a supporting option arriving at the right time.
- Key rule: Supporting players cannot enter the passing lane until the ball has moved.
Coaching Focus
- Timing of movement, not just positioning
- Supporting at different heights (under, beside, beyond)
- Reading the ball carrier’s situation
Progression
- Add a fourth defender
- Limit possession players to 2 touches
Technical / Small-Group (15–25 mins)
Setup
- Area: 40x30m, directional
- Players: 4v4 + 1 neutral (neutral plays as central midfielder)
- Targets: End zones behind each team
Description
- Attacking team aims to progress through midfield.
- Neutral supports play but must arrive after the first pass.
- Defenders apply realistic pressure.
- To score, the attacking team must:
- Play forward
- With clear support arriving at the right moment
- On regain, defenders counter into the opposite end zone.
Coaching Focus
- Supporting runs timed to the pass, not early
- Distance of support (not too close, not too far)
- Body shape to receive facing forward
Progression
- Remove the neutral (4v4)
- Allow defenders to press aggressively
Tactical / Group Game (20–30 mins)
Setup
- Area: 55x40m, central emphasis
- Players: 7v7 + GK
- Goals: Normal goals
Description
- Normal game flow with quick restarts.
- Team scores double if they:
- Progress through midfield
- With a clearly timed supporting movement that enables forward play
- Coach allows play to flow to encourage recognition.
Coaching Focus
- Midfielders reading when to support vs hold
- Supporting from different angles and heights
- Collective rhythm in midfield connections
Progression
- Remove scoring incentive
- Play fully normal rules
Transfer / End Game (15–25 mins)
Setup
- Directional game, maximum 7v7
- Full width, appropriate length
- Normal rules
Description
- Free play with minimal intervention.
- Coach observes whether players naturally time their support rather than crowding the ball.
Coaching Focus
- Players arriving to support at the right moment
- Ball carrier trusting support to arrive
- Team spacing through midfield
Coaching Information (U14)
Element | Detail
Objective / Outcomes | Team supports the ball with correct timing to enable forward midfield play
Technical / Tactical Detail | Support timing, spacing, body shape, reading pressure
Coaching Messages (Belco Voice) | “Wait… now help.” • “Arrive on time.” • “Support the play.”
Common Mistakes & Fixes | Early support → “Hold, then go.” • Too close → “Give space.”
Praise Language | “Perfect timing.” • “That support helped us play forward.”
Behaviours to Reward | Awareness, patience, movement, connection
Reflection Question | “When was the best moment to support the ball?”
Session Design Note | Delayed movement builds smarter, more effective midfield support
Language Note | Reinforce Belco terms: support, timing, connect
Belconnen United FC – Compact • Brave • Intelligent
(Light-blue separator line above; Segoe UI 8 pt grey text)
