heading: U15 Coach Pack
content:
BELCONNEN UNITED FC
TECHNICAL DEPARTMENT
——————————————
COACH PACK 5
TACTICAL INTEGRATION PHASE – U15
——————————————
Beginning to act and think as one unit
——————————————
Prepared by:
Scott Conlon – Technical Director
Belconnen United FC
V2026.03

heading: 1. U15 Message to the Coaches
headingPath: COACH PACK 3 (U15) – TACTICAL INTEGRATION PHASE > 1. U15 Message to the Coaches
content:
This phase is about execution — not theory, not comfort, but consistent performance of what they already know.
The players must now apply principles under full tempo: build-up through pressure, collective pressing, game rhythm, and emotional control.
Your job is to turn understanding into instinct.
Every player must feel how their action affects the team — when to step, when to wait, when to speed up or slow down the game.
You coach less technique now and more timing, perception, and behaviour.
Demand precision but stay consistent with language. Don’t add complexity — refine it.
Your tone should be firm but clear; your feedback sharp and simple.
This phase defines our tactical identity: a team that controls rhythm, reacts together, and presses with purpose.
Your job is to blend Build & Control the Tempo, Collective Compactness, and Aggressive Recovery into one match rhythm.
“At U15, it’s not new ideas — it’s the same ideas done faster, together, and under pressure.”

heading: 2. U15 Player Characteristics
headingPath: COACH PACK 3 (U15) – TACTICAL INTEGRATION PHASE > 2. U15 Player Characteristics
content:
U15 players are entering a period of significant physical and cognitive transition.Their ability to understand structure and tactical cause–effect increases sharply, but they need emotional stability and clarity in leadership.
Typical traits:
- Rapid growth and changes in coordination — technique may fluctuate during this period.
- Improved game insight — beginning to see team shape, pressing triggers, and space relations.
- Heightened competitive instinct — emotionally invested in winning but need perspective.
- Developing autonomy — respond best when given responsibility and explanation.
- Able to manage tactical correction if linked to clear purpose (“why this matters”).
Coach takeaway:
Lead with clarity and calmness. The coach’s tone should balance challenge with trust.
At this stage, it’s not just what players do, but why and when — the art of control through understanding.

heading: 3. U15 Phase Overview
headingPath: COACH PACK 3 (U15) – TACTICAL INTEGRATION PHASE > 3. U15 Phase Overview
content:
Area | Key Points
Age Phase | U15 – Tactical Development Phase
Development Focus | Tactical execution at match tempo. Understanding timing, pressing cues, and rhythm control.
Session Rhythm | 2 sessions per week. 40% tactical shape work, 40% transition/pressing, 20% situational technical refinement.
Training Ratio | Tactical : Technical = 2 : 1. Tactical work must always end in realistic SSG or directional play.
Game Focus | Coordinated team pressing, structured build-up, tempo variation and composure under pressure.
Belco Identity Seeds | 🔹 Build & Control the Tempo (through rhythm control) 🔹 Collective Compactness (through defensive structure) 🔹 Aggressive Recovery (through pressing and transitions) 🔹 Duel Mentality (through emotional intensity).
Coach Role | Refine habits, reinforce game speed, and raise standards. Maintain tactical discipline without killing creativity.

heading: 4. U15 Coaching Emphasis
headingPath: COACH PACK 3 (U15) – TACTICAL INTEGRATION PHASE > 4. U15 Coaching Emphasis
content:
Coaching Dimension | Emphasis for This Phase | Practical Examples / Notes
Session Rhythm | High tempo with controlled pauses. Every drill connects to game tempo or transition. | Build-up → high-press → counter-transition.
Coaching Voice | Short, assertive, collective cues. | “Compact!” “Go!” “Tempo!” “Fast five!”
Correction Style | Freeze for structure, replay for tempo. | “Hold shape — now go again at speed.”
Technical Detail | Execution at match pace — body orientation, weight of pass, pressing angles. | Correction in rhythm, not isolation.
Tactical Habits | Recognising pressing triggers, adjusting team distances, maintaining tempo after regain. | “Back pass — we go.” “Closed ball — compact.”
Psychological Theme | Professional mentality. Emotional control under stress, work ethic without panic. | “Calm brain, fast legs.”
Language Focus | Team cues dominate. Players must respond collectively. | “Three go — lock rest.” “Compact to attack.”
Coach Behaviours to Reward | Players leading communication or tempo changes. | “Good — you started the press.” “You controlled the speed.”
Croatian Guideline | “Discipline creates freedom.” Structure allows the team to play fast and confident. | Keep players inside a clear game model.

heading: 5. U15 Technical Focus List
headingPath: COACH PACK 3 (U15) – TACTICAL INTEGRATION PHASE > 5. U15 Technical Focus List
content:
Purpose
At U15, players learn to control the game collectively.
Technique now serves tactical precision — every pass, touch, and run exists to move the opponent, manage tempo, or protect structure.
This is the phase where Croatian tactical training becomes dominant: football problems solved through realistic, high-tempo games.
The Belco identity remains the anchor — compact, brave, and intelligent — now applied with rhythm, discipline, and control.
1. Technical Precision Under Tactical Tempo
Focus | What It Looks Like | How It’s Trained
First touch under opponent press | Controls tempo, maintains rhythm of play. | 6v6–8v8 build-up under pressure, directional zones.
Timing of release | Waits for draw, releases with intent. | Drive–Draw–Play progressions; 4v3 overloads.
Tempo variation through passing | Uses short–fast vs long–slow to shift rhythm. | Positional games with tempo targets (time-limited sequences).
Technical resilience under fatigue | Sustains quality late in game. | Repetition in high-tempo possession with transition.
Belco links: Build Through Thirds, Tempo Control, Overloads. |  | 
2. Positional Awareness and Receiving Technique
Focus | What It Looks Like | How It’s Trained
Receiving to face forward in compact space | Turns body between lines to connect play. | 7v7+3 positional grids; third-line passes.
Angled support and scanning | Pre-orients to next action. | Triangle rondos with direction constraint.
First-touch protection | Uses body to protect and retain tempo. | Central build-up zones; 1v1 + support neutral.
Receiving on move | Adjusts speed to meet ball in open lane. | 6v6 directional play; one-touch follow constraint.
Belco links: Build Through Thirds, Bounce & Third-Man, Open Ball. |  | 
3. Collective Combination & Positional Rotation
Focus | What It Looks Like | How It’s Trained
Third-man play in match tempo | Midfield trio breaks line through movement. | 5v5–7v7 positional triangles; time-limited scoring.
Bounce + third-man linked with rotation | Dynamic triangles, under–beyond–behind coordination. | Functional rotation patterns with opponents.
Overload-to-isolate movement | Creates advantage then isolates for finish. | 7v5 build-up → 1v1 wide isolation.
Role interchange with spacing discipline | Players rotate without losing team shape. | 8v8 structured positional games.
Belco links: Overloads, Drive–Draw–Play, Bounce & Third-Man. |  | 
4. Ball Carrying for Control and Creation
Focus | What It Looks Like | How It’s Trained
Drive into midfield to commit | CB or CM carries to draw striker. | 6v6 build-up with carry trigger.
Change of direction at tempo | Keeps rhythm but beats press. | Small-field directional 4v4 + gates.
Recognition of trigger to drive | Identifies open-ball space. | Medium-sized transition games (6v6–8v8).
Carry vs release decision | Chooses best moment to fix or pass. | 5v5–6v6 constraint: drive or play points.
Belco links: Drive–Draw–Play, Open Ball, Tempo Control. |  | 
5. Defensive Technique in Tactical System
Focus | What It Looks Like | How It’s Trained
Compact press coordination | Unit shifts as one, line steps on cue. | 8v8 + target pressing zones.
Press–cover–balance detail | Nearest engages, second covers, third protects. | 4v4–6v6 half-pitch block defending.
Counter-pressing shape | Immediate reaction from unit — trap on one side. | 7v7 transition games with recovery rule.
Body shape in duels | Front foot, low centre, control direction. | 1v1s in wide channels; delayed pressure.
Belco links: Compactness, Forward Pressing, Duel Mentality. |  | 
6. Finishing and Final-Third Actions
Focus | What It Looks Like | How It’s Trained
Timing of assist-zone runs | Arrives behind last line as ball enters channel. | Wide overload → cut-back games.
Second-line finishes | Midfielders time late arrivals. | 6v6 attacking transitions with scoring zones.
Finishing from compact spaces | Quick, controlled strikes under pressure. | 5v5 penalty-box games.
Combination to finish | Third-man or bounce before final pass. | 4v4 + GK half-pitch rotations.
Belco links: Assist Zone, Open Ball, Overloads. |  | 
7. Tactical–Cognitive Habits
Focus | What It Looks Like | How It’s Trained
Pre-scan under pressure | Adjusts early based on opponent movement. | 4v2–6v3 rondos with direction.
Anticipation of transitions | Prepares defensive shape when attacking. | Continuous 8v8 transition flow.
Team rhythm control | Identifies when to slow or accelerate play. | Full-pitch build-up to finish, tempo rules.
Collective adjustment | Moves as one to opponent press or overload. | 11v11 phase play; trigger recognition.
Belco links: Tempo Control, Compactness, Counter-Pressing. |  |

heading: 6. U15 Language Bank (part 1)
headingPath: COACH PACK 3 (U15) – TACTICAL INTEGRATION PHASE > 6. U15 Language Bank
content:
This chapter defines the shared tactical language used across the Belco program to describe the game by moments and by thirds: in possession, out of possession, and transition. Rather than prescribing patterns or movements, the language bank provides collective concept cues that allow teams to recognise situations, act together, and control tempo under match pressure.
The language is deliberately short, visual, and repeatable, enabling players to self-correct in real time and coaches to intervene briefly without over-coaching. Organising the game through the back, middle, and front thirds reflects how decisions actually occur on the pitch, while transition language connects these moments into one continuous game flow. This approach mirrors Croatian and European methodology, where tactical understanding is built through shared terminology, repetition, and realism, not constant instruction.
From U14 onward, the language remains consistent across age groups, while the expectation of execution increases. What begins as recognition at U14–15 becomes a performance standard at U16 and above. This continuity ensures players are not relearning football concepts each season, but instead developing the ability to read the game, manage moments, and take ownership as they prepare for senior football.
U15 BUILD-UP FROM THE BACK
Belco Identity: Build & Control the Tempo · Create Superiority
Focus | Collective Language Cue | Croatian Tactical Logic
Structure First | “Set the shape.” “Back three ready.” | Shape before speed. Players recognise build-up positions automatically.
Create the Free Man | “Who’s spare?” “Find the free man.” | Numerical superiority is the foundation of progression.
Support on Different Lines | “Different lines.” “Two options.” | Prevents flat play; creates forward and diagonal solutions.
Attract to Progress | “Invite them.” “Bring him, then play.” | Draw pressure deliberately to open space elsewhere.
Progress with Intent | “Step in if free.” “Break a line.” | Forward play only when advantage is created — not forced.
U15 PROGRESSING THROUGH MIDFIELD
Belco Identity: Create Superiority · Build & Control the Tempo
Focus | Language Cues | Purpose / Croatian Logic
Open Ball Recognition | “Open ball — trigger run!” “Now we go!” | Read and react collectively to forward-facing player.
Third-Man Combinations | “Bounce and go.” “Third man opens the lane.” | Classic Croatian progression habit.
Drive–Draw–Play | “Drive inside — draw him — play outside.” | Central rhythm for overloads and rhythm control.
Level & Outside | “Stay level — make the blind run.” | Used in rotations and wide triangle play.
Pivot Use | “Middle man controls tempo.” “Play through the pivot.” | Encourages midfield rhythm management.
Tempo Control | “Slow → quick → slow.” “We set the rhythm.” | Players manage flow instead of reacting to opponents.
U15 FINISHING FROM POSITIONAL PLAY
Belco Identity: Create Superiority · Duel Mentality
Focus | Language Cues | Purpose / Croatian Logic
Assist Zone | “Go to the assist zone!” “Deliver back — not over!” | Attacking pattern: penetrate to byline and cut back.
Cut-Back Mentality | “Drive the line — cut back with purpose.” | High-probability finish model; second-line arrival.
Drive–Draw–Play | “Draw him wide — play inside.” | Overload and isolate wide duels.
Level & Outside | “Stay outside — inside opens.” | Wide positioning creates channel for the third man.
Finishing Focus | “Arrive late — attack the cut-back.” “Hit the target, don’t blast.” | Finishing efficiency and timing.
U15 PRESSING ORGANISATION
Belco Identity: Collective Compactness · Aggressive Recovery
Focus | Language Cues | Purpose / Croatian Logic
Press Triggers | “Closed ball — press!” “Return ball — step in!” | Triggers pressing cues based on visual signals.
Nearest Three Go | “Nearest three go together!” | Collective, compact pressing shape.
Compactness | “Stay tight — squeeze the line.” “Protect the core.” | Croatian hallmark; central compactness before aggression.
Compact to Attack | “Win it — play forward!” “Press to play!” | Pressing becomes a way to create attacking rhythm.
Recovery Voice | “Shape first, then step.” “Don’t chase alone.” | Emphasises collective patience.
U15 DEFENDING THE MIDDLE THIRD
Belco Identity: Collective Compactness · Build & Control the Tempo
Focus | Language Cues | Purpose / Croatian Logic
Defensive Shape | “Compact block — control distance.” | Mid-block control and discipline.
Reading Cues | “Closed ball — stay.” “Open ball — drop.” | Players read visual signals, not coach voice.
Communication | “Talk the line.” “Move as one.” | Ownership of shape.
Delay Discipline | “Hold — delay — then step.” | Teaches timing of defensive pressure.
Transition Ready | “Compact to break.” | Always ready to shift into attack.
U15 TRANSITION TO ATTACK
Belco Identity: Build & Control the Tempo · Aggressive Recovery
Focus | Language Cues | Purpose / Croatian Logic
First Pass After Regain | “Find open ball.” “Win → play forward.” | Prioritise tempo and direction on regain.
Support Runs | “Run to help the regain.” “Sprint to support.” | Creates fast, connected transition.
Drive–Draw–Play | “Drive to draw — release fast.” | Controlled acceleration after regain.
Tempo Control | “Fast brain, calm feet.” | Keeps quality while increasing tempo.
U15 TRANSITION TO DEFENCE
Belco Identity: Aggressive Recovery · Collective Compactness
Focus | Language Cues | Purpose / Croatian Logic

heading: 6. U15 Language Bank (part 2)
headingPath: COACH PACK 3 (U15) – TACTICAL INTEGRATION PHASE > 6. U15 Language Bank
content:
Immediate Reaction | “Lose it? Close it!” “Nearest three press.” | 5-second regain principle.
Compact Recovery | “Protect the middle.” “Get narrow fast.” | Prevent central progression.
Tempo Delay | “Slow them down.” “Buy us seconds.” | Intelligent delay for regrouping.
Team Reaction | “Everyone reacts — together.” | No passengers mentality.
U15 SWITCH OF PLAY & WIDTH MANAGEMENT
Belco Identity: Build & Control the Tempo · Create Superiority
Focus | Language Cues | Purpose / Croatian Logic
Recognition | “Play one way — finish the other.” “Far side free.” | Recognise imbalance.
Return Ball Use | “Reset — change side!” | Teaches circulation to break structure.
Width | “Stretch to control.” “Outside line ready.” | Creates space to advance inside.
Level & Outside | “Stay level — open the angle.” | Shape management cue for wide players.
Tempo Rhythm | “Slow → quick → slow.” | Change rhythm to move opponent.
U15 TEAM MENTALITY & GAME INTELLIGENCE
Belco Identity: Build & Control the Tempo · Duel Mentality
Concept Focus | Language Cues | Purpose / Croatian Logic
Tempo Ownership | “We control the rhythm.” “Don’t chase the game.” | Reinforces control mindset.
Resilience | “Next ball.” “React, reset, repeat.” | Croatian hallmark: composure + continuity.
Confidence | “Play brave.” “Trust the structure.” | Encourages self-coaching.
Communication | “One voice, one shape.” | Unity and leadership.
Reflection | “What picture did you see?” | Encourages autonomy and decision reflection.

heading: 7. U15 Annual Cycle Progression
headingPath: COACH PACK 3 (U15) – TACTICAL INTEGRATION PHASE > 7. U15 Annual Cycle Progression
content:
Structure: The U15 season consists of 7 Cycles · 4–5 weeks each · 2 sessions per week (~28–30 weeks)
Focus: Executing collective principles under match tempo, intelligent game management, maintaining compactness and rhythm control.
Belco Language Layer:
- “Open ball, play brave.”
- “Compact to attack.”
- “Control the game.”
U15 cycles are numbered Cycle 1 to Cycle 7
Cycle | Theme | Duration | Week 1 | Week 2 | Week 3 | Week 4 | Week 5
1 | Controlled Build-Up & Outlets | 5 weeks | Build-Up Recognition – Read the Press Find the Open Ball | Use the GK as Pivot Create Exit Routes | Manipulate the Press – Draw and Release Control to Break | Build Through Width Play Brave Under Pressure | Whole-Team Build-Up Game Rhythm to Break Lines
2 | Positional Play in Progression | 4 weeks | Maintain Triangles – Structure in Possession Create Overload | Third-Man Runs Break Lines Through Cooperation | Change the Tempo From Calm to Accelerate | Positional Progression Game Create Superiority and Finish | 
3 | Final Third Decision-Making | 4 weeks | Final Third Entries – Timing to Support Recognise Decision | Cut-Back or Cross? – Read the Picture Play Forward with Courage | Combination to Finish Quality over Rush | Collective Finishing Game Calm Execution at Tempo | 
4 | High Press & Counter-Press | 5 weeks | Press Triggers Closed and Return Balls Compact to Attack | Synchronised Pressing Nearest 3 Go Line Coordination | Counter-Press to Regain First Five Seconds Count | Press and Finish Win It, Go Forward | Pressing Game Scenario Tempo and Discipline
5 | Defending Compactness & Depth | 4 weeks | Compact Block – Protect Central Zone Stay Connected | Depth Control – Read the Line Manage Space Behind | Coordination Across Lines Move as a Unit | Controlled Press Compact but Aggressive | 
6 | Transition Control (Both Directions) | 4 weeks | Win → Play → Organise Balance Attack Risk | Transition to Defence Counter-Press Organisation | Recover and Regain Shape Play Forward from Regain | Rhythm Game Tempo Control After Regain | 
7 | Game Management & Tempo | 4 weeks | Control the Rhythm When to Speed or Slow | Situational Pressing Recognise Triggers | Tactical Fouls and Compact Recovery Emotional Control | Close the Game Protect the Lead with Intelligence | 
Cycle Duration Notes
Cycle | Reason for 5 Weeks
1. Controlled Build-Up & Outlets | Deepest and most detailed tactical block in the Croatian U15 year. Involves all units, requires rhythm, timing, and courage under press.
4. High Press & Counter-Press | Demanding both physically and mentally; Croatians repeat this block mid- and late-season for reinforcement.

heading: 8 U15 Training Sessions
headingPath: COACH PACK 3 (U15) – TACTICAL INTEGRATION PHASE > 8 U15 Training Sessions
content:
Training Chapter – Coach Standard (Coach Pack Version)
Belco training sessions are not copied from Croatian drills.
They are built on the same philosophy and session structure, adapted to our players, coaches, and environment.
Every session follows a clear learning sequence:
- Prepare the player (uvodni)
- Develop the main idea (glavni)
- Transfer learning into the game (završni)
These phases describe purpose, not drills.
Coaches are not expected to run fixed exercises. They are expected to:
- respect the session flow
- connect activities to the game
- increase realism and pressure as the session progresses
- use shared language consistently
The session examples that follow show one way to apply this methodology. Activities can be changed, adapted, or replaced, as long as the learning intent, progression, and behavioural standards remain.
We don’t copy exercises.We copy how learning is structured.

heading: U15 Session Structure
headingPath: COACH PACK 3 (U15) – TACTICAL INTEGRATION PHASE > 8 U15 Training Sessions > U15 Session Structure
content:
How Belco Training Sessions Are Structured (U15)
At U15, Belco training sessions consolidate the tactical foundation phase and begin to prepare players for the rhythm and demands of older youth football.
Players are now expected not only to recognise game situations, but to:
- execute decisions with consistency
- act with rhythm and discipline
- maintain behaviours under pressure
This does not mean teaching systems or fixed team structures.Learning still happens through game situations, but expectations are firmer and consequences are clearer.
Croatian Learning Logic at U15
Sessions follow a Croatian learning sequence, where:
- collective behaviour emerges through game rhythm, not instruction
- situations are solved through connection, timing, and decision quality
- early conditions may support rhythm and confidence
- pressure and equality increase progressively
- free play reveals whether behaviours hold without support
The focus at this age is reliable collective execution, not perfect solutions.
How the Session Progresses at U15
Across the session:
- players move quickly from preparation into demand
- assistance is reduced earlier than at U14
- decisions must hold under fatigue and pressure
- players are expected to self-correct
The coach’s role shifts clearly toward:
- setting standards
- reinforcing discipline and intent
- demanding attention and responsibility
The session always follows three connected phases, consistent with Croatian architecture.
Phase 1 – Play with the Ball
This phase prepares players to enter the session thinking in game terms, with immediate focus and intent.
At U15, this phase:
- is ball-based and continuous
- includes interaction and decision-making
- introduces direction and tempo early
- demands concentration from the first action
Early advantages (space, time, or numbers) may be used briefly to support rhythm and sharpness, but players are expected to organise and act quickly.
The emphasis is on:
- quality of movement
- awareness of teammates and space
- establishing rhythm
This phase is not about structure or patterns.It is about readiness to play with purpose.
Coaches should:
- set expectations early
- reinforce tempo and focus
- correct disengagement immediately
Phase 2 – Learning Game
This is the core learning phase of the session.
Players operate in directional, game-realistic situations that require:
- coordinated decision-making
- timing of actions
- responsibility within the moment
Learning is driven by:
- realistic spacing
- transitions
- repeated exposure to the same problem
Overloads may still appear briefly to support rhythm, but the aim is to move quickly toward soft equality, where execution quality matters.
The coach:
- reinforces discipline and responsibility
- corrects attention and intent
- avoids providing solutions
Players are expected to feel the demands of the situation and adjust together.
Phase 3 – Football Game
The session finishes with a free or lightly conditioned football game.
This phase tests whether players can:
- maintain rhythm and spacing
- execute decisions under pressure
- manage the game without prompts
Conditions are minimal.Numbers are even or close to even.The game should reflect real football demands.
Coach intervention is minimal.Observation focuses on whether collective behaviours hold naturally.
This phase confirms learning through behaviour, not explanation.
Coach Guidance (U15)
At U15, effective coaching means:
- prioritising collective execution over explanation
- using overloads only to support early rhythm
- allowing equality and pressure to reveal understanding
- trusting the game to confirm learning
The coach sets the standard —the players meet it.
Key Reminder
U15 is about reliability.
If the team can:
- recognise situations together
- execute with rhythm under pressure
- maintain behaviour without prompts
then the session has done its job.

heading: 9. U15 Correction & Feedback Language
headingPath: COACH PACK 3 (U15) – TACTICAL INTEGRATION PHASE > 9. U15 Correction & Feedback Language
content:
Purpose
Correction now targets team tempo, collective standards, and emotional control.
This is where the Croatian coach stops coaching “the football” and starts coaching how the players play the football.
They see it as the transition from teaching to mentoring performance.
U15 Correction Philosophy (Croatian Model)
Type of Feedback | When Used | Croatian Approach | Belco Example Language
Instant (Trigger Reinforcement) | In-game, high tempo. | Keep shape but sharpen reaction. | “Tempo!” / “Compact!” / “Now!”
Freeze & Standard Reset | Only when collective discipline drops. | Reset the principle, not the play. | “Hold — we press together, not alone. Go again.”
Guided Reflection | Half-time or stoppage. | Ask about tempo control and discipline. | “Did we chase the game, or control it?”
Reinforce Success | When team recovers rhythm quickly. | Praise emotional recovery. | “Excellent — lost shape, fixed it instantly.”
Collective Reminder | After sustained lapse. | Reset mindset. | “Everyone — calm brain, fast legs.”
Croatian Correction Principles (U15)
1️⃣ Correct rhythm and mentality, not errors.
2️⃣ One correction = one trigger.
3️⃣ Coach collective responsibility.
4️⃣ Set standards publicly, emotion privately.
U15 Belco–Croatian Common Coaching Language (U15 Level)
Action Type | Correction Cue | Positive Reinforcement
Tempo Control | “Calm, then fast.” “Set rhythm.” | “Good — you slowed the game.”
Pressing & Transition | “Fast five!” “Compact to attack.” | “Perfect trigger — we regained.”
Structure | “Hold lines!” “Return ball!” | “Great spacing — we stayed connected.”
Mentality | “Calm brain.” “Next action.” | “Love that — quick recovery.”
Team Discipline | “Together!” “Stay compact!” | “Excellent — we moved as one.”
Tone & Delivery
- Voice: controlled, steady — no emotional spikes.
- Corrections: short and collective; use body language (gesture compactness, tempo with hand).
- After error: reset rhythm instantly — don’t dwell.
- Post-game: reflect with players on triggers, not score.
“In Croatia, by U15, correction is about standards — not mistakes.”

heading: 10. U15 Game Day Guidance
headingPath: COACH PACK 3 (U15) – TACTICAL INTEGRATION PHASE > 10. U15 Game Day Guidance
content:
Purpose
The match is no longer an “extension of training” — it is the test of learning transfer.
Players must now operate with minimal external instruction.
Croatian coaches use matchday at U15 to measure how well the team can self-manage rhythm, compactness, and pressing cues.
U15 Game Day Objectives
Element | Guidance
Game Intent | Execute the game model under match tempo: build calmly, react fast, control transitions. The focus is on consistency — not new ideas.
Coach Voice | Fewer words, firmer tone. Use trigger–standard language: “Compact!”, “Fast five!”, “Tempo!”, “Calm brain, fast legs!”
Player Guidance | Players lead organisation. Centre-backs and pivots command spacing; wide players manage tempo switches.
Substitution Focus | Role-specific changes. Reinforce tactical discipline — “Same role, same triggers.”
Half-Time Reflection | 2 key prompts:
 | 1️⃣ “Did we manage tempo or chase it?”
 | 2️⃣ “Were our distances right in transition?”
Coach Behaviour | Emotionally neutral but demanding. Croatians say: “No energy from the sideline, only rhythm.” Coach composure = team composure.
Croatian Matchday Principles (U15)
1️⃣ “Coach through rhythm, not emotion.”
2️⃣ “Triggers create control.”
3️⃣ “Leadership must transfer to players.”
Example Match Dialogue
“Compact — wait!”
“Fast five — step!”
“Good tempo — stay calm.”
“Reset — return ball.”
“Trust the structure.”
At this phase, every word either reinforces tempo or discipline.

heading: U15 Coach Matchday Standards
headingPath: COACH PACK 3 (U15) – TACTICAL INTEGRATION PHASE > 10. U15 Game Day Guidance > U15 Coach Matchday Standards
content:
Purpose
The coach sets the tone for the match environment.Professional detail builds credibility.Players mirror what they see — composure, preparation, and precision.
Coach Matchday Standards
APPEARANCE
Element | Standard
Belconnen Attire | Official Belconnen polo or approved top. Unified, consistent look across staff.
Footwear | Soccer footwear during warm-up and match. We look like football people, not spectators.
Sunglasses | Remove when speaking to players. Eye contact is essential for message absorption and connection.
Presentation | Neat, professional, deliberate. Our unified appearance signals control and precision.
GEAR
Item | Expectation
Balls | Pumped appropriately before arrival at pitch.
Bibs | Organised and accessible.
Cones | Blue and white matchday cones set and ready.
PREPAREDNESS
Element | Expectation
Substitution Plan | Rotation structure pre-planned. Know your intent before kick-off.
Team Talk | Clear theme linked to weekly focus. No improvisation.
Arrival Time | Minimum 45–60 minutes before kick-off. Field checked and organised.
Sideline Behaviour
Element | Standard
Coach Voice | Calm, clear, rhythmic.
Emotional Control | No visible frustration or emotional reactions.
Referee Interaction | Respectful at all times. Model behaviour.
Opposition Interaction | Professional greeting pre and post match.
Player Interaction | Eye contact, short cues, consistent language from training.
Parent Interaction | Friendly but brief. No tactical debate during game.
Belco Matchday Identity
1️⃣ Calm voice, clear cues.2️⃣ Detail before emotion.3️⃣ Teach relationships, not results.4️⃣ Represent the program with pride.

heading: U15 Pre-Match Warm-Up
headingPath: COACH PACK 3 (U15) – TACTICAL INTEGRATION PHASE > 10. U15 Game Day Guidance > U15 Pre-Match Warm-Up
content:
PRE-MATCH WARM-UP (25–30 MINUTES TOTAL)
Objective: Prepare tactically and mentally for full-speed execution — press timing, build-up precision, and emotional control.
The pre-match warm-up is not fitness, not motivation, and not teaching.
Its job is to prepare players to execute what they already know with calm, clarity, and control.
Warm-ups should:
- progress from individual activation to collective organisation
- reflect how the team intends to play
- introduce the ball early
- become quieter and more player-led as kick-off approaches
Coaches use the warm-up to observe emotional tempo and spacing, not to run drills or give speeches.If the warm-up is loud, rushed, or emotional, it has missed its purpose.
Block | Duration | Focus | Example Activity | Coach Cues / Tone
1️⃣ Dynamic Mobility & Activation | 5 mins | Loosen, control energy, rhythm. | Jog–lunge–hip openers–short accelerations (players in pairs). | “Stay calm, control pace.” “We move as one.”
2️⃣ Technical Precision | 5–6 mins | Ball feel and body balance at tempo. | Pairs: two-touch passing, far-foot receive, quick rotation. | “Clean touch, calm body.” “See before play.”
3️⃣ Positional Rondos (by units) | 5-6 mins | Pressing cues & compactness within lines. | Defenders 4v2 (closed-ball recovery cues), mids 5v3, forwards 3v3 + target. | “Compact.” “Press trigger — closed!”
4️⃣ Tactical Rehearsal (Pattern) | 6-7 mins | Build-up or press rehearsal. | 7v5 build-up pattern or coordinated high press. | “Open — stretch!” “Return — reset tempo.”
5️⃣ Directional Game / Finishing Activation | 5 mins | Rhythm and intent to goal. | 5v5 directional play; finish from cut-back zone. | “Tempo control.” “Attack with purpose.”
Detail:
- The warm-up has a visible shape — each line rehearses its movement.
- The coach observes the group’s emotional tempo — steady, composed.
- Final minutes are quiet; players set internal rhythm.
“Warm-up is not energy — it’s control.”

heading: U15 Pre-Match Talk
headingPath: COACH PACK 3 (U15) – TACTICAL INTEGRATION PHASE > 10. U15 Game Day Guidance > U15 Pre-Match Talk
content:
PRE-MATCH TALK (3–4 MINUTES)
Purpose
Establish clarity and calm authority.
At this age, the talk feels like a senior dressing room — serious tone, few words, one clear tactical identity.
Structure
Step | Coach Focus | Example Language (Croatian senior tone)
1️⃣ Calm Command | Start still, clear voice, eye contact. | “Eyes here — stay calm. One message today.”
2️⃣ Tactical Intent | State what defines your match. | “We control tempo. Build with patience. Press on the closed ball.”
3️⃣ Unit Triggers & Standards | One for each line. | “Back line — compact and ready.” “Midfield — control rhythm.” “Front — press together.”
4️⃣ Emotional Control Cue | Reset mental tempo. | “Calm brain, fast legs.”
5️⃣ Close with Professional Calm | No shout, no slogan — silence after last line. | “Trust structure. Tempo is ours.” (pause)
Croatian Pre-Match Principles (U15)
1️⃣ “Less emotion, more standard.”
2️⃣ “Coach with silence and authority.”
3️⃣ “The tone of your voice sets the team’s pulse.”

heading: U15 Half-Time Talk
headingPath: COACH PACK 3 (U15) – TACTICAL INTEGRATION PHASE > 10. U15 Game Day Guidance > U15 Half-Time Talk
content:
HALF-TIME TALK (less than 8 MINUTES)
Purpose
Reset tactical rhythm and emotional discipline.
The U15 coach evaluates how well the team controls the game, not just how they play it.
The half-time message is professional, calm, and centred on tempo control.
Structure
Step | Coach Focus | Example Language (Croatian discipline tone)
1️⃣ Regain Calm | Silence first, players drink, coach waits. | “Breathe. Reset.”
2️⃣ Acknowledge Discipline | Reinforce control, not hype. | “We stayed compact and reacted fast — good discipline.”
3️⃣ One Tactical Correction | Keep it structural. | “We’re rushing the first pass — slow the tempo before going forward.”
4️⃣ Leadership Reflection | Hand voice to captains. | “Midfield, what’s our tempo like?”
5️⃣ Close with One Identity Cue | End on a clear standard. | “Calm brain, fast legs. Compact, together — second half, same control.”
Half-Time Rules (U15)
Rule | Meaning for Coach
1️⃣ Coach rhythm, not emotion. | You regulate tempo through your own control.
2️⃣ Praise calmness, not noise. | Reward discipline.
3️⃣ Fewer words = more authority. | Short talk, strong silence.
4️⃣ Finish with identity language. | Reinforce game model through trigger cues.
Example Complete U15 Half-Time Talk
“Good half — we stayed compact and controlled rhythm.
We’re rushing a bit on the first pass — slow it, set tempo, then play forward.
Calm brain, fast legs. Trust our structure — tempo is ours.”
Coach Behaviour Summary
Aspect | Croatian Standard for U15
Voice | Calm, deliberate, low — no spikes.
Body Language | Still posture, slow gestures.
Focus | Tempo, control, and professional standard.
Timing | 3–4 mins pre-match, 3–5 mins half-time.
Consistency | Same rhythm language every match.
Key Takeaway:“At U15, the coach no longer creates intensity — they create control. Calm tone = tactical authority.”

heading: U15 Pre-Match Routine - Timeline
headingPath: COACH PACK 3 (U15) – TACTICAL INTEGRATION PHASE > 10. U15 Game Day Guidance > U15 Pre-Match Routine - Timeline
content:
Reference: All timings work backwards from KO
Time (from KO) | Moment | Coach Focus | Coach Behaviour Standard | Key Reminder
KO–60 | Arrival | Settle & organise | Calm presence · logistics only · no football talk | Emotional state first
KO–50 | Change / Prep | Individual routine | Minimal voice · observe only | Not a coaching window
KO–45 | Main Team Talk | Tactical clarity | One picture · shared language · calm tone | Clarity > motivation Less than 6mins
KO–40 | Warm-Up Begins | Activate & reinforce | Reduce voice · use constraints · players take over | Movement reinforces ideas
KO–10 | Final Reminder | Trigger recall | One sentence · one trigger · then silence | Cue, not a talk
KO–4 | Leave Change Room | Emotional control | No talking · calm body language | Restraint = authority
KO | Kick-Off | Observe & manage | Correct standards only · no constant instruction | Players control the game
NON-NEGOTIABLE WARNINGS (FOR COACHES)
Warning Sign | What It Means
Repeating yourself | You’re talking too long
Adding late information | It should have been coached earlier
Emotional build-up | Clarity is being lost
Coaching constantly after KO | Players aren’t owning the game
CROATIAN MATCHDAY PRINCIPLES (REMEMBER)
- Calm beats intensity
- Structure beats speeches
- Triggers beat shouting
- Restraint signals authority
Pre-match talks don’t add ideas — they remove doubt.

heading: U15 Pre-Match Routine - Detail
headingPath: COACH PACK 3 (U15) – TACTICAL INTEGRATION PHASE > 10. U15 Game Day Guidance > U15 Pre-Match Routine - Detail
content:
PURPOSE OF MATCHDAY ROUTINE
- Create calm, clarity, and readiness
- Reinforce shared tactical language
- Protect players from over-coaching
- Ensure the match is a performance moment, not a teaching session
Training does the teaching.Matchday protects execution.
MATCHDAY FLOW (KO-BASED)
🕒 KO–60 MINUTES | ARRIVAL
Focus: Settle, organise, decompress
Coach Behaviour
- Calm presence
- No tactical talk
- Logistics only (kit, hydration, admin)
Croatian Logic
- Players must arrive emotionally neutral
- No football information before they are ready to receive it
🕒 KO–50 MINUTES | CHANGE / PREP
Focus: Individual routine
Coach Behaviour
- Minimal voice
- Observe, don’t instruct
- Allow players to settle into their own rhythm
Standard
- This is not a coaching window
🕒 KO–45 MINUTES | MAIN TEAM TALK (BRIEF)
Focus: Tactical clarity, not motivation
Coach Behaviour
- Calm tone
- One tactical picture only
- Use shared language (already trained)
- No emotional build-up
Content Rules
- 1 collective focus
- 2–3 known triggers (maximum)
- No new information
- No individuals singled out
Croatian Principle
Clarity replaces motivation.
🕒 KO–40 MINUTES | WARM-UP BEGINS (≈25 MIN)
Focus: Physical + cognitive activation
Coach Behaviour
- Minimal instruction
- Reinforce ideas through constraints, not voice
- Gradually hand control to players
Standard
- Coaching volume decreases as KO approaches
- Final phase of warm-up is player-led
🕒 KO–10 MINUTES | FINAL REMINDER (≈1 MIN)
Focus: Trigger recall only
Coach Behaviour
- One sentence
- One reminder
- Calm delivery
Example
“Closed ball — we go together.”
Then silence.
Croatian Logic
- This is a cue, not a talk
🕒 KO–4 MINUTES | LEAVE CHANGE ROOM
Focus: Emotional control
Coach Behaviour
- No talking
- Calm body language
- Observe only
🕒 KO | KICK-OFF
Coach Role
- Observe
- Correct only when standards drop
- No constant instruction
If the coach is controlling the game verbally, the players are not.
MATCHDAY WARNINGS (NON-NEGOTIABLE)
For Coaches
- If you repeat yourself, you are talking too long
- If you add information late, it should not have been added at all
- If emotion rises, clarity drops
Red Flags
- Long pre-match speeches
- Tactical changes introduced on matchday
- Emotional hype before KO
These are anti-development behaviours.
CROATIAN MATCHDAY PHILOSOPHY (SUMMARY)
- Calm beats intensity
- Structure beats speeches
- Triggers beat shouting
- Restraint signals authority
Pre-match talks don’t add ideas — they remove doubt.

heading: U15 Game Framework
headingPath: COACH PACK 3 (U15) – TACTICAL INTEGRATION PHASE > 10. U15 Game Day Guidance > U15 Game Framework
content:
Game Framework – U14+ (11-a-Side)
- Base Shape: 1-4-3-3
- Key Roles:
- Pivot: Control rhythm, connect lines.
- 8s/10s: Create overloads, run beyond.
- Wingers: Stretch, attack assist zone.
- Back Four: Build triangles, defend compact.
- Team Instructions:
- Play through, not around.
- Open-ball = go forward.
- Compact when we lose it.
- Tempo control = identity.

heading: U15 Individual Competencies
headingPath: COACH PACK 3 (U15) – TACTICAL INTEGRATION PHASE > 11. U15 Phase Outcomes > U15 Individual Competencies
content:
Theme: Execution and control under match intensity.
By the end of this phase, the player can:
- Recognise and manage tempo (when to accelerate or slow play).
- Sustain one-touch quality under full-pressure tempo.
- Identify and exploit overloads using drive–draw–play.
- Read pressing triggers and initiate compact reactions.
- Recover structure within seconds after ball loss.
- Show discipline in maintaining distances and defensive shape.
- Make effective forward decisions in the final third.
- Perform technically clean actions under fatigue.
- Use both feet confidently in match tempo.
- Maintain emotional control in competitive conditions.
“Discipline under tempo defines tactical maturity.”

heading: U15 Group Competencies
headingPath: COACH PACK 3 (U15) – TACTICAL INTEGRATION PHASE > 11. U15 Phase Outcomes > U15 Group Competencies
content:
Collective Habits
Objective: Execute the collective model under full match tempo.
Team Competencies:
- Control rhythm across thirds (tempo shifts within possession).
- Build compactly with forward-thinking circulation.
- Execute pressing traps in wide or middle zones.
- Compact and expand collectively depending on phase.
- Maintain correct rest-defence (shape behind the ball).
- Recognise when to manage the game — protect a lead through tempo.
- Counter-attack through structure, not chaos.
- Maintain communication across units (verbal and gestural).
- Execute structured set-pieces with understanding of zones.
- Display discipline in compactness and distances for full 80–90 minutes.
“The team moves in rhythm, not emotion.”

heading: U15 sessions
headingPath: U15 sessions
content:
U15 – CYCLE 1Controlled Build-Up & Outlets (5 Weeks)
Week 1• Session 1 – Build-Up Recognition: Read the Press• Session 2 – Find the Open Ball
Week 2• Session 1 – Use the GK as Pivot• Session 2 – Create Exit Routes
Week 3• Session 1 – Manipulate the Press: Draw and Release• Session 2 – Control to Break

content:
BELCONNEN UNITED FC – SESSION PLAN | CYCLE 1 | WEEK 1 | SESSION 1
U15 | Controlled Build-Up & Outlets | Week 1 | Build-Up Recognition – Read the Press
Session Outcomes
Recognise pressing cues early and choose the correct build-up response as a connected team.
Activation – Uvodni dio / Game Activation (15 mins)
Setup
- Area: 30x25m, directional, one end target zone
- Players: 5v3 + GK (build-up team overload)
- Equipment: Balls, cones, 1 mini goal or target gate
Description
- Play starts with GK. Build-up team aims to pass through the target zone or mini goal.
- Pressing team presses actively; on regain they counter into the opposite end zone.
- Reset from GK after goal or out-of-play.
- Overload allows time to see who presses, from where, and with what speed.
Coaching Focus
Collective Cues
- Team spreads to show clear first and second options
- Ball-near players support short; far players stay available, not hiding
- Team pauses together when press is set, then plays when pressure commits
Individual Cues
- Scan early to identify the first presser
- First touch away from pressure
- If pressed fast, secure; if delayed, play forward
Progression
- Reduce space slightly to speed up pressing decisions
Situational Practice – Solving the Situation (20 mins)
Setup
- Area: 45x30m, split into two vertical corridors
- Players: 5v5 + GK
- Targets: Big goal vs 2 mini goals
- Transition: Immediate counter on regain
Description
- Build-up team attacks the big goal, opposition attacks mini goals.
- Pressing team chooses either high press or screen-and-delay.
- Build-up team must read the press and choose to play through, around, or recycle.
Coaching Focus
Collective Cues
- Team stays compact to support quick circulation
- When one corridor is pressed, opposite side stays open and ready
- Tempo changes together once the press is broken
Individual Cues
- Recognise press speed: sprint = bounce or switch; jog = turn and play
- Communicate pressure early
- Don’t rush forward if the press hasn’t committed
Progression
- Add a 6-second rule to attack after breaking the press
- Limit GK to two touches to increase realism
Situational Game – Collective Execution (25 mins)
Setup
- Area: 55x40m, full width
- Players: 6v6 + GK
- Goals: Full-size goals
Description
- All restarts begin with GK.
- Condition: Goal counts double if the team breaks the first line of pressure before scoring.
- Game demands real-time reading of press and collective response.
Coaching Focus
Collective Cues
- Team recognises pressing triggers and stays connected behind the ball
- Build-up attracts pressure to create space elsewhere
- Shape adjusts as the press changes
Individual Cues
- Receive side-on to see pressure and outlets
- Take responsibility to recycle when needed
- Play brave when the press is beaten
Progression
- Remove scoring condition to increase realism
Free Play – Transfer Game (20 mins)
Setup
- Area: 60x45m
- Players: 7v7 including GKs
- Directional game with normal rules
Description
- Free play with minimal intervention.
- Coach observes only.
Coaching Focus
- Observation only
- Look for players reading pressing cues, managing tempo, and staying connected
Coaching Information (U15)
Element | Detail
Objective / Outcomes | Read pressing cues and select the correct build-up solution under tempo
Technical / Tactical Detail | Scanning, body shape, spacing, timing of circulation, tempo control
Coaching Messages (Belco Voice) | “Read the press.” • “Compact to attack.” • “Open ball, play brave.”
Common Mistakes & Fixes | Forcing play into pressure → “What’s the press telling you?” • Team too stretched → “Reconnect first.”
Praise Language | “Good read.” • “Calm decision.” • “Well connected.”
Behaviours to Reward | Composure, communication, collective patience, intelligent bravery
Reflection Question | “How did you know when to play forward or recycle?”
Session Design Note | Overloads early support recognition; realism increases toward the end
Language Note | Belco Framework vocabulary used to support recognition
Belconnen United FC – Compact • Brave • Intelligent

content:
BELCONNEN UNITED FC – SESSION PLAN | CYCLE 1 | WEEK 1 | SESSION 2
U15 | Controlled Build-Up & Outlets | Week 1 | Build-Up Recognition – Find the Open Ball
Session Outcomes
Recognise the open ball in build-up and connect as a team to play forward with control.
Activation – Uvodni dio / Game Activation (15 mins)
Setup
- Area: 35x25m, rectangular, split into two horizontal zones
- Players: 6v4 (build-up team of 6 incl. GK vs 4 pressers)
- Equipment: Balls, cones, 2 mini goals on halfway line
Description
- Play starts with GK. Build-up team aims to play through or around the press and score in either mini goal.
- Pressing team wins the ball and attacks the big goal immediately.
- Reset from GK after goal or out-of-play.
- Numerical overload (6v4) supports early success and calm recognition of the open ball.
Coaching Focus
Collective Cues
- Back line stays connected; distances allow support behind and beside the ball
- Ball-side support is close, far-side players stay available but patient
- Team recognises when the ball is free and plays forward together
Individual Cues
- First touch prepares the next action
- Scan before receiving to see pressure and outlets
- Play brave when the ball is open; secure when it’s closed
Progression
- Reduce area slightly to increase pressure and speed of decision
Situational Practice – Solving the Situation (20 mins)
Setup
- Area: 45x30m, directional
- Players: 5v5 + 1 neutral (build-up team includes GK)
- Targets: Big goal vs 2 mini goals
- Transition: Immediate counter-attack on regain
Description
- Build-up team attacks the big goal, opposition attacks mini goals.
- Neutral supports the team in possession, creating soft overloads.
- Focus on recognising when the press is committed and switching or finding the free player.
Coaching Focus
Collective Cues
- Shape stays compact to allow quick support under pressure
- When one side is closed, the team shifts to open the far side
- Tempo changes together once the open ball is found
Individual Cues
- Be patient under pressure; don’t force the first option
- Move early to show for the next pass
- Play forward when space opens, not after it closes
Progression
- Remove the neutral to move toward equality
- Add a 5-second rule to attack after breaking the press
Situational Game – Collective Execution (25 mins)
Setup
- Area: 55x40m, full width
- Players: 6v6 + GK
- Goals: Full-size goals with GKs
Description
- Game-realistic build-up from GK every restart.
- Condition: Goals count double if the team switches play or breaks a line in build-up before scoring.
- Focus on collective recognition and timing, not speed alone.
Coaching Focus
Collective Cues
- Team stays compact to support the ball and control rebounds
- Build-up side recognises when to attract pressure and release
- Movement ahead of the ball creates clear outlets
Individual Cues
- Receive side-on to see the next pass
- Communicate early to help the ball carrier
- Take responsibility to play when the moment is right
Progression
- Remove scoring condition to increase realism and free decision-making
Free Play – Transfer Game (20 mins)
Setup
- Area: 60x45m
- Players: 7v7 including GKs
- Directional game with normal rules
Description
- Free play with minimal conditions.
- Coach observes only; allow the game to flow.
Coaching Focus
- Observation only
- Look for players recognising the open ball, managing tempo, and staying connected under pressure
Coaching Information (U15)
Element | Detail
Objective / Outcomes | Recognise pressure, find the open ball, and connect as a unit in build-up
Technical / Tactical Detail | Spacing between lines, body shape to receive, timing of support, tempo control
Coaching Messages (Belco Voice) | “Open ball, play brave.” • “Compact to attack.” • “Control the game.”
Common Mistakes & Fixes | Forcing forward pass → “Secure it, then switch.” • Team stretched → “Reconnect behind the ball.”
Praise Language | “Good patience.” • “Brave decision.” • “Well connected.”
Behaviours to Reward | Composure, compact support, communication, intelligent risk
Reflection Question | “What told you the ball was open in build-up?”
Session Design Note | Early overloads support clarity; realism increases toward the end
Language Note | Belco Framework vocabulary used to support recognition
Belconnen United FC – Compact • Brave • Intelligent

content:
BELCONNEN UNITED FC – SESSION PLAN | CYCLE 1 | WEEK 2 | SESSION 1
U15 | Controlled Build-Up & Outlets | Week 2 | Use the GK as Pivot
Session Outcomes
Use the goalkeeper as a pivot to secure build-up, connect the team, and control tempo under pressure.
Activation – Uvodni dio / Game Activation (15 mins)
Setup
- Area: 30x25m, rectangular, directional
- Players: 5v3 + GK (build-up team overload)
- Equipment: Balls, cones, 2 mini goals on halfway line
Description
- Play starts with GK every time. Build-up team scores by playing into either mini goal.
- Pressing team presses to win and counters immediately into the opposite mini goal.
- GK acts as a free pivot to recycle, switch, or draw pressure.
- Overload supports calm decisions and early success using the GK.
Coaching Focus
Collective Cues
- Team stays connected to the GK with short, clear angles
- When the ball is closed, the team resets through the GK together
- Shape stays compact to support quick circulation
Individual Cues
- Show early to support the GK, don’t hide behind markers
- First touch opens the body to see both sides
- Be brave to play back to control the game
Progression
- Limit GK to two touches to increase tempo
Situational Practice – Solving the Situation (20 mins)
Setup
- Area: 45x30m, full width
- Players: 5v5 + GK
- Targets: Big goal vs 2 mini goals
- Transition: Immediate counter-attack on regain
Description
- Build-up team attacks the big goal; opposition attacks mini goals.
- GK must be used at least once in the build-up before scoring.
- Focus on recognising when to play forward and when to reset through the GK.
Coaching Focus
Collective Cues
- Back line stays split to give the GK clear passing lanes
- Team shifts together when the ball goes back to the GK
- Tempo slows through the GK, then accelerates forward
Individual Cues
- Scan before passing back to set the next action
- Communicate early with the GK
- Don’t rush forward options if the press is set
Progression
- Remove mandatory GK touch to increase realism
- Add a 6-second rule to attack after breaking the press
Situational Game – Collective Execution (25 mins)
Setup
- Area: 55x40m
- Players: 6v6 + GK
- Goals: Full-size goals with GKs
Description
- All restarts from the GK.
- Condition: Goals count double if the team resets through the GK before progressing to score.
- Game tests collective trust in the GK under match tempo.
Coaching Focus
Collective Cues
- Team recognises pressure and chooses to recycle together
- GK acts as the spare player to control rhythm
- Distances stay short to protect against transition
Individual Cues
- Receive with body shape to play forward or back
- Stay disciplined in position during recycle
- Play brave when the press opens
Progression
- Remove scoring condition to fully normalise decision-making
Free Play – Transfer Game (20 mins)
Setup
- Area: 60x45m
- Players: 7v7 including GKs
- Directional game with normal rules
Description
- Free play with minimal conditions.
- Coach observes only.
Coaching Focus
- Observation only
- Look for players using the GK naturally to manage tempo and stay connected
Coaching Information (U15)
Element | Detail
Objective / Outcomes | Use the GK to secure build-up, reset play, and control game tempo
Technical / Tactical Detail | Angles to GK, body shape, timing of recycle, compact support
Coaching Messages (Belco Voice) | “Use the GK.” • “Compact to attack.” • “Control the game.”
Common Mistakes & Fixes | Avoiding the GK → “Reset and reconnect.” • Rushing forward → “Secure first.”
Praise Language | “Good control.” • “Calm reset.” • “Well connected.”
Behaviours to Reward | Composure, trust in teammates, communication, intelligent decisions
Reflection Question | “When did using the GK help us control the game?”
Session Design Note | Overloads early support clarity; realism increases toward the end
Language Note | Belco Framework vocabulary used to support recognition
Belconnen United FC – Compact • Brave • Intelligent

content:
BELCONNEN UNITED FC – SESSION PLAN | CYCLE 1 | WEEK 2 | SESSION 2
U15 | Controlled Build-Up & Outlets | Week 2 | Create Exit Routes
Session Outcomes
Create and use clear exit routes in build-up to escape pressure and progress as a connected team.
Activation – Uvodni dio / Game Activation (15 mins)
Setup
- Area: 35x25m, rectangular, directional
- Players: 6v4 + GK (build-up team overload)
- Equipment: Balls, cones, 2 wide target gates
Description
- Play starts with GK. Build-up team scores by playing through either wide gate.
- Pressing team presses to win and counters into the opposite end zone.
- Overload supports early success in identifying first exit options under pressure.
Coaching Focus
Collective Cues
- Team shapes early to offer at least two exit routes
- Ball-near support stays close; far side holds width and patience
- When pressure comes central, exits are created outside or behind
Individual Cues
- Move early to show as an outlet
- Body shape open to play forward or wide
- If the route is blocked, recycle quickly
Progression
- Reduce space to increase pressure and speed of execution
Situational Practice – Solving the Situation (20 mins)
Setup
- Area: 45x30m, split into three vertical channels
- Players: 5v5 + GK
- Targets: Big goal vs 2 mini goals
- Transition: Immediate counter-attack on regain
Description
- Build-up team attacks the big goal; opposition attacks mini goals.
- Goals count only if the team uses an exit route (wide or bounce back) before finishing.
- Channels guide recognition without fixing players to positions.
Coaching Focus
Collective Cues
- One player attracts pressure; others move to create the exit
- Team shifts together when the ball moves into an exit channel
- Exit is used to progress, not just to escape
Individual Cues
- Scan to see which route is open
- Take the first safe forward option after exit
- Communicate early to support the ball carrier
Progression
- Remove channel markings to increase realism
- Remove scoring condition once behaviour is consistent
Situational Game – Collective Execution (25 mins)
Setup
- Area: 55x40m, full width
- Players: 6v6 + GK
- Goals: Full-size goals with GKs
Description
- All restarts from GK.
- Condition: Goals count double if the team breaks pressure using a clear exit route in build-up.
- Game demands collective movement and timing to create and use exits.
Coaching Focus
Collective Cues
- Team stays compact to support exits and protect transitions
- Exit routes are prepared before pressure arrives
- Tempo increases once the exit is used
Individual Cues
- Receive side-on to see the next action
- Be brave to play out of pressure when the route is on
- Stay disciplined if your role is to hold space
Progression
- Remove scoring condition to fully normalise decision-making
Free Play – Transfer Game (20 mins)
Setup
- Area: 60x45m
- Players: 7v7 including GKs
- Directional game with normal rules
Description
- Free play with minimal conditions.
- Coach observes only.
Coaching Focus
- Observation only
- Look for players creating exits naturally and progressing with control under pressure
Coaching Information (U15)
Element | Detail
Objective / Outcomes | Create and use exit routes to escape pressure and progress play
Technical / Tactical Detail | Angles of support, spacing, body shape, timing of movement, tempo control
Coaching Messages (Belco Voice) | “Create an exit.” • “Compact to attack.” • “Open ball, play brave.”
Common Mistakes & Fixes | Standing still → “Move to create the route.” • Forcing central play → “Where’s the exit?”
Praise Language | “Good movement.” • “Clear exit.” • “Calm progression.”
Behaviours to Reward | Initiative, communication, composure, intelligent risk
Reflection Question | “What helped us create the exit under pressure?”
Session Design Note | Overloads early support clarity; realism increases toward the end
Language Note | Belco Framework vocabulary used to support recognition
Belconnen United FC – Compact • Brave • Intelligent

content:
BELCONNEN UNITED FC – SESSION PLAN | CYCLE 1 | WEEK 3 | SESSION 1
U15 | Controlled Build-Up & Outlets | Week 3 | Manipulate the Press – Draw and Release
Session Outcomes
Attract pressure deliberately and release the ball at the right moment to progress as a connected team.
Activation – Uvodni dio / Game Activation (15 mins)
Setup
- Area: 35x25m, rectangular, directional
- Players: 6v4 + GK (build-up team overload)
- Equipment: Balls, cones, 2 wide target gates
Description
- Play starts with the GK. Build-up team scores by playing through either wide gate.
- Pressing team looks to win and counter into a central end zone.
- Build-up team is encouraged to hold the ball briefly to attract pressure, then release to the free side.
- Overload supports calm manipulation of the press without rushing decisions.
Coaching Focus
Collective Cues
- Team stays compact so pressure comes toward the ball
- Ball-side players invite the press; far side stays available and patient
- Release happens once the press commits, not before
Individual Cues
- Carry or hold the ball to fix the presser
- Scan while holding to see the free option
- Play brave when the moment to release is clear
Progression
- Reduce space to increase pressing intensity and timing demands
Situational Practice – Solving the Situation (20 mins)
Setup
- Area: 45x30m, split into two vertical corridors
- Players: 5v5 + GK
- Targets: Big goal vs 2 mini goals
- Transition: Immediate counter-attack on regain
Description
- Build-up team attacks the big goal; opposition attacks mini goals.
- Team must draw pressure in one corridor before switching or releasing into the other.
- Corridors guide recognition without locking players into fixed positions.
Coaching Focus
Collective Cues
- Team shifts together to overload the ball side
- Free side players time movement to receive after release
- Tempo changes as a group once the press is broken
Individual Cues
- Be comfortable holding the ball under pressure
- Move early to become the release option
- Don’t force the release if pressure hasn’t committed
Progression
- Remove corridor markings to increase realism
- Add a 6-second rule to attack after release
Situational Game – Collective Execution (25 mins)
Setup
- Area: 55x40m, full width
- Players: 6v6 + GK
- Goals: Full-size goals with GKs
Description
- All restarts from GK.
- Condition: Goals count double if the team clearly attracts pressure before switching or breaking a line.
- Game demands collective patience, timing, and connection.
Coaching Focus
Collective Cues
- Team recognises when to slow the game to draw the press
- Distances stay short to support the ball under pressure
- Release triggers movement ahead of the ball
Individual Cues
- Receive side-on to protect the ball
- Communicate when you’re free to receive
- Take responsibility to play when the press is beaten
Progression
- Remove scoring condition to normalise decision-making
Free Play – Transfer Game (20 mins)
Setup
- Area: 60x45m
- Players: 7v7 including GKs
- Directional game with normal rules
Description
- Free play with minimal conditions.
- Coach observes only.
Coaching Focus
- Observation only
- Look for players manipulating pressure, releasing at the right moment, and managing tempo
Coaching Information (U15)
Element | Detail
Objective / Outcomes | Draw the press and release at the right moment to progress play
Technical / Tactical Detail | Ball retention under pressure, spacing, timing of movement, tempo control
Coaching Messages (Belco Voice) | “Draw them in.” • “Release together.” • “Control the game.”
Common Mistakes & Fixes | Releasing too early → “Hold it, invite them.” • Team too spread → “Reconnect first.”
Praise Language | “Good patience.” • “Well timed release.” • “Brave control.”
Behaviours to Reward | Composure, connection, communication, intelligent bravery
Reflection Question | “What showed you the press was ready to be released?”
Session Design Note | Overloads early support clarity; realism increases toward the end
Language Note | Belco Framework vocabulary used to support recognition
Belconnen United FC – Compact • Brave • Intelligent

content:
BELCONNEN UNITED FC – SESSION PLAN | CYCLE 1 | WEEK 3 | SESSION 2
U15 | Controlled Build-Up & Outlets | Week 3 | Control to Break
Session Outcomes
Control possession to fix the opponent, then break lines with timing and collective support.
Activation – Uvodni dio / Game Activation (15 mins)
Setup
- Area: 35x25m, rectangular, directional
- Players: 6v4 + GK (build-up team overload)
- Equipment: Balls, cones, 2 mini goals on halfway line
Description
- Play starts with GK. Build-up team scores by breaking the pressing line and playing into either mini goal.
- Pressing team presses to win and counters immediately into a central end zone.
- Build-up team must circulate first to settle the ball before looking to break.
- Overload supports calm control before acceleration.
Coaching Focus
Collective Cues
- Team stays compact to secure the ball under pressure
- Circulation moves the press side to side before the break
- Breaking action is supported immediately behind and ahead
Individual Cues
- First touch protects the ball
- Scan to recognise when defenders are fixed
- Break forward when space opens, not when crowded
Progression
- Reduce space to increase tempo and pressing pressure
Situational Practice – Solving the Situation (20 mins)
Setup
- Area: 45x30m, split into two horizontal zones
- Players: 5v5 + GK
- Targets: Big goal vs 2 mini goals
- Transition: Immediate counter-attack on regain
Description
- Build-up team attacks the big goal; opposition attacks mini goals.
- Team must complete a minimum number of passes in the back zone before breaking into the attacking zone.
- Focus is on patience first, then decisive forward action.
Coaching Focus
Collective Cues
- Team recognises when the opponent is organised vs stretched
- Support players position to secure the ball after the break
- Tempo changes together from control to speed
Individual Cues
- Don’t rush the forward option
- Move early to support the breaking action
- Be disciplined if your role is to hold balance
Progression
- Remove pass requirement once behaviour is consistent
- Allow free movement between zones
Situational Game – Collective Execution (25 mins)
Setup
- Area: 55x40m, full width
- Players: 6v6 + GK
- Goals: Full-size goals with GKs
Description
- All restarts from GK.
- Condition: Goals count double if the team controls possession before breaking a line to score.
- Game tests patience, timing, and collective recognition under match tempo.
Coaching Focus
Collective Cues
- Team stays connected during circulation
- Breaking actions are supported immediately
- Compactness protects against counter-attack after the break
Individual Cues
- Receive side-on to play forward quickly
- Take responsibility to break when the moment is clear
- Recover quickly if the break fails
Progression
- Remove scoring condition to increase realism
Free Play – Transfer Game (20 mins)
Setup
- Area: 60x45m
- Players: 7v7 including GKs
- Directional game with normal rules
Description
- Free play with minimal intervention.
- Coach observes only.
Coaching Focus
- Observation only
- Look for players controlling rhythm, fixing opponents, and breaking lines with support
Coaching Information (U15)
Element | Detail
Objective / Outcomes | Control possession to fix the opponent, then break lines collectively
Technical / Tactical Detail | Ball security, spacing, timing of support, tempo change, compactness
Coaching Messages (Belco Voice) | “Control the game.” • “Fix them, then break.” • “Compact to attack.”
Common Mistakes & Fixes | Forcing the break → “Set it first.” • No support after break → “Follow the play.”
Praise Language | “Good patience.” • “Well timed break.” • “Connected.”
Behaviours to Reward | Composure, collective timing, communication, intelligent bravery
Reflection Question | “What told you it was the right moment to break?”
Session Design Note | Overloads early support clarity; realism increases toward the end
Language Note | Belco Framework vocabulary used to support recognition
Belconnen United FC – Compact • Brave • Intelligent

- Play starts with the GK. Build-up team scores by playing through either wide gate.

- Transition: Immediate counter-attack on regain

- Team shifts together as the ball moves across the pitch

Setup
- Area: 60x45m
- Players: 7v7 including GKs
- Directional game with normal rules
Description
- Free play with minimal intervention.
- Coach observes only.
Coaching Focus
- Observation only
- Look for players using width naturally to progress and manage tempo
Coaching Information (U15)
Element | Detail
Objective / Outcomes | Use width in build-up to stretch the press and progress play
Technical / Tactical Detail | Width and spacing, body shape, timing of switch, collective support
Coaching Messages (Belco Voice) | “Use the width.” • “Open ball, play brave.” • “Compact to attack.”
Common Mistakes & Fixes | Crowding central areas → “Stretch them wide.” • Late switches → “Move it earlier.”
Praise Language | “Good width.” • “Well switched.” • “Calm control.”
Behaviours to Reward | Patience, communication, collective movement, intelligent risk

content:
BELCONNEN UNITED FC – SESSION PLAN | CYCLE 1 | WEEK 4 | SESSION 2
U15 | Controlled Build-Up & Outlets | Week 4 | Play Brave Under Pressure
Session Outcomes
Make brave, controlled decisions under pressure while staying connected as a team.
Activation – Uvodni dio / Game Activation (15 mins)
Setup
- Area: 30x25m, rectangular, directional
- Players: 6v4 + GK (build-up team overload)
- Equipment: Balls, cones, 2 mini goals on halfway line
Description
- Play starts with GK. Build-up team scores by playing into either mini goal.
- Pressing team presses aggressively; on regain they counter into a central end zone.
- Build-up team is encouraged to play forward when possible, but secure the ball if the press closes.
- Overload supports confidence to attempt brave actions without panic.
Coaching Focus
Collective Cues
- Team stays compact to give the ball carrier support under pressure
- Passing options are set before the ball arrives
- When the ball is open, the team steps forward together
Individual Cues
- Protect the ball with body shape
- Scan early to recognise pressure and space
- Play brave when supported; recycle if isolated
Progression
- Reduce space to increase pressure and decision speed
Situational Practice – Solving the Situation (20 mins)
Setup
- Area: 45x30m, directional
- Players: 5v5 + GK
- Targets: Big goal vs 2 mini goals
- Transition: Immediate counter-attack on regain
Description
- Build-up team attacks the big goal; opposition attacks mini goals.
- Pressing team is encouraged to press high and fast.
- Build-up team must decide when to play through pressure and when to reset to keep control.
Coaching Focus
Collective Cues
- Support stays close to allow brave forward passes
- Team reacts together after a risky action—either to progress or to recover
- Tempo is managed collectively, not forced individually
Individual Cues
- Take responsibility to receive under pressure
- First touch sets up the next action
- Stay calm after mistakes and reconnect
Progression
- Add a 6-second rule to attack after breaking pressure
- Remove GK touch limits to increase realism
Situational Game – Collective Execution (25 mins)
Setup
- Area: 55x40m, full width
- Players: 6v6 + GK
- Goals: Full-size goals with GKs
Description
- All restarts from GK.
- Condition: Goals count double if the team plays forward through pressure in build-up before scoring.
- Game demands courage, connection, and decision-making under match tempo.
Coaching Focus
Collective Cues
- Team recognises moments to step in and support the ball
- Compactness protects against immediate transition after loss
- Brave decisions are backed up by movement and communication
Individual Cues
- Receive side-on to play forward quickly
- Don’t hide after a mistake—show again
- Choose bravery with awareness, not risk for risk’s sake
Progression
- Remove scoring condition to fully normalise behaviour
Free Play – Transfer Game (20 mins)
Setup
- Area: 60x45m
- Players: 7v7 including GKs
- Directional game with normal rules
Description
- Free play with minimal conditions.
- Coach observes only.
Coaching Focus
- Observation only
- Look for players showing bravery under pressure while staying connected and composed
Coaching Information (U15)
Element | Detail
Objective / Outcomes | Play forward bravely under pressure while maintaining collective control
Technical / Tactical Detail | Body shape, support angles, decision timing, compactness, tempo control
Coaching Messages (Belco Voice) | “Open ball, play brave.” • “Stay connected.” • “Control the game.”
Common Mistakes & Fixes | Hiding from the ball → “Show again.” • Forcing play alone → “Where’s your support?”
Praise Language | “Brave choice.” • “Good composure.” • “Well supported.”
Behaviours to Reward | Courage, resilience, communication, intelligent decision-making
Reflection Question | “What helped you decide to play forward under pressure?”
Session Design Note | Overloads early support confidence; realism and pressure increase toward the end
Language Note | Belco Framework vocabulary used to support recognition

Connect the whole team in build-up to control the game and progress collectively under match pressure.

Collective Cues
- Team stays compact from GK to highest player
- Distances allow the ball to move faster than the press
- Shape adjusts together as the ball travels
Individual Cues
- Scan to see both pressure and teammates

Collective Cues

- Tempo increases only when the team is set
Individual Cues

Collective Cues

- Whole team reacts together on loss or progression

content:
BELCONNEN UNITED FC – SESSION PLAN | CYCLE 1 | WEEK 5 | SESSION 2
U15 | Controlled Build-Up & Outlets | Week 5 | Rhythm to Break Lines
Session Outcomes
Control rhythm in build-up to draw pressure and break lines at the right moment as a team.
Activation – Uvodni dio / Game Activation (15 mins)
Setup
- Area: 35x25m, rectangular, directional
- Players: 6v4 + GK (build-up team overload)
- Equipment: Balls, cones, 2 mini goals on halfway line
Description
- Play starts with GK. Build-up team scores by breaking a line and playing into either mini goal.
- Pressing team presses actively; on regain they counter into a central end zone.
- Build-up team must circulate calmly before accelerating forward.
- Overload supports early rhythm control and recognition of when to speed up.
Coaching Focus
Collective Cues
- Team stays compact to allow controlled circulation
- Ball speed changes move the press, not panic it
- Forward action happens once pressure is fixed
Individual Cues
- First touch sets tempo
- Scan to recognise when defenders step or hold
- Play brave when the line opens
Progression
- Reduce space to increase tempo demands and pressure
Situational Practice – Solving the Situation (20 mins)
Setup
- Area: 45x30m, split into two horizontal zones
- Players: 5v5 + GK
- Targets: Big goal vs 2 mini goals
- Transition: Immediate counter-attack on regain
Description
- Build-up team attacks the big goal; opposition attacks mini goals.
- Team must circulate in the back zone before breaking into the attacking zone.
- Focus on slowing the game to set rhythm, then accelerating to break lines.
Coaching Focus
Collective Cues
- Team recognises when to slow play to draw pressure
- Support players position to receive after the break
- Tempo change is collective, not individual
Individual Cues
- Don’t rush the forward pass
- Move early to support the breaking action
- Stay disciplined if your role is to balance behind the ball
Progression
- Remove zone restriction once rhythm control is consistent
- Add a 6-second rule to attack after breaking the line
Situational Game – Collective Execution (25 mins)
Setup
- Area: 55x40m, full width
- Players: 6v6 + GK
- Goals: Full-size goals with GKs
Description
- All restarts from GK.
- Condition: Goals count double if the team clearly controls rhythm before breaking a line to score.
- Game demands timing, patience, and collective awareness under match tempo.
Coaching Focus
Collective Cues
- Team stays connected during circulation
- Breaking actions are supported immediately
- Compactness protects against transition after the break
Individual Cues
- Receive side-on to play forward quickly
- Take responsibility to break when the moment is clear
- Recover quickly if the forward action fails
Progression
- Remove scoring condition to increase realism
Free Play – Transfer Game (20 mins)
Setup
- Area: 60x45m
- Players: 7v7 including GKs
- Directional game with normal rules
Description
- Free play with minimal intervention.
- Coach observes only.
Coaching Focus
- Observation only
- Look for players managing rhythm, fixing opponents, and breaking lines with support
Coaching Information (U15)
Element | Detail
Objective / Outcomes | Control rhythm to draw pressure and break lines at the right moment
Technical / Tactical Detail | Ball speed, spacing, timing of movement, tempo change, compactness
Coaching Messages (Belco Voice) | “Control the game.” • “Set it, then break.” • “Compact to attack.”
Common Mistakes & Fixes | Forcing tempo → “Slow it first.” • No support after break → “Follow the play.”
Praise Language | “Good rhythm.” • “Well timed.” • “Connected.”
Behaviours to Reward | Composure, collective timing, communication, intelligent bravery
Reflection Question | “What told you it was time to change the tempo?”
Session Design Note | Overloads early support clarity; realism increases toward the end
Language Note | Belco Framework vocabulary used to support recognition
