content:
BELCONNEN UNITED FC – SESSION PLAN | CYCLE 7 | WEEK 3 | SESSION 1
U15 | Game Management & Tempo | Week 3 | Tactical Fouls and Compact Recovery
Session Outcomes
Recognise when to stop a dangerous transition legally and recover quickly into a compact team shape.
Activation – Uvodni dio / Game Activation (15 mins)
Setup
- Area: 30x25m, directional
- Players: 5v3 (possession team) vs 3 defenders
- Equipment: Balls, cones, 2 mini goals at each end
Description
- Possession team keeps the ball and scores in either mini goal.
- Defenders press to regain.
- On loss, the team that lost the ball must decide to delay legally, contain, or recover shape.
- Coach frames “tactical foul” as last-resort, legal contact to stop a clear transition when recovery is impossible.
- Overload supports early clarity in decision-making.
Coaching Focus
Collective Cues
- Nearest player delays; others recover inside
- Team collapses quickly to protect central space
- Recovery into compact shape happens immediately after the stop
Individual Cues
- Read if recovery is possible before committing
- Use body position to slow or block, not reckless contact
- Recover goal-side immediately after the action
Progression
- Reduce space to increase speed of transition decisions
Situational Practice – Solving the Situation (20 mins)
Setup
- Area: 45x30m, directional
- Players: 5v5
- Targets: Big goal vs 2 mini goals
- Transition Rule: On loss, defenders choose delay/recover or controlled stop if outnumbered
Description
- One team attacks the big goal.
- On losing possession, the attacking team must manage the defensive transition.
- If outnumbered and the opponent is breaking centrally, a controlled stop (legal contact, blocking run) is allowed to halt momentum.
- Team then recovers into a compact block quickly.
Coaching Focus
Collective Cues
- Team recognises danger moments together
- Central recovery happens immediately after the stop
- Lines reconnect quickly to deny next action
Individual Cues
- Don’t dive in—delay if support is arriving
- If stopping play, make it safe and controlled
- React instantly after the whistle or stop
Progression
- Add a 5-second rule for attackers to exploit transition moments
Situational Game – Collective Execution (25 mins)
Setup
- Area: 55x40m
- Players: 6v6 + GK
- Goals: Full-size goals with GKs
Description
- All restarts from GK.
- Condition: Defensive team scores double if they stop a dangerous transition and recover into a compact shape within five seconds.
- Emphasis is on game management, not constant fouling.
Coaching Focus
Collective Cues
- Team identifies when recovery is possible vs when danger must be stopped
- Compact shape is restored immediately after the action
- Central protection remains the priority
Individual Cues
- Make smart decisions under pressure
- Stay calm and disciplined in contact
- Organise teammates quickly after the stop
Progression
- Remove scoring condition to increase realism
Free Play – Transfer Game (20 mins)
Setup
- Area: 60x45m
- Players: 7v7 including GKs
- Directional game with normal rules
Description
- Free play with minimal intervention.
Coaching Focus
- Observation only
- Look for players managing transitions intelligently: delaying, stopping danger when needed, and recovering shape quickly
Coaching Information (U15)
Element | Detail
Objective / Outcomes | Manage dangerous transitions through intelligent stopping and compact recovery
Technical / Tactical Detail | Delay vs stop decisions, recovery runs, central protection, compact shape
Coaching Messages (Belco Voice) | “Delay or stop.” • “Protect the middle.” • “Recover together.”
Common Mistakes & Fixes | Reckless challenge → “Can you delay?” • No recovery → “Get back inside.”
Praise Language | “Smart decision.” • “Good recovery.” • “Well managed.”
Behaviours to Reward | Awareness, discipline, composure, collective responsibility
Reflection Question | “What told us we needed to stop the transition instead of chasing?”
Session Design Note | Focus is on game management and recovery, not encouraging fouls
Language Note | Belco Framework vocabulary used to support recognition
Belconnen United FC – Compact • Brave • Intelligent

BELCONNEN UNITED FC – SESSION PLAN | CYCLE 7 | WEEK 3 | SESSION 2
U15 | Game Management & Tempo | Week 3 | Emotional Control
Session Outcomes
Maintain emotional control under pressure to make clear decisions, protect team shape, and manage the game.
Activation – Uvodni dio / Game Activation (15 mins)
Setup
- Area: 30x25m, directional
- Players: 5v3 (possession team) vs 3 defenders
- Equipment: Balls, cones, 2 mini goals at each end
Description
- Possession team keeps the ball and scores in either mini goal.
- Defenders press to regain.
- Coach introduces mild stressors (score kept, countdowns, quick restarts).
- Focus is on staying calm after mistakes, fouls, or lost duels.
- Overload supports early success while emotions are tested.
Coaching Focus
Collective Cues
- Team resets shape quickly after errors
- Players support each other verbally after setbacks
- Compactness is prioritised over reaction
Individual Cues
- First response after a mistake is recovery, not reaction
- Breathe, scan, and play the next action
- Avoid emotional fouls or rushed decisions
Progression
- Reduce space or add time pressure to increase emotional stress
Situational Practice – Solving the Situation (20 mins)
Setup
- Area: 45x30m, directional
- Players: 5v5
- Targets: Big goal vs 2 mini goals
- Transition Rule: After a foul, turnover, or missed chance, play restarts immediately
Description
- One team attacks the big goal; the other defends.
- Emotional moments are created through quick restarts and contested duels.
- Teams must reorganise quickly and continue playing with discipline.
- Focus is on controlling reactions to refereeing decisions, mistakes, and pressure.
Coaching Focus
Collective Cues
- Team regroups immediately after emotional moments
- Shape and roles are restored quickly
- Communication remains calm and constructive
Individual Cues
- Let go of the previous action
- Stay disciplined in contact and challenges
- Make the simple decision under pressure
Progression
- Add score targets or consequence for emotional reactions (free kick, restart advantage)
Situational Game – Collective Execution (25 mins)
Setup
- Area: 55x40m
- Players: 6v6 + GK
- Goals: Full-size goals with GKs
Description
- All restarts from GK.
- Coach allows physical play and quick decisions to test emotional control.
- Condition: Goals count double if scored after the team shows clear composure following a mistake, foul, or lost duel.
- Game demands calm leadership and shared control under fatigue.
Coaching Focus
Collective Cues
- Team stays connected after emotional triggers
- Leaders help reset tempo and shape
- Compactness and structure calm the game
Individual Cues
- Control body language
- Focus on the next action only
- Choose discipline over frustration
Progression
- Remove scoring condition to increase realism
Free Play – Transfer Game (20 mins)
Setup
- Area: 60x45m
- Players: 7v7 including GKs
- Directional game with normal rules
Description
- Free play with minimal intervention.
Coaching Focus
- Observation only
- Look for players staying calm after mistakes, managing tempo, and supporting teammates emotionally
Coaching Information (U15)
Element | Detail
Objective / Outcomes | Maintain emotional control to support clear decision-making
Technical / Tactical Detail | Recovery after error, body language, simple decision choice, compact reset
Coaching Messages (Belco Voice) | “Next action.” • “Stay calm.” • “Control the game.”
Common Mistakes & Fixes | Complaining → “Play on.” • Rushing → “Breathe and reset.”
Praise Language | “Good composure.” • “Strong response.” • “Well managed.”
Behaviours to Reward | Composure, leadership, discipline, positive communication
Reflection Question | “How did staying calm help the team in tough moments?”
Session Design Note | Emotional control underpins all game management behaviours
Language Note | Belco Framework vocabulary used to support recognition

BELCONNEN UNITED FC – SESSION PLAN | CYCLE 7 | WEEK 4 | SESSION 1
U15 | Game Management & Tempo | Week 4 | Close the Game
Session Outcomes
Manage the final phase of the game by protecting the lead, controlling tempo, and defending with discipline and intelligence.
Activation – Uvodni dio / Game Activation (15 mins)
Setup
Area: 30x25m, directional
Players: 5v3 (possession team) vs 3 defenders
Equipment: Balls, cones, 2 mini goals at each end
Description
Possession team starts 1–0 up and aims to keep the lead by managing the ball.
Defending team presses to win and score.
Focus for possession team: keep the ball, draw pressure, and reset shape.
Overload supports early success in calm decision-making under “end-game” pressure.
Coaching Focus
Collective Cues
Team stays compact around the ball
Support angles are short and safe
Shape is prioritised over forcing forward play
Individual Cues
Protect the ball with first touch
Choose safe options under pressure
Recover position immediately after passing
Progression
Reduce space or add a countdown to increase pressure
Situational Practice – Solving the Situation (20 mins)
Setup
Area: 45x30m, directional
Players: 5v5
Targets: Big goal vs 2 mini goals
Scenario: One team leads by one goal with 5 “minutes” remaining
Description
Leading team must manage the game: keep possession, slow tempo, and defend compactly when needed.
Trailing team presses aggressively to score.
On regain, leading team looks to secure the ball first before attacking space.
Practice focuses on calm execution under scoreboard pressure.
Coaching Focus
Collective Cues
Team recognises when to keep the ball vs clear danger
Compact shape is restored quickly after loss
Tempo is slowed through possession and positioning
Individual Cues
Don’t force risky passes
Use the body to shield and draw fouls legally
Stay disciplined when defending the lead
Progression
Limit touches for the trailing team to increase pressing intensity
Situational Game – Collective Execution (25 mins)
Setup
Area: 55x40m
Players: 6v6 + GK
Goals: Full-size goals with GKs
Description
Game starts with one team leading by one goal.
All restarts from GK.
Condition: Leading team scores double if they maintain the lead through controlled possession and compact defending.
Emphasis is on decision-making, discipline, and emotional control.
Coaching Focus
Collective Cues
Team manages tempo deliberately
Compactness increases as pressure rises
Clear communication to organise and calm the group
Individual Cues
Make simple, reliable decisions
Stay focused on your role
Avoid emotional fouls or rushed clearances
Progression
Remove scoring condition to fully normalise behaviour
Free Play – Transfer Game (20 mins)
Setup
Area: 60x45m
Players: 7v7 including GKs
Directional game with normal rules
Description
Free play with minimal intervention.
Coaching Focus
Observation only
Look for players managing the game: slowing tempo, protecting space, and staying disciplined under pressure
Coaching Information (U15)
Element | Detail
Objective / Outcomes | Protect a lead through tempo control and compact defending
Technical / Tactical Detail | Ball protection, support angles, compact recovery, time management
Coaching Messages (Belco Voice) | “Control the game.” • “Value the ball.” • “Stay compact.”
Common Mistakes & Fixes | Forcing forward play → “Keep it.” • Dropping too deep → “Hold the line.”
Praise Language | “Calm decision.” • “Well managed.” • “Disciplined play.”
Behaviours to Reward | Composure, discipline, communication, intelligent risk management
Reflection Question | “What helped us protect the lead without panicking?”
Session Design Note | End-game scenarios build composure and decision-making under pressure
Language Note | Belco Framework vocabulary used to support recognition

content:
BELCONNEN UNITED FC – SESSION PLAN | CYCLE 7 | WEEK 4 | SESSION 2
U15 | Game Management & Tempo | Week 4 | Protect the Lead with Intelligence
Session Outcomes
Protect a lead through intelligent decisions — controlling tempo, space, and risk while staying compact and connected.
Activation – Uvodni dio / Game Activation (15 mins)
Setup
Area: 30x25m, directional
Players: 5v3 (possession team) vs 3 defenders
Equipment: Balls, cones, 2 mini goals at each end
Description
Possession team starts 1–0 up and aims to keep the lead.
Defending team presses aggressively to score.
Possession team scores by completing controlled passes or drawing pressure before switching play.
Overload supports calm ball use and intelligent risk management.
Coaching Focus
Collective Cues
Team stays compact around the ball
Support distances are short and safe
Shape is prioritised over forward risk
Individual Cues
First touch protects the ball
Choose the safe option under pressure
Recover position immediately after passing
Progression
Reduce space or add a countdown to increase end-game pressure
Situational Practice – Solving the Situation (20 mins)
Setup
Area: 45x30m, directional
Players: 5v5
Targets: Big goal vs 2 mini goals
Scenario: One team leads by one goal with limited time remaining
Description
Leading team manages the game: keeping the ball, slowing tempo, and defending compactly when needed.
Trailing team presses hard and attacks directly.
On regain, the leading team secures possession first, then looks to play into space only when clear.
Focus is on intelligent control, not just possession for its own sake.
Coaching Focus
Collective Cues
Team recognises when to keep the ball vs clear danger
Compact shape is restored quickly after loss
Tempo is controlled through ball circulation and positioning
Individual Cues
Don’t force forward passes
Shield the ball and draw pressure
Stay disciplined when defending the lead
Progression
Limit touches for the trailing team to raise pressing intensity
Situational Game – Collective Execution (25 mins)
Setup
Area: 55x40m
Players: 6v6 + GK
Goals: Full-size goals with GKs
Description
Game begins with one team leading by one goal.
All restarts from GK.
Condition: Leading team scores double if they protect the lead through controlled possession, compact defending, and smart decisions.
Emphasis is on calm execution and shared responsibility under pressure.
Coaching Focus
Collective Cues
Team manages tempo deliberately
Compactness increases as pressure rises
Clear communication to organise and calm the group
Individual Cues
Make simple, reliable decisions
Stay focused on your role
Avoid emotional fouls or rushed clearances
Progression
Remove scoring condition to fully normalise behaviour
Free Play – Transfer Game (20 mins)
Setup
Area: 60x45m
Players: 7v7 including GKs
Directional game with normal rules
Description
Free play with minimal intervention.
Coaching Focus
Observation only
Look for players protecting the lead through tempo control, compact defending, and intelligent choices
Coaching Information (U15)
Element | Detail
Objective / Outcomes | Protect a lead through intelligent game management
Technical / Tactical Detail | Ball security, support angles, compact recovery, time awareness
Coaching Messages (Belco Voice) | “Value the ball.” • “Control the game.” • “Stay compact.”
Common Mistakes & Fixes | Forcing attacks → “Keep it.” • Panic defending → “Hold shape.”
Praise Language | “Calm decision.” • “Well managed.” • “Disciplined play.”
Behaviours to Reward | Composure, discipline, communication, intelligent risk
Reflection Question | “What decisions helped us protect the lead without panicking?”
Session Design Note | Final session reinforces intelligent control under end-game pressure
Language Note | Belco Framework vocabulary used to support recognition
Belconnen United FC – Compact • Brave • Intelligent

heading: U16+ Coach Pack
content:
BELCONNEN UNITED FC
TECHNICAL DEPARTMENT
——————————————
COACH PACK 6
TECHNICAL DEVELOPMENT PHASE – U16 and U18
——————————————
Control the Game as One Unit
——————————————
Prepared by:
Scott Conlon – Technical Director
Belconnen United FC
V2026.03

heading: 1. U16+ Message to the Coaches
headingPath: COACH PACK 4 (U16+) – PROFESSIONAL DEVELOPMENT PHASE > 1. U16+ Message to the Coaches
content:
This is the final stage before professional football.
You’re no longer coaching habits — you’re refining identity.
The players should already know the language, the structure, and the rhythm.
Now, you demand consistency, intelligence, and control.
The Croatians say, “At 16, the game should speak.”
That means the coach’s role is to shape behaviours through scenario and standard — not by over-talking.
Every exercise must represent real football: full direction, full tempo, full accountability.
You manage more than the ball now — you manage emotion, rhythm, and mentality.
Use our shared language precisely: “Open — tempo.”, “Compact to attack.”, “Trust the structure.”
That voice connects them to Belco’s senior identity.
This phase is about professional rhythm — decision speed, body discipline, emotional calm.
You are now building players who can perform Belco football under pressure: structured, collective, fearless.
“At U16, it’s no longer about potential — it’s about readiness.”

heading: 2. U16+ Player Characteristics
headingPath: COACH PACK 4 (U16+) – PROFESSIONAL DEVELOPMENT PHASE > 2. U16+ Player Characteristics
content:
U16+ players are entering the final phase of their youth development.
They can process tactical information quickly, manage emotional challenges, and are capable of independent adjustment during matches.
However, they need consistency, clarity, and challenge to reach professional standard.
Typical traits:
- Physically capable but still developing endurance for sustained tempo.
- Tactically aware — can understand match models, opponent adjustments, and in-game problem solving.
- Psychologically sensitive to pressure and feedback — benefit from trust and accountability.
- Competitive mindset developing — driven by standards, not praise.
- Begin to view themselves as young professionals — need to be treated as such.
Coach takeaway:
The coach’s tone must evolve from teaching to managing performance.
The relationship shifts toward mentoring — demanding high standards but allowing players to own solutions.
The key coaching trait: calm authority. Correct with precision, reinforce with detail, and hold consistency over emotion.

heading: 3. U16+ Phase Overview
headingPath: COACH PACK 4 (U16+) – PROFESSIONAL DEVELOPMENT PHASE > 3. U16+ Phase Overview
content:
The U16+ stage is where Belco players evolve from learners to performers — from understanding the game to executing it at full tempo, under pressure, and with consistency.
Here, players refine the tactical, technical, and psychological qualities that allow them to adapt and thrive in senior football.
Area | Key Points
Age Phase | U16 – Professional Preparation Phase
Development Focus | Tactical maturity, emotional balance, match-level tempo control. Team intelligence replaces coach direction.
Session Rhythm | 2–3 sessions per week. 40% tactical collective work, 40% transition and pressing, 20% situational technical. Match intensity and realism every session.
Training Ratio | Tactical : Technical = 2 : 1. Use 8v8 to 11v11 patterns, scenario training, and conditioned games.
Game Focus | Control rhythm, manage transitions, press as a unit, dominate duels. Matches simulate professional tempo.
Belco Identity Seeds | 🔹 Build & Control the Tempo (full expression) 🔹 Create Superiority (through dynamic overloads and third-man play) 🔹 Collective Compactness (team unity and spacing) 🔹 Aggressive Recovery (counter-pressing mindset) 🔹 Duel Mentality (competitive control).
Coach Role | Guide professionalism: demand precision, emotional maturity, and communication. Connect every action to team purpose.

heading: 4. U16+ Coaching Emphasis
headingPath: COACH PACK 4 (U16+) – PROFESSIONAL DEVELOPMENT PHASE > 4. U16+ Coaching Emphasis
content:
Coaching Dimension | Emphasis for This Phase | Practical Examples / Notes
Session Rhythm | High-quality, high-intensity. Short rest, match realism. | Scenario games (10–12 mins), tactical restarts, transition blocks.
Coaching Voice | Minimal words, exact timing. Use identity cues only. | “Tempo.” “Compact.” “Fast five.” “Trust it.”
Correction Style | Correct through standards and reflection. Use short tactical pauses, not long lectures. | “We lost tempo there — why?” “Reset and show me again.”
Technical Detail | Execution speed, precision under fatigue, control of first touch and passing rhythm. | Always in context — no isolated drills.
Tactical Habits | Collective spacing, automatic reaction to open/closed cues, intelligent pressing coordination. | “Closed = hold.” “Open = go.”
Psychological Theme | Professional behaviour — calm control, resilience, responsibility. | “Don’t chase the game — manage it.”
Language Focus | Identity and match control language. | “Tempo is a weapon.” “Compact to attack.” “Play brave.”
Coach Behaviours to Reward | Leadership, voice, self-correction. | “Good — you fixed it before I had to.”
Croatian Guideline | “The team must think faster than the opponent, not run faster.” | Train intelligence under pressure, not chaos.

heading: 5. U16+ Technical Focus List
headingPath: COACH PACK 4 (U16+) – PROFESSIONAL DEVELOPMENT PHASE > 5. U16+ Technical Focus List
content:
(Professional Development – Execution Phase, Belco Identity)
Purpose
At U16+, technical work exists only inside tactical and physical realism.
The player must now perform techniques under match tempo, pressure, and consequence — the hallmark of professional consistency.
The Croatian principle “igra kao metoda” (the game as the method) remains central, but rhythm and load are now managed through game design: small, medium, and big formats.
Belco’s aim at this stage:
Perform every Belco principle at professional speed — with control, intelligence, and resilience.
1. U16+ Technical Consistency Under Match Tempo
Focus | What It Looks Like | How It’s Trained
First touch at full speed | Controls under immediate pressure, plays forward instantly. | 11v11 build-up circuits under opposition press.
Decision-making with fatigue | Executes same quality late in session. | 6v6–8v8 continuous transition games.
Two-touch efficiency | Uses minimal contacts to control tempo. | Medium-field rondo → positional transfer.
Ball security in tight areas | Retains and releases intelligently in compact zones. | 5v5 central congestion games, recovery triggers.
Belco links: Build Through Thirds, Tempo Control, Duel Mentality. |  | 
2. U16+ Receiving and Distribution Under Tactical Load
Focus | What It Looks Like | How It’s Trained
Receiving under multiple pressure lines | Finds correct angle to maintain rhythm. | 8v8 positional build-up through 3 zones.
Progressive passing choice | Plays forward when space opens, not by habit. | 11v11 build-up patterns; press recognition.
Body orientation as trigger for next phase | Turns hips to open forward lane. | Positional games with limited touch and direction rule.
Precision under vertical compactness | Breaks lines without losing team shape. | 6v6 half-pitch play with high pressing block.
Belco links: Build Through Thirds, Open-Ball Triggers, Compactness. |  | 
3. U16+ Collective Combination & Advanced Rotation
Focus | What It Looks Like | How It’s Trained
Third-man & bounce at professional tempo | Automatic in midfield and attacking zones. | 7v7 directional + transition rules.
Positional interchange in structure | Rotation without disorganisation. | 11v11 functional rehearsals; role rotation.
Overload-to-isolate to finish | Create, release, and exploit space with control. | 8v8 wide build-up → 1v1 finish.
Timing and coordination of unit movements | Move as a collective, not individual effort. | 6v6 build-up + counter-press transition.
Belco links: Drive–Draw–Play, Overloads, Bounce & Third-Man. |  | 
4. U16+ Ball Carrying & Penetration at Speed
Focus | What It Looks Like | How It’s Trained
Drive through lines when space opens | Recognises and exploits open-ball cues. | 8v8 directional game; carry zone triggers.
Carry to commit and release | Pulls defender to create space for next pass. | 6v6 central corridor overload → release.
Tempo change under opponent press | Slows to attract, bursts to break. | Half-pitch 8v8; controlled press vs escape.
Carry decision under tactical plan | Chooses to drive or recycle depending on tempo. | Full-pitch pattern games, tempo management rule.
Belco links: Drive–Draw–Play, Open Ball, Tempo Control. |  | 
5. U16+ Defensive Technique & Tactical Resilience
Focus | What It Looks Like | How It’s Trained
Pressing line coordination | Front unit presses, back line locks behind. | 11v11 high-press rehearsal.
Body control in defensive duels | Clean, low centre, delay when outnumbered. | 1v1–2v2 isolated duels + immediate transition.
Compactness recovery | Reorganises instantly when press broken. | 8v8 transition with 3-second compact rule.
Counter-press recognition | First reaction: regain before retreat. | 6v6–7v7 continuous transitions.
Belco links: Compactness, Forward Pressing, Counter-Pressing, Duel Mentality. |  | 
6. U16+ Finishing & Final-Third Precision
Focus | What It Looks Like | How It’s Trained
Finishing from dynamic cut-backs | Arrives from second line, balanced and composed. | 11v11 or 8v8 wide overload → cut-back drill.
Quick combinations in final third | Bounce, third-man, or wall pass before finish. | 5v5 penalty-box rotations.
Finishing under tactical fatigue | Maintains technique late in sequence. | Continuous finishing circuit post-possession game.
Decision-making at end phase | Chooses between shot, extra pass, or recycle. | Game-based finishing with reward for best decision.
Belco links: Assist Zone, Overloads, Open Ball. |  | 
7. U16+ Tactical–Cognitive and Psychological Habits
Focus | What It Looks Like | How It’s Trained
Reading tempo and match flow | Adjusts intensity to game moment. | Full-pitch scenario games with coach tempo calls.
Anticipating next phase | Repositions during ball travel. | Continuous 11v11 possession with turnover rule.
Tactical communication | Verbal and visual coordination between units. | 9v9 structured rehearsal; leadership cues.
Resilience and emotional control | Maintains performance under stress. | Competitive 6v6 tournaments, immediate replays.
Belco links: Tempo Control, Compactness, Duel Mentality. |  | 
Repetition Through Football Conditioning (6-Week Cycle Rhythm)
Cycle Segment | Focus Rhythm | Example Context
Weeks 1–2 | Big games – tactical conditioning, endurance (11v11/9v9). | Build-up and compactness patterns.
Weeks 3–4 | Medium games – cognitive load and speed of play (8v8/7v7). | Overloads, transitions, rhythm control.
Weeks 5–6 | Small games – high-intensity, anaerobic, repetition (5v5/6v6). | Pressing, finishing, duels, counter-press.
Integration logic:
- Technical actions now trained under match intensity.
- Principle repetition remains, but variation comes through field size, tempo and tactical focus.
- Recovery integrated naturally through tempo management and game rhythm.

heading: 6. U16+ Language Bank (part 1)
headingPath: COACH PACK 4 (U16+) – PROFESSIONAL DEVELOPMENT PHASE > 6. U16+ Language Bank
content:
This chapter defines the shared tactical language used across the Belco program to describe the game by moments and by thirds: in possession, out of possession, and transition. Rather than prescribing patterns or movements, the language bank provides collective concept cues that allow teams to recognise situations, act together, and control tempo under match pressure.
The language is deliberately short, visual, and repeatable, enabling players to self-correct in real time and coaches to intervene briefly without over-coaching. Organising the game through the back, middle, and front thirds reflects how decisions actually occur on the pitch, while transition language connects these moments into one continuous game flow. This approach mirrors Croatian and European methodology, where tactical understanding is built through shared terminology, repetition, and realism, not constant instruction.
From U14 onward, the language remains consistent across age groups, while the expectation of execution increases. What begins as recognition at U14–15 becomes a performance standard at U16 and above. This continuity ensures players are not relearning football concepts each season, but instead developing the ability to read the game, manage moments, and take ownership as they prepare for senior football.
BUILDING FROM THE BACK (Back Third)
Belco Identity: Build & Control the Tempo · Create Superiority
At U16, build-up is about control under pressure, not safety.
U16+ Build-Up Standards
Focus | U16 Coach Language | Croatian Expectation
Structure Is Non-Negotiable | “Be in shape.” “Structure now.” | Players arrive in build positions automatically. No reminders.
Free Man Must Appear | “Someone must be free.” | If no free man exists, structure or spacing has failed.
Lines, Not Levels | “Stop being flat.” “Different lines.” | Vertical staggering is expected, not coached.
Attract Pressure on Purpose | “Invite him.” “Fix him, then play.” | Build-up must provoke pressure, not avoid it.
Progress or Reset Immediately | “Step in or reset.” | Indecision is unacceptable at this age.
PROGRESSING THROUGH MIDFIELD (Middle Third)
Belco Identity: Create Superiority · Control the Game
This is the truth zone at U16.
U16+ Middle-Third Standards
Focus | U16 Coach Language | Croatian Expectation
Open Ball Is Mandatory Action | “Open ball — punish it.” | Facing forward must result in penetration.
Penetrate with Quality | “Break a line.” “Hurt them.” | Progression must change the opponent’s shape.
Third Man Is Automatic | “Where’s the third?” | Support runs are expected before the ball arrives.
Pivot Controls the Match | “Through the six.” “Set us.” | Pivot manages rhythm, not just circulation.
Tempo Is Owned, Not Felt | “Change the speed.” | Team dictates rhythm regardless of pressure.
FINISHING FROM POSITIONAL PLAY (Front Third)
Belco Identity: Create Superiority · Duel Mentality
U16 final third = efficiency under pressure.
U16+ Final-Third Standards
Focus | U16 Coach Language | Croatian Expectation
Width Must Stretch the Block | “Hold the line.” | Width is positional discipline, not preference.
Assist Zone Is the Target | “Get to the line.” | Penetration beats volume.
Cut-Back or No Ball | “Back, not blind.” | Low-percentage actions are challenged immediately.
Timing Beats Speed | “Arrive on time.” | Movement quality > physical output.
Duels Have Purpose | “Beat him to play.” | 1v1s are used to free others, not isolate.
U16+ PRESSING ORGANISATION (Front Third)
Belco Identity: Collective Compactness · Aggressive Recovery
At U16, pressing is a tool, not an emotion.
U16 Pressing Standards
Focus | U16 Coach Language | Croatian Expectation
Press Only on Triggers | “Closed ball.” “Return ball.” | Pressing without a trigger is a tactical error.
Nearest Three Act Instantly | “Three go — now.” | Delay kills pressing; reaction must be immediate.
Core Is Non-Negotiable | “Protect the middle.” | Central space is defended even while pressing high.
Shape Before Speed | “Fix the shape.” “Then step.” | Sprinting without structure is punished at this age.
Win It to Play | “Win it — play forward.” | Pressing must produce attacking advantage.
U16+ DEFENDING THE MIDDLE THIRD (Mid-Block)
Belco Identity: Collective Compactness · Control the Game
This replaces “low block” thinking.
U16 Mid-Block Standards
Focus | U16 Coach Language | Croatian Expectation
Distances Must Be Right | “Control the distances.” | Compactness is measured, not guessed.
Read the Ball State | “Closed — hold.” “Open — drop.” | Players read pictures, not instructions.
Move as a Unit | “Together.” “Step with the line.” | Any individual action breaks the block.
Delay with Purpose | “Hold them.” “Force wide.” | Defence shapes the opponent’s decisions.
Defend to Attack | “Win it and break.” | Regain must transition immediately into intent.
TRANSITION TO ATTACK
Regain → Possession
Belco Identity: Build & Control the Tempo · Aggressive Recovery
At U16, transition forward is about speed of thought, not chaos.
U16 Transition-to-Attack Standards
Focus | U16 Coach Language | Croatian Expectation
First Look Is Forward | “Win — look forward.” | Players scan immediately on regain. Backward first touch is challenged.
Open Ball Must Be Used | “Open ball — hurt them.” | Facing forward = mandatory penetration.
Support Is Immediate | “Support now.” “Run to help.” | Nearest options must appear instantly.
Fix Before You Release | “Drive to commit.” | Ball carrier attracts pressure before passing.

heading: 6. U16+ Language Bank (part 2)
headingPath: COACH PACK 4 (U16+) – PROFESSIONAL DEVELOPMENT PHASE > 6. U16+ Language Bank
content:
Control the Speed | “Fast brain, calm feet.” | Quality is protected even at high tempo.
TRANSITION TO DEFENCE
Loss → Out of Possession
Belco Identity: Aggressive Recovery · Collective Compactness
At U16, losing the ball is a collective emergency.
U16+ Transition-to-Defence Standards
Focus | U16 Coach Language | Croatian Expectation
Immediate Reaction | “Lose it — close it.” | No pause. No complaints. Reaction is instant.
Nearest Three Engage | “Three go — now.” | Local pressure buys time for the block.
Protect the Core First | “Protect the middle.” | Central space is defended before anything else.
Delay with Intelligence | “Slow them.” “Buy time.” | Defenders shape play, not chase it.
Recover as a Unit | “Everyone reacts.” | Any passenger breaks the team.
U16+ GAME MANAGEMENT & TEAM INTELLIGENCE
Focus | U16 Coach Language | Croatian Expectation
1️⃣ Control the Game State | “Manage the game.” “Read the moment.” | Players recognise score, time, momentum — and adjust behaviour.
2️⃣ Tempo Is a Weapon | “Slow it.” “Speed it up.” | Tempo is used deliberately to regain control or press advantage.
3️⃣ Emotional Control | “Calm.” “No rush.” | Emotional reactions are corrected immediately.
4️⃣ Responsibility in Duels | “Win your duel.” “Do your job.” | Individual battles serve the team structure, not ego.
5️⃣ Leadership from Players | “Talk.” “Organise.” | Communication must come from the pitch, not the bench.

heading: 7. U16+ Annual Session Theme Progression
headingPath: COACH PACK 4 (U16+) – PROFESSIONAL DEVELOPMENT PHASE > 7. U16+ Annual Session Theme Progression
content:
Structure: The U16+ season consists of 6 Cycles · 5–6 weeks each · 2 sessions per week (~30 weeks)
Focus: Full team tactical application, tempo and rhythm control, mental resilience, match execution.
Belco Language Layer:
- “Open ball, set tempo.”
- “Compact to attack.”
- “Tempo is a weapon.”
- “Stay brave under pressure.”
U16+ cycles are numbered Cycle 1 to Cycle 6
Cycle | Theme | Duration | Week 1 | Week 2 | Week 3 | Week 4 | Week 5 | Week 6
1 | Team Build-Up & Game Control | 6 weeks | Collective Structure – Back + Mid Connection Calm in First Phase | Open Ball Decisions – Recognise Triggers Create Support Angles | Midfield Rhythm – Pivot and Rotation Set the Tempo | Build Through Thirds From Calm to Acceleration | Game Control Scenario Circulate vs. Penetrate | Tactical Game Maintain Rhythm Under Pressure
2 | Breaking Lines & Creating Superiority | 5 weeks | Create Superiority – Find the Free Man Third-Man Runs | Dynamic Width Winger and Fullback Connection | Positional Overloads – Central or Wide Break Pressure | Penetration Game Timing and Courage to Play Through | Collective Progression Break Lines to Finish | 
3 | High Press & Counter-Pressing | 5 weeks | Press Triggers – Closed and Return Balls Compact Together | Synchronised Lines – Front + Mid Unit Press Protect Behind | Counter-Pressing Shape Nearest 3 React Instantly | Win it High – Transition to Finish First Five Seconds | Pressing Game Maintain Structure at Tempo | 
4 | Compact Defending & Line Coordination | 5 weeks | Compactness Fundamentals 3 Lines Moving as One | Cover & Depth – Read the Line Track Runners | Defensive Shifting – Protect Core Delay Smart | Compact Press Win Forward, Stay Together | Defensive Scenario Game Shape Under Stress | 
5 | Transition & Game Management | 5 weeks | Immediate Reaction Win → Play Forward | Manage the Moment When to Counter vs. Secure | Control the Emotional Tempo Stay Calm to Play Fast | Organise While Attacking Rest Defence Awareness | Match Scenarios – Protect Lead Change Tempo Intelligently | 
6 | Finishing Phase & Winning Mentality | 4–5 weeks | Create to Finish Combination in Final Third | Finishing Under Pressure Decision Over Power | Second Line Finishing – Arrive Late Controlled Runs | Resilience in Finishing Compete in Fatigue | Game Simulation Winning Mentality Challenge | 
U16+ Cycle Duration Notes
Cycle | Duration | Reason
1. Team Build-Up & Game Control | 6 weeks | Deepest team-tactical layer in Croatia’s U16 phase. Involves every unit; repeated across season.
2. Breaking Lines & Creating Superiority | 5 weeks | Requires detailed positional and tempo understanding.
3–5 | 5 weeks | Pressing, compactness, and game management demand high repetition and maturity.
6. Finishing & Mentality | 4–5 weeks | Used as final phase of season, blending technical and psychological work.

heading: 8 U16+ Training Sessions
headingPath: COACH PACK 4 (U16+) – PROFESSIONAL DEVELOPMENT PHASE > 8 U16+ Training Sessions
content:
Training Chapter – Coach Standard (Coach Pack Version)
Belco training sessions are not copied from Croatian drills.
At this age, session structure supports learning — it must never restrict play or creativity.
They are built on the same philosophy and session structure, adapted to our players, coaches, and environment.
Every session follows a clear learning sequence:
- Prepare the player (uvodni)
- Develop the main idea (glavni)
- Transfer learning into the game (završni)
These phases describe purpose, not drills.
Coaches are not expected to run fixed exercises. They are expected to:
- respect the session flow
- connect activities to the game
- increase realism and pressure as the session progresses
- use shared language consistently
The session examples that follow show one way to apply this methodology. Activities can be changed, adapted, or replaced, as long as the learning intent, progression, and behavioural standards remain.
We don’t copy exercises.We copy how learning is structured.

heading: U16+ Session Structure
headingPath: COACH PACK 4 (U16+) – PROFESSIONAL DEVELOPMENT PHASE > 8 U16+ Training Sessions > U16+ Session Structure
content:
How Belco Training Sessions Are Structured (U16+)
At U16+, Belco training sessions enter the performance preparation phase. Players are now preparing for senior football, where actions must be executed at speed, under pressure, and with consequence.
At this stage:
- understanding is assumed
- decision-making must be immediate
- execution must be reliable
- collective behaviours must hold under fatigue
Sessions still follow the same Croatian session architecture used throughout the pathway.What changes is the standard.
The structure is no longer a guide — it is the baseline expectation.
Croatian Learning Logic at U16+
Sessions follow Croatian senior-preparation logic, where:
- learning continues through performance and consequence
- decisions must be fast, connected, and repeatable
- overloads are brief and purposeful
- equality and realism arrive early
- free play exposes the true performance level
At U16+, the session is designed to reveal truth, not protect confidence.
How the Session Progresses at U16+
Across the session:
- preparation is shorter and sharper
- pressure arrives earlier
- assistance is reduced quickly
- behaviours are tested under fatigue
The coach’s role is to:
- demand standards
- reinforce clarity and intent
- intervene briefly and purposefully
- trust the game to expose reality
The session always follows three connected phases, consistent with Croatian architecture.
Phase 1 – Play with the Ball
This phase prepares players to enter the session already in game mode.
At U16+, this phase:
- is directional and ball-based
- connects immediately to game moments
- sharpens the first decision of the session
- establishes rhythm and focus
Activities often begin from:
- regains
- loss of control
- forward options
- transition moments
Any overloads are brief and intentional, used only to establish rhythm or clarity — not comfort.
The emphasis is on:
- collective awareness
- discipline in movement
- immediate decision-making
This phase is not about repetition.It is about readiness to perform.
Coaches should:
- demand focus from the first action
- correct poor habits immediately
- keep communication short and clear
Phase 2 – Learning Game
This is the core performance-learning phase of the session.
Players are exposed to live, connected game situations where:
- attacking and defending decisions are linked
- transitions add consequence
- risk must be managed collectively
Learning occurs through:
- realistic spacing
- equality or near-equality
- repeated exposure to demanding situations
Overloads may appear briefly, but equality and pressure are introduced quickly.
The focus is on:
- decision logic
- timing of collective actions
- control within moments
The coach:
- reinforces ownership and responsibility
- uses brief cues, not explanation
- allows the game to apply pressure
Players are expected to self-correct and adapt.
Phase 3 – Football Game
The session finishes with a realistic football game.
This phase tests whether players can:
- control game moments
- maintain behaviours under fatigue
- execute decisions without prompts
Conditions are minimal.Flow is normal.Restarts are realistic.
The focus is not shape, but:
- tempo control
- recognition of moments
- collective decision quality
Coach intervention is minimal.Observation reveals whether behaviours are stable.
This phase exposes performance truth.
Coach Expectations (U16+)
At U16+, effective coaching means:
- assuming understanding
- demanding execution
- reducing assistance early
- allowing equality to reveal standards
- trusting free play to expose reality
The coach sets the bar —the game measures it.
Key Reminder
U16+ is about control and reliability.
If players can:
- manage game moments together
- make intelligent decisions under fatigue
- execute without coach input
then they are moving toward senior readiness.
If they cannot, the answer is not more instruction —it is more honest football.

heading: 9. U16+ Correction & Feedback Language
headingPath: COACH PACK 4 (U16+) – PROFESSIONAL DEVELOPMENT PHASE > 9. U16+ Correction & Feedback Language
content:
Purpose
Correction is now professional coaching — concise, tactical, standard-driven.
Players must already know the why; your job is to maintain collective precision and mindset.
Croatians call this phase “coaching maturity” — the ability to change behaviour with ten words or less.
U16+ Correction Philosophy (Croatian Model)
Type of Feedback | When Used | Croatian Approach | Belco Example Language
Instant (Trigger) | During game; same cues as training. | Maintain tempo and standard. | “Tempo.” “Compact.” “Reset.”
Freeze (Rare) | Only for tactical reset — never for emotion. | Reposition whole team, explain short, replay. | “Hold — shape’s too wide. Compact — again.”
Guided Reflection | Half-time or post-match. | Encourage leadership-led feedback. | “What triggered our press?” “When did we lose control?”
Reinforce Success | High-quality rhythm, composure, or press. | Praise identity behaviour. | “Excellent — calm build, fast regain.”
Collective Reminder | When team drops standard. | Reset emotional tone. | “Everyone — back to calm.” “Our standard is control.”
U16+ Croatian Correction Principles (U16)
1️⃣ Correct standard, not mistake.2️⃣ Coach team intelligence, not emotion.3️⃣ Minimal intervention, maximal clarity.4️⃣ Use identity language only — no new terms.5️⃣ Correction is a leadership act.
Belco–Croatian Common Coaching Language (U16 Level)
Action Type | Correction Cue | Positive Reinforcement
Build & Tempo | “Calm.” “Tempo.” “Switch now.” | “Good — we controlled rhythm.”
Superiority Creation | “Overload left.” “Third man.” | “Perfect rotation — found the extra.”
Compactness | “Line up.” “Close gap.” | “Excellent spacing — no holes.”
Aggressive Recovery | “Fast five!” “Press!” | “Brilliant regain — immediate.”
Duel Mentality | “Win it clean.” “Next action.” | “Good — stayed brave.”
Tone & Delivery
- Voice: calm, low, precise — no reactionary noise.
- Timing: choose key moments; over-talking destroys composure.
- After correction: replay silently — players must feel tempo.
- Body language: steady posture, no gestures of frustration.
“At U16, the coach represents professionalism.
The message is fewer words — higher standards.”

heading: 10. U16+ Game Day Guidance
headingPath: COACH PACK 4 (U16+) – PROFESSIONAL DEVELOPMENT PHASE > 10. U16+ Game Day Guidance
content:
Purpose
At this stage, matchday is not about teaching — it’s about executing the identity.Croatians treat the U16 match as a mirror of professional football:tactical clarity, rhythm control, emotional calm.Your players must now drive the game, not wait for you.
Your sideline voice should reflect that: minimal, measured, intentional.The coach now manages tempo, emotion, and standard — not every touch.
U16+ Game Day Objectives
Element | Guidance
Game Intent | Express the full Belco identity: build through structure, control rhythm, recover aggressively, and dominate duels. The objective is not just to apply — it’s to own the model.
Coach Voice | Sparse and professional: short cues that reinforce standards. “Tempo.” “Compact.” “Trust structure.” “Fast five.” Use tone, not volume.
Player Guidance | Leadership must come from within. The captain and central players manage shape and tempo. You guide from rhythm and structure, not emotion.
Substitution Focus | Role clarity. Each substitute knows the tactical and emotional requirement before entering.
Half-Time Reflection | Use professional prompts:
 | 1️⃣ “What moment do we control?”
 | 2️⃣ “Where can we shift tempo?”
 | 3️⃣ “Do we trust structure under pressure?”
Coach Behaviour | Fully composed. Model the professionalism expected of senior football. No shouting, no constant gestures — calm equals control.
Croatian Matchday Principles (U16)
1️⃣ “Silence is part of coaching.” — players must self-correct.
2️⃣ “Standards replace motivation.” — precision, not passion.
3️⃣ “Coach the moment, not the mood.” — respond to rhythm, not emotion.
Example Match Dialogue
“Tempo — calm.”
“Compact — hold.”
“Closed ball — reset.”
“Fast five — press now!”
“Stay brave, trust it.”
Every phrase must be part of the identity vocabulary.

heading: U16+ Coach Matchday Standards
headingPath: COACH PACK 4 (U16+) – PROFESSIONAL DEVELOPMENT PHASE > 10. U16+ Game Day Guidance > U16+ Coach Matchday Standards
content:
Purpose
The coach sets the tone for the match environment.Professional detail builds credibility.Players mirror what they see — composure, preparation, and precision.
Coach Matchday Standards
APPEARANCE
Element | Standard
Belconnen Attire | Official Belconnen polo or approved top. Unified, consistent look across staff.
Footwear | Soccer footwear during warm-up and match. We look like football people, not spectators.
Sunglasses | Remove when speaking to players. Eye contact is essential for message absorption and connection.
Presentation | Neat, professional, deliberate. Our unified appearance signals control and precision.
GEAR
Item | Expectation
Balls | Pumped appropriately before arrival at pitch.
Bibs | Organised and accessible.
Cones | Blue and white matchday cones set and ready.
PREPAREDNESS
Element | Expectation
Substitution Plan | Rotation structure pre-planned. Know your intent before kick-off.
Team Talk | Clear theme linked to weekly focus. No improvisation.
Arrival Time | Minimum 45–60 minutes before kick-off. Field checked and organised.
Sideline Behaviour
Element | Standard
Coach Voice | Calm, clear, rhythmic.
Emotional Control | No visible frustration or emotional reactions.
Referee Interaction | Respectful at all times. Model behaviour.
Opposition Interaction | Professional greeting pre and post match.
Player Interaction | Eye contact, short cues, consistent language from training.
Parent Interaction | Friendly but brief. No tactical debate during game.
Belco Matchday Identity
1️⃣ Calm voice, clear cues.2️⃣ Detail before emotion.3️⃣ Teach relationships, not results.4️⃣ Represent the program with pride.

heading: U16+ Pre-Match Warm-Up
headingPath: COACH PACK 4 (U16+) – PROFESSIONAL DEVELOPMENT PHASE > 10. U16+ Game Day Guidance > U16+ Pre-Match Warm-Up
content:
PRE-MATCH WARM-UP (30 MINUTES TOTAL)
Objective: Replicate professional match preparation — controlled activation, tempo management, and tactical rhythm.
At U16, the pre-match warm-up is a performance preparation, not a learning session.
Its role is to bring players into the match with clarity, control, and readiness to execute under pressure.
Warm-ups at this age must:
- progress quickly from activation to collective organisation
- reflect the team’s intended behaviours in possession and out of possession
- reduce coach voice as kick-off approaches
- allow players to take responsibility for rhythm and focus
Coaches use the warm-up to observe standards: tempo, spacing, communication, and emotional control. Intervention is brief and corrective only. There is no teaching, no motivation, and no emotional build-up.
The table below provides one compliant example. Activities may change, but the sequence, intensity, and restraint must remain.
Block | Duration | Focus | Example Activity | Coach Cues / Tone
1️⃣ Physical Activation (Dynamic) | 5-6 mins | Controlled mobility, rhythm, and activation. | Dynamic mobility with short accelerations; band resistance or plyo elements. | “Set rhythm.” “Control speed.”
2️⃣ Technical Precision (Pairs or Trios) | 4-5 mins | Passing quality, first touch, visual scanning. | Triangles: one-touch pass, bounce-pass, third-man play. | “Sharp but calm.” “Scan early.”
3️⃣ Positional Rondos (by Units) | 6-7 mins | Coordination and structure in lines. | Defenders 5v3 (build triggers), midfielders 5v2 (pivot control), attackers 4v3 (press cues). | “Compact.” “Open-ball stretch.”
4️⃣ Tactical Pattern or Press Rehearsal | 6-7 mins | Build-up and counter-press rhythm. | 8v5 build-up to finishing, or 7v7 press structure. | “Return ball — reset.” “Fast five!”
5️⃣ Finishing / Directional Game | 5 mins | Confidence in attacking rhythm. | 4v4 or 5v5 to large goal, focus on cut-back zone finishing. | “Calm before finish.” “Be decisive.”
Detail:
- Coaches don’t drive intensity; they observe posture, communication, and detail.
- The team captain leads transitions between blocks.
- The warm-up looks and feels like a pro team routine — quiet, precise, rhythmic.
“By U16, the coach prepares behaviour, not just bodies.”

heading: U16+ Pre-Match Talk
headingPath: COACH PACK 4 (U16+) – PROFESSIONAL DEVELOPMENT PHASE > 10. U16+ Game Day Guidance > U16+ Pre-Match Talk
content:
PRE-MATCH TALK (3–4 MINUTES)
Purpose
Set tactical focus and professional standard.Players should leave understanding how we control the match — not what to do on the ball.
Structure
Step | Coach Focus | Example Language (Croatian senior tone)
1️⃣ Establish Calm Presence | Players seated; tone low, direct. | “Eyes up — stay calm. One message today.”
2️⃣ Reinforce Tactical Identity | Define game approach with identity cues. | “We build through structure, control tempo, recover fast.”
3️⃣ Unit Leadership Prompts | Short lines from captain or key players. | “Back line — compact.” “Midfield — manage tempo.” “Front — press together.”
4️⃣ Standard Reminder | Professional mentality and tempo. | “Calm brain, fast legs. We control the rhythm.”
5️⃣ End with Controlled Silence | One final sentence, pause. | “Trust structure. Tempo is ours.” (pause — no extra words)
Croatian Pre-Match Principles (U16)
1️⃣ “Professional tone, minimal words.”
2️⃣ “Leadership moves to players.”
3️⃣ “Clarity is strength — silence is control.”

heading: U16+ Half-Time Talk
headingPath: COACH PACK 4 (U16+) – PROFESSIONAL DEVELOPMENT PHASE > 10. U16+ Game Day Guidance > U16+ Half-Time Talk
content:
HALF-TIME TALK (less than 10 MINUTES)
Purpose
Manage emotional tempo, tactical precision, and self-discipline.
The U16 half-time talk mirrors a senior team debrief — calm, short, and focused on standards and rhythm.
Structure
Step | Coach Focus | Example Language (Croatian senior tone)
1️⃣ Regain Calm | Begin silent for 10 seconds; make eye contact. | (Silence) / “Reset — focus.”
2️⃣ Identify Standard of the Half | Name what was done well. | “We controlled tempo well for 25 minutes.”
3️⃣ One Tactical or Emotional Correction | Only one — collective, not individual. | “When we lost shape, we chased the game — stay compact, stay calm.”
4️⃣ Leadership Reflection | Captain or line leader speaks. | “Midfield — what do we fix first?”
5️⃣ Finish with Identity Cue | Reinforce the Belco-Croatian DNA. | “Calm brain, fast legs. Compact and brave — trust our rhythm.”
Croatian Half-Time Rules (U16)
Rule | Meaning for Coach
1️⃣ Coach standard, not score. | The game is feedback, not judgment.
2️⃣ Stay calm under pressure. | Players mirror your energy.
3️⃣ Keep ownership with players. | Use questions, not orders.
4️⃣ End on identity language. | Always connect to rhythm, compactness, or tempo.
Example Complete U16 Half-Time Talk
“Good half — we controlled tempo and pressed well.
When we lost shape, we rushed — calm before fast.
Compact together, trust structure, control rhythm.
Calm brain, fast legs — tempo is ours.”
U16+ Coach Behaviour Summary
Aspect | Croatian Standard for U16
Voice | Low, calm, deliberate — no reactionary tone.
Body Language | Upright, hands calm, minimal gestures.
Focus | Standard, composure, tactical discipline.
Timing | 3–4 mins pre-match, 4–5 mins half-time.
Consistency | Same phrasing as training — “Calm brain, fast legs.” “Tempo control.”
Key Takeaway:
“At U16, the coach speaks less and leads more.
The message is clarity, composure, and collective responsibility —
professional habits through Croatian rhythm.”

heading: U16+ Pre-Match Routine - Timeline
headingPath: COACH PACK 4 (U16+) – PROFESSIONAL DEVELOPMENT PHASE > 10. U16+ Game Day Guidance > U16+ Pre-Match Routine - Timeline
content:
Reference: All timings work backwards from KO
Time (from KO) | Moment | Coach Focus | Coach Behaviour Standard | Key Reminder
KO–60 | Arrival | Settle & organise | Calm presence · logistics only · no football talk | Emotional state first
KO–50 | Change / Prep | Individual routine | Minimal voice · observe only | Not a coaching window
KO–45 | Main Team Talk | Tactical clarity | One picture · shared language · calm tone | Clarity > motivation Less than 6mins
KO–40 | Warm-Up Begins | Activate & reinforce | Reduce voice · use constraints · players take over | Movement reinforces ideas
KO–10 | Final Reminder | Trigger recall | One sentence · one trigger · then silence | Cue, not a talk
KO–4 | Leave Change Room | Emotional control | No talking · calm body language | Restraint = authority
KO | Kick-Off | Observe & manage | Correct standards only · no constant instruction | Players control the game
NON-NEGOTIABLE WARNINGS (FOR COACHES)
Warning Sign | What It Means
Repeating yourself | You’re talking too long
Adding late information | It should have been coached earlier
Emotional build-up | Clarity is being lost
Coaching constantly after KO | Players aren’t owning the game
CROATIAN MATCHDAY PRINCIPLES (REMEMBER)
- Calm beats intensity
- Structure beats speeches
- Triggers beat shouting
- Restraint signals authority
Pre-match talks don’t add ideas — they remove doubt.

heading: U16+ Pre-Match Routine - Detail
headingPath: COACH PACK 4 (U16+) – PROFESSIONAL DEVELOPMENT PHASE > 10. U16+ Game Day Guidance > U16+ Pre-Match Routine - Detail
content:
PURPOSE OF MATCHDAY ROUTINE
- Create calm, clarity, and readiness
- Reinforce shared tactical language
- Protect players from over-coaching
- Ensure the match is a performance moment, not a teaching session
Training does the teaching.Matchday protects execution.
MATCHDAY FLOW (KO-BASED)
🕒 KO–60 MINUTES | ARRIVAL
Focus: Settle, organise, decompress
Coach Behaviour
- Calm presence
- No tactical talk
- Logistics only (kit, hydration, admin)
Croatian Logic
- Players must arrive emotionally neutral
- No football information before they are ready to receive it
🕒 KO–50 MINUTES | CHANGE / PREP
Focus: Individual routine
Coach Behaviour
- Minimal voice
- Observe, don’t instruct
- Allow players to settle into their own rhythm
Standard
- This is not a coaching window
🕒 KO–45 MINUTES | MAIN TEAM TALK (BRIEF)
Focus: Tactical clarity, not motivation
Coach Behaviour
- Calm tone
- One tactical picture only
- Use shared language (already trained)
- No emotional build-up
Content Rules
- 1 collective focus
- 2–3 known triggers (maximum)
- No new information
- No individuals singled out
Croatian Principle
Clarity replaces motivation.
🕒 KO–40 MINUTES | WARM-UP BEGINS (≈25 MIN)
Focus: Physical + cognitive activation
Coach Behaviour
- Minimal instruction
- Reinforce ideas through constraints, not voice
- Gradually hand control to players
Standard
- Coaching volume decreases as KO approaches
- Final phase of warm-up is player-led
🕒 KO–10 MINUTES | FINAL REMINDER (≈1 MIN)
Focus: Trigger recall only
Coach Behaviour
- One sentence
- One reminder
- Calm delivery
Example
“Closed ball — we go together.”
Then silence.
Croatian Logic
- This is a cue, not a talk
🕒 KO–4 MINUTES | LEAVE CHANGE ROOM
Focus: Emotional control
Coach Behaviour
- No talking
- Calm body language
- Observe only
🕒 KO | KICK-OFF
Coach Role
- Observe
- Correct only when standards drop
- No constant instruction
If the coach is controlling the game verbally, the players are not.
MATCHDAY WARNINGS (NON-NEGOTIABLE)
For Coaches
- If you repeat yourself, you are talking too long
- If you add information late, it should not have been added at all
- If emotion rises, clarity drops
Red Flags
- Long pre-match speeches
- Tactical changes introduced on matchday
- Emotional hype before KO
These are anti-development behaviours.
CROATIAN MATCHDAY PHILOSOPHY (SUMMARY)
- Calm beats intensity
- Structure beats speeches
- Triggers beat shouting
- Restraint signals authority
Pre-match talks don’t add ideas — they remove doubt.

heading: U16+ Game Framework
headingPath: COACH PACK 4 (U16+) – PROFESSIONAL DEVELOPMENT PHASE > 10. U16+ Game Day Guidance > U16+ Game Framework
content:
Game Framework – U14+ (11-a-Side)
- Base Shape: 1-4-3-3
- Key Roles:
- Pivot: Control rhythm, connect lines.
- 8s/10s: Create overloads, run beyond.
- Wingers: Stretch, attack assist zone.
- Back Four: Build triangles, defend compact.
- Team Instructions:
- Play through, not around.
- Open-ball = go forward.
- Compact when we lose it.
- Tempo control = identity.

heading: U16+ Individual Competencies
headingPath: COACH PACK 4 (U16+) – PROFESSIONAL DEVELOPMENT PHASE > 11. U16+ Phase Outcomes > U16+ Individual Competencies
content:
Theme: Consistency, composure, and professional standards.
By the end of this phase, the player can:
- Sustain high technical quality under full match speed and fatigue.
- Adapt decisions instantly based on game rhythm and pressure.
- Control and vary tempo of possession to manage the match.
- Lead tactical organisation through communication and example.
- Perform in multiple roles within the team structure.
- Maintain compactness and balance across units.
- Execute counter-press and recovery runs instinctively.
- Deliver technically precise passes, crosses, and finishes under fatigue.
- Display emotional stability and resilience in adverse moments.
- Reflect independently on performance using football language.
“At this level, the player shows professional habits — not just ability.”

heading: U16+ Group Competencies
headingPath: COACH PACK 4 (U16+) – PROFESSIONAL DEVELOPMENT PHASE > 11. U16+ Phase Outcomes > U16+ Group Competencies
content:
Objective: Apply tactical principles autonomously and maintain professional game control.
Team Competencies:
- Maintain compactness and structure under fatigue or stress.
- Control tempo of match collectively — accelerate or secure.
- Adjust pressing and block height intelligently.
- Dominate transitions through anticipation, not reaction.
- Build pressure gradually and suffocate space systematically.
- Protect space behind line with coordinated timing.
- Sustain possession with control and purpose — not just safety.
- Use collective triggers (open-ball, return-ball, third-man) automatically.
- Execute team shape adjustments mid-game with minimal instruction.
- Demonstrate resilience and unity — emotional regulation as a team behaviour.
“Professional team maturity = compactness, communication, composure.”

heading: U16 sessions
headingPath: U16 sessions
content:
U16+ – CYCLE 1Team Build-Up & Game Control (6 Weeks)
Week 1• Session 1 – Collective Structure: Back + Mid Connection• Session 2 – Calm in the First Phase

heading: U16 Cycle 1, week 1, session 1
headingPath: U16 sessions > U16 Cycle 1, week 1, session 1
content:
BELCONNEN UNITED FC – SESSION PLAN | CYCLE 1 | WEEK 1 | SESSION 1
(Segoe UI 9 pt, grey text, Belco-blue underline)
U16 | Team Build-Up & Game Control | Week 1 | Collective Structure – Back + Mid Connection
Session Outcome
Build collective connection between back line and midfield to control the game through calm circulation, compact support, and disciplined decisions.
Activation – Uvodni dio / Game Activation (15 mins)
Setup
Area: 40 x 30m, directional (two end zones)
Players: 5v3 + GK (9 total; rotate defenders every 2–3 mins)
Organisation: Back 3 + 2 mids vs 3 pressers
Equipment: Balls, cones, 2 mini-goals
Description
Play starts with GK. Team in possession must connect back to mid before playing into an end zone.
If defenders win it, immediate counter to mini-goals.
Reset always from GK to reinforce first decision after regain.
Overload supports clarity and confidence early.
Coaching Focus
Collective Cues
Back line stays connected and compact behind the ball.
Midfield shows early and on angles, not flat.
Ball speed matches pressure: calm when free, quick when pressed.
Individual Cues
First touch opens the ball forward when possible.
Scan before receiving; know your next pass.
If pressed from one side, protect the ball and bounce support.
Progression
Limit touches for back players to increase tempo.
Situational Practice – Solving the Situation (20 mins)
Setup
Area: 45 x 35m, divided into back + mid zones
Players: 4v4 + 2 neutral mids (soft equality)
Targets: Score by finding a midfielder who plays forward into goal zone
Equipment: Balls, cones, 2 goals
Description
Directional game focused on playing through midfield.
Neutrals link play but must move to stay connected.
On loss, immediate press for 5 seconds; if broken, recover compactly.
Forces teams to manage risk together.
Coaching Focus
Collective Cues
Distances between back and mid stay short.
One player supports underneath the ball at all times.
Team stays compact to attack and compact to defend.
Individual Cues
If the mid is marked, then recycle and reset.
If pressure is late, then play forward with bravery.
Take responsibility to slow the game when needed.
Progression
Remove one neutral to move toward equality and raise pressure.
Situational Game – Collective Game Control (25 mins)
Setup
Field: 60 x 40m
Teams: 6v6 + GK
Goals: Full-size or large goals
Restarts: Always from GK
Description
Game-realistic practice with clear restarts.
Goals only count if the team connects through back + mid before finishing.
Normal football flow; coach manages tempo, not play.
Coaching Focus
Collective Cues
Stay compact while attacking to protect against transition.
Protect what’s behind the ball when playing forward.
Recognise when to press, when to hold, when to reset.
Individual Cues
Make decisions based on the moment, not your position.
Support the ball after passing; don’t admire it.
Communicate early to organise the next action.
Progression
Remove scoring condition to increase realism.
Free Play – Transfer Game (20 mins)
Setup
Game: 7v7, directional with goals
Conditions: None (free play)
Description
Let the game breathe.
Minimal coach intervention; observe decision-making under fatigue.
Coaching Focus
Observation only.
Look for calm build-up, compact support, and smart risk choices.
Coaching Information (U16)
Element | Detail
Objective / Outcomes | Team consistently connects back + mid to control tempo and reduce chaos
Technical / Tactical Detail | Spacing between lines, open body shape, timing of support, ball speed, balance
Coaching Messages (Belco Voice) | “Open ball, play brave.” • “Compact to attack.” • “Control the game.”
Common Mistakes & Fixes | Lines too far apart → “Shorten the pitch.” • Forcing forward play → “Reset and breathe.”
Praise Language | “Good decision.” • “You protected the team.” • “That’s game control.”
Behaviours to Reward | Scanning, communication, disciplined recycling, collective pressing
Reflection Question | How did your decision after the first pass affect the next phase?
Session Design Note | Overloads support clarity early; full realism by tactical phase
Language Note | Belco Framework vocabulary supports recognition and responsibility
Belconnen United FC – Compact • Brave • Intelligent(Light-blue separator line above; Segoe UI 8 pt grey text)

heading: U16 Cycle 1, week 1, session 2
headingPath: U16 sessions > U16 Cycle 1, week 1, session 2
content:
BELCONNEN UNITED FC – SESSION PLAN | CYCLE 1 | WEEK 1 | SESSION 2
(Segoe UI 9 pt, grey text, Belco-blue underline)
U16 | Team Build-Up & Game Control | Week 1 | Calm in First Phase
Session Outcome
Maintain calm and collective control in the first phase to secure possession, connect forward, and protect against transition.
Activation – Uvodni dio / Game Activation (15 mins)
Setup
Area: 35 x 30m, directional
Players: 4v2 + GK (rotate pressers every 2 mins)
Organisation: GK + back 3 + pivot vs 2 pressers
Equipment: Balls, cones, 2 mini-goals
Description
Play starts from GK. Objective is to complete 3 passes in first phase before playing into a mini-goal.
If pressers win it, immediate finish on mini-goals.
Reset always from GK to rehearse first decision after restart.
Overload allows clarity and repetition of calm behaviours.
Coaching Focus
Collective Cues
Distances stay short between GK, backs, and pivot.
Ball-side support underneath the ball at all times.
Team stays compact to attack, ready to defend loss.
Individual Cues
First touch sets the next action; don’t rush.
Scan early; know the safe option.
If under pressure, protect the ball and reset.
Progression
Reduce space by 5m to increase tempo and pressure.
Situational Practice – Solving the Situation (20 mins)
Setup
Area: 45 x 35m, split into first phase and midfield zones
Players: 5v4 (soft equality)
Targets: Progress from first phase into midfield zone under control
Equipment: Balls, cones, 2 goals
Description
Directional game focused on securing first phase before advancing.
Team must connect through GK or pivot before moving forward.
On loss in first phase, immediate 5-second counter-press; if broken, recover compactly.
Coaching Focus
Collective Cues
Back line spreads to open passing angles, not gaps.
Pivot stays connected, not hidden.
Team recognises when to slow the game.
Individual Cues
If the press is set, then recycle and reset.
If pressure is late, then play forward calmly.
Take responsibility for the first safe pass.
Progression
Add a neutral pressing player to increase realism.
Situational Game – Collective Game Control (25 mins)
Setup
Field: 60 x 40m
Teams: 6v6 + GK
Goals: Full-size or large goals
Restarts: All from GK
Description
Game-realistic practice.
Goals count double if build-up includes calm first-phase connection (GK + back + mid).
Normal football flow; coach intervenes only between rounds.
Coaching Focus
Collective Cues
Stay calm in the first pass after restart.
Protect space behind the ball when playing out.
Recognise when to press, play, or reset.
Individual Cues
Decisions linked in chains, not isolated actions.
Communicate early to organise first phase.
Value possession without losing intent.
Progression
Remove double-goal condition to increase realism.
Free Play – Transfer Game (20 mins)
Setup
Game: 7v7, directional with goals
Conditions: None
Description
Free play under fatigue.
Minimal coach input.
Coaching Focus
Observation only.
Look for calm first decisions holding under pressure.
Coaching Information (U16)
Element | Detail
Objective / Outcomes | Team secures first phase consistently and controls tempo
Technical / Tactical Detail | Body shape, first touch, spacing, passing angles, balance
Coaching Messages (Belco Voice) | “Open ball, play brave.” • “Protect what’s behind the ball.” • “Control the game.”
Common Mistakes & Fixes | Rushed passes → “Slow the moment.” • GK isolated → “Reconnect underneath.”
Praise Language | “That’s calm.” • “Good reset.” • “You protected us.”
Behaviours to Reward | Scanning, communication, discipline, intelligent recycling
Reflection Question | How did your first decision affect our next action?
Session Design Note | Overloads early for clarity; realism increases through the game
Language Note | Belco Framework vocabulary supports recognition
Belconnen United FC – Compact • Brave • Intelligent(Light-blue separator line above; Segoe UI 8 pt grey text)

heading: U16 Cycle 1, week 2, session 1
headingPath: U16 sessions > U16 Cycle 1, week 2, session 1
content:
BELCONNEN UNITED FC – SESSION PLAN | CYCLE 1 | WEEK 2 | SESSION 1
(Segoe UI 9 pt, grey text, Belco-blue underline)
U16 | Team Build-Up & Game Control | Week 2 | Open Ball Decisions – Recognise Triggers
Session Outcome
Recognise open-ball moments and choose the correct collective response to play forward, secure possession, or reset control.
Activation – Uvodni dio / Game Activation (15 mins)
Setup
Area: 40 x 30m, directional
Players: 5v3 + GK (rotate defenders every 2–3 mins)
Organisation: Back 3 + 2 mids vs 3 pressers
Equipment: Balls, cones, 2 mini-goals
Description
Play starts from GK.
Team scores by playing forward within 3 seconds when the ball is open.
If no forward option is on, possession must be recycled through GK.
Defenders counter to mini-goals on regain.
Reset always from GK to reinforce recognition after restarts.
Coaching Focus
Collective Cues
Team shape supports the ball to keep it open.
Distances stay short to allow quick bounce passes.
Players ahead of the ball prepare early for forward play.
Individual Cues
First touch opens the body to see both sides.
Scan before receiving to recognise pressure.
If the ball is open, play brave; if not, secure it.
Progression
Reduce allowed time to 2 seconds for forward decision.
Situational Practice – Solving the Situation (20 mins)
Setup
Area: 45 x 35m, split into back and mid zones
Players: 4v4 + 1 neutral (soft overload)
Targets: Progress into final zone under control
Equipment: Balls, cones, 2 goals
Description
Directional game where goals only count after an open-ball decision.
Neutral supports the team in possession to help create clarity.
On loss, immediate press for 5 seconds; if broken, recover compactly.
Forces collective recognition of when to accelerate or pause.
Coaching Focus
Collective Cues
Support angles create clear forward lanes.
Back players stay connected to protect against loss.
Team moves together when playing forward.
Individual Cues
If the receiver has time and space, then play forward.
If the receiver is closed, then reset and keep control.
Take responsibility for calling the trigger.
Progression
Remove the neutral to move toward equality.
Situational Game – Collective Game Control (25 mins)
Setup
Field: 60 x 40m
Teams: 6v6 + GK

U16 | Team Build-Up & Game Control | Week 2 | Create Support Angles
Session Outcome
Create and maintain clear support angles around the ball to keep possession secure, progress play, and protect against transition.
Activation – Uvodni dio / Game Activation (15 mins)
Setup
Area: 40 x 30m, directional
Players: 5v3 + GK (rotate pressers every 2–3 mins)
Organisation: Back 3 + 2 mids vs 3 pressers
Equipment: Balls, cones, 2 mini-goals
Description
Play starts from GK.
Team in possession scores by completing a pass with two visible support angles available.
If defenders win it, immediate counter to mini-goals.
Reset always from GK to rehearse support shape after regain.
Overload provides clarity to see angles early.
Coaching Focus
Collective Cues
Triangle shapes form naturally around the ball.
Distances are short enough to support, long enough to stretch pressure.
One player supports underneath, one offers forward or wide angle.
Individual Cues
Move early to show on an angle, not straight.
Scan to arrive facing forward when possible.
If no angle is on, adjust your run to help the ball carrier.
Progression
Limit back players to two touches to raise tempo.
Situational Practice – Solving the Situation (20 mins)
Setup
Area: 45 x 35m, split into back and mid zones
Players: 4v4 + 1 neutral (soft overload)
Targets: Progress into final zone after creating a clear angle
Equipment: Balls, cones, 2 goals
Description
Directional game focused on creating angles before the pass.
Neutral supports possession but must constantly adjust position.
On loss, immediate 5-second press; if broken, recover compactly.
Organisation forces players to move off the ball to solve pressure.
Coaching Focus
Collective Cues
Team moves as the ball moves to keep angles alive.
Back players stay connected to protect space behind the ball.
Support arrives early, not after the pass.
Individual Cues
If the ball carrier is facing pressure, then drop or slide to open a lane.
If the ball carrier is open, then stretch forward to receive.
Take responsibility to help the player on the ball.
Progression
Remove the neutral to move toward equality.
Situational Game – Collective Game Control (25 mins)
Setup
Field: 60 x 40m
Teams: 6v6 + GK
Goals: Full-size or large goals
Restarts: All from GK
Description
Game-realistic practice.
Goals count double if the attack includes clear support angles in the build-up.
Normal football flow; coach manages rhythm between rounds.
Coaching Focus
Collective Cues
Stay compact while attacking to keep angles close.
Protect what’s behind the ball when committing forward.
Recognise when to support underneath or ahead.
Individual Cues
Decisions based on the moment, not fixed positions.
After passing, move to create the next angle.
Communicate early to organise support.
Progression
Remove scoring condition to increase realism.
Free Play – Transfer Game (20 mins)
Setup

Conditions: None
Description
Free play with minimal intervention.

If defenders win it, counter immediately to mini-goals.
Reset always from GK to reinforce first-phase clarity.
Coaching Focus
Collective Cues
Distances between thirds stay short to keep connection.
Ball moves faster than players when progressing.
Team stays compact behind the ball to protect transition.
Individual Cues

Move early to offer a line into the next third.
If forward lane is blocked, recycle and reset.
Progression
Limit midfield players to two touches to increase speed of connection.

Neutrals play as connectors between zones.
On loss, immediate 5-second press; if broken, recover compactly into the middle third.
Organisation forces teams to manage risk and spacing collectively.
Coaching Focus
Collective Cues
One line supports underneath, one prepares to receive ahead.
Team shifts together as the ball moves between thirds.
Balance is maintained so loss doesn’t expose the back line.
Individual Cues
If the next third is open, then play forward with intent.
If the next third is blocked, then secure and reset.
Take responsibility to move off the ball to open lanes.
Progression
Remove one neutral to increase pressure and realism.

Protect what’s behind the ball when entering the final third.
Recognise when to progress and when to pause.
Individual Cues
Decisions based on the moment, not the zone.
Support the ball immediately after passing.
Communicate to organise the next line of play.
Progression
Remove scoring condition to increase realism.

Conditions: None
Description
Free play under fatigue.

Observation only.
Look for natural progression through thirds without prompts.

Collective Cues
Team stays compact to keep circulation safe.

If the ball is open, play brave and penetrate.

Circulation draws opponents before penetration.
Balance is maintained to protect transition.

Take responsibility to call the decision.

Remove one neutral to increase realism and risk.

Remove scoring condition to increase realism.

Conditions: None
Description
