BELCONNEN UNITED FC – SESSION PLAN | CYCLE 1 | WEEK 6 | SESSION 1
(Segoe UI 9 pt, grey text, Belco-blue underline)
U16 | Team Build-Up & Game Control | Week 6 | Tactical Game
Session Outcome
Execute collective game control across realistic match conditions by recognising moments, managing risk, and making connected decisions in all phases.
Activation – Uvodni dio / Game Activation (15 mins)
Setup
Area: 45 x 35m, directional
Players: 5v4 + GK
Organisation: Back 3 + 2 mids vs 4 pressers
Equipment: Balls, cones, 2 mini-goals
Description
Play starts from GK.
Team scores by completing 4 controlled passes and then choosing to progress or reset.
If defenders win it, immediate counter to mini-goals.
Reset always from GK to reinforce first decision under pressure.
Near-equality introduces realistic tactical stress early.
Coaching Focus
Collective Cues
Team stays compact to keep options connected.
Support underneath the ball is constant.
Ball speed matches pressure and picture.
Individual Cues
Scan before receiving; know the next action.
If pressure is set, secure and reset.
Communicate to organise the next phase.
Progression
Reduce required passes to 3 to increase decision speed.
Situational Practice – Solving the Situation (20 mins)
Setup
Area: 60 x 40m
Players: 6v6 + GK
Roles: Free positions; players solve roles in play
Equipment: Balls, cones, goals
Description
Directional game with live tactical scenarios (coach calls short cues between rounds: protect lead, break press, slow the game).
On loss, immediate 5-second press; if broken, recover compactly.
Forces teams to adapt collectively without shape-led instruction.
Coaching Focus
Collective Cues
Team adjusts tempo and risk together.
Compactness protects transitions.
Organisation changes with the scenario, not the shape.
Individual Cues
If protecting, then secure and support.
If opportunity appears, then accelerate with numbers.
Take ownership of reading the moment.
Progression
Remove coach cues; players must recognise scenarios themselves.
Situational Game – Collective Game Control (25 mins)
Setup
Field: 65 x 45m
Teams: 7v7
Goals: Full-size or large goals
Restarts: Normal match flow
Description
Fully realistic tactical game.
No constraints beyond football rules.
Coach intervenes only between rounds.
Coaching Focus
Collective Cues
Control the game through connection and compactness.
Protect what’s behind the ball at all times.
Recognise when to press, play forward, or reset.
Individual Cues
Decision chains across phases, not single actions.
Responsibility for moments, not positions.
Clear communication to guide teammates.
Progression
None – full realism.
Free Play – Transfer Game (20 mins)
Setup
Game: 7v7, free play
Conditions: None
Description
Let the game run.
Coach observes only.
Coaching Focus
Observation only.
Look for intelligent decisions holding under fatigue and pressure.
Coaching Information (U16)
Element | Detail
Objective / Outcomes | Team controls the game tactically across all moments
Technical / Tactical Detail | Tempo control, spacing, balance, decision timing, transitions
Coaching Messages (Belco Voice) | “Control the game.” • “Recognise the next moment.” • “Protect what’s behind the ball.”
Common Mistakes & Fixes | Forcing play → “Secure first.” • Passive defending → “Step together.”
Praise Language | “Good control.” • “You read the moment.” • “That’s leadership.”
Behaviours to Reward | Game awareness, communication, discipline, ownership
Reflection Question | Which decision helped us control the game most today?
Session Design Note | Minimal constraints to test true game understanding
Language Note | Belco Framework vocabulary supports shared recognition

content:
BELCONNEN UNITED FC – SESSION PLAN | CYCLE 2 | WEEK 1 | SESSION 1
(Segoe UI 9 pt, grey text, Belco-blue underline)
U16 | Breaking Lines & Creating Superiority | Week 1 | Create Superiority – Find the Free Man
Session Outcome
Create numerical or positional superiority by recognising pressure and finding the free player to progress the game with control.
Activation – Uvodni dio / Game Activation (15 mins)
Setup
Area: 40 x 30m, directional
Players: 5v3 + GK (rotate defenders every 2–3 mins)
Organisation: Back 3 + 2 mids vs 3 pressers
Equipment: Balls, cones, 2 mini-goals
Description
Play starts from GK.
Team scores by finding the free man before playing into a mini-goal.
Pressers score by winning and finishing quickly.
Reset always from GK to rehearse recognition after regain.
Overload supports early clarity in seeing superiority.
Coaching Focus
Collective Cues
Team stays compact to keep distances playable.
Ball movement attracts pressure to free a teammate.
Support players position on different lines and angles.
Individual Cues
Scan before receiving to see pressure and free space.
If two opponents press, look away from pressure.
First touch sets the angle to play out.
Progression
Limit touches for the back line to increase speed of recognition.
Situational Practice – Solving the Situation (20 mins)
Setup
Area: 50 x 35m, divided into two horizontal zones
Players: 4v4 + 1 neutral (soft superiority)
Targets: Score after playing through the free man
Equipment: Balls, cones, 2 goals
Description
Directional game where the neutral always plays with the team in possession.
Goals only count if the attack includes a pass to the free player.
On loss, immediate 5-second press; if broken, recover compactly.
Organisation forces teams to draw pressure and release at the right moment.
Coaching Focus
Collective Cues
Players ahead of the ball adjust to create a free option.
Team recognises where the overload is and uses it.
Balance is maintained to protect what’s behind the ball.
Individual Cues
If pressure comes, then find the spare.
If you are free, then show early and clearly.
Take responsibility to move, not hide.
Progression
Remove the neutral to move toward equality and increase difficulty.
Situational Game – Collective Game Control (25 mins)
Setup
Field: 60 x 40m
Teams: 6v6 + GK
Goals: Full-size or large goals
Restarts: All from GK
Description
Game-realistic practice.
Goals count double if the team breaks a line by finding the free man.
Normal football flow; coach manages tempo between rounds.
Coaching Focus
Collective Cues
Compactness helps create and use superiority.
Team moves together to support the free player.
Protect space behind the ball when committing forward.
Individual Cues
Decisions linked: attract → release → support.
Pass quality matches the moment.
Communicate to identify the spare player.
Progression
Remove scoring condition to increase realism.
Free Play – Transfer Game (20 mins)
Setup
Game: 7v7, directional with goals
Conditions: None
Description
Free play under fatigue.
Minimal coach intervention.
Coaching Focus
Observation only.
Look for natural recognition of superiority and free players.
Coaching Information (U16)
Element | Detail
Objective / Outcomes | Team consistently finds the free man to create superiority
Technical / Tactical Detail | Scanning, body shape, timing of movement, spacing, balance
Coaching Messages (Belco Voice) | “Recognise the next moment.” • “Open ball, play brave.” • “Control the game.”
Common Mistakes & Fixes | Forcing into pressure → “Where’s the spare?” • Static players → “Move to be free.”
Praise Language | “Good recognition.” • “You found the spare.” • “That breaks the line.”
Behaviours to Reward | Awareness, off-ball movement, communication, intelligent risk
Reflection Question | What helped you recognise where the free man was today?
Session Design Note | Superiority is clear early; realism increases through equality
Language Note | Belco Framework vocabulary supports shared recognition
Belconnen United FC – Compact • Brave • Intelligent(Light-blue separator line above; Segoe UI 8 pt grey text)

BELCONNEN UNITED FC – SESSION PLAN | CYCLE 2 | WEEK 1 | SESSION 2
(Segoe UI 9 pt, grey text, Belco-blue underline)
U16 | Breaking Lines & Creating Superiority | Week 1 | Third-Man Runs
Session Outcome
Break opposition lines by recognising when to use the third-man run to play forward with speed while maintaining collective balance.
Activation – Uvodni dio / Game Activation (15 mins)
Setup
Area: 40 x 30m, directional
Players: 5v3 + GK (rotate defenders every 2–3 mins)
Organisation: Back 3 + 2 mids vs 3 pressers
Equipment: Balls, cones, 2 mini-goals
Description
Play starts from GK.
Team scores by using a third-man combination before finishing into mini-goals.
Direct passes into goals without a third-man action do not count.
If defenders win it, immediate counter to mini-goals.
Reset always from GK to rehearse recognition after regain.
Overload helps players clearly see passing and running relationships.
Coaching Focus
Collective Cues
Ball attracts pressure to create space for the third man.
Distances between players allow one-touch bounce passes.
Team stays compact behind the ball to protect loss.
Individual Cues
First receiver sets the ball, not themselves.
Third man moves early, not after the pass.
Scan to see both the ball and space behind the line.
Progression
Limit first receiver to one touch to sharpen timing.
Situational Practice – Solving the Situation (20 mins)
Setup
Area: 50 x 35m, divided into two horizontal zones
Players: 4v4 + 1 neutral (soft superiority)
Targets: Score after breaking a line with a third-man run
Equipment: Balls, cones, 2 goals
Description
Directional game focused on third-man runs through midfield lines.
Neutral helps maintain overload and clarity.
Goals only count if the third man receives facing forward.
On loss, immediate 5-second press; if broken, recover compactly.
Forces players to coordinate movement and timing.
Coaching Focus
Collective Cues
One player checks short to draw a defender.
One player prepares to run beyond the line.
Team moves together to support the run.
Individual Cues
If you play the bounce pass, then hold your position.
If space opens behind the defender, then run through it.
Take responsibility to time the run, not force it.
Progression
Remove the neutral to increase pressure and realism.
Situational Game – Collective Game Control (25 mins)
Setup
Field: 60 x 40m
Teams: 6v6 + GK
Goals: Full-size or large goals
Restarts: All from GK
Description
Game-realistic practice.
Goals count double if the attack includes a clear third-man run breaking a line.
Normal football flow with minimal stoppages.
Coaching Focus
Collective Cues
Compactness helps support third-man actions.
Balance is maintained when players run beyond the ball.
Team recognises when third-man play is on or not.
Individual Cues
Decisions linked: set → run → support.
Quality of the set pass matters as much as the run.
Communicate early to organise timing.
Progression
Remove scoring condition to increase realism.
Free Play – Transfer Game (20 mins)
Setup
Game: 7v7, directional with goals
Conditions: None
Description
Free play under fatigue.
Minimal coach intervention.
Coaching Focus
Observation only.
Look for natural third-man runs without prompts.
Coaching Information (U16)
Element | Detail
Objective / Outcomes | Team breaks lines using third-man runs with control
Technical / Tactical Detail | Timing of movement, weight of pass, scanning, spacing, balance
Coaching Messages (Belco Voice) | “Recognise the next moment.” • “Open ball, play brave.” • “Compact to attack.”
Common Mistakes & Fixes | Late runs → “Go early.” • Forcing the pass → “Is the line really open?”
Praise Language | “Great timing.” • “You broke the line.” • “That’s intelligent play.”
Behaviours to Reward | Awareness, off-ball movement, discipline, intelligent risk
Reflection Question | What told you the third-man run was on today?
Session Design Note | Superiority clarifies timing early; realism increases through equality
Language Note | Belco Framework vocabulary supports shared recognition

BELCONNEN UNITED FC – SESSION PLAN | CYCLE 2 | WEEK 2 | SESSION 1
(Segoe UI 9 pt, grey text, Belco-blue underline)
U16 | Breaking Lines & Creating Superiority | Week 2 | Dynamic Width
Session Outcome
Create and use dynamic width to stretch opponents, open central lanes, and break lines while staying compact and protected.
Activation – Uvodni dio / Game Activation (15 mins)
Setup
Area: 45 x 30m, directional
Players: 5v3 + GK (rotate pressers every 2–3 mins)
Organisation: Back 3 + 2 mids vs 3 pressers
Equipment: Balls, cones, 2 mini-goals
Description
Play starts from GK.
Team scores by switching play to a wide player who arrived late, then finishing into mini-goals.
Wide players cannot start on the line; width must be created by movement, not position.
If defenders win it, immediate counter to mini-goals.
Reset always from GK to rehearse first decision and timing of width.
Coaching Focus
Collective Cues
Team stays compact centrally before stretching wide.
Width appears at the right moment, not permanently.
Opposite side tucks in to protect transition.
Individual Cues
Scan to see when central pressure is set.
Arrive wide on the move, not early.
If wide lane is closed, recycle and reset.
Progression
Limit central players to two touches to increase speed of switch.
Situational Practice – Solving the Situation (20 mins)
Setup
Area: 60 x 40m
Players: 4v4 + 2 wide neutrals (soft superiority)
Roles: Neutrals operate in wide channels but can rotate inside
Equipment: Balls, cones, 2 goals
Description
Directional game focused on creating width to free central space.
Goals count only if the attack uses width and then plays inside.
On loss, immediate 5-second press; if broken, recover compactly centrally.
Organisation forces players to recognise when width creates superiority.
Coaching Focus
Collective Cues
Central players attract pressure before releasing wide.
Far-side players stay connected to protect what’s behind the ball.
Team moves together when switching play.
Individual Cues
If central space is closed, then find width.
If width draws a defender, then attack inside.
Take responsibility to time your movement wide.
Progression
Remove one wide neutral to move toward equality.
Situational Game – Collective Game Control (25 mins)
Setup
Field: 65 x 45m
Teams: 6v6 + GK
Goals: Full-size or large goals
Restarts: All from GK
Description
Game-realistic practice.
Goals count double if width is used dynamically to break a line.
Normal football flow; coach manages rhythm between rounds.
Coaching Focus
Collective Cues
Compact to attack before stretching wide.
Protect space behind the ball when switching.
Recognise when width creates advantage and when it doesn’t.
Individual Cues
Decisions linked: attract → stretch → penetrate.
Quality of switch matters more than speed.
Communicate early to organise movement.
Progression
Remove scoring condition to increase realism.
Free Play – Transfer Game (20 mins)
Setup
Game: 7v7, directional with goals
Conditions: None
Description
Free play under fatigue.
Minimal coach intervention.
Coaching Focus
Observation only.
Look for natural use of dynamic width without prompts.
Coaching Information (U16)
Element | Detail
Objective / Outcomes | Team uses dynamic width to create superiority and break lines
Technical / Tactical Detail | Timing of movement, body shape, switch quality, spacing, balance
Coaching Messages (Belco Voice) | “Compact to attack.” • “Recognise the next moment.” • “Open ball, play brave.”
Common Mistakes & Fixes | Staying wide too early → “Arrive, don’t wait.” • Switching with no pressure → “Attract first.”
Praise Language | “Good timing.” • “You stretched them.” • “That opened the game.”
Behaviours to Reward | Awareness, movement off the ball, discipline, intelligent risk
Reflection Question | What told you width would help us break the line today?
Session Design Note | Superiority clarifies timing early; equality tests decision quality
Language Note | Belco Framework vocabulary supports shared recognition

BELCONNEN UNITED FC – SESSION PLAN | CYCLE 2 | WEEK 2 | SESSION 2
(Segoe UI 9 pt, grey text, Belco-blue underline)
U16 | Breaking Lines & Creating Superiority | Week 2 | Winger and Fullback Connection
Session Outcome
Create superiority on the side by connecting winger and fullback through timing, rotation, and shared decision-making to break lines with balance.
Activation – Uvodni dio / Game Activation (15 mins)
Setup
Area: 45 x 30m, directional
Players: 5v3 + GK (rotate defenders every 2–3 mins)
Organisation: Back 3 + winger + pivot vs 3 pressers
Equipment: Balls, cones, 2 mini-goals
Description
Play starts from GK.
Team scores by connecting winger and fullback (overlap, underlap, or bounce) before finishing into mini-goals.
Winger and fullback must both be involved in the action.
If defenders win it, immediate counter to mini-goals.
Reset always from GK to rehearse timing and spacing on the side.
Overload supports clarity in recognising side superiority.
Coaching Focus
Collective Cues
Side stays connected to the rest of the team.
One player holds width, one moves to connect.
Far side tucks in to protect transition.
Individual Cues
Scan early to see defender’s position.
If winger receives inside, fullback overlaps.
If winger stays wide, fullback supports underneath.
Progression
Limit side players to two touches to sharpen timing.
Situational Practice – Solving the Situation (20 mins)
Setup
Area: 60 x 40m, with wide channels
Players: 4v4 + 2 wide neutrals (winger/fullback roles)
Roles: Neutrals operate as side connectors and can rotate roles
Equipment: Balls, cones, 2 goals
Description
Directional game focused on creating 2v1s on the side.
Goals count only if the attack uses a winger–fullback connection to break a line.
On loss, immediate 5-second press; if broken, recover compactly centrally.
Organisation forces timing, communication, and shared responsibility.
Coaching Focus
Collective Cues
Central players attract pressure before releasing wide.
Side connection is supported underneath and behind.
Team shifts across to stay compact.
Individual Cues
If the defender jumps the winger, then play the fullback.
If the defender holds the fullback, then winger drives inside.
Take responsibility to communicate the action.
Progression
Remove one wide neutral to increase realism and pressure.
Situational Game – Collective Game Control (25 mins)
Setup
Field: 65 x 45m
Teams: 6v6 + GK
Goals: Full-size or large goals
Restarts: All from GK
Description
Game-realistic practice.
Goals count double if the attack clearly breaks a line through winger–fullback connection.
Normal football flow with minimal stoppages.
Coaching Focus
Collective Cues
Use the side to create superiority, not isolation.
Protect what’s behind the ball when committing the fullback.
Recognise when to combine and when to recycle.
Individual Cues
Decisions linked: receive → connect → support.
Timing of run matters more than speed.
Communicate early to organise roles.
Progression
Remove scoring condition to increase realism.
Free Play – Transfer Game (20 mins)
Setup
Game: 7v7, directional with goals
Conditions: None
Description
Free play under fatigue.
Minimal coach intervention.
Coaching Focus
Observation only.
Look for natural winger–fullback connections without prompts.
Coaching Information (U16)
Element | Detail
Objective / Outcomes | Team creates superiority on the side through winger–fullback connection
Technical / Tactical Detail | Timing of runs, body shape, spacing, communication, balance
Coaching Messages (Belco Voice) | “Compact to attack.” • “Recognise the next moment.” • “Open ball, play brave.”
Common Mistakes & Fixes | Both players wide → “One connects.” • Early overlaps → “Wait for the trigger.”
Praise Language | “Great connection.” • “Good timing.” • “That broke the line.”
Behaviours to Reward | Awareness, communication, discipline, intelligent rotation
Reflection Question | What told you how to connect on the side today?
Session Design Note | Superiority clarifies relationships early; equality tests timing
Language Note | Belco Framework vocabulary supports shared recognition
Belconnen United FC – Compact • Brave • Intelligent(Light-blue separator line above; Segoe UI 8 pt grey text)

BELCONNEN UNITED FC – SESSION PLAN | CYCLE 2 | WEEK 3 | SESSION 1
(Segoe UI 9 pt, grey text, Belco-blue underline)
U16 | Breaking Lines & Creating Superiority | Week 3 | Positional Overloads – Central or Wide
Session Outcome
Recognise where superiority exists and choose collectively whether to overload centrally or wide to break lines with control and balance.
Activation – Uvodni dio / Game Activation (15 mins)
Setup
Area: 45 x 30m, directional
Players: 5v3 + GK (rotate defenders every 2–3 mins)
Organisation: Back 3 + 2 mids vs 3 pressers
Equipment: Balls, cones, 2 mini-goals
Description
Play starts from GK.
Team scores by creating an overload either centrally or wide, then finishing into mini-goals.
Coach calls “central” or “wide” every 60–90 seconds to guide early recognition.
If defenders win it, immediate counter to mini-goals.
Reset always from GK to rehearse scanning and first decision.
Overload supports clarity in identifying where the spare player is.
Coaching Focus
Collective Cues
Team stays compact to keep options connected.
Ball movement attracts pressure before choosing the overload.
Opposite side tucks in to protect transition.
Individual Cues
Scan to see numbers, not just space.
If central is crowded, move the ball wide.
If wide is locked, connect inside and overload centrally.
Progression
Remove coach calls; players must recognise overload location themselves.
Situational Practice – Solving the Situation (20 mins)
Setup
Area: 60 x 40m, with central zone and wide channels marked
Players: 4v4 + 2 neutrals (soft superiority)
Roles: Neutrals can move freely to create overloads
Equipment: Balls, cones, 2 goals
Description
Directional game focused on choosing the correct overload.
Goals count only if the attack uses a clear central or wide overload.
On loss, immediate 5-second press; if broken, recover compactly.
Organisation forces teams to read pressure and numbers together.
Coaching Focus
Collective Cues
Team shifts together toward the chosen overload.
One area is overloaded while the other is protected.
Balance is maintained to protect what’s behind the ball.
Individual Cues
If defenders collapse centrally, then exploit wide.
If wide defenders jump out, then play through the middle.
Take responsibility to move and create the overload.
Progression
Remove one neutral to increase pressure and realism.
Situational Game – Collective Game Control (25 mins)
Setup
Field: 65 x 45m
Teams: 6v6 + GK
Goals: Full-size or large goals
Restarts: All from GK
Description
Game-realistic practice.
Goals count double if the team clearly creates and uses a positional overload to break a line.
Normal football flow with minimal stoppages.
Coaching Focus
Collective Cues
Compact to attack before shifting overload.
Protect space behind the ball when committing numbers.
Recognise when to switch overload zones.
Individual Cues
Decisions linked: attract → overload → penetrate.
Support the overload immediately after passing.
Communicate to organise movement.
Progression
Remove scoring condition to increase realism.
Free Play – Transfer Game (20 mins)
Setup
Game: 7v7, directional with goals
Conditions: None
Description
Free play under fatigue.
Minimal coach intervention.
Coaching Focus
Observation only.
Look for natural recognition of central vs. wide overloads.
Coaching Information (U16)
Element | Detail
Objective / Outcomes | Team chooses and uses positional overloads to break lines

(Segoe UI 9 pt, grey text, Belco-blue underline)
U16 | Breaking Lines & Creating Superiority | Week 3 | Break Pressure
Session Outcome
Break opponent pressure collectively by recognising cues, using support and movement, and progressing the ball without losing balance.
Activation – Uvodni dio / Game Activation (15 mins)
Setup
Area: 45 x 30m, directional
Players: 5v4 + GK

Description
Play starts from GK.
Team scores by breaking the first line of pressure (pass, carry, or third-man) before finishing into mini-goals.
Pressing team scores by winning and finishing quickly.
Reset always from GK to rehearse first decision under pressure.
Near-equality introduces realistic pressure immediately.
Coaching Focus
Collective Cues
Team stays compact to keep options connected.
Support underneath the ball appears early.
Ball speed matches pressure—no panic.
Individual Cues
Scan early to see where pressure is coming from.
If pressed from one side, play away from pressure.
First touch protects the ball and opens the next action.
Progression
Reduce space by 5m to increase intensity.
Situational Practice – Solving the Situation (20 mins)
Setup
Area: 60 x 40m, divided into two horizontal zones
Players: 6v6 + GK
Targets: Progress into the next zone after breaking pressure
Equipment: Balls, cones, goals
Description
Directional game focused on breaking coordinated pressure.
Defending team presses aggressively as a unit.
On loss, immediate 5-second counter-press; if broken, recover compactly.
Organisation forces players to connect decisions across lines.
Coaching Focus
Collective Cues
Team recognises where pressure is strongest.
One player draws pressure; another prepares to receive free.
Balance is maintained to protect what’s behind the ball.
Individual Cues
If pressure is tight centrally, then play wide or bounce.
If pressure jumps wide, then connect inside.
Take responsibility to move and help the ball carrier.
Progression
Add a rule: goals count double after breaking pressure in one action.
Situational Game – Collective Game Control (25 mins)
Setup
Field: 65 x 45m
Teams: 7v7
Goals: Full-size or large goals
Restarts: Normal match flow
Description
Game-realistic practice.
No restrictions—players must break pressure through football decisions.
Coach intervenes only between rounds.
Coaching Focus
Collective Cues
Control the game by breaking pressure calmly.
Stay compact to support the next action.
Protect space behind the ball when pressure is broken.
Individual Cues
Decisions linked: draw → release → support.
Stay composed even when pressed.
Communicate to organise movement.
Progression
None – full realism.
Free Play – Transfer Game (20 mins)
Setup
Game: 7v7, free play
Conditions: None
Description
Let the game run under fatigue.
Minimal coach intervention.
Coaching Focus
Observation only.
Look for calm pressure-breaking decisions holding naturally.

Element | Detail

Play starts from GK.
Team scores by penetrating forward within 3 seconds once the ball is open.
If penetration isn’t on, the team must recycle through GK.
Defenders counter to mini-goals on regain.
Reset always from GK to reinforce recognition of penetration cues.
Overload supports early clarity in seeing forward options.
Coaching Focus
Collective Cues
Team stays compact to support penetration.
Forward runs prepare early when the ball opens.
Players behind the ball hold balance.
Individual Cues
Scan to recognise space between or behind lines.
First touch opens forward when penetration is on.
If closed, secure and reset without forcing.
Progression
Reduce allowed decision time to 2 seconds to sharpen recognition.
Situational Practice – Solving the Situation (20 mins)
Setup
Area: 60 x 40m, divided into two horizontal zones
Players: 4v4 + 1 neutral (soft superiority)
Targets: Score after a penetrating action breaks a line
Equipment: Balls, cones, 2 goals
Description
Directional game focused on choosing the right penetration moment.
Neutral supports possession and helps draw pressure.
Goals count only if penetration breaks a defensive line.
On loss, immediate 5-second press; if broken, recover compactly.
Organisation forces teams to read pressure collectively.
Coaching Focus
Collective Cues
Team attracts pressure before penetrating.
Support runs follow the penetration quickly.
Balance is maintained to protect what’s behind the ball.
Individual Cues
If the defender steps out, then penetrate behind.
If space is tight, then circulate and wait.
Take responsibility to drive forward when it’s on.
Progression
Remove the neutral to increase realism and pressure.
Situational Game – Collective Game Control (25 mins)
Setup
Field: 65 x 45m
Teams: 6v6 + GK
Goals: Full-size or large goals
Restarts: All from GK
Description
Game-realistic practice.
Goals count double if the attack includes a clear penetration action that breaks a line.
Normal football flow with minimal stoppages.
Coaching Focus
Collective Cues
Recognise when penetration creates advantage.
Stay compact to attack and protect transition.
Reset quickly if penetration is stopped.
Individual Cues
Decisions linked: open ball → penetrate → support.
Quality of the first forward action matters.
Communicate to organise follow-up runs.
Progression
Remove scoring condition to increase realism.
Free Play – Transfer Game (20 mins)
Setup
Game: 7v7, directional with goals
Conditions: None
Description
Free play under fatigue.
Minimal coach intervention.
Coaching Focus
Observation only.
Look for correct penetration decisions holding naturally.

Element | Detail

Session Outcome
Press as a synchronised front and midfield unit to close space together, force predictable play, and regain control quickly.
Activation – Uvodni dio / Game Activation (15 mins)
Setup
Area: 40 x 30m, directional
Players: 5v4 + GK

Equipment: Balls, cones, 2 mini-goals
Description
Play starts from GK.
Pressing team scores by winning the ball within 6 seconds with front and midfield lines moving together.
Build-up team scores by connecting 5 passes.
Reset always from GK to rehearse starting distances and timing.
Near-equality demands coordination, not chasing.
Coaching Focus
Collective Cues
Front and midfield lines stay connected vertically.
First line presses; second line steps to close space behind.
Team squeezes forward together.
Individual Cues
Scan to check distance to the next line before pressing.
If the striker jumps, midfield steps with them.
Angle runs to force play into pressure.
Progression
Reduce area length by 5m to sharpen line connection.

Setup
Area: 60 x 40m
Players: 6v6 + GK
Targets: Regain and score within 8 seconds
Equipment: Balls, cones, goals

Organisation forces timing and shared responsibility.
Coaching Focus
Collective Cues
Front line triggers; midfield locks in behind.
Gaps between lines are closed quickly.
Team shifts sideways as one block.
Individual Cues
If the forward presses, then step up behind them.
If the ball escapes the first line, then engage immediately.
Take responsibility for holding your line.
Progression
Add a rule: goals only count if the regain comes from a synchronised press.

Press and recover as two connected lines.
Stay compact to protect space behind the press.

Individual Cues
Decisions linked: trigger → step → squeeze.
Don’t overrun the press—stay connected.
Communicate early to organise the unit.

Remove scoring condition to increase realism.

Collective Cues
Distances between lines stay short.
One player always protects depth.
Team presses forward but stays connected underneath.

If you jump, make sure someone covers.

Collective Cues
Team squeezes space but keeps a safety player.
Back players read the press and adjust depth.
Recovery runs are immediate if the press is broken.
Individual Cues
If the press is secure, then step and squeeze.
If the ball is played behind, then recover quickly and delay.
Take responsibility to protect the team, not just win the ball.

Add a rule: build-up team scores double if they exploit space behind the press.

Setup
Field: 65 x 45m
Teams: 7v7
Goals: Full-size or large goals
Restarts: Normal match flow
Description

Goals count double if scored after preventing a ball played behind the press and regaining possession.
No other constraints.
Coaching Focus
Collective Cues
Protect what’s behind the ball while pressing.
Stay compact vertically and horizontally.
Recognise when to press and when to drop.
Individual Cues
Decisions linked: press → cover → recover.
Don’t step out without protection.
Communicate early to organise depth.
Progression
Remove scoring condition to increase realism.
Free Play – Transfer Game (20 mins)
Setup
Game: 7v7, free play
Conditions: None
Description
Free play under fatigue.
Coach observes only.
Coaching Focus
Observation only.
Look for natural protection of space behind the press.

Element | Detail

Praise Language | “Good protection.” • “You balanced the press.” • “That saved us.”
Behaviours to Reward | Discipline, awareness, recovery effort, communication

Add a rule: goals count double if scored within 8 seconds of regain.

BELCONNEN UNITED FC – SESSION PLAN | CYCLE 5 | WEEK 3 | SESSION 1
(Segoe UI 9 pt, grey text, Belco-blue underline)
U16 | Transition & Game Management | Week 3 | Control the Emotional Tempo
Session Outcome
Control emotional responses during key moments by choosing calm, connected actions that stabilise the game.
Activation – Uvodni dio / Game Activation (15 mins)
Setup
Area: 40 x 30m, directional
Players: 5v4 + GK
Organisation: Possession team vs pressing team
Equipment: Balls, cones, 2 mini-goals
Description
Play starts from GK.
Coach calls emotional triggers during play (e.g. lost duel, missed chance, bad pass).
After a trigger, the team has 3 seconds to respond calmly: secure, reset, or protect.
Calm response = point.
Rash action leads to immediate counter.
Reset always from GK to repeat emotional moments.
Near-equality exposes emotional pressure without chaos.
Coaching Focus
Collective Cues
Team slows together after emotional moments.
Compact shape restores control.
Communication increases after mistakes.
Individual Cues
Take one calm touch after an error.
Lift head before next action.
If frustrated, secure and reset.
Progression
Reduce response time to 2 seconds.
Situational Practice – Solving the Situation (20 mins)
Setup
Area: 60 x 40m
Players: 6v6 + GK
Organisation: Directional game with emotional triggers built in
Equipment: Balls, cones, goals
Description
Directional game focused on emotional control during transitions.
Triggers include: turnover, foul, missed finish, or conceded goal.
Team must manage tempo for the next 10 seconds (secure or slow).
Goals only count if the team shows control after the trigger.
Organisation forces players to recognise emotional swings and stabilise.
Coaching Focus
Collective Cues
Team regroups immediately after setbacks.
Tempo is lowered to regain control.
Compactness protects emotional reactions.
Individual Cues
If emotion is high, then slow the game.
If teammates rush, then help them reset.
Take responsibility for calming the moment.
Progression
Add a rule: emotional reactions (complaints, rushed shots) concede a point.
Situational Game – Collective Game Control (25 mins)
Setup
Field: 65 x 45m
Teams: 7v7
Goals: Full-size or large goals
Restarts: Normal match flow
Description
Game-realistic practice.
No artificial constraints.
Coach observes emotional responses to fouls, goals, and mistakes.
Interventions only between rounds.
Coaching Focus
Collective Cues
Team controls the game emotionally.
Compact to regain rhythm.
Recognise when to slow or reset the game.
Individual Cues
Decisions linked: mistake → response → control.
Don’t chase emotional wins.
Communicate calmly under pressure.
Progression
None – full realism.
Free Play – Transfer Game (20 mins)
Setup
Game: 7v7, free play
Conditions: None
Description
Free play under fatigue.
Minimal coach intervention.
Coaching Focus
Observation only.
Look for calm decision-making after emotional moments.
Coaching Information (U16)
Element | Detail
Objective / Outcomes | Team controls emotional responses to manage tempo and decisions
Technical / Tactical Detail | First touch control, scanning, spacing, tempo regulation
Coaching Messages (Belco Voice) | “Control the game.” • “Recognise the next moment.” • “Compact to reset.”
Common Mistakes & Fixes | Rushing after errors → “Slow it down.” • Emotional fouls → “Stay composed.”
Praise Language | “Good control.” • “You stayed calm.” • “That settled us.”
Behaviours to Reward | Composure, leadership, communication, discipline
Reflection Question | Which moments tested our emotional control the most today?
Session Design Note | Emotional control is trained through realistic stress, not lectures
Language Note | Belco Framework vocabulary supports recognition and self-regulation
Belconnen United FC – Compact • Brave • Intelligent(Light-blue separator line above; Segoe UI 8 pt grey text)

content:
BELCONNEN UNITED FC – SESSION PLAN | CYCLE 5 | WEEK 3 | SESSION 2
(Segoe UI 9 pt, grey text, Belco-blue underline)
U16 | Transition & Game Management | Week 3 | Stay Calm to Play Fast
Session Outcome
Stay calm in possession and transition moments so the team can accelerate play at the right time without losing balance.
Activation – Uvodni dio / Game Activation (15 mins)
Setup
Area: 40 x 30m, directional
Players: 5v4 + GK
Organisation: Possession team vs pressing team
Equipment: Balls, cones, 2 mini-goals
Description
Play starts from GK.
On regain, the team must secure the first action (control pass or touch) before accelerating.
If the first action is calm and connected, team can attack mini-goals at speed.
Rushed first actions result in immediate turnover.
Reset always from GK to repeat calm-to-fast moments.
Near-equality exposes pressure without chaos.
Coaching Focus
Collective Cues
Team connects quickly around the first pass.
Compact shape supports calm first actions.
Acceleration comes after control, not before.
Individual Cues
First touch settles the moment.
Lift head before playing fast.
If rushed, secure and reset.
Progression
Reduce space by 5m to increase pressure on calm control.
Situational Practice – Solving the Situation (20 mins)
Setup
Area: 60 x 40m
Players: 6v6 + GK
Organisation: Directional game with frequent transitions
Equipment: Balls, cones, goals
Description
Directional game focused on calm first action → fast second action.
Goals only count if the attack includes a controlled first action after regain.
If the first action is rushed, possession automatically changes.
On loss, immediate reaction to protect what’s behind the ball.
Organisation forces players to manage tempo intelligently.
Coaching Focus
Collective Cues
Team recognises when to slow before speeding up.
Support angles appear early to help calm control.
Compactness protects failed accelerations.
Individual Cues
If the regain is under pressure, then secure first.
If the ball is settled, then play fast.
Take responsibility for setting the team’s tempo.
Progression
Add a rule: fast attacks must finish within 8 seconds after the calm action.
Situational Game – Collective Game Control (25 mins)
Setup
Field: 65 x 45m
Teams: 7v7
Goals: Full-size or large goals
Restarts: Normal match flow
Description
Game-realistic practice.
No artificial constraints.
Focus is on natural tempo control—calm when needed, fast when available.
Coach intervenes only between rounds.
Coaching Focus
Collective Cues
Team controls rhythm through calm possession.
Acceleration is collective, not individual.
Shape behind the ball supports fast play.
Individual Cues
Decisions linked: regain → calm → accelerate.
Don’t confuse speed with rush.
Communicate to organise tempo changes.
Progression
None – full realism.
Free Play – Transfer Game (20 mins)
Setup
Game: 7v7, free play
Conditions: None
Description
Free play under fatigue.
Minimal coach intervention.
Coaching Focus
Observation only.
Look for calm control leading naturally into fast attacks.
Coaching Information (U16)
Element | Detail
Objective / Outcomes | Team uses calm actions to unlock fast, effective play
Technical / Tactical Detail | First touch quality, scanning, support angles, tempo change
Coaching Messages (Belco Voice) | “Calm first.” • “Then play fast.” • “Control the game.”
Common Mistakes & Fixes | Rushing first action → “Set it first.” • Slow after control → “Go now.”
Praise Language | “Good calm.” • “You set the tempo.” • “That unlocked speed.”
Behaviours to Reward | Composure, awareness, leadership, intelligent acceleration
Reflection Question | How did staying calm help us play faster today?
Session Design Note | Calm control is trained as the trigger for speed, not the opposite
Language Note | Belco Framework vocabulary supports rhythm and control

content:
BELCONNEN UNITED FC – SESSION PLAN | CYCLE 5 | WEEK 4 | SESSION 1
(Segoe UI 9 pt, grey text, Belco-blue underline)
U16 | Transition & Game Management | Week 4 | Organise While Attacking
Session Outcome
Attack with intent while staying organised behind the ball to control transitions and protect the next moment.
Activation – Uvodni dio / Game Activation (15 mins)
Setup
Area: 40 x 30m, directional
Players: 5v4 + GK
Organisation: Attacking team vs defending team
Equipment: Balls, cones, 2 mini-goals
Description
Play starts from GK.
Attacking team scores by progressing forward with at least two players organised behind the ball.
If possession is lost with no protection behind, defending team counters to mini-goals.
Reset always from GK to rehearse attacking shape and balance.
Near-equality highlights organisation over numbers forward.
Coaching Focus
Collective Cues
Team attacks with clear protection underneath.
Distances between lines stay connected.
Players behind the ball are active, not passive.
Individual Cues
If you go forward, check who stays.
Scan before joining the attack.
Be ready to react immediately on loss.
Progression
Reduce area length by 5m to increase transition pressure.
Situational Practice – Solving the Situation (20 mins)
Setup
Area: 60 x 40m
Players: 6v6 + GK
Organisation: Directional game with emphasis on attacking balance
Equipment: Balls, cones, goals
Description
Directional game focused on organising behind the attack.
Goals only count if the attacking team maintains numerical balance or cover behind the ball.
On loss, immediate transition—counter-press if organised, recover if not.
Organisation forces players to read risk while attacking.
Coaching Focus
Collective Cues
Team recognises when to commit numbers and when to hold.
Compactness behind the ball supports counter-pressing.
Shape allows quick reaction after loss.
Individual Cues
If the attack builds, then hold a covering position.
If teammates push on, then organise underneath.
Take responsibility for the team’s balance.
Progression
Add a rule: goals conceded immediately after loss count double.
Situational Game – Collective Game Control (25 mins)
Setup
Field: 65 x 45m
Teams: 7v7
Goals: Full-size or large goals
Restarts: Normal match flow
Description
Game-realistic practice.
No artificial constraints.
Players must manage attacking ambition with defensive organisation.
Coach intervenes only between rounds.
Coaching Focus
Collective Cues
Attack without losing team structure.
Protect what’s behind the ball while building.
Recognise when to secure possession after attacks.
Individual Cues
Decisions linked: attack → organise → react.
Don’t all arrive on the same line.
Communicate to manage balance.
Progression
None – full realism.
Free Play – Transfer Game (20 mins)
Setup
Game: 7v7, free play
Conditions: None
Description
Free play under fatigue.
Minimal coach intervention.
Coaching Focus
Observation only.
Look for natural organisation while attacking.
Coaching Information (U16)
Element | Detail
Objective / Outcomes | Team stays organised behind the ball while attacking
Technical / Tactical Detail | Distances, covering positions, counter-press readiness
Coaching Messages (Belco Voice) | “Organise behind.” • “Protect what’s behind the ball.” • “Control the game.”
Common Mistakes & Fixes | Everyone going forward → “Who’s protecting?” • Flat attacking lines → “Stagger and connect.”
Praise Language | “Good balance.” • “You protected us.” • “That allowed us to attack again.”
Behaviours to Reward | Awareness, discipline, communication, responsibility
Reflection Question | How did staying organised help our attacks today?
Session Design Note | Organisation in attack underpins transition control
Language Note | Belco Framework vocabulary supports recognition and balance
Belconnen United FC – Compact • Brave • Intelligent(Light-blue separator line above; Segoe UI 8 pt grey text)

BELCONNEN UNITED FC – SESSION PLAN | CYCLE 5 | WEEK 4 | SESSION 2
(Segoe UI 9 pt, grey text, Belco-blue underline)
U16 | Transition & Game Management | Week 4 | Rest Defence Awareness
Session Outcome
Attack with awareness of rest defence so the team is prepared to counter-press, delay, or recover immediately after loss.

Setup
Area: 40 x 30m, directional
Players: 5v4 + GK
Organisation: Attacking team vs defending team
Equipment: Balls, cones, 2 mini-goals
Description
Play starts from GK.
Attacking team scores by progressing forward with clear rest-defence positioning (minimum two players connected behind the ball).
If possession is lost and rest defence is poor, defending team counters to mini-goals.
Reset always from GK to repeat attacking–transition moments.
Near-equality highlights awareness over numbers forward.
Coaching Focus
Collective Cues
Team attacks with protection underneath.
Distances between rest-defence players stay connected.
Shape behind the ball allows immediate counter-press or delay.
Individual Cues
Scan before joining the attack.
If you go, someone must stay.
Be ready to react instantly on loss.
Progression
Reduce area length by 5m to increase transition pressure.
Situational Practice – Solving the Situation (20 mins)
Setup
Area: 60 x 40m
Players: 6v6 + GK
Organisation: Directional game emphasising rest-defence roles
Equipment: Balls, cones, goals
Description
Directional game focused on rest defence during attacks.
Goals only count if the attacking team maintains balance behind the ball.
On loss:
If rest defence is organised → counter-press.
If not → recover and delay.
Organisation forces players to read risk while attacking.
Coaching Focus
Collective Cues
Team recognises who holds rest-defence positions.
Compactness behind the attack supports immediate reaction.
Balance is adjusted as the attack develops.
Individual Cues
If teammates push high, then organise underneath.
If the ball is central, then protect central rest defence.
Take responsibility for protecting the team, not chasing goals.
Progression
Add a rule: goals conceded directly after loss count double.
Situational Game – Collective Game Control (25 mins)
Setup
Field: 65 x 45m
Teams: 7v7
Goals: Full-size or large goals
Restarts: Normal match flow
Description
Game-realistic practice.
No artificial constraints.
Players manage attacking ambition with rest-defence discipline.
Coach intervenes only between rounds.
Coaching Focus
Collective Cues
Attack without losing protection.
Protect what’s behind the ball at all times.
Recognise when to counter-press and when to recover.
Individual Cues
Decisions linked: attack → organise → react.
Don’t all arrive on the same line.
Communicate to maintain balance.
Progression
None – full realism.
Free Play – Transfer Game (20 mins)
Setup
Game: 7v7, free play
Conditions: None
Description
Free play under fatigue.
Minimal coach intervention.
Coaching Focus
Observation only.
Look for natural rest-defence habits during attacks.
Coaching Information (U16)
Element | Detail
Objective / Outcomes | Team attacks with awareness of rest defence to control transitions
Technical / Tactical Detail | Distances, positioning behind the ball, counter-press readiness
Coaching Messages (Belco Voice) | “Organise behind.” • “Protect what’s behind the ball.” • “Control the game.”
Common Mistakes & Fixes | Everyone attacking → “Who’s protecting?” • Flat rest defence → “Stagger and connect.”
Praise Language | “Good protection.” • “You were ready to react.” • “That kept us safe.”
Behaviours to Reward | Awareness, discipline, communication, responsibility
Reflection Question | How did our rest defence help us react after losing the ball today?

BELCONNEN UNITED FC – SESSION PLAN | CYCLE 5 | WEEK 5 | SESSION 1
(Segoe UI 9 pt, grey text, Belco-blue underline)
U16 | Transition & Game Management | Week 5 | Match Scenarios – Protect Lead
Session Outcome
Protect a narrow lead by controlling tempo, staying compact, and making disciplined decisions across all phases.
Activation – Uvodni dio / Game Activation (15 mins)
Setup
Area: 40 x 30m, directional
Players: 5v4 + GK
Organisation: Team protecting a lead vs attacking team
Equipment: Balls, cones, 2 mini-goals
Description
Play starts from GK.
One team is 1–0 up with 6 minutes left (scenario announced).
Defending team scores by keeping possession for 10 seconds, forcing backward play, or winning a foul.
Attacking team scores by quick progression to mini-goals.
Reset always from GK to repeat lead-protection moments.
Near-equality highlights decision-making under scoreboard pressure.
Coaching Focus
Collective Cues
Team stays compact and connected.
Tempo is slowed deliberately.
Central space is protected first.
Individual Cues
Secure first touch under pressure.
If nothing is on, recycle and organise.
Avoid risky passes in central areas.
Progression
Reduce time allowed to keep possession to increase pressure.
Situational Practice – Solving the Situation (20 mins)
Setup
Area: 60 x 40m
Players: 6v6 + GK
Organisation: Scoreboard scenario (leading by one goal)
Equipment: Balls, cones, goals
Description
Directional game focused on protecting a lead.
Attacking team presses aggressively.
Defending team wins by managing the game: keeping the ball, forcing wide play, or delaying attacks.
On regain, team decides whether to secure or relieve pressure.
Organisation forces players to balance possession, territory, and risk.
Coaching Focus
Collective Cues
Team recognises when to slow the game.
Compact shape supports ball security.
Clear communication to manage pressure moments.
Individual Cues
If pressed heavily, then secure and reset.
If space opens wide, then relieve pressure.
Take responsibility for calming the moment.
Progression
Add a rule: goals conceded from central turnovers count double.
Situational Game – Collective Game Control (25 mins)
Setup
Field: 65 x 45m
Teams: 7v7
Goals: Full-size or large goals
Scenario: One team protecting a one-goal lead
Restarts: Normal match flow
Description
Game-realistic match scenario.
Coach reminds players of score and time only between rounds.
Focus on tempo control, compact defending, and smart possession.
Players manage the game without constant coach input.
Coaching Focus
Collective Cues
Control the game, not the scoreline emotionally.
Stay compact to defend and secure possession.
Recognise moments to clear lines vs keep the ball.
Individual Cues
Decisions linked: secure → organise → delay or play out.
Don’t force attacking actions.
Communicate to manage time and shape.
Progression
None – full realism.
Free Play – Transfer Game (20 mins)
Setup
Game: 7v7, free play
Conditions: Scoreboard remains active (coach tracks score/time silently)
Description
Free play under fatigue.
Coach observes only.
Coaching Focus
Observation only.
Look for natural lead-protection behaviours without reminders.
Coaching Information (U16)
Element | Detail
Objective / Outcomes | Team protects a lead through compactness, tempo control, and discipline
Technical / Tactical Detail | Ball security, spacing, clearing vs securing decisions
Coaching Messages (Belco Voice) | “Control the game.” • “Protect what’s behind the ball.” • “Stay compact.”
Common Mistakes & Fixes | Forcing attacks → “Secure first.” • Panicking → “Slow it down.”
Praise Language | “Good control.” • “You managed the moment.” • “That killed the pressure.”
Behaviours to Reward | Composure, discipline, communication, game awareness
Reflection Question | Which moments required us to slow the game down today?
Session Design Note | Match scenarios train decision-making under emotional pressure
Language Note | Belco Framework vocabulary supports recognition and control
Belconnen United FC – Compact • Brave • Intelligent(Light-blue separator line above; Segoe UI 8 pt grey text)

BELCONNEN UNITED FC – SESSION PLAN | CYCLE 5 | WEEK 5 | SESSION 2
(Segoe UI 9 pt, grey text, Belco-blue underline)
U16 | Transition & Game Management | Week 5 | Change Tempo Intelligently
Session Outcome
Recognise when to slow the game and when to accelerate, changing tempo intelligently to control the match.
Activation – Uvodni dio / Game Activation (15 mins)
Setup
Area: 40 x 30m, directional
Players: 5v4 + GK
Organisation: Possession team vs pressing team
Equipment: Balls, cones, 2 mini-goals
Description
Play starts from GK.
Possession team must change tempo within each sequence: at least one slow action (secure/reset) followed by one fast action (forward pass, run, or shot).
Goals only count if both tempo phases are shown.
If tempo change is forced or rushed, possession turns over.
Reset always from GK to repeat rhythm decisions.
Near-equality keeps decisions realistic.
Coaching Focus
Collective Cues
Team recognises when the game needs calming.
Support appears early to allow tempo change.
Compact shape protects poor acceleration.
Individual Cues
Scan before speeding up.
If the team is stretched, slow it.
Accelerate only when support is ready.
Progression
Reduce space by 5m to increase pressure on tempo decisions.
Situational Practice – Solving the Situation (20 mins)
Setup
Area: 60 x 40m
Players: 6v6 + GK
Organisation: Directional game with tempo conditions
Equipment: Balls, cones, goals
Description
Directional game focused on intelligent tempo change.
Coach calls “hold” or “go” only early; later players must decide themselves.
Goals count double if the attack shows clear slow → fast rhythm.
On loss, immediate reaction to protect what’s behind the ball.
Organisation forces players to read pressure, spacing, and numbers.
Coaching Focus
Collective Cues
Team stays connected while slowing the game.
Acceleration is collective, not individual.
Balance is held during fast attacks.
Individual Cues
If pressure is high, then secure and reset.
If space opens and support is set, then accelerate.
Take responsibility for setting or changing the rhythm.
Progression
Remove coach calls; all tempo decisions are player-led.
Situational Game – Collective Game Control (25 mins)
Setup
Field: 65 x 45m
Teams: 7v7
Goals: Full-size or large goals
Restarts: Normal match flow
Description
Game-realistic practice.
No artificial constraints.
Focus is on natural tempo changes within the game.
Coach intervenes only between rounds.
Coaching Focus
Collective Cues
Control the game by controlling rhythm.
Slow the game to regain structure.
Speed up when the moment is right.
Individual Cues
Decisions linked: read → slow or go → support.
Don’t confuse speed with panic.
Communicate to organise tempo shifts.
Progression
None – full realism.
Free Play – Transfer Game (20 mins)
Setup
Game: 7v7, free play
Conditions: None
Description
Free play under fatigue.
Minimal coach intervention.
Coaching Focus
Observation only.
Look for intelligent, player-led tempo changes.
Coaching Information (U16)
Element | Detail
Objective / Outcomes | Team changes tempo intelligently to control the game
Technical / Tactical Detail | Scanning, first touch, support angles, balance, rhythm
Coaching Messages (Belco Voice) | “Control the game.” • “Recognise the next moment.” • “Slow to go fast.”
Common Mistakes & Fixes | Forcing speed → “Is it on?” • Staying slow too long → “Go now.”
Praise Language | “Good rhythm.” • “You changed the tempo.” • “That controlled the game.”
Behaviours to Reward | Awareness, composure, leadership, intelligent risk
Reflection Question | Which moments today needed us to slow down before speeding up?
Session Design Note | Tempo control is the final expression of game management
Language Note | Belco Framework vocabulary supports rhythm and control

(Segoe UI 9 pt, grey text, Belco-blue underline)
U16 | Finishing Phase & Winning Mentality | Week 1 | Create to Finish
Session Outcome
Create scoring opportunities through collective play and finish with composure, support, and responsibility.
Activation – Uvodni dio / Game Activation (15 mins)
Setup
Area: 40 x 30m, directional
Players: 5v4 + GK
Organisation: Attacking unit vs defending unit
Equipment: Balls, cones, 2 mini-goals
Description
Play starts from GK.
Attacking team scores by creating a clear chance before finishing (minimum two connected actions before shot).
Direct shots without creation do not count.
Defending team counters to mini-goals on regain.
Reset always from GK to repeat creation habits.
Near-equality reinforces decision-making, not rushed finishing.
Coaching Focus
Collective Cues
Team creates chances together, not individually.
Support arrives early around the ball.
Players behind the ball protect balance after shots.
Individual Cues
Scan before shooting.
If the shot isn’t on, create for a teammate.
Move after passing to support the finish.

Limit attackers to two touches in the final action.

Players: 6v6 + GK
Organisation: Directional game focused on chance creation

Attacking team must break defensive structure before finishing.
On loss, immediate reaction to counter-press or recover.
Organisation forces patience and shared responsibility in the final third.

Collective Cues
Team recognises when to create vs when to finish.
Width and depth stretch the defence before the shot.
Compactness behind the attack supports second balls.
Individual Cues
If the defence is set, then create.
If a teammate shoots, then attack rebounds.
Take responsibility for the quality of the chance, not just the shot.

Add a rule: goals from first-time finishes count double if created collectively.

Setup
Field: 65 x 45m
Teams: 7v7

Game-realistic practice.

Emphasis on creating chances before finishing under pressure.

Collective Cues
Create clear chances through patience and connection.
Avoid low-percentage shots.
Protect what’s behind the ball after finishing.
Individual Cues
Decisions linked: create → finish → react.
Don’t rush the moment.
Communicate to organise support runs.
Progression
None – full realism.
Free Play – Transfer Game (20 mins)
Setup
Game: 7v7, free play
Conditions: None
Description
Free play under fatigue.
Minimal coach intervention.
Coaching Focus
Observation only.
Look for intelligent chance creation leading to composed finishes.
Coaching Information (U16)
Element | Detail
Objective / Outcomes | Team creates quality chances before finishing
Technical / Tactical Detail | Support angles, timing of runs, shot selection, balance
Coaching Messages (Belco Voice) | “Create first.” • “Finish with support.” • “Control the moment.”
Common Mistakes & Fixes | Rushed shots → “Can we create more?” • Isolated finishes → “Who’s with you?”
Praise Language | “Good creation.” • “You improved the chance.” • “That was patient.”

BELCONNEN UNITED FC – SESSION PLAN | CYCLE 6 | WEEK 1 | SESSION 2
(Segoe UI 9 pt, grey text, Belco-blue underline)
U16 | Finishing Phase & Winning Mentality | Week 1 | Combination in Final Third
Session Outcome
Combine intelligently in the final third to create higher-quality finishes while staying connected and balanced.
Activation – Uvodni dio / Game Activation (15 mins)
Setup
Area: 40 x 30m, directional
Players: 5v4 + GK
Organisation: Attacking unit vs defending unit in the final third
Equipment: Balls, cones, 2 mini-goals
Description
Play starts from GK.
Attacking team scores by finishing after a combination action (wall pass, third-man, overlap, or set-and-go).
Solo actions or first-touch shots without combination do not count.
Defenders counter to mini-goals on regain.
Reset always from GK to repeat final-third combination moments.
Near-equality encourages decision-making under pressure.

Collective Cues

Goals: Full-size or large goals

Restarts: Normal match flow
Description
Full game simulation.
Players manage finishing, transitions, and mentality with no prompts during play.
Coach intervenes only between rounds to reset scenarios.
Coaching Focus
Collective Cues
Control the game emotionally.
Finish with intent, not panic.
Protect what’s behind the ball after shots.
Individual Cues
Decisions linked: scenario → create → finish → react.
Don’t force the moment.
Communicate under pressure.
Progression
None – full realism.
Free Play – Transfer Game (20 mins)
Setup
Game: 7v7, free play
Conditions: Score and time remain live
Description
Free play under fatigue.
Coach observes only.
Coaching Focus
Observation only.
Look for game-realistic finishing behaviour transferring naturally.
Coaching Information (U16)
Element | Detail
Objective / Outcomes | Team applies finishing and mentality principles in game scenarios
Technical / Tactical Detail | Chance selection, transition reaction, emotional control
Coaching Messages (Belco Voice) | “Control the moment.” • “Finish the action.” • “Recognise the scenario.”
Common Mistakes & Fixes | Forcing shots → “What’s the situation?” • Switching off → “Next action.”
Praise Language | “Good decision.” • “You managed the moment.” • “That was mature.”
Behaviours to Reward | Game awareness, composure, responsibility, competitiveness
Reflection Question | Which scenario felt most difficult to manage today, and why?
Session Design Note | Game simulation tests learning under full realism
Language Note | Belco Framework vocabulary supports recognition and control
Belconnen United FC – Compact • Brave • Intelligent(Light-blue separator line above; Segoe UI 8 pt grey text)

heading: U16 Cycle 6, week 5, session 2
headingPath: U16 sessions > U16 Cycle 6, week 5, session 2
content:
BELCONNEN UNITED FC – SESSION PLAN | CYCLE 6 | WEEK 5 | SESSION 2
(Segoe UI 9 pt, grey text, Belco-blue underline)
U16 | Finishing Phase & Winning Mentality | Week 5 | Winning Mentality Challenge
Session Outcome
Demonstrate winning mentality by competing for outcomes under pressure, fatigue, and consequence while staying connected and disciplined.
Activation – Uvodni dio / Game Activation (15 mins)
Setup
Area: 40 x 30m, directional
Players: 5v4 + GK
Organisation: Two teams competing in short, scored rounds
Equipment: Balls, cones, 2 mini-goals
Description
Play starts from GK.
Each round lasts 90 seconds.
Teams score by finishing a chance or forcing a defensive win (block, save, regain).
Losing team starts the next round defending.
Reset always from GK to reinforce consequence and urgency.
Near-equality keeps the challenge competitive and fair.
Coaching Focus
Collective Cues
Team competes together from first to last second.
Support and balance are maintained even when chasing.
Immediate reaction after goals or misses.
Individual Cues
Commit fully to each action.
If you lose a duel, react instantly.
Compete for rebounds and second balls.
Progression
Reduce rounds to 60 seconds to increase urgency.
Situational Practice – Solving the Situation (20 mins)
Setup
Area: 60 x 40m
Players: 6v6 + GK
Organisation: Challenge-based directional game
Equipment: Balls, cones, goals
Description
Directional game built around competitive challenges:
First to score
Score within time limit
Hold a lead for 60 seconds
Losing team faces an immediate consequence (defend first, start under pressure).
On loss, immediate reaction to counter-press or recover.
Organisation tests mentality, not patterns.
Coaching Focus
Collective Cues
Team responds to pressure together.
Compactness increases when protecting a lead.
Clear communication during decisive moments.
Individual Cues
If the game is on the line, then simplify.
If a teammate shoots, then attack the rebound.
Take responsibility for decisive actions.
Progression
Add scoring bonuses for goals after second actions or under pressure.
Situational Game – Collective Game Control (25 mins)
Setup
Field: 65 x 45m
Teams: 7v7
Goals: Full-size or large goals
Scenario: Winner-stays-on format
Restarts: Normal match flow
Description
Game-realistic competitive format.
Winning team stays on; losing team rotates or restarts under pressure.
No coaching during play.
Focus is on handling consequence and pressure.
Coaching Focus
Collective Cues
Team competes for every moment.
Protect what’s behind the ball when ahead.
Push together when chasing.
Individual Cues
Decisions linked: compete → decide → execute.
Don’t hide from pressure moments.
Communicate to organise teammates.
Progression
None – full realism.
Free Play – Transfer Game (20 mins)
Setup
Game: 7v7, free play
Conditions: Score and time live; winner-stays-on mentality
Description
Free play under fatigue and consequence.
Coach observes only.
Coaching Focus
Observation only.
Look for players embracing pressure and competing to win.
Coaching Information (U16)
Element | Detail
Objective / Outcomes | Team shows winning mentality through competition and decision-making
Technical / Tactical Detail | Duels, second actions, finishing decisions, balance
Coaching Messages (Belco Voice) | “Compete.” • “Finish the action.” • “Control the moment.”
Common Mistakes & Fixes | Avoiding pressure → “Step into it.” • Switching off → “Next action.”
Praise Language | “You competed.” • “You wanted the moment.” • “That was strong.”
Behaviours to Reward | Courage, responsibility, resilience, competitiveness
Reflection Question | Which moments showed our winning mentality today?
Session Design Note | Winning mentality is revealed under consequence, not comfort
