heading: Player Development Curriculum
content:
BELCONNEN UNITED FC
TECHNICAL DEPARTMENT
——————————————
PLAYER DEVELOPMENT CURRICULUM
——————————————
2026 Edition | Aligned with top European models
——————————————
Prepared by:
Scott Conlon – Technical Director
Belconnen United FC
V2026.03

heading: 1.1 Foreword – 'The Belco Way'
headingPath: Belconnen United FC – Player Development Curriculum (Master Document) > 1. Introduction & Vision > 1.1 Foreword – 'The Belco Way'
content:
This document represents years of reflection, learning, and collaboration. It brings together ideas from around the world — Croatian structure, Dutch creativity, and Australian resilience — and translates them into something uniquely Belconnen.
The Belco Way isn’t just about how we train or play. It’s about creating an environment where players think for themselves, where coaches challenge with purpose, and where everyone understands why we do what we do.
Our aim is simple: to develop intelligent footballers who can solve problems, adapt under pressure, and express themselves with confidence.
Each principle in this curriculum is a thread that connects our youngest players to our senior teams. The language changes as players grow, but the message stays the same — play with identity, courage, and intent.
This curriculum will evolve as we do. It’s a guide, not a script — a foundation for coaches to build from and players to flourish within.

heading: 1.2 Purpose of the Curriculum
headingPath: Belconnen United FC – Player Development Curriculum (Master Document) > 1. Introduction & Vision > 1.2 Purpose of the Curriculum
content:
This curriculum exists to give structure and continuity to the Belconnen United program — to put into writing the identity we’ve been building on the field over recent seasons.
It defines how we play, how we coach, and how we think about football at every level. Our aim is not to reinvent the game, but to document the Belco identity so that future coaches, players, and leaders can carry it forward with clarity and purpose.
The Belconnen curriculum is a seamless journey from junior to senior football. It begins by instilling a love for the ball, progresses through the complexities of team play, and culminates in the demands of senior football. Each phase feeds into the next: technical mastery acquired in the first stage becomes the foundation for tactical understanding in the second, which in turn empowers players to execute under pressure in the third.
The curriculum provides:
- Consistency – every team speaks the same football language.
- Alignment – clear connection between junior, youth, and senior football.
- Longevity – a reference point that preserves our philosophy when staff change.
- Clarity – shared understanding of what success looks like beyond results.
At its heart, this document captures who we are and how we want to play. It ensures that long after this coaching group moves on, the Belco Way remains strong — a style, a mindset, and a culture that endures.

heading: 1.3 Club Identity & Playing Philosophy
headingPath: Belconnen United FC – Player Development Curriculum (Master Document) > 1. Introduction & Vision > 1.3 Club Identity & Playing Philosophy
content:
At Belconnen United FC, our identity is clear — we value intelligent, brave football.Every player, from U10s through to First Grade, should understand why we play a certain way and how their role contributes to the team.
Our philosophy is built around developing players who can see, decide, and act under real football conditions.
We believe that learning happens through the game itself — through repetition in decision-rich, competitive environments.
Every touch, pass, and movement must have context and purpose.
We don’t train patterns that never appear in a match. We build adaptable players who can solve problems and apply their technique under pressure.
Our sessions reflect this: realistic, game-based, and focused on developing football intelligence alongside technical quality.
We draw influence from Croatian structure, Dutch creativity, and the tactical clarity of Europe’s top academies — but our goal is not to copy them.
It’s to define our own identity: football that is controlled yet dynamic, disciplined yet expressive.We attack with intent, defend with organisation, and play with confidence and connection.
Belconnen football is recognisable — in how our teams build, press, and react.
We value tempo control, pressing as a unit, positional understanding, and the bravery to play forward.
These ideas aren’t just for senior teams; they are introduced early and refined through each phase of development.
This playing philosophy gives our club direction and alignment.
It ensures that every coach teaches within a shared framework, every player develops within a consistent environment, and every team represents the same Belco identity — with or without the ball.

heading: 1.4 Long-Term Player Development Model (U10–Senior)
headingPath: Belconnen United FC – Player Development Curriculum (Master Document) > 1. Introduction & Vision > 1.4 Long-Term Player Development Model (U10–Senior)
content:
Our program is built around a clear, long-term model that connects every age group to the same destination — producing intelligent, adaptable players capable of performing at senior level.
We don’t see player development as a series of isolated age groups, but as a continuous journey with defined phases. Each phase has its own learning focus, language, and performance expectations — but all share the same principles of play and football identity.
The Belco Pathway
Phase | Age Range | Focus | Primary Outcomes
Technical Development Phase | U10–U13 | Skill acquisition and game awareness | Players learn to control, pass, and dribble under pressure. Emphasis on confidence in 1v1s, learning game principles through small-sided formats, and developing a strong technical base within game-like environments.
Tactical Development Phase | U14–U15 | Understanding team roles and tactical concepts | Players learn how principles shape decisions. They develop positional understanding, learn to apply pressing, spacing, and combination play. Focus is on teamwork, structure, and tactical adaptability.
Professional Development Phase | U16–Senior | Application and performance at speed | Players learn to execute with precision under pressure. Emphasis on decision speed, control of tempo, and mastering the team’s game model. This phase connects development to performance and prepares players for senior football.
Alignment Across Phases
- Every phase builds on the one before it — repetition with progression.
- Players graduate between phases already familiar with the club’s principles, cues, and language.
- Coaches use the same Belco DNA across all sessions, adjusted for age and understanding.
Outcome
By the time a player reaches senior football, they should not be adapting to a new way of playing — they should be mastering one they’ve lived through for years.That is the purpose of our long-term model: to ensure continuity, identity, and readiness at every level.
Development Continuum – The Belco Pathway
Phase | Learning Focus | Game Connection | Player Behaviours | Outcome
🟦 Technical Development (U10–U13) | Learn | Discover the game through repetition and play | Curiosity, bravery on the ball, confidence in 1v1s | Technical foundation under real pressure
🟩 Tactical Development (U14–U15) | Understand | Apply principles in structured game environments | Awareness, anticipation, teamwork | Tactical discipline and positional intelligence
🟥 Professional Development (U16–Senior) | Master | Perform with speed, control, and identity | Leadership, precision, adaptability | Game-ready players who embody the Belco Way

heading: 1.5 Connection between Youth & Senior Teams
headingPath: Belconnen United FC – Player Development Curriculum (Master Document) > 1. Introduction & Vision > 1.5 Connection between Youth & Senior Teams
content:
Belconnen’s identity is built on alignment — not imitation.
Our youth teams don’t copy the seniors; they prepare players to thrive when they arrive there. Every age group builds the habits, principles and language that lead into senior football.
The senior environment sets the standards: pressing intensity, tempo control, positional structure and professionalism. The youth pathway exists to develop those standards step by step — technically, tactically and mentally.
By the time a player reaches First Grade, the language, expectations and football concepts feel familiar. The jump in speed is natural, not overwhelming.
Our coaches share the same reference points:
- Same principles of play: The five Belco pillars run from U10s to seniors.
- Same language: Terms like open-ball, assist zone, counter-press, and compactness mean the same thing across the program.
- Same style markers: Building through thirds, controlled tempo, pressing high and dominating transitions.
This alignment allows for seamless movement of players between age groups and into the senior setup. When a player steps up, they don’t just fill a spot — they already play the Belco way.
The ultimate measure of success is not youth trophies — it’s the number of players ready to influence senior football and beyond with confidence, clarity, and identity.

heading: 2.1 Style of Play Overview
headingPath: Belconnen United FC – Player Development Curriculum (Master Document) > 2. Philosophy of Play > 2.1 Style of Play Overview
content:
At Belconnen United, our football is built on intelligence, control, and collective movement. We aim to play with intent — to dominate through rhythm, structure, and understanding, not through chaos or luck. Our teams play with purpose in every phase of the game.
We value the ball, but we value what we do because of the ball even more. Possession is not a statistic; it’s a tool for control. We move the ball with meaning, to provoke, to draw, to open. When we have it, we seek to create superiority — in numbers, in position, or in timing. When we don’t, we press as a unit, compact and coordinated, ready to regain and attack again.
This style takes inspiration from the Croatian football school — intelligent players who read the game, not just play it. We develop footballers who see two passes ahead, who adjust their positioning by instinct, and who can dictate the tempo of play. The ball moves quickly, but decisions move faster. Every Belco player learns to scan, anticipate, and act — because our football is defined by awareness and reaction speed, not athleticism alone.
We believe in controlled aggression — the courage to press, to recover, to dominate transitions. Our teams are compact in defence but expressive in attack. We defend as eleven and attack as eleven. Every line contributes: centre-backs who build, midfielders who connect, forwards who initiate pressing.
The Belco way is not rigid. It’s structured freedom. Players are guided by clear principles, not strict patterns. Within our framework, creativity is encouraged — provided it serves the collective idea. A Belco player must know why they act, not just how.
From our juniors through to First Grade, our goal is the same:
- Control the game through intelligent positioning and purposeful possession.
- Press with intent and unity when out of possession.
- Transition decisively — either forward to punish, or secure to rebuild.
Ultimately, our style of play reflects our club’s identity: intelligent football, played with control, courage, and collective purpose.
Freedom Within Structure
At Belconnen United, we value creativity — but not chaos.
Freedom only has value when it exists within a structure. Our players are encouraged to make decisions, solve problems, and express themselves — but always from a position that serves the team’s shape and intention.
In positional play, freedom doesn’t mean players can go anywhere or do anything. That kind of freedom is chaos.
Instead, players are free within their role — to make choices, to interpret moments, and to use their individual qualities to create advantages.
This balance between structure and freedom is what makes the game intelligent.It allows us to stay connected, maintain our positional discipline, and still let the players’ instincts shine through.
Our players don’t play in positions — they play through positions.
Training With the Best to Become the Best
At Belconnen United, we’re proud to bring the best players together.
It’s not something we hide from — it’s something we value. When talented players train alongside other talented players every week, the standard naturally rises. That’s how development accelerates.
Just like the top Croatian clubs, we believe that the training environment defines the player, not just the weekend result.
If competition in matches isn’t enough to stretch us, we create it ourselves.
We run inter-club games — 16s vs 18s, 14s vs 16s — to challenge our players physically, tactically, and mentally.
At Belco, being “too strong” isn’t a problem; it’s a responsibility.Our role is to keep raising the level and ensure that every player is tested, pushed, and learning in every session — surrounded by the best, becoming the best.

heading: 2.2 Game Model Summary (how we play, why we play this way) (part 1)
headingPath: Belconnen United FC – Player Development Curriculum (Master Document) > 2. Philosophy of Play > 2.2 Game Model Summary (how we play, why we play this way)
content:
This document outlines the senior football philosophy at Belconnen United FC. It reflects our shift away from the Australian skills-based curriculum toward a more holistic, game-based model rooted in the Croatian methodology. This model trains players in the realities of the game: technical execution under pressure, spatial awareness, adaptive decision-making, and emotional control.
The heartbeat of our approach lies in four key concepts:
- Creating and exploiting numerical superiority (overloads)
- Recognising and using the open-ball moment
- Accessing the assist zone through coordinated movement
- Making fast, correct decisions in the first second after transition
This philosophy is clear, practicable, and actionable. It is applied differently in each third of the pitch and in all four moments of the game. Our training and tactical plans develop players who recognise and act on these patterns instinctively.
Our game model is built around control — control of tempo, space, and rhythm. We believe the team that controls these three elements controls the match. Every Belco team, from juniors to seniors, plays within the same tactical framework, scaled by age and learning phase.
We divide the game into four core moments: In Possession, Out of Possession, Transition to Attack, and Transition to Defence.
Each moment is connected — the way we build influences how we defend, and how we defend dictates how we attack.
🟦 In Possession (BP)
We want to build with purpose and progress through the thirds.Our possession is not passive — every pass serves a purpose.We prioritise structure, scanning, and combinations to move the opponent, not just the ball.
Back Third – Build and Connect
We play from the back with confidence, composure, and clear structure.
Centre backs create width, one midfielder drops in to form a 3v2 or 4v3 advantage, and the goalkeeper becomes part of the play.
We value intelligent dribbling to commit opponents and open new passing lanes.
Fullbacks provide width while midfielders rotate and support.
Key Principles:
- Create overloads to progress (e.g. 3v2, 4v3).
- Goalkeeper as an active distributor.
- Calm circulation until the open-ball moment.
- Structure over speed — build to draw, not to rush.
Middle Third – Create the Open-Ball Moment
The middle third is where our football identity shows.
We aim to create the open-ball moment — when the carrier has time, space, and vision to play forward.
Once that happens, movement triggers everywhere: runs beyond, bounce passes, third-man combinations, or drive-draw-play actions.
Key Principles:
- Maintain central superiority (numerical or positional).
- Rotate to unbalance the opponent.
- Find the “free” player early.
- Prepare to accelerate forward once open-ball appears.
Front Third – Access the Assist Zone
We attack the final third with precision and synchronised movement.
Our focus is the assist zone — byline or half-space areas that allow controlled cutbacks into scoring zones.
Blind-side runs, 3rd-man movements, and quick 2v1s create chaos.
Key Principles:
- Reach the assist zone and attack the second line.
- Combine quickly in tight areas.
- Execute calm, controlled finishes (“small touch, big finish”).
- Attack with at least three in the box, one underneath.
🟥 Out of Possession (BPO)
Our defensive mentality is proactive — we defend forward.
Every player presses, delays, or covers with intent.
We stay compact vertically and horizontally, never giving up the central corridor.
Front Third – High Pressing with Triggers
We press as a unit, never as individuals.
Pressing starts from our front line and is triggered by cues: bad touch, back pass, sideline trap, or isolated receiver.
Our aim is to make the opponent predictable — force long, win second balls.
Key Principles:
- Press on triggers, not emotions.
- Use cover shadows to block the inside pass.
- Immediate reaction — pressing is a mindset.
- 5-second rule on loss of possession.
Middle Third – Compactness and Patience
If the press is bypassed, we drop into compact shape.
We guide play wide, protecting central channels.
We delay progression through discipline and intelligent spacing.
Key Principles:
- Compact vertically (no gaps between lines).
- First defender delays, second covers, third balances.
- Shift as a unit; don’t chase individually.
Back Third – Protect the Centre
Our back third defends the “golden square” — the central penalty area.
We prefer to force low-quality crosses than allow central penetration.
Composure, body shape, and collective communication define our defending.
Key Principles:
- Protect the central channel.
- Block cutbacks and through balls.
- Defend the box with numbers and bravery.
- Scan, mark, and clear with purpose.
Every defensive action starts with the duel. Individual battles set the tone for collective compactness. Our players must compete — to press, block, recover, and win their ground with intelligence and courage.
🔁 Transition to Attack
This moment separates good teams from elite ones.
When we regain the ball, we decide quickly:
- If the opponent is disorganised → attack immediately with verticality and intent.
- If they are set → secure, circulate, and rebuild.
Key Principles:
- First 4 seconds define the moment.
- Play forward if possible.
- Counter at pace with support runners.
- If counter not on → value possession and reset structure.
🔄 Transition to Defence
Losing the ball triggers an instant reaction.

heading: 2.2 Game Model Summary (how we play, why we play this way) (part 2)
headingPath: Belconnen United FC – Player Development Curriculum (Master Document) > 2. Philosophy of Play > 2.2 Game Model Summary (how we play, why we play this way)
content:
The closest player presses immediately; support players collapse around the ball.
If regain isn’t possible, the team recovers shape fast.
Key Principles:
- First defender delays and disrupts.
- Nearest three counter-press if possible.
- If not, retreat compactly and regroup.
- Mentality: defend the moment, not the space.
Why We Play This Way
We believe in a style that reflects clarity, control, and collective intelligence.
Every action — whether building, pressing, or transitioning — is guided by our five core pillars:
1️⃣ Build & Control the Tempo
2️⃣ Create Superiority
3️⃣ Collective Compactness
4️⃣ Aggressive Recovery
5️⃣ Duel Mentality.
These pillars define how Belco teams think, act, and adapt.This is the Belco way — football built on intelligence, identity, and intent.
This is the Belco way — the connection between intelligence, identity, and intent.

heading: 2.3 8 Principles of Play
headingPath: Belconnen United FC – Player Development Curriculum (Master Document) > 2. Philosophy of Play > 2.3 8 Principles of Play
content:
“The principles of play in attack, defence and transition form the framework of all football learning.Every technical or tactical action must be guided by at least one of these principles.”
So here’s their system — translated and structured for your curriculum:
ATTACKING PRINCIPLES
Croatian Principle | Practical Meaning | Linked Belco Pillar
Width and Depth | Stretch the field horizontally and vertically to open space. | Build & Control the Tempo
Support | Provide angles before and after the pass to keep options alive. | Create Superiority
Mobility | Constant movement to disorganise the opponent’s block. | Create Superiority
Penetration | Play through or beyond lines when possible. | Build & Control the Tempo / Duel Mentality
Tempo Control | Recognise when to circulate and when to accelerate. | Build & Control the Tempo
Combination & Cooperation | Use 2v1s, wall passes, and third-man runs to create superiority. | Create Superiority
Finishing & Final Pass | Choose the right moment and method to end the attack. | Duel Mentality
DEFENDING PRINCIPLES
Croatian Principle | Practical Meaning | Linked Belco Pillar
Delay | Slow the opponent’s attack to allow team shape recovery. | Duel Mentality / Compactness
Pressure & Cover | First defender applies pressure, second provides cover and balance. | Collective Compactness
Compactness | Keep distances short between lines and players. | Collective Compactness
Balance | Occupy central and weak-side spaces while pressing. | Collective Compactness
Communication & Coordination | Defend collectively, reacting as a unit to triggers. | Collective Compactness
Aggressiveness & Anticipation | Step in early, compete for every ball. | Duel Mentality / Aggressive Recovery
Control of Depth | Line discipline, reading runs behind. | Collective Compactness
TRANSITION PRINCIPLES
Croatian Principle | Practical Meaning | Linked Belco Pillar
Immediate Reaction | Act instantly after ball loss or regain — no pause. | Aggressive Recovery / Duel Mentality
Counter-Press (After Loss) | Closest players press to win the ball before opponent can counter. | Aggressive Recovery
Compact Recovery (After Loss) | If pressing fails, regain compact defensive shape quickly. | Collective Compactness
First Pass Forward (After Regain) | Play forward with intent to exploit open space. | Build & Control the Tempo
Support on Transition | Teammates run to support ball carrier in counter. | Create Superiority
Control of Emotion | Stay calm, decide quickly under changing conditions. | Duel Mentality / Build & Control the Tempo
How They Sit in the Curriculum
Now, within Belco’s framework:
- The Principles = Football logic (Croatian layer).
- The Pillars = Belco identity expression (how we live the principles).
Here’s how it connects visually:
Game Phase | Principles | Belco Pillar
In Possession | Width & Depth, Support, Mobility, Penetration, Tempo Control, Cooperation, Finishing | Build & Control the Tempo Create Superiority Duel Mentality
Out of Possession | Delay, Pressure, Cover, Compactness, Balance, Aggressiveness | Collective Compactness Duel Mentality
Transition (A→D) | Immediate Reaction, Counter-Press, Compact Recovery | Aggressive Recovery Collective Compactness
Transition (D→A) | First Pass Forward, Support, Control of Emotion | Build & Control the Tempo Create Superiority Duel Mentality
The Short Version
You can summarise the Principles of Play Framework like this:
- In Possession: Width, Depth, Support, Mobility, Penetration, Tempo, Finishing
- Out of Possession: Delay, Pressure, Cover, Compactness, Balance, Aggression
- Transitions: React, Regain, Recover / Play Forward, Support, Control Emotion
“All principles are connected to the Belco identity pillars of Build & Control the Tempo, Create Superiority, Collective Compactness, Aggressive Recovery, and Duel Mentality.”

heading: 2.4 How Principles Connect Across Phases
headingPath: Belconnen United FC – Player Development Curriculum (Master Document) > 2. Philosophy of Play > 2.4 How Principles Connect Across Phases
content:
Each pillar is introduced early and grows in tactical depth as players mature.
The language, speed, and complexity change — but the principles themselves never do.
From U10 to First Grade, every coach speaks the same football language, so players move seamlessly through the pathway.
Pillar | Technical Phase (U10–13) | Tactical Foundation (U14) / Integration (U15) | Professional Preparation (16 +)
1️⃣ Build & Control the Tempo | “Back → Mid → Front.” “Sometimes fast, sometimes calm.” | “Play out with structure; use the pivot to control rhythm.” “Circulate until the gap, then accelerate.” | “Decide the rhythm of the game — control tempo to break lines or protect the result.”
2️⃣ Create Superiority | “2 v 1 is fun — find a friend!” “Drive → Draw → Play.” | “Extra man = advantage — recognise and use it.” “Third-man and rotation to unbalance.” | “Seek positional and numerical superiority in every third; manipulate opponents through coordinated movement.”
3️⃣ Collective Compactness | “Stay close, defend together.” “Small to defend, big to attack.” | “Keep vertical and horizontal distances tight.” “Protect central corridors.” | “Compactness gives control — one unit moves, all move.”  “Line coordination through communication.”
4️⃣ Aggressive Recovery | “Lose it? Win it back quick!” “React straight away.” | “Immediate reaction — press for five seconds or recover shape.” | “Counter-press to kill transitions; recover to dominate tempo and mentality.”
5️⃣ Duel Mentality | “Win your battle!” “Be brave with the ball.” | “Your duel decides the play — compete and recover.” | “Win both physical and tactical duels; impose personality under pressure.”
Framework Integration
Each pillar is supported by specific frameworks that evolve through the phases:
Phase | Key Frameworks Expressed
Technical (U10–13) | Open Ball · Overloads · Drive–Draw–Play · Compactness · Duel Mentality
Tactical (U14–15) | Building Through Thirds · Level & Outside · Assist Zone · Pressing · Counter-Press
Professional (16 +) | Control the Tempo · Return Ball · Secure or Counter · Game Management

heading: 3.1 Technical Development Phase (U10–U13)
headingPath: Belconnen United FC – Player Development Curriculum (Master Document) > 3. Player Development Framework > 3.1 Technical Development Phase (U10–U13)
content:
Purpose of the Phase
The Technical Development Phase (U10–U13) is the foundation of the Belco player pathway — where players learn to play football with the ball, not around it.
At this stage, the goal is to build complete technical fluency, game intelligence through small formats, and a strong football personality.
For Belco, this phase defines our identity: we teach football through the ball.
Every session builds confidence, control, and courage — the tools that later support tactical understanding and tempo control.
Even in small formats, players learn to stay connected — attack and defend together, react instantly when the ball changes hands.
Purpose Summary:
- Develop ball mastery under pressure (both feet, all surfaces).
- Create habitual bravery — comfort taking risks with and without the ball.
- Form the base for tactical recognition (space, timing, direction).
- Instil the Belco mentality: compact, connected, and expressive.
Key Learning Priorities
Croatian methodology defines this as the “technical explosion” period — where learning is fastest, coordination is peaking, and repetition turns into instinct.
“Every repetition must live inside a decision — technique is only valuable when it solves a game moment.”
Area | Croatian Focus | Belco Integration
Ball Mastery & Manipulation | Dribbling, feints, turns, direction changes | Belco’s Drive–Draw–Play foundation — ability to carry and commit.
First Touch & Receiving | Receiving on the move, open body, orienting touch | Builds Open-Ball awareness — always ready to play forward.
Passing & Combination Play | Short-passing accuracy, wall passes, bounce passes | Links to Overloads and third-man runs — small-group synergy.
1v1 & Duel Mentality | Win your duel offensively and defensively | Core Belco DNA — “If you win your duel, you change the game.”
Finishing | Instinctive shooting, both feet, varied situations | Early exposure to Assist Zone thinking — ground-based, smart finishing.
Scanning & Decision Speed | Awareness before touch, pre-orientation | Prepares for tactical play — builds habits to read Open/Closed Ball cues.
Basic Positional Play | Recognise width, depth, and balance | Sets up understanding of Compactness and Level & Outside.
🔹 Croatian principle: repetition with meaning — high-touch, game-like learning.🔹 Belco addition: control + decision — never isolation.
Player Profile by the End of Phase
By the end of U13, a Belco player should display:
Technical Attributes
- Confident with the ball under all pressures.
- Two-footed control, carry, and release.
- Can manipulate tempo — slow to draw, fast to play.
Tactical Understanding (Emerging)
- Recognises Open Ball / Closed Ball cues.
- Understands width and compactness in team shape.
- Operates intelligently in small overloads (2v1, 3v2).
Physical & Coordination
- Agile, rhythmic, and well-balanced through football actions.
- Performs repeated high-intensity technical efforts naturally.
Psychological & Emotional Traits
- Brave in possession — no fear of mistakes.
- Enjoys 1v1 duels.
- Curious, creative, and increasingly self-driven.
Belco Summary:
“By the end of U13, they play with confidence, courage, and control — they own the ball, they see the game, and they love the duel.”
Progression into the Tactical Phase (Ages 14–15)
The technical phase builds the individual foundation for the next stage — the collective game.
Technical Phase (10–13) | Tactical Phase (14–15)
Learning what to do with the ball | Learning when and why to do it
Individual mastery | Collective understanding
Small-group principles (2v1s, 3v2s) | Team principles (lines, compactness, pressing)
Instinctive play | Structured play
Develop bravery | Apply intelligence
Bridge Concepts
- Drive–Draw–Play → develops into Overloads and Open-Ball timing.
- Compactness and Support Play → evolve into Pressing and Transition Play.
- 1v1 Duel Mentality → becomes 1v1 within team structure.
- Tempo Awareness → matures into Tempo Control.
Croatian Purpose Statement:
“We teach children to dominate the ball before we teach them to dominate the game.”
Belco Adaptation:
“We don’t rush tactics until technique becomes instinct.Once they can control the ball under pressure, they can control the game.”

heading: 3.2 Tactical Development Phase (U14–U15)
headingPath: Belconnen United FC – Player Development Curriculum (Master Document) > 3. Player Development Framework > 3.2 Tactical Development Phase (U14–U15)
content:
Purpose of the Phase
The Tactical Development Phase (U14–U15) marks the bridge between learning how to play and understanding how to think and act as a team.
In the Croatian model, this is when a player’s technical foundation becomes the tool for collective intelligence — decision-making, timing, and role clarity within a coordinated structure.
For Belco, this is where our identity becomes visible on the pitch.
The aim is to produce football thinkers — players who read the game, control tempo, and apply Belco principles (Compactness, Open Ball, Overloads, Tempo Control, Duel Mentality) with real tactical understanding.
Purpose Summary:
- Connect individual technique to collective tactics.
- Develop understanding of principles of play in all four moments.
- Build game maturity — control of tempo, rhythm, and compactness.
- Develop instinctive reaction after loss — transition from playmaker to presser in one movement.
- Form the base for professional-level positional awareness and decision-making.
Key Learning Priorities
Area | Croatian Focus | Belco Integration
Game Understanding | Introduce team shape, roles, coordinated movement | Build recognition of Open Ball / Closed Ball cues to dictate line behaviour.
Positional Play | Occupy width, depth, and interior spaces intelligently | Apply Level & Outside and Overloads to create progression options.
Team Compactness | Keep vertical + horizontal distances short | Teach defend forward and together — Compactness as non-negotiable.
Transitions (Both Ways) | React instantly on loss or regain — decide to counter or secure in the first seconds. | Link Secure or Strike and Counter-Pressing frameworks.
Pressing & Defensive Structure | Pressing triggers, coordination between lines | Press as a unit — 1v1 everywhere but always with cover and balance.
Build-Up & Progression | Controlled progression through thirds | Apply Building Through Thirds and Drive–Draw–Play.
Tempo Control | When to circulate, when to accelerate | Teach players to recognise moments to rest vs moments to break.
Duel Mentality (Applied) | Duels within tactical structure | “Win your duel in the context of the team objective.”
🔹 Croatian Principle: “Understanding comes from the game itself — small groups within full context.”🔹 Belco Addition: Tactical learning never removes bravery; we play intelligent football, not safe football.
Player Profile by the End of Phase
Technical Attributes
- Maintains technical quality under tactical and physical pressure.
- Passes, receives, and dribbles with positional intent.
- Uses first touch and body shape based on context, not comfort.
Tactical Understanding
- Reads Open / Closed / Return Ball cues instinctively.
- Understands spacing, balance, and team compactness in both phases.
- Recognises pressing triggers and builds through thirds with structure.
- Transitions naturally between attack and defence — reacts, reorganises, and restores team compactness.
Physical & Coordination
- Develops game-specific endurance and explosive actions.
- Balances pressing intensity with controlled recovery.
Psychological & Emotional Traits
- Tactical patience — knows when not to force.
- Collective mindset — understands role in the system.
- Growing leadership and self-regulation.
- Maintains Duel Mentality within team mission.
Belco Summary:
“By the end of U15, they no longer just play well — they think well.They recognise moments, connect principles, and play the Belco way without being told.”
Progression into the Professional Phase (Ages 16+)
This phase transforms a technically skilled player into a system-intelligent footballer — ready to operate within senior tactical demands.
Tactical Phase (14–15) | Professional Phase (16+)
Learns principles of play | Executes team model and match plan
Understands roles and spaces | Adapts role to system and opponent
Practices pressing, compactness, overloads | Applies pressing schemes, rotations, tempo control
Plays with tactical awareness | Plays with game management and efficiency
Builds collective intelligence | Delivers professional consistency
Bridge Concepts
- Compactness & Pressing → evolve into professional defensive discipline.
- Overloads & Building Through Thirds → become controlled positional dominance.
- Open / Closed / Return Ball recognition → enables in-game tactical adjustment.
- Tempo Control → matures into match-management maturity.
Croatian Philosophy:
“The player must learn how to think — not just where to stand.”
Belco Adaptation:
“By 15, our players see the whole game.They recognise moments, adjust tempo, and act with purpose — the beginning of a professional mind.”

heading: 3.3 Professional Development Phase (U16+)
headingPath: Belconnen United FC – Player Development Curriculum (Master Document) > 3. Player Development Framework > 3.3 Professional Development Phase (U16+)
content:
Purpose of the Phase
The Professional Development Phase (U16 and above) is the final stage of the Croatian player pathway — where individual ability and tactical understanding mature into professional performance habits.
At this level, training is no longer about learning the game’s basics — it’s about refining execution, consistency, and intelligence under match pressure.
Players learn to manage the game, not just play it.
For Belco, this phase turns a developing footballer into a complete, reliable, and intelligent performer — capable of applying our principles (Open Ball, Compactness, Tempo Control, Overloads, Duel Mentality, etc.) in full 11v11 contexts against real opposition demands.
Purpose Summary:
- Prepare players technically, tactically, and mentally for senior-level football.
- Teach them how to manage tempo, control momentum, and handle adversity.
- Develop game consistency — professional behaviours on and off the pitch.
- Translate Belco’s principles into a team game model that wins matches.
Key Learning Priorities
Area | Croatian Focus | Belco Integration
Game Management & Tactical Maturity | Adapt decisions to scoreline, tempo, and opponent | Teach Tempo Control and Secure or Strike as match management tools.
Team Identity & System Play | Apply tactical principles within 11v11 team model | Implement Open/Closed/Return Ball, Compactness, and Pressing Frameworks collectively.
Transition Mastery | Reading moments of change — when to counter, when to secure | Apply Counter-Pressing and Secure or Strike with intelligence and risk awareness.
Professional Consistency | Repeat performance quality every week | Daily habits, communication, and responsibility in team structure.
Role Adaptability | Understand multiple positions within tactical system | Ability to execute principles from any line or role.
Psychological Resilience | Composure, focus, response to mistakes | Reinforce Duel Mentality — mental toughness and accountability.
Decision Efficiency | Fewer touches, quicker reads, higher impact | Emphasise Open Ball as decision cue for forward play.
🔹 Croatian Guiding Principle: “The player must now play the game as a professional would — with intelligence, control, and reliability.”🔹 Belco Integration: Principles become automatic — the team’s identity drives performance, not individual reaction.
Player Profile by the End of the Phase
Technical Attributes:
- Clean, efficient execution under full-speed pressure.
- Controls the ball and tempo — first touch sets next action.
- Applies technical tools to tactical intent (e.g., body shape for pressing trap, tempo shift to create overload).
Tactical Understanding:
- Reads Open / Closed / Return Ball instantly to organise team movement.
- Knows pressing triggers and compactness cues instinctively.
- Recognises game states — when to accelerate, when to protect, when to manage.
- Contributes to team identity — not just individual moments.
Physical & Coordination:
- Match-ready conditioning: repeat high-intensity efforts within tactical structure.
- Efficient movers — no wasted energy, always positionally intelligent.
Psychological / Emotional Traits:
- Professional consistency — same effort and quality every session.
- Self-management: understands recovery, preparation, and mental reset.
- Competitive resilience — thrives in high-pressure environments.
- Leadership and accountability — “my actions affect the team.”
Belco Summary:
“By 16+, they don’t just play football — they manage football.
They understand tempo, momentum, and responsibility. They are ready for senior demands.”
How Earlier Belco Principles Evolve
Earlier Belco Principle | Professional Application
Compactness | Becomes collective team organisation — lines coordinated, distances controlled, defending forward as one.
Open / Closed / Return Ball | Translates into real-time match reading — lines adjust instantly to ball pressure and direction.
Overloads | Moves from small-group tactic to team-wide positional superiority (5v4 or 6v5 structures).
Duel Mentality | Evolves into individual responsibility within the collective — every duel supports the team’s press or buildup.
Tempo Control | Becomes match management — using rhythm to control game flow, calm pressure, or finish matches intelligently.
Drive–Draw–Play | Becomes tactical execution — breaking lines with carry, timing, and manipulation of pressure.
Secure or Strike | Full-team decision model — quick read of opponent’s shape in transition, leading to structured counter or controlled reset.
🔹 This is the maturity point — when principles become habits.
Players no longer “apply” the Belco frameworks consciously; they live them instinctively.
Preparing for Senior Football
The Professional Phase ensures players:
- Can read and adapt to the tempo and structure of adult competition.
- Have tactical flexibility to play within any team system.
- Show maturity in transitions — both emotional and tactical.
- Deliver consistent performance standards every week.
Players leaving this phase should:
- Be capable of contributing to senior-level tactical systems.
- Understand how to manage tempo, protect leads, and control chaos.
- Display leadership, communication, and professional mindset.
Together, these three phases form the Belco Development Pathway — a clear, connected journey from mastering the ball to mastering the game.

heading: 3.4 Learning Outcomes by Phase
headingPath: Belconnen United FC – Player Development Curriculum (Master Document) > 3. Player Development Framework > 3.4 Learning Outcomes by Phase
content:
(Aligned with the Croatian Development Model and Belco Football Philosophy)
Domain | Technical Development Phase (Ages 10–13) | Tactical Development Phase (Ages 14–15) | Professional Development Phase (Ages 16+)
TECHNICAL | • Can control the ball with all parts of both feet in movement.• Can receive under pressure and play forward within two touches.• Can dribble to escape pressure or commit an opponent (Drive–Draw–Play).• Can strike the ball cleanly with both feet in varied situations.• Can combine in small groups (2v1s, 3v2s) using bounce passes and wall plays.• Can manipulate first touch to face forward (Open-Ball cue). | • Can maintain quality of touch and decision at match tempo.• Can break lines through pass, dribble, or third-man run.• Can control and play through tight spaces while under pressure.• Can recognise when to play one-touch vs. secure possession.• Can vary tempo of touch to attract or eliminate pressure.• Can execute technical actions within tactical structure (positional play, pressing). | • Can execute technical actions at high intensity and under full pressure.• Can control tempo and rhythm with first touch and passing speed.• Can deliver accurate long or disguised passes when game requires.• Can receive and release under physical and spatial pressure in any zone.• Can apply technique consistently across 90 minutes.• Can perform technical actions specific to role and system.
TACTICAL | • Adjusts position when ball travels.• Can find or create overloads (2v1, 3v2).• Can recognise when the ball is open and make a forward run. • Can stay compact and connected when defending. • Can press and recover quickly after losing the ball (reaction habits). • Can support play underneath or wide depending on ball location. | • Can recognise Open/Closed/Return Ball cues and adjust team shape accordingly.• Can coordinate pressing actions (first defender, cover, balance).• Can play through thirds with control and purpose.• Can create and exploit overloads in different zones.• Can control game tempo by circulating or accelerating play.• Can manage transitions: when to counter, when to secure. | • Can apply team principles fluidly in all four moments of play.• Can manage tempo and match rhythm based on score and opponent.• Can maintain compactness across units under fatigue or stress.• Can organise and lead pressing traps as part of team structure.• Can read opponent shape and adjust system behaviour in real time.• Can demonstrate tactical discipline while showing initiative to solve problems.
Domain | Technical Development Phase (Ages 10–13) | Tactical Development Phase (Ages 14–15) | Professional Development Phase (Ages 16+)
PSYCHOLOGICAL | • Shows confidence to take risks on the ball. • Responds positively after mistakes. • Competes in every duel with effort and bravery. • Shows curiosity to learn and improve through feedback. • Demonstrates focus for full training sessions and small matches. | • Maintains composure under pressure and in tight situations. • Shows persistence when the game is physically or mentally demanding. • Recovers quickly from errors and stays engaged. • Shows belief in team structure and role responsibility. • Demonstrates concentration and consistency across match phases. | • Displays professional discipline and mental endurance. • Controls emotions and maintains focus under provocation or adversity. • Shows tactical patience and emotional stability during match swings. • Leads others through example and communication. • Demonstrates accountability and match-to-match consistency.
SOCIAL / BEHAVIOURAL | • Communicates with teammates to share space and combine. • Shows respect for coaches, referees, and teammates. • Works cooperatively within small groups. • Displays enthusiasm and enjoyment for training and matches. • Demonstrates responsibility for own gear, preparation, and effort. | • Communicates clearly in and out of possession (press, cover, support). • Contributes to collective discipline and compactness. • Shows empathy and encouragement toward teammates. • Takes ownership of role in the team model. • Demonstrates growing professionalism in preparation and behaviour. | • Leads communication on and off the field. • Supports and motivates teammates during pressure moments. • Displays professionalism in preparation, punctuality, and recovery habits. • Balances competitiveness with composure and respect. • Represents club standards in attitude, teamwork, and leadership.
Learning outcomes provide reference points for Belconnen United coaches when designing sessions and evaluating player development across the three phases.
Summary
- Ages 10–13 (Technical Phase): Build confidence, mastery, and joy in football — everything through the ball.
- Ages 14–15 (Tactical Phase): Connect technique to team understanding — recognise moments, play with intelligence.
- Ages 16+ (Professional Phase): Execute with consistency, discipline, and maturity — manage the game, lead the team.
Belco Core: Compactness, Open Ball, Tempo Control, Overloads, and Duel Mentality — applied progressively, lived instinctively.

heading: 3.5 Identity Map: How the Phases Link
headingPath: Belconnen United FC – Player Development Curriculum (Master Document) > 3. Player Development Framework > 3.5 Identity Map: How the Phases Link
content:
The Belco Identity Map shows how our five pillars evolve across the three player development phases — Technical (U10–13), Tactical (U14–15), and Professional (16+).
Each pillar represents a football behaviour that grows in complexity:
first learned through repetition and small-sided freedom, then refined through tactical understanding and finally mastered under professional tempo and pressure.
This map connects our football language — how every skill, habit, and tactical action builds towards the same senior model.
It’s the thread that ties a player’s journey from learning to play → learning to think → learning to perform.
In Possession
Pillar | Technical Phase (10–13) | Tactical Phase (14–15) | Professional Phase (16+)
1️⃣ Build & Control the Tempo | Play through thirds using small-sided formats (3v3+GK). Learn “Back → Mid → Front.” Early GK involvement. | Controlled build-up using pivot and width; start reading pressure types. Learn to circulate until the gap, then accelerate. | Manipulate tempo deliberately. Change rhythm to control the match; adjust build-up structure based on opponent press.
2️⃣ Create Superiority | Experience overloads (2v1s, 3v2s). Drive–Draw–Play introduced as attacking habit. | Create numerical or positional superiority to break lines (3v2 midfield rotations). Introduce third-man and “+1” idea. | Overload-to-isolate model: generate local superiority to release 1v1s. Combine positional rotations and timing to sustain dominance.
3️⃣ Collective Compactness (in possession) | Stay connected even when attacking — support behind the ball. | Compact between lines to allow immediate counter-pressing if lost. | Maintain team distances to control transitions and protect rest defence.
4️⃣ Aggressive Recovery (from possession) | Immediate reaction when losing the ball in small games — “Lose it? Win it back!” | Team counter-press with nearest three; organise behind the ball. | Full-structure counter-press and recovery balance across units.
5️⃣ Duel Mentality | Compete to protect or regain the ball in every action; enjoy the fight. | Duels connected to team purpose — timing, cover, and reaction. | Professional resilience: duels as tactical and psychological weapon.
Out of Possession
Pillar | Technical Phase (10–13) | Tactical Phase (14–15) | Professional Phase (16+)
1️⃣ Build & Control the Tempo (Defensive Rhythm) | Learn to delay and control attacker’s tempo. | Collective control through coordinated pressing and recovery. | Team manages defensive rhythm to dictate opponent’s play.
2️⃣ Create Superiority (Defensive Support) | “Two vs one to win the ball.” Learn cover and balance. | Build defensive triangles (1st, 2nd, 3rd defender). | Collective superiority — squeeze zones, force direction, win together.
3️⃣ Collective Compactness | “Stay close, defend together.” Compactness introduced in 4v4/5v5. | Full-line compactness (vertical + horizontal). Compactness as non-negotiable. | Synchronised 11v11 distances; defend forward with rest defence organised.
4️⃣ Aggressive Recovery | 3–5s reaction after losing possession. | Counter-press nearest 3, rest secure. | Zonal pressing traps, return-ball triggers, organised re-engagement.
5️⃣ Duel Mentality | 1v1 habits — body shape, timing, recovery after loss. | Duels within system — individual battle inside team structure. | Professional consistency — physical, technical, and emotional duels.
Transitions & Connecting Ideas
Moment | Technical Phase | Tactical Phase | Professional Phase
Transition to Attack | Immediate awareness — play forward if free, secure if pressured. | Decide in first 3–5s whether to counter or build. | Control tempo after regain — balance risk vs security.
Transition to Defence | Instant reaction after loss; small-group counter-press. | Collective counter-press or recover compactly. | Organised rest-defence triggers, full-team coordination.
The Belco Identity Map defines a continuous developmental pathway — ensuring every player, coach, and team in the program speaks the same football language.
- Ages 10–13 (Technical Phase): Build habits — repetition through game play, mastery through action.
- Ages 14–15 (Tactical Phase): Build intelligence — coordinated understanding, timing, and compactness.
- Ages 16+ (Professional Phase): Build identity — automatic execution of principles under match pressure.
This progression ensures our players don’t just learn the game — they understand it.Belco’s way is consistent, connected, and clear: from technique, to awareness, to tactical fluency, to instinct.That is what creates the Belco player — confident, intelligent, and ready to perform.
Our framework flows from technique → to perception → to team coordination → to instinct.

heading: 3.6 Language Ladder: Shared Terminology Evolution
headingPath: Belconnen United FC – Player Development Curriculum (Master Document) > 3. Player Development Framework > 3.6 Language Ladder: Shared Terminology Evolution
content:
We use language progression as a teaching tool, moving from simple, motor-action phrases (for clarity and rhythm) → to relational terms (space, timing, compactness) → to collective and tactical phrasing (lines, superiority, triggers).
Technical Phase (U10–13) | Tactical Phase (U14–15) | Professional Phase (16+) | Belco Pillar / Croatian Intent
“Back → Mid → Front.” “Play through, not over.” | “Build with calm.” “Use pivot to connect lines.” | “Control rhythm across thirds.” “Play with structure and tempo.” | Build & Control the Tempo
“Pass, move, get it back.” “Find a friend.” “2v1 – we win!” | “Drive–Draw–Play.” “Create the extra player.” | “Maintain superiority through rotation.” “Break lines intentionally.” | Create Superiority
“Stay close, no big holes.” “Defend together.” | “Compact lines, protect the middle.” “Step on closed ball.” | “Control distances vertically & horizontally.” “Compact, defend forward.” | Collective Compactness
“Lose it? Win it back!” “Chase it for 5 seconds.” | “Press together – nearest 3 go.” “Trigger press from closed or return ball.” | “Counter-press collectively to control territory.” “React before opponent settles.” | Aggressive Recovery
“Win your battle.” “Be brave, don’t hide.” | “Your duel decides the play.” “Recover and react instantly.” | “Dominate 1v1s in both phases.” “Professional resilience in every duel.” | Duel Mentality
Language progression for the frameworks.
Technical Phase (U10–13) (Action-based & Habit Language) | Tactical Phase (U14–15) (Concept & Relationship Language) | Professional Phase (16+) (Tactical & Collective Language) | Framework
“Pass and follow.” “Give and go.” “Bounce to support.” | “Use the bounce to break lines.” “Third-man runs to connect units.” | “Automatic third-man actions.” “Synchronised unit rotations.” | Bounce & Third-Man Play
“Run when your mate can see you.” “If he’s free, go forward.” | “Open-ball = trigger run.” “React when ball carrier faces forward.” | “Read open/closed cues instantly.” “Vertical dismarking & depth timing.” | Open-Ball Recognition
“Run to the line, pass it back.” “Arrive late to score.” | “Attack the assist zone.” “Second-line finishes from cut-backs.” | “Deliver from assist zones with precision.” “Controlled arrivals and finishing runs.” | Assist Zone & Cut-Backs
“Lose it? Win it back!” “Chase it for 5 seconds.” | “Press together – nearest 3 go.” “Win it back before they breathe.” | “Trigger press from closed or return ball.” “Counter-press collectively to control territory.” | Forward Pressing & Counter-Pressing

heading: 4. Training Methodology
headingPath: Belconnen United FC – Player Development Curriculum (Master Document) > 4. Training Methodology
content:
The Belco Training Methodology defines how we teach — how our principles, identity, and developmental aims come to life on the pitch.
It provides a consistent framework that guides every coach in the program, ensuring that every training session looks and feels like Belco football.
Our approach is built on three core ideas:
- Realism: players learn best in game-like situations that demand perception, decision, and execution.
- Repetition with variation: the same principles appear across different formats so habits become instinctive, not robotic.
- Guided discovery: the coach shapes the environment and the constraints, allowing players to find solutions within the game.
This methodology is not a rigid formula. It’s a flexible model that allows each coach to express their style while still delivering sessions that reinforce the same principles — Open Ball, Compactness, Overloads, Tempo Control, Duel Mentality, and Collective Identity.
Every phase of development applies this methodology at a different depth:
- The Technical Phase focuses on freedom, repetition, and exploration.
- The Tactical Phase adds coordination, structure, and collective understanding.
- The Professional Phase sharpens tempo, precision, and game management.
Together, this creates a unified way of teaching the Belco game — one where players develop intelligence through doing, and where learning is always connected to how we want to play.

heading: 4.11. Learning Through the Game
headingPath: Belconnen United FC – Player Development Curriculum (Master Document) > 4. Training Methodology > 4.1 Coaching & Learning Approach > 4.11. Learning Through the Game
content:
Belco training is built on the belief that the game itself is the best teacher.
Players learn by solving real football problems — not by repeating isolated patterns. Every exercise has game meaning: a target, an opponent, direction, and decisions.
- We coach football, not drills. The structure always links back to our philosophy — Open Ball, Compactness, Overloads, Tempo Control, Duel Mentality.
- Sessions look and feel like football: there’s opposition, space, and consequence.
- The aim is understanding, not just repetition — we want players to recognise why actions work, not just copy movements.
Croatian academies call this approach “learning through play with logic.”At Belco, we call it football-real learning — real tempo, real spacing, real outcomes.

heading: 4.12. Realism and Repetition
headingPath: Belconnen United FC – Player Development Curriculum (Master Document) > 4. Training Methodology > 4.1 Coaching & Learning Approach > 4.12. Realism and Repetition
content:
We balance repetition with realism — repetition through variety, not monotony.
Belco sessions follow the same idea: repeat key situations under changing pressures.
- Repetition: players experience the same football moments (e.g., Open-Ball penetration, Compactness under pressure) many times per week.
- Realism: each repetition changes — new opponent, new angle, different tempo.
- Progression: exercises move from simple → complex, from 3v3 → 7v7 → 11v11, but the principle stays constant.
- Tempo: sessions always reach match rhythm — every exercise ends with game speed.
Example:
A U13 session might start with 4v2 rondos to reinforce overloads → then 5v5 directional game → finish with 7v7 transition game applying Drive–Draw–Play.Same idea, increasing realism and pressure each step.

heading: 4.13. Decision-Making and Discovery
headingPath: Belconnen United FC – Player Development Curriculum (Master Document) > 4. Training Methodology > 4.1 Coaching & Learning Approach > 4.13. Decision-Making and Discovery
content:
Croatian methodology is built around guided discovery — players are led to the answer, not told it.
Belco coaches ask, “What did you see?” instead of “Why didn’t you do it?”
- Questions > Instructions: prompts like “What changes when the ball is open?” or “Where’s the extra player?” develop understanding.
- Choice and consequence: players make decisions, experience outcomes, and learn by solving.
- Game intelligence: we train awareness — recognising triggers (open/closed ball, space behind, overloads) and responding instinctively.
- Problem-solving: sessions are designed to create decisions under pressure, not just technical execution.
This approach creates players who think, not just perform — football thinkers with Belco identity.

heading: 4.14. Constraints-Led Coaching
headingPath: Belconnen United FC – Player Development Curriculum (Master Document) > 4. Training Methodology > 4.1 Coaching & Learning Approach > 4.14. Constraints-Led Coaching
content:
Rather than over-explaining, we design conditions that nudge players toward the right behaviour.Croatian coaches often say, “Set the game right, and the game teaches.”
At Belco:
- Use constraints, not control. Example: limit touches to teach tempo, or reward goals from cut-backs to emphasise assist-zone behaviour.
- Shape the field to represent real tactical pictures — wide channels, compact central zones, thirds, or overload areas.
- Reward principles: scoring after pressing, combining through overloads, or finishing from open-ball actions.
This keeps sessions competitive and alive while reinforcing the key principles naturally through design, not instruction.

heading: 4.15. The Coach’s Role and Interventions
headingPath: Belconnen United FC – Player Development Curriculum (Master Document) > 4. Training Methodology > 4.1 Coaching & Learning Approach > 4.15. The Coach’s Role and Interventions
content:
Croatian coaching is active, observational, and relational.The coach reads the flow, times the intervention, and connects the player’s decision to the team principle.
At Belco, we adopt the same discipline:
- Observe first: let players feel the moment before stopping it.
- Freeze sparingly: stop play to show structure or spacing, then replay it fast.
- Use short cues: “Compact now,” “Ball’s open, run!” — clear, simple, purposeful.
- Praise recognition: when a player reads an open-ball or reacts to a return-ball cue, highlight it.
Our coaches believe every intervention should clarify the game, not complicate it.
Belco sessions follow the same: we coach football reality, not theory.

heading: 4.16. Building Independent Thinkers
headingPath: Belconnen United FC – Player Development Curriculum (Master Document) > 4. Training Methodology > 4.1 Coaching & Learning Approach > 4.16. Building Independent Thinkers
content:
By the Professional Phase, the coach becomes more of a manager of standards than a provider of answers.
Players are expected to self-correct and communicate — reading cues, managing tempo, and guiding each other.
Belco coaches gradually release control as players mature:
- U10–13: guide and model.
- U14–15: question and connect.
- U16+: challenge and refine.
We want to produce autonomous, creative footballers who make decisions faster than opponents.

heading: 4.17. Connection to Belco Principles
headingPath: Belconnen United FC – Player Development Curriculum (Master Document) > 4. Training Methodology > 4.1 Coaching & Learning Approach > 4.17. Connection to Belco Principles
content:
Our five core principles remain the lens for every exercise and coaching behaviour:
Principle | Coach’s Teaching Emphasis
Open Ball | Recognition and reaction speed – “Can we go?”
Compactness | Team spacing and unity – “Defend together, defend forward.”
Tempo Control | Awareness of rhythm – “Slow to draw, fast to play.”
Overloads | Cooperation and timing – “Find the extra man.”
Duel Mentality | Mindset and resilience – “Win your moment.”
These principles are not themes we visit; they are lenses we coach through.
Every Belco session — regardless of topic — contains moments that reinforce one or more of these.
Summary: How Belco Learning Works
The Belco coaching approach mirrors the Croatian model — game-based, principle-driven, and human-focused.
Players learn by doing, thinking, and deciding under realistic conditions.
The coach’s role is to design the game, shape the challenge, and guide understanding through sharp interventions.
This approach builds players who are intelligent, brave, and adaptable — capable of reading the game, managing tempo, and executing our football identity at any level.
We coach football through the game — not the other way around.

heading: 4.2 Session Structure & Flow
headingPath: Belconnen United FC – Player Development Curriculum (Master Document) > 4. Training Methodology > 4.2 Session Structure & Flow
content:
Session Flow Overview
Belco sessions follow the same structure used in top European academies — a progressive football flow that starts with technical activation and ends with full game application.
The design always connects to our Belco principles — Open Ball, Compactness, Overloads, Tempo Control, Duel Mentality — but the rhythm remains natural, like a real match: preparation → repetition → decision → execution.
Logic of Flow:
“From simple to complex, from individual to collective, from low intensity to match intensity.”
Each part of the session builds toward realism:
- Activation / Warm-Up – prepare body and brain for football rhythm.
- Game Focus 1 (Rondo / Positional Play) – small-sided decision-making and repetition.
- Game Focus 2 (Main Phase / Tactical Scenario) – apply the principle in a realistic team context.
- End Game / Transfer Phase – free play under match conditions.
- Reset / Reflection – low-intensity debrief or final challenge.
This flow ensures that every Belco session moves with rhythm, repetition, and realism — the same structure the Croatian FA calls “training through the game.”

heading: 4.21 Component 1 – Activation / Warm-Up Purpose
headingPath: Belconnen United FC – Player Development Curriculum (Master Document) > 4. Training Methodology > 4.2 Session Structure & Flow > 4.21 Component 1 – Activation / Warm-Up Purpose
content:
Objective: prepare physically, mentally, and technically for football actions.
Croatian warm-ups are always ball-based — short, dynamic, and decision-loaded.
Structure:
- Duration: 10–15 min (all phases).
- Format: movement preparation → reaction → ball activation.
- Content:
- Dynamic movement & coordination (1–2 mins).
- Ball familiarisation (partners, volleys, turns).
- Small group duels, touches, and direction changes.
Belco Link:
- Reinforces Duel Mentality (compete early).
- Introduces Tempo Control (fast-slow rhythm changes).
- Always includes decision-making — no static stretching or isolated touches.
Example:
- U12: 3v1 rondos with movement constraints → quick changeover race.
- U16: technical warm-up with 2v2 reactions into short transition press.

heading: 4.22 Component 2 – Game Focus 1: Rondo / Positional Play
headingPath: Belconnen United FC – Player Development Curriculum (Master Document) > 4. Training Methodology > 4.2 Session Structure & Flow > 4.22 Component 2 – Game Focus 1: Rondo / Positional Play
content:
Objective: sharpen technical-tactical habits through repetition under pressure.This is the Croatian “learning block” — where players repeat core actions that appear every game (receiving, support, overloads, pressing reactions).
Structure:
- Duration: 15–25 min.
- Format: small-sided (3v1, 4v2, 5v3, 6v4, etc.).
- Purpose: repetition with decision-making — every touch has consequence.
- Progression: increase size, complexity, or overload level.
Belco Link:
- Overloads – create +1 and use it.
- Open Ball – recognition and reaction cues.
- Compactness – distances between players, both in and out of possession.
- Tempo Control – circulate, then accelerate.
Example:
- U11: 4v2 rondo (2-touch max) → find end player = point.
- U15: 5v3 positional game → play through gate = point; encourages through-the-third play.
- U17: 6v6 + 3 neutral in zones → replicate build-up structure with real spacing.

heading: 4.23 Component 3 – Game Focus 2: Main Tactical Phase
headingPath: Belconnen United FC – Player Development Curriculum (Master Document) > 4. Training Methodology > 4.2 Session Structure & Flow > 4.23 Component 3 – Game Focus 2: Main Tactical Phase
content:
Objective: apply the session’s principle in structured, realistic team play.
This is the “Croatian main game” — always directional, competitive, and related to match moments.
Structure:
- Duration: 30–40 min.
- Format: small or medium-sided games (6v6 → 8v8 → 11v11).
- Focus: one main Belco principle per session — Compactness, Open Ball, Pressing, Overloads, etc.
- Progression: from controlled patterns → free play with specific triggers.
Belco Link:
- Compactness – spacing and line movement.
- Pressing – 1v1 responsibility and rolling-on.
- Overloads – team coordination to create +1.
- Open Ball – penetration timing and decision.
- Tempo Control – recognition of slow/fast phases.
Example:
- U12: 5v5 transition game → points for regaining ball within 5s (counter-press habit).
- U15: 7v7 build-up → must play through pivot before scoring (build through thirds).
- U18: 11v11 block → practice pressing triggers from return-ball situation.

heading: 4.24 Component 4 – End Game / Transfer Phase
headingPath: Belconnen United FC – Player Development Curriculum (Master Document) > 4. Training Methodology > 4.2 Session Structure & Flow > 4.24 Component 4 – End Game / Transfer Phase
content:
Objective: transfer training theme into a free, match-real context.
The Croatians always finish with open play — where players make independent decisions without constraint.
Structure:
- Duration: 15–20 min.
- Format: game format (6v6, 8v8, 11v11).
- Rules: minimal conditions — just scoring or directional play.
- Purpose: freedom and realism — watch for transfer of learning.
Belco Link:
- Observe whether players apply session principle instinctively:
- Open Ball → forward runs
- Compactness → collective squeeze
- Tempo → circulation before penetration
- Duel Mentality → compete to win moments
Coach Role:
Step back and observe. Let the principle appear naturally. Reinforce correct moments in reflection, not by stopping play.

heading: 4.25 Component 5 – Reset & Reflection
headingPath: Belconnen United FC – Player Development Curriculum (Master Document) > 4. Training Methodology > 4.2 Session Structure & Flow > 4.25 Component 5 – Reset & Reflection
content:
Objective: physically and mentally transition out of session; anchor learning.Croatian sessions end with calm tempo and connection — not abrupt stopping.
Structure:
- Duration: 5–10 min.
- Format: light passing, recovery runs, or short team talk.
- Content:
- Revisit the day’s principle: “Where did we see the Open Ball?”
- Player reflection: “When did we stay compact? When did we press?”
- Simple stretching and recovery habits.
Managing Intensity and Transitions
Croatian coaches talk about the rhythm of the session — rise, sustain, drop.Belco coaches manage energy and focus the same way:
- Rise: warm-up and early games build rhythm, light and sharp.
- Sustain: main game holds top intensity (match tempo).
- Drop: reflection and recovery — learning sinks in.
Coach cues:
- Keep transitions tight — no long pauses or explanations.
- Use visual signals (cones reset, quick water, restart).
- Adjust load by size and rest, not by reducing realism.
- Always finish with a feeling of football completeness — competition, purpose, joy.
Phase Variations
Age Phase | Session Duration | Focus | Structure Example
10–13 (Technical Phase) | 60–75 min | Technical actions in game form, repetition through play | 10 min warm-up → 15 min rondo → 25 min 4v4/5v5 main game → 15 min end game
14–15 (Tactical Phase) | 90 min | Principle-based team play and tactical links | 10 min activation → 20 min positional game → 40 min tactical scenario → 15 min game + reflection
16+ (Professional Phase) | 90–100 min | Team model, tempo management, match application | 10 min activation → 15 min positional rondo → 45 min 11v11 tactical phase → 20 min managed game
Summary: Building Identity Through Structure
This session structure gives Belco coaches a consistent rhythm — recognisable, game-based, and principle-driven.
It ensures that every player, at every age, trains through realistic football situations while constantly reinforcing Belco’s DNA: bravery on the ball, compactness without it, and tempo control through intelligence.
By repeating this flow week after week, players develop not just skill — but Belco understanding:
“We train the game the way we want to play the game.”

heading: 4.3 Coaching Behaviours & Interventions
headingPath: Belconnen United FC – Player Development Curriculum (Master Document) > 4. Training Methodology > 4.3 Coaching Behaviours & Interventions
content:
Overview
In the Belco model – inspired by Croatian methodology – coaching is about creating thinkers, not followers.
Coaches shape learning through how they interact — tone, timing, and presence — not by constant correction.
The Croatian term “vođenje igre” (guiding the game) captures the idea: the coach sets direction, but the players drive the learning.
Belco coaching behaviours mirror that philosophy — calm, purposeful, and player-centred. We don’t instruct every second; we guide, question, and connect actions back to our principles:Open Ball. Compactness. Overloads. Tempo Control. Duel Mentality.

heading: 4.31 Observation & Timing
headingPath: Belconnen United FC – Player Development Curriculum (Master Document) > 4. Training Methodology > 4.3 Coaching Behaviours & Interventions > 4.31 Observation & Timing
content:
Croatian methodology teaches patience: observe first, speak later.
The best interventions come when the coach has seen the full picture — what happened before, during, and after the key moment.
Belco Standards for Timing:
- Let the play run: players must experience the moment before you fix it.
- Freeze rarely, guide often: stop play only when the learning point affects the team’s principle.
- Intervene at the transition: natural pauses (throw-in, foul, water break) allow reflection without breaking rhythm.
- Keep tempo: feedback is short, clear, and emotionally calm — we maintain the session’s rhythm.
Example:
See a CM turn on an open ball but teammates stay static.
Let the play finish → quick intervention: “What happens when he’s open? Can we run beyond?”
Replay fast — keep the energy alive.
Croatian coaches value rhythm — the game should breathe, not stop-start.

heading: 4.32 Communication Style
headingPath: Belconnen United FC – Player Development Curriculum (Master Document) > 4. Training Methodology > 4.3 Coaching Behaviours & Interventions > 4.32 Communication Style
content:
Belconnen coaching communication is clear, calm, and confident.
The tone is directive when needed, supportive always. Our coaches match that approach — authority through clarity, not volume.
Core Behaviours:
- Positive body language: open stance, upright, visible presence.
- Simple tone: steady, assertive, encouraging.
- Coach from angles: not behind the play — position to see both the ball and player spacing (Croatian standard).
- One message at a time: avoid stacking instructions; clarity beats content.
- Catch good behaviours: highlight recognition moments (“Yes — you saw the open ball!”).
The aim is to create a consistent emotional climate: calm intensity.
Players must feel trusted to act, not scared to err.

heading: 4.33 Questioning & Discovery
headingPath: Belconnen United FC – Player Development Curriculum (Master Document) > 4. Training Methodology > 4.3 Coaching Behaviours & Interventions > 4.33 Questioning & Discovery
content:
Belconnen coaches use guided discovery to deepen player understanding — a balance of questions and challenges that drive awareness.
Belco adopts the same rhythm: less telling, more asking.
Typical Pattern:
- See it: let the moment play.
- Ask it: short, purposeful question.
- Show it: quick visual reset or replay.
- Reinforce it: repeat and reward recognition.
Examples of Guided Questions:
Principle | Question Prompt
Open Ball | “When he faced forward, what could we have done?”
Compactness | “How tight were we when the ball went wide?”
Overloads | “Where was our extra player? Did we find him?”
Tempo Control | “Could we have slowed it first?”
Pressing | “When did the trigger appear?”
Duel Mentality | “What did you do after losing your 1v1?”
Questions turn principles into thinking habits.
Players start linking actions to ideas — the mark of both Croatian and Belco coaching.

heading: 4.33 Feedback Language
headingPath: Belconnen United FC – Player Development Curriculum (Master Document) > 4. Training Methodology > 4.3 Coaching Behaviours & Interventions > 4.33 Feedback Language
content:
Our coaching language is short, repeatable, and principle-driven.
Belco Coaching Cues (By Principle):
Principle | Positive Reinforcement | Correction / Challenge
Build Through the Thirds | “Good — through, not over.” | “Find the next man, don’t skip the game.”
Open Ball | “Yes — face forward, drive!” | “He’s open — why didn’t we move?”
Compactness | “Lines tight — perfect shape.” | “We’re stretched — close the gap!”
Overloads (Drive–Draw–Play) | “You fixed him — now play!” | “You passed too early — draw first.”
Tempo Control | “Right call — slow to draw, fast to play.” | “We rushed it — next time, secure first.”
Pressing | “Great trigger read — all together.” | “We pressed alone — go as one.”
Duel Mentality | “Strong! Won your moment.” | “Lost it? React — win it back!”
Our coaches avoid filler talk — every phrase links to a football picture. Belco sessions should sound the same: clear, few words, always about the game.

heading: 4.34 Phase Adaptation
headingPath: Belconnen United FC – Player Development Curriculum (Master Document) > 4. Training Methodology > 4.3 Coaching Behaviours & Interventions > 4.34 Phase Adaptation
content:
Communication and coaching style evolve through the phases, matching cognitive maturity and tactical readiness.
Phase | Coach Role | Communication Style | Player Experience
U10–13 (Technical Phase) | Instructor–Mentor | Demonstrate and model; use vivid, action-based cues (“Stay close,” “Find a friend,” “Run when he’s open”). | High support, visible feedback. Players copy and repeat.
U14–15 (Tactical Phase) | Guide–Facilitator | Question and connect: “Why did we press now?” “Who’s free when we overload?” | Players begin linking principle → decision. Active learning.
U16+ (Professional Phase) | Collaborator–Manager | Demand and refine: short cues, reflective questioning (“How did we control tempo there?”). | Players self-correct; focus on standards, not reminders.
This phase progression mirrors Croatian coach development philosophy — early direction, then guided ownership, then shared accountability.

heading: 4.35 Managing Flow, Energy & Engagement
headingPath: Belconnen United FC – Player Development Curriculum (Master Document) > 4. Training Methodology > 4.3 Coaching Behaviours & Interventions > 4.35 Managing Flow, Energy & Engagement
content:
Belconnen sessions feel alive — coaches manage rhythm and energy as much as content.
Practical Standards:
- Keep sessions flowing — move quickly between exercises.
- Use voice for tempo shifts (slow–calm tone in build-up, quick–sharp in transition).
- Encourage with presence: stay near the action, use movement to connect with players.
- Emotional balance — firm on standards, calm under pressure.
- End with positivity — highlight one learning success before players leave.
We watch the game, choose our moment, then connect our feedback to a principle.
We question before we tell, reward recognition over repetition, and coach the person behind the player.
The Belco Way: Calm presence. Clear voice. Football-first communication.

heading: 4.41 Philosophy
headingPath: Belconnen United FC – Player Development Curriculum (Master Document) > 4. Training Methodology > 4.4 Constraints-Led Coaching Approach > 4.41 Philosophy
content:
The constraints-led approach is at the heart of Croatian methodology and Belco teaching.Players learn by solving football problems — not repeating coach instructions.The field, task, and scoring rule become the teacher.
European academies use this method because it builds perception–action intelligence — players connect what they see with what they do.
As the Croatians say:
“Set the game right, and the game teaches.”
At Belco, every constraint connects back to our pillars — helping players apply the right behaviours instinctively:
- Build & Control the Tempo – structure and rhythm.
- Create Superiority – cooperation and overloads.
- Collective Compactness – spacing and unity.
- Aggressive Recovery – reaction after loss.
- Duel Mentality – competitiveness and resilience.
This is football learned through experience, not explanation.

heading: 4.42 Applying Constraints to Belco Pillars
headingPath: Belconnen United FC – Player Development Curriculum (Master Document) > 4. Training Methodology > 4.4 Constraints-Led Coaching Approach > 4.42 Applying Constraints to Belco Pillars
content:
Each pillar can be reinforced by manipulating space, time, direction, rule, or reward — just as Croatian coaches do.
1️⃣ Build & Control the Tempo
Objective: Teach players to manage rhythm and connect through thirds.Constraints:
- Field split into thirds — must connect each third before scoring.
- Time limit in build-up zone → triggers tempo change once secure.
