- Double points for goals after patient circulation through all thirds.Effect: Players learn when to play slow, when to play fast — tempo becomes tactical, not emotional.
2️⃣ Create Superiority
Objective: Recognise and exploit overloads and positional advantages.Constraints:
- One neutral (floater) always available → team must find the +1.
- Goals only count after combination involving neutral or third man.
- Points for breaking pressure with Drive–Draw–Play or bounce sequence.Effect: Players see superiority, not just hear it. They create advantage through cooperation and timing.
3️⃣ Collective Compactness
Objective: Keep team distances tight, defending and attacking as one.Constraints:
- Field width limited; all players must be within central 40m.
- Defensive lines zoned (10–12m spacing) → penalty if exceeded.
- Bonus if regain occurs from compact block.Effect: Players learn spatial discipline — “small to defend, big to attack.”
4️⃣ Aggressive Recovery
Objective: Build pressing mentality and reaction instinct.Constraints:
- 5-second rule after loss — recover or reorganise.
- Extra point for regaining ball in attacking half.
- Team goal worth double if scored within 8 seconds of regain.Effect: Trains instant transition and mental aggression — press together, recover together.
5️⃣ Duel Mentality
Objective: Instil resilience and competitiveness in every action.Constraints:
- Points for each duel win (defensive or attacking).
- Losing side restarts immediately — no recovery time.
- “Winner stays on” rotations in 1v1 or 2v2s.Effect: Players develop toughness, pride, and emotional control — Croatian football character.

heading: 4.43 Phase Progression – Adjusting Constraints by Age
headingPath: Belconnen United FC – Player Development Curriculum (Master Document) > 4. Training Methodology > 4.4 Constraints-Led Coaching Approach > 4.43 Phase Progression – Adjusting Constraints by Age
content:
Phase | Focus | Constraint Type | Example
U10–13 (Technical) | Simplicity, fun, repetition through play | Visual & physical: small grids, zones, colour targets | 3v3 in thirds; must pass through each zone before scoring; bounce pass = bonus point
U14–15 (Tactical) | Understanding and decision-making | Tactical & cognitive: triggers, overloads, 5s rule | 6v6 positional game: score only after bounce pass; regain = double points; open-ball = forward run
16+ (Professional) | Realism and game tempo | Match constraints and time pressure | 11v11: goal counts only after build-up through pivot or assist zone; monitor tempo and transition time
Croatian principle: as players mature, constraints evolve from physical to cognitive — the challenge shifts from “can you do it?” to “can you recognise it?”.

heading: 4.44 Freedom Within Structure
headingPath: Belconnen United FC – Player Development Curriculum (Master Document) > 4. Training Methodology > 4.4 Constraints-Led Coaching Approach > 4.44 Freedom Within Structure
content:
The misconception is that constraints limit creativity.
In truth, good constraints create freedom.
They define the problem — not the solution.
Example:
Constraint: must connect all thirds before scoring.
Structure: defined.
Freedom: how they connect — short, long, dribble, bounce, or switch.
That’s freedom within rules — the foundation of intelligent football.

heading: 4.45 Summary: Teaching Through the Game
headingPath: Belconnen United FC – Player Development Curriculum (Master Document) > 4. Training Methodology > 4.4 Constraints-Led Coaching Approach > 4.45 Summary: Teaching Through the Game
content:
The constraints-led approach ensures Belco players learn football through football.
Every rule, line, or condition connects perception to action.
It mirrors Croatian coaching philosophy:
“Decision first, correction second.”
Players don’t memorise patterns — they develop adaptive intelligence.
They recognise cues, solve problems, and express the Belco identity:
- Control the tempo.
- Create superiority.
- Stay compact.
- Recover aggressively.
- Compete for every moment.
The Belco Way:
We don’t tell players what to do — we design environments that help them discover it.

heading: 4.5 Example Session Templates per Phase
headingPath: Belconnen United FC – Player Development Curriculum (Master Document) > 4. Training Methodology > 4.5 Example Session Templates per Phase
content:
This final part of Belco’s Training Methodology turns philosophy into practice.
The following session templates show how our principles — Build Through the Thirds, Overloads, Open Ball, Compactness, Pressing, Tempo Control, and Duel Mentality — come alive on the training pitch across each development phase.
Each model reflects the rhythm of Croatian academy design: real football, progressive structure, and decision-based learning.
The purpose is not to prescribe fixed sessions, but to give coaches a clear framework for creating their own, consistent with Belco identity — compact, brave, and intelligent.
Every Belco session connects principle to practice, player to decision, and training to match reality.

heading: 4.51 Technical Development Phase (U10–13)
headingPath: Belconnen United FC – Player Development Curriculum (Master Document) > 4. Training Methodology > 4.5 Example Session Templates per Phase > 4.51 Technical Development Phase (U10–13)
content:
Session Duration: 60–75 minutesObjective: Develop individual technique and perception within realistic, small-sided contexts.Learning Focus: Ball control under pressure, decision-making, small-group cooperation, attacking and defending transitions.Belco Principles: Overloads, Open Ball, Duel Mentality, Compactness
Session Overview
This phase builds the football foundation — every exercise links technique to decision.The structure mirrors Croatian U11–U13 academy rhythm: short blocks, constant tempo, repetition through variation.
Session Template
Phase | Duration | Activity Example | Learning Purpose / Belco Connection
1. Warm-Up / Activation | 10–12 min | 3v1 rondos or 4v2 keep-ball – players rotate after each interception. | Develop scanning, awareness, and body shape. Reinforce Overloads (+1 advantage) and Duel Mentality (react to loss).
2. Game Focus 1: Positional Skill | 15–20 min | 4v4+2 directional possession game (2 end players act as links). | Decision-making: play forward when possible, secure when pressured. Teaches Build Through Thirds in small form.
3. Game Focus 2: Attack & Transition | 20–25 min | 5v5 or 6v6 transition game — when possession changes, new team has 5 seconds to attack goal. | Quick reaction after loss = Counter-Pressing. Reaction after win = Open-Ball Trigger. Compactness through close distances.
4. End Game | 15–20 min | 6v6 free play – small pitch to keep distances short, natural compactness. | Apply all principles in real flow. Encourage bravery to play forward, recovery on loss.
5. Reflection / Reset | 5 min | Stretching + short review discussion (“When did we see the open ball?”). | Anchor principle understanding.
Coaching Focus
- Language: short, vivid cues (“Stay close,” “Run when he’s free,” “Fix him, then pass”).
- Style: model and praise action, not theory.
- Constraints: reward forward play, compact defending, and regains within 5s.
- Intensity: high rhythm, minimal stoppages, strong emotional engagement.
Belco Summary
The Technical Phase session should feel alive — constant decisions, teamwork, and competition.
Players learn to see the game early: where space opens, when to press, how to combine.
The rhythm is quick, joyful, and competitive — every game looks like football, not a drill.
Identity Check: Compact when defending. Brave when attacking. Always ready to duel.

heading: 4.52 Tactical Development Phase (U14–15)
headingPath: Belconnen United FC – Player Development Curriculum (Master Document) > 4. Training Methodology > 4.5 Example Session Templates per Phase > 4.52 Tactical Development Phase (U14–15)
content:
Session Duration: 90 minutesObjective: Develop collective understanding and tactical coordination in realistic game scenarios.Learning Focus: Linking units, recognising triggers, structured pressing, and progression through thirds.Belco Principles: Build Through the Thirds, Overloads, Bounce & Third-Man Play, Compactness, Forward Pressing
Session Overview
This phase focuses on game understanding and principle connection.
Croatian methodology calls it “transition from learning actions to understanding play” — every exercise has purpose and positional relevance.
Session Template
Phase | Duration | Activity Example | Learning Purpose / Belco Connection
1. Activation / Warm-Up | 10–15 min | 6v2 positional rondo – players must play through the pivot once per sequence. | Warm-up coordination and rhythm. Repetition of Build Through Thirds structure.
2. Game Focus 1: Positional Play / Overload Creation | 20–25 min | 6v4 in middle third – team in possession must find +1 advantage before progressing to end zone. | Trains Drive–Draw–Play and Overloads recognition. Emphasise movement to create extra man.
3. Game Focus 2: Tactical Scenario (Main Phase) | 30–35 min | 8v8 build-up game with direction – full team shape. Build from GK → CB → pivot → front third. | Apply Build Through Thirds, Tempo Control, and Compactness. Add triggers for Open-Ball or Return-Ball transitions.
4. End Game / Transfer Phase | 15–20 min | 9v9 or 11v11 game – coach encourages full press after goal kicks or turnovers. | Apply full principle connection: build-up, press, regain, counter. Observe if actions appear instinctively.
5. Reflection | 5 min | Quick review or “player voice” – ask: “When did we overload well?” | Link actions to team understanding.
Coaching Focus
- Language: relational and conceptual (“Find the extra player,” “Control tempo,” “Step when the ball’s closed”).
- Style: guided discovery — question rather than instruct.
- Constraints: scoring bonuses for goals after third-man action or regain in attacking third.
- Intensity: match-tempo segments; maintain realism with natural transitions.
Belco Summary
The Tactical Phase session should feel like a team learning to think together.
Spacing, timing, and communication dominate. The coach connects everything back to Belco’s pillars: when to build, when to press, when to run.
Players start to understand the “why” behind actions, and team identity emerges.
Identity Check: Build with calm, press with courage, stay compact, and play forward with intent.

heading: 4.53 Professional Development Phase (16+)
headingPath: Belconnen United FC – Player Development Curriculum (Master Document) > 4. Training Methodology > 4.5 Example Session Templates per Phase > 4.53 Professional Development Phase (16+)
content:
Session Duration: 90 minutes
Objective: Automate Belco playing principles at full tempo; prepare players for senior demands.
Learning Focus: Tactical precision, tempo management, situational adaptability, and professional intensity.
Belco Principles: Tempo Control, Open Ball, Compactness, Pressing, Assist Zone, Overloads, Duel Mentality
Session Overview
This phase mirrors Croatian elite U17–U19 training — full-game realism, tactical repetition, and player ownership.Every minute simulates match tempo and tactical context; every activity reinforces the game model.
Session Template
Phase | Duration | Activity Example | Learning Purpose / Belco Connection
1. Activation / Positional Warm-Up | 10–15 min | 7v3 positional rondo with target goals – team must play through pivot and break to small gates. | Activate scanning, tempo awareness. Connect Build Through Thirds and Open-Ball cues.
2. Game Focus 1: Tactical Repetition | 20–25 min | 11v6 build-up scenario – back four + pivots vs pressers; must progress into middle third. | Reinforce Build Through Thirds patterns under pressure; link to Compactness behind ball.
3. Game Focus 2: Full-Team Tactical Application (Main Game) | 30–35 min | 11v11 situational game – work on controlled possession to counter-press transition. | Combine Tempo Control, Pressing, and Counter-Pressing in one flow. Introduce real transition management.
4. Finishing / Assist Zone Scenario | 15 min | 7v6 in final third – goal counts only from cut-back or second-line run. | Reinforces Assist Zone attacking pattern and second-line timing.
5. Reflection / Cool Down | 5–10 min | Players lead quick debrief – “What controlled the tempo today?” | Reinforces accountability and professionalism.
Coaching Focus
- Language: tactical, precise, outcome-based (“Secure first, accelerate later,” “Play from structure,” “Press on the trigger”).
- Style: minimal stoppages, player-led corrections, focus on tempo and standard.
- Constraints: match-specific — directional, time-limited, scoring rules tied to principles.
- Intensity: full game rhythm; physical and cognitive fatigue managed by timing, not reduced realism.
Summary
The Professional Phase session should feel like senior football: disciplined structure, quick transitions, and intelligent control of rhythm.Coaches manage standards and game tempo — not constant instruction. Players own their decisions, self-correct, and sustain Belco principles automatically.
Identity Check: Intelligent tempo. Brave pressure. Controlled aggression. Belco football with purpose.

heading: 5. Season Planning
headingPath: Belconnen United FC – Player Development Curriculum (Master Document) > 5. Season Planning
content:
Season planning is where the Belco identity becomes reality — the point where philosophy meets periodisation.
It connects how we play with when and how we train across the year, ensuring that every week serves a purpose in the player’s long-term development.
Belco’s approach draws from Croatian periodisation logic — rhythm, repetition, and realism — but is adapted to the Australian season cycle. Each phase of the year builds on the last: technical growth in pre-season, tactical consistency through the middle, and controlled performance toward the finish.
This chapter explains how to manage that rhythm through macro, meso, and micro cycles, ensuring players and coaches stay connected to the same learning direction from October to September.
Goal: to create a year of purposeful training — balanced, progressive, and true to our playing identity.

heading: 5.1 Annual Cycle (Macro)
headingPath: Belconnen United FC – Player Development Curriculum (Master Document) > 5. Season Planning > 5.1 Annual Cycle (Macro)
content:
Purpose
The Annual Framework (Macro Cycle) defines the season’s rhythm — balancing learning, performance, and recovery across the full Australian year.
It ensures Belco players develop through structured cycles that connect identity → understanding → performance, following the same developmental logic used in Croatian academies.
Croatian methodology teaches that a season should breathe: build, consolidate, and perform — never static, never dictated by results.
Belco applies this rhythm inside the Australian season structure, ensuring every phase develops both the player and the collective identity.

heading: 5.11. Australian Season Structure Overview
headingPath: Belconnen United FC – Player Development Curriculum (Master Document) > 5. Season Planning > 5.1 Annual Cycle (Macro) > 5.11. Australian Season Structure Overview
content:
Season Block | Period | Duration | Primary Focus | Belco Objective
Post-Season | Oct–Nov | 4–6 weeks | Team formation, connection, identity reset | Build cohesion and early energy
Pre-Season | Feb–Apr | 8–10 weeks | Identity building and tactical foundation | Re-establish Belco pillars and collective rhythm
Early-Season | Apr–Jun | 10–12 weeks | Tactical consistency and performance stabilisation | Transfer identity to match reality
Late-Season | Jul–Sep | 10–12 weeks | Refinement, tempo management, competitive resilience | Peak performance with clear Belco identity

heading: 5.12. Macro → Meso → Micro Rhythm
headingPath: Belconnen United FC – Player Development Curriculum (Master Document) > 5. Season Planning > 5.1 Annual Cycle (Macro) > 5.12. Macro → Meso → Micro Rhythm
content:
Croatian academies plan using connected cycles: long-term vision (Macro), development blocks (Meso), and weekly rhythm (Micro).
Belco mirrors this structure while adapting cycle length to the player’s developmental stage.
Cycle Level | Typical Duration | Purpose | Belco Adaptation
Macro Cycle | 10–12 months | Full-year rhythm – balance identity, competition, and recovery | Oct–Sep (Australia’s annual season)
Meso Cycle | 3–6 weeks | Focused learning block tied to an identity pillar or theme | 3 weeks (U10–13), 4–5 weeks (U14–15), 6 weeks (U16+)
Micro Cycle | 7 days | Weekly rhythm – manage training load, principle repetition | Early-week load → mid-week learning → match → recovery
Each layer carries the same DNA — every session, week, and month reinforces the five Belco pillars through Croatian-style repetition and game realism.
1. Off-Season (Oct–Nov): Team Connection & Identity Foundation
Duration: 4–6 weeks
Purpose:Lay the foundation for the new season — establish team connection, reintroduce Belco playing identity, and form relationships through light, competitive, game-based work.
Croatian logic:This period builds group cohesion before tactical learning begins — low intensity, high enjoyment, always with the ball.
Belco focus:
- Team Formation: Blend new and returning players through competitive games.
- Connection: Emphasise trust, communication, and leadership.
- Identity Reset: Revisit the five pillars (Build & Control the Tempo, Create Superiority, Collective Compactness, Aggressive Recovery, Duel Mentality) through short, enjoyable games.
- Physical: Low-load, coordination-based football actions.
- Psychological: Positive mindset — confidence, curiosity, and belonging.
Example Activities:
- 3v3 or 4v4 identity games (e.g., connect all thirds before scoring; regain in 5 seconds).
- “Pillar mini-games” — one pillar explored per week through short match formats.
- Mixed-age club tournaments to build cohesion and culture.
Outcome:Teams finish connected, confident, and aligned — identity is re-anchored before structured pre-season begins.
2. Pre-Season (Feb–Apr): Identity Re-Activation
Duration: 8–10 weeks
Purpose:Rebuild physical base and tactical structure through football. The Croatian approach views this as the teaching phase — the foundation of identity and tempo.
Belco focus:
- Physical: Progressive aerobic base through ball work.
- Technical: Repetition of core actions (first touch, Drive–Draw–Play, pressing reactions).
- Tactical: Establish Build & Control the Tempo, Compactness, and Create Superiority.
- Psychological: Renew group confidence and rhythm.
Example Sessions:
- 7v7 build-up and progression through thirds.
- Compact pressing games with 5-second recovery rules.
Outcome:Teams finish pre-season fit, connected, and tactically aligned — ready for match tempo.
3. Early-Season (Apr–Jun): Performance Stabilisation
Duration: 10–12 weeks
Purpose:Transfer learning into consistent match performance.
Croatian logic:Competition is part of learning — results matter less than clarity of idea.
Belco focus:
- Physical: Maintain rhythm, balance match load and recovery.
- Technical: Efficient execution under pressure.
- Tactical: Reinforce Open-Ball timing, Compactness, and Aggressive Recovery.
- Psychological: Stability — confidence through clarity, not control.
Example Sessions:
- 9v9 or 11v11 scenario work (return-ball and pressing triggers).
- Small-sided tactical games linking Create Superiority and Tempo Control.
Outcome:Players perform the Belco model naturally under match stress — identity becomes habit.
4. Late-Season (Jul–Sep): Refinement & Peak Performance
Duration: 10–12 weeks
Purpose:Reach tactical sharpness and psychological resilience while maintaining freshness.
Croatian logic:The final block refines, not reinvents — finish sharp, not heavy.
Belco focus:
- Physical: Reduced load; maintain intensity, manage recovery.
- Technical: Precision under fatigue.
- Tactical: Match management — tempo control, transitions, and compactness.
- Psychological: Competitive composure — emotional balance in decisive games.
Example Sessions:
- Tactical 9v9 games at match intensity.
- Pressing/transition scenarios under fatigue.
- Game management exercises: defending a lead, chasing late goals.
Outcome:Teams express full Belco identity under competitive tension — intelligent, composed, connected.

heading: 5.13 Maintaining Learning Cycles Within Competition
headingPath: Belconnen United FC – Player Development Curriculum (Master Document) > 5. Season Planning > 5.1 Annual Cycle (Macro) > 5.13 Maintaining Learning Cycles Within Competition
content:
Even during match-heavy blocks, Belco keeps learning alive through short meso cycles:
- Each 3–6 week block targets one or two pillars (e.g., Compactness & Aggressive Recovery, Build-Up & Create Superiority).
- Micro-cycles follow a weekly flow: early-week repetition → mid-week tactical scenario → weekend application → reflection.
- Match reviews directly feed the next week’s training theme.
This creates a constant learn → apply → reflect → repeat rhythm — the same progression used across Croatian academies.
5.14 Evolution of Load and Focus
Phase | Load Character | Tactical Depth | Key Learning Emphasis
Off-Season | Very low | Identity foundation | Connect, rebuild culture
Pre-Season | Building | Core principles | Identity, tempo, compactness
Early-Season | Moderate–High | Game understanding | Consistency, transitions
Late-Season | Moderate | Game management | Tempo control, resilience
Croatian philosophy defines intensity as intelligent rhythm management, not endless effort.Belco follows that model — build slow, sustain long, finish sharp.
Summary: The Belco Macro Cycle
The Belco year follows a rhythm of recovery → rebuild → reinforce → refine.Every phase keeps football at the centre — learning cycles never stop, and identity remains constant.
Phase | Core Purpose
Off-Season: Connect and unite. | 
Pre-Season: Re-establish identity. | 
Early-Season: Stabilise and apply. | 
Late-Season: Refine and perform. | 
Guiding Principle:
We don’t chase short-term peaks — we build long-term players.
Every session, every phase, every season strengthens the same football identity:
Compact, Brave, Intelligent — and Belco.

heading: 5.2 Six-Week Blocks (Meso)
headingPath: Belconnen United FC – Player Development Curriculum (Master Document) > 5. Season Planning > 5.2 Six-Week Blocks (Meso)
content:
Belco Hybrid Periodisation Model
Purpose
Belconnen United FC’s meso-cycle model blends Croatian tactical periodisation with Dutch rhythm-based conditioning, forming one unified system of football learning and performance management.
Age Phase | Primary Model | Purpose
U10–15 (Technical & Tactical Phases) | Croatian rhythm | Develop intelligence through repetition and variation.
U16+ (Professional Phase) | Dutch rhythm | Sustain performance through structured game-size load control.
Both share the same intent:
keep the ball central, the learning real, and the rhythm intelligent.
Belco Principle: Rhythm replaces rotation.
We don’t change topics to stay fresh — we change rhythm to deepen understanding.
The idea stays constant; the experience evolves.

heading: 5.21 Technical & Tactical Phases (U10–15): Croatian Rhythm
headingPath: Belconnen United FC – Player Development Curriculum (Master Document) > 5. Season Planning > 5.2 Six-Week Blocks (Meso) > 5.21 Technical & Tactical Phases (U10–15): Croatian Rhythm
content:
Model SummaryFor younger phases, Belco follows the Croatian developmental logic — learning waves, not fitness blocks.Each cycle rises, sustains, then refreshes.Cycle duration evolves with age:
- U10–13: 3-week cycles
- U14–15: 4–5 week cycles
Each block builds around one or two connected Belco pillars.
Stage | Weeks | Purpose | Tactical Focus | Load Regulation
Rise Phase | 1–2 | Reconnect players to key principles and increase rhythm. | Small-sided games (3v3–6v6) for duels, compactness, and tempo. | High repetition, short rest.
Sustain Phase | 2–3 | Apply principles at team-unit level and coordinate behaviours. | Medium games (6v6–8v8), positional play, transitions. | Cognitive and tactical peak.
Refresh Phase | Final week | Consolidate learning through open play. | Larger games (8v8–11v11), full-game realism. | Reduced load, tactical clarity.
Principle Connection
Each cycle links 2–3 Belco pillars to create an integrated learning picture:
- Build & Control the Tempo + Collective Compactness
- Create Superiority + Open-Ball Play
- Aggressive Recovery + Duel Mentality
Learning deepens through repetition with variation — the same principles appear across different games, spaces, and intensities.
Learning Emphasis by Age
Age Phase | Core Focus | Coach Role
U10–13 (Technical) | Learn the five pillars technically through game repetition. | Demonstrate, guide, and reinforce cues (“Find the spare man,” “Play when it’s open”).
U14–15 (Tactical) | Apply principles collectively with tempo and structure. | Question and connect actions (“When is the ball open?” “Where’s our compactness?”).
Load Management
- Load is managed through football design, not isolated fitness.
- Small games raise intensity; medium and large games manage rhythm.
- Recovery uses creative, low-density formats (e.g. bounce tournaments, small transition games).
Croatian Logic: Repeat the principle, change the picture.Development happens through rhythm, realism, and variation — not endless new topics.

heading: 5.22 Professional Phase (U16+): Dutch Rhythm (Verheijen-Inspired)
headingPath: Belconnen United FC – Player Development Curriculum (Master Document) > 5. Season Planning > 5.2 Six-Week Blocks (Meso) > 5.22 Professional Phase (U16+): Dutch Rhythm (Verheijen-Inspired)
content:
Model SummaryFrom U16 upward, Belco adopts a Dutch-style load rhythm — controlling physiological stress through game size and pitch density while maintaining tactical continuity.Every block lasts six weeks, following a deliberate big → medium → small game cycle.
Stage | Weeks | Game Type Focus | Physical Objective | Tactical Integration
Weeks 1–2 (Big Games) | 10v10–11v11 | Aerobic foundation, rhythm control | Build & Control the Tempo, Compactness | 
Weeks 3–4 (Medium Games) | 6v6–8v8 | Mixed aerobic–anaerobic, unit coordination | Create Superiority, Open-Ball Play, Aggressive Recovery | 
Weeks 5–6 (Small Games) | 3v3–5v5 | Anaerobic sharpness, high reaction speed | Duel Mentality, Counter-Pressing, Finishing | 
Seasonal Rhythm Alignment
Season Block | Rhythm Focus | Key Goals
Post-Season (Oct–Nov) | Big → Medium | Connection, identity reset, tempo reintroduction.
Pre-Season (Feb–Apr) | Full 6-week cycle (Big → Medium → Small) | Physical and tactical base.
Early-Season (Apr–Jun) | Medium → Big | Tactical stability, tempo management.
Late-Season (Jul–Sep) | Medium → Small → Big | Sharpness, freshness, and composure.
Dutch Logic: The field is the fitness.Load and performance are managed through game design — not running volumes.

heading: 5.23 Integration Across the Pathway
headingPath: Belconnen United FC – Player Development Curriculum (Master Document) > 5. Season Planning > 5.2 Six-Week Blocks (Meso) > 5.23 Integration Across the Pathway
content:
Phase | Model | Purpose | Rhythm Logic | Load Regulation | Game Type Balance
U10–13 (Technical) | Croatian | Build principle understanding through repetition and discovery. | Rise–Sustain–Refresh | Tactical realism via game space. | 60% small, 30% medium, 10% large
U14–15 (Tactical) | Croatian | Apply principles collectively and under pressure. | Rise–Sustain–Refresh | Cognitive tempo, tactical depth. | 40% small, 40% medium, 20% large
U16+ (Professional) | Dutch | Manage performance rhythm through game size. | Big–Medium–Small | Pitch density and match realism. | 30% small, 40% medium, 30% large
This ensures a single continuous pathway:
- Younger players learn intelligence through rhythm and variation.
- Older players sustain performance through rhythm and control.
Always football-first, always principle-driven.

heading: 5.24 Review and Transition Between Blocks
headingPath: Belconnen United FC – Player Development Curriculum (Master Document) > 5. Season Planning > 5.2 Six-Week Blocks (Meso) > 5.24 Review and Transition Between Blocks
content:
At the end of each meso-cycle, coaches:
- Evaluate principle transfer — are actions automatic?
- Review load and freshness — is rhythm balanced?
- Adjust game-type ratios, not football ideas.
Example:If counter-press intensity drops late in the block → next rhythm begins with small-sided transition games before reintroducing structure.
Summary: One Rhythm, Two Expressions
- Croatian rhythm creates intelligent, game-literate players.
- Dutch rhythm builds efficient, tactically stable performers.Both are united under the five Belco pillars — repeated, realistic, and football-centred.
Belco Identity
We teach rhythm, not routines.
We coach football, not fitness.
We train the idea — and the idea never changes.
Reflection: The Belco Periodisation Identity
Belco’s model is a deliberate synthesis of the world’s two most intelligent football schools.
From Croatia we inherit learning through rhythm — repetition, variation, decision.
From the Netherlands we adopt performance through rhythm — intensity, recovery, precision.
Together, they form a complete system:
- Young players learn through rhythm.
- Older players perform through rhythm.
It respects the player’s mind and body, built entirely through football realism.Belco trains the idea to build the player — then manages the rhythm to sustain performance.That balance defines who we are: intelligent football, lived with purpose and tempo.

heading: 5.3 Weekly Themes (Micro)
headingPath: Belconnen United FC – Player Development Curriculum (Master Document) > 5. Season Planning > 5.3 Weekly Themes (Micro)
content:
Purpose of the Micro Cycle
The micro cycle defines how Belconnen United FC manages rhythm, load, and learning inside each training week.
With two sessions plus a match, the goal is to:
- Maximise realism and repetition without overload.
- Maintain clear tactical and technical focus.
- Sustain a natural rhythm: train → refine → perform → recover.
This fits Belco’s hybrid periodisation model:
- U10–15: Croatian rhythm — repetition through variation, cognitive intensity, and progressive game size.
- U16+: Dutch rhythm — tempo and field-size management (big → medium → match) for performance balance.
Belco Philosophy:
Every week is football-shaped — learning happens through the game, not around it.

heading: 5.31 Technical Phase (U10–13): Croatian Learning Rhythm
headingPath: Belconnen United FC – Player Development Curriculum (Master Document) > 5. Season Planning > 5.3 Weekly Themes (Micro) > 5.31 Technical Phase (U10–13): Croatian Learning Rhythm
content:
Objective:Develop technical fluency and game intelligence through realistic football scenarios that connect Belco’s pillars —
Build & Control the Tempo, Create Superiority, Collective Compactness, Aggressive Recovery, and Duel Mentality.
Weekly Structure (2 Sessions + Match)
Day | Session Type | Game Format | Belco Pillar Emphasis | Load / Intensity | Rationale
Session 1 (Early Week) | Technical + Decision Learning | Small → Medium (3v3–7v7) | Create Superiority, Collective Compactness, Duel Mentality | Moderate–High | High-touch repetition in small spaces builds coordination, reactions, and cooperation habits.
Session 2 (Mid / Late Week) | Game Application / Match Prep | Medium → Large (7v7–9v9) | Build & Control the Tempo, Aggressive Recovery | Moderate | Reinforce team structure and tempo control; prepare match rhythm through positional realism.
Weekend Match | Game Performance & Transfer | Full Game | All pillars integrated | Variable | Match serves as the true learning environment — assess principle transfer and collective rhythm.
Rhythm Logic
- Early week → repetition and control (Croatian repetition).
- Midweek → structure and tempo rehearsal.
- Weekend → performance and reflection.
Croatian Method:
Repeat the idea through new pictures — not new topics.

heading: 5.32 Tactical Phase (U14–15): Croatian Tactical Rhythm
headingPath: Belconnen United FC – Player Development Curriculum (Master Document) > 5. Season Planning > 5.3 Weekly Themes (Micro) > 5.32 Tactical Phase (U14–15): Croatian Tactical Rhythm
content:
Objective:Strengthen tactical coordination, timing, and tempo management across units — how to build, press, and recover as one.
Weekly Structure (2 Sessions + Match)
Day | Session Type | Game Format | Belco Pillar Emphasis | Load / Intensity | Rationale
Session 1 (Early Week) | Reconnection + Tactical Learning | Small–Medium (4v4–8v8) | Build & Control the Tempo, Create Superiority, Collective Compactness | Moderate–High | Reconnect tactical habits after match; restore rhythm and line coordination.
Session 2 (Mid / Late Week) | Scenario Game / Match Preparation | Medium–Large (7v7–11v11) | Aggressive Recovery, Open-Ball Decision, Duel Mentality | Moderate | Apply pillars under match conditions — collective pressing, tempo recognition, compact attacking transitions.
Weekend Match | Game Performance | Full Game | All pillars integrated | Variable | Competitive transfer of the week’s principles — observe recognition and reaction speed.
Rhythm Logic
- Session 1 → rebuild rhythm and coordination.
- Session 2 → tactical realism and match readiness.
- Match → collective transfer and feedback loop.
Belco Cue:
“Same pillars, different pressures — learn it, apply it, live it.”

heading: 5.33 Professional Phase (U16+): Dutch Performance Rhythm
headingPath: Belconnen United FC – Player Development Curriculum (Master Document) > 5. Season Planning > 5.3 Weekly Themes (Micro) > 5.33 Professional Phase (U16+): Dutch Performance Rhythm
content:
Objective:Manage physical and tactical performance through intelligent game-size rhythm — big → medium → match — controlling load while maintaining identity.
Weekly Structure (2 Sessions + Match)
Day | Session Type | Game Format | Belco Pillar Emphasis | Load / Intensity | Rationale
Session 1 (Early Week) | Big Game / Tactical Recovery | 10v10–11v11 | Build & Control the Tempo, Collective Compactness | Moderate | Reset tactical rhythm and structure; aerobic recovery through controlled possession and build-up.
Session 2 (Mid / Late Week) | Medium–Small Games / Tactical Peak | 5v5–8v8 | Aggressive Recovery, Create Superiority, Duel Mentality | High | Peak anaerobic and cognitive load — pressing, transitions, and 1v1 sharpness.
Weekend Match | Match Performance | Full Game | All pillars integrated | Variable | Tactical and physical peak — control rhythm and tempo through Belco principles.
Rhythm Logic
- Early week → tactical recovery and rhythm restoration.
- Midweek → decision and reaction peak.
- Match → controlled execution and tempo management.
Dutch Method:
The field defines the fitness — tempo defines the load.

heading: 5.34 Integration Across the Belco System
headingPath: Belconnen United FC – Player Development Curriculum (Master Document) > 5. Season Planning > 5.3 Weekly Themes (Micro) > 5.34 Integration Across the Belco System
content:
Age Phase | Model Type | Weekly Focus | Game Format Rhythm | Load Control | Outcome
U10–13 (Technical) | Croatian Rhythm | Learning and repetition with variation | Small → Medium → Match | Game realism | Technical mastery, brave decision-making.
U14–15 (Tactical) | Croatian Rhythm | Tactical coordination and collective play | Medium → Large → Match | Cognitive tempo | Intelligent, compact team play.
U16+ (Professional) | Dutch Rhythm | Performance rhythm and tempo control | Big → Medium → Match | Field size and recovery balance | Tactical sharpness and resilience.
Each micro-cycle fits within the 3–6 week meso rhythm (rise → sustain → refresh), together forming the macro-seasonal flow aligned with the Australian football calendar.
The match defines the week — every session builds toward it and recovers from it.
Summary: Belco’s Weekly Rhythm
The Belco weekly model keeps learning football-based, efficient, and identity-driven.Two sessions are enough — if the rhythm, variation, and realism are right.
Model | Focus | Key Principles
Croatian Rhythm (U10–15) | Repetition with variation — small-to-large learning. | Build & Control the Tempo, Create Superiority, Compactness, Aggressive Recovery, Duel Mentality.
Dutch Rhythm (16+) | Performance through tempo and field rhythm. | Tempo Control, Compactness, Aggressive Recovery, Superiority, Duel Mentality.
Belco Standard:
Every week feels like football — intelligent rhythm, brave decisions, and total commitment to our identity.

heading: 5.4 Curriculum Map (All Phases – 4 Cycles Each)
headingPath: Belconnen United FC – Player Development Curriculum (Master Document) > 5. Season Planning > 5.4 Curriculum Map (All Phases – 4 Cycles Each)
content:
Belco’s season operates on an Australian calendar, divided into four development blocks.
Each block builds rhythm, connection, and tactical fluency through Belco’s Eight Principles of Play.
The curriculum maps below outline how those principles evolve across the season for each developmental phase.
Technical Development Phase (U10–13)
Croatian Rhythm: Repetition with variation. Tactical ideas introduced through small-sided realism. Emphasis on connection, decision-making, and technical execution under pressure.
Season Block | Duration | Core Belco Pillars Emphasised | Supporting Themes | Example Applications / Session Focus | Matchplay Integration / Outcome
Post-Season (Oct–Nov) | 4–6 wks | Duel Mentality, Collective Compactness | Team connection, small-group defending, confidence in possession | 3v3 / 4v4 competitive games with tight distances and quick transitions | Build connection and energy; identity formed through competition and teamwork.
Pre-Season (Feb–Apr) | 8–10 wks | Build & Control the Tempo, Create Superiority | Receiving and supporting angles, linking lines (Back → Mid → Front) | 5v5–7v7 rondos and directional games with build-up triggers | Players recognise rhythm shifts and learn to progress through the thirds intelligently.
Early Season (Apr–Jun) | 10–12 wks | Collective Compactness, Aggressive Recovery | Quick support after loss, regains in 5s, defensive shape | 6v6 positional or transition games with recovery rules | Team presses and reacts together — compact, organised, and quick to win the ball back.
Late Season (Jul–Sep) | 10–12 wks | Tempo Control, Duel Mentality | Managing rhythm, reacting under fatigue, finishing under pressure | 7v7–9v9 conditioned games – control tempo before final attack | Composed and brave under match pressure; rhythm awareness and competitive resilience show clearly.
Tactical Development Phase (U14–15)
Croatian Rhythm: Tactical understanding and coordinated movement. Learning through realism and linked principles. Greater emphasis on tempo, compactness, and pressing structure.
Season Block | Duration | Core Belco Pillars Emphasised | Supporting Themes | Example Applications / Session Focus | Matchplay Integration / Outcome
Post-Season (Oct–Nov) | 4–6 wks | Collective Compactness, Duel Mentality | Rebuild team structure, connection between lines, short distances | 5v5–7v7 games with compactness zones and recovery incentives | Reconnect tactically; team defends and competes together with purpose and unity.
Pre-Season (Feb–Apr) | 8–10 wks | Build & Control the Tempo, Create Superiority | Unit coordination, build-up rhythm, forward play on open-ball moments | 8v8–10v10 positional games with controlled tempo and overload creation | Identity re-established through composure, rhythm control, and coordinated progression.
Early Season (Apr–Jun) | 10–12 wks | Aggressive Recovery, Collective Compactness | High press organisation, counter-pressing, rolling-on defenders | 6v6–9v9 transition and pressing scenarios — regain within 5 seconds | Team presses collectively, maintains compact distances, and recovers quickly on loss.
Late Season (Jul–Sep) | 10–12 wks | Tempo Control, Duel Mentality | Game management, finishing rhythm, emotional control under fatigue | 8v8–11v11 scenario games — managing tempo shifts and decisive moments | Players show maturity — composure, bravery, and rhythm awareness in competitive situations.
Professional Phase (U16+)
Dutch Rhythm: Performance-driven periodisation using field size and tempo rhythm (big → medium → small). Tactical and physical preparation managed through structured game-size rotation while keeping Belco principles central.
Season Block | Duration | Core Belco Pillars Emphasised | Supporting Themes | Example Applications / Session Focus | Matchplay Integration / Outcome
Post-Season (Oct–Nov) | 4–6 wks | Collective Compactness, Duel Mentality | Identity reset, rhythm rebuilding, technical sharpness | 10v10 recovery games (aerobic rhythm) + 4v4 duels for reaction and competitiveness | Cohesion and intensity re-established; players reconnect to tactical shape and mentality.
Pre-Season (Feb–Apr) | 8–10 wks | Build & Control the Tempo, Aggressive Recovery | Tactical conditioning, system alignment, collective pressing and transition | 11v11 → 8v8 → 6v6 (big–medium–small) progression; conditioning through tactical play | Players achieve tactical clarity, pressing synchronisation, and full physical readiness.
Early Season (Apr–Jun) | 10–12 wks | Create Superiority, Build & Control the Tempo | Breaking lines, third-man actions, positional combinations | 8v8–10v10 tactical rotations; structured build-up and open-ball penetration | Consistent rhythm and composure; principles appear naturally under match intensity.
Late Season (Jul–Sep) | 10–12 wks | Tempo Control, Collective Compactness, Duel Mentality | Game management, recovery through control, resilience in fatigue | 8v8–11v11 tactical rehearsals, smaller pitches for tempo sharpness | Peak competitive performance — tactical maturity, tempo mastery, and psychological strength.
Summary: Building Rhythm, Reinforcing Identity
This Curriculum Map ensures Belco’s Eight Principles are revisited across all four season blocks — first learned technically, then applied tactically, and finally executed professionally.
- Croatian rhythm (U10–15): repetition with variation, tactical understanding before physical load.
- Dutch rhythm (16+): structured tempo and game-size rotation to sustain performance and freshness.
- Across all phases: Open Ball, Compactness, Overloads, Tempo Control, Pressing, and Duel Mentality remain central.
Each player experiences football through the same lens — intelligent rhythm, brave decisions, and complete connection between training and matchplay.
Belco Standard:
Our curriculum repeats the same ideas in new situations — rhythm builds understanding, identity builds performance.

heading: 5.5 Managing Load, Fatigue, Enjoyment, and Variety
headingPath: Belconnen United FC – Player Development Curriculum (Master Document) > 5. Season Planning > 5.5 Managing Load, Fatigue, Enjoyment, and Variety
content:
Belco Philosophy: Rhythm, Not Exhaustion
Belco’s load management approach follows the Croatian principle of rhythm and the Dutch method of football-based conditioning — we manage intensity through the game, not by separating football from fitness.
With two sessions and a match per week, the goal is to:
- Keep players fresh, focused, and competitive, not fatigued.
- Maintain consistent football rhythm (learn → rehearse → perform).
- Use pitch size, tempo, and task design to regulate load naturally.
Belco Standard: “We build fitness through football — tempo, space, and decisions.”

heading: 5.51 Load Regulation Through Game Design
headingPath: Belconnen United FC – Player Development Curriculum (Master Document) > 5. Season Planning > 5.5 Managing Load, Fatigue, Enjoyment, and Variety > 5.51 Load Regulation Through Game Design
content:
Belco coaches manage physical and cognitive load by adjusting the game itself:
Adjustment Lever | Effect on Load / Intensity | Practical Example
Pitch Size | Bigger pitch = aerobic rhythm; smaller pitch = anaerobic and cognitive sharpness | 10v10 (big) early week → 6v6 (small) midweek
Player Numbers | Fewer players = higher engagement; more players = lower decision density | 3v3 for duels; 9v9 for shape and tempo
Duration & Rests | Shorter games with minimal rest = intensity; longer games with guided recovery = rhythm | 4x3min high-pressing games vs. 2x10min positional play
Constraints | Tactical rules create mental load and control effort | Condition “must press within 5s” or “score from assist zone”
Ball Tempo | Coaches use tempo cues — speed up when open-ball appears, slow when compact | Reinforces Tempo Control and Open-Ball principles
These levers allow Belco coaches to shape the session’s intensity, realism, and fatigue profile without external running or conditioning drills.

heading: 5.52 Weekly Load Rhythm (2 + 1 Structure)
headingPath: Belconnen United FC – Player Development Curriculum (Master Document) > 5. Season Planning > 5.5 Managing Load, Fatigue, Enjoyment, and Variety > 5.52 Weekly Load Rhythm (2 + 1 Structure)
content:
Session | Focus | Load Type | Purpose
Session 1 (Early Week) | Learning and repetition | Moderate–High (cognitive + technical) | Rebuild tempo and structure; high decision volume, small–medium games.
Session 2 (Mid / Late Week) | Tactical rehearsal and match readiness | Moderate (tactical + tempo rhythm) | Prepare the team physically and mentally for match play; big–medium formats.
Match Day | Application and transfer | Variable (competitive intensity) | Real expression of principles; performance is the output of rhythm.
Load naturally rises and falls through the week — no extra conditioning is required.

heading: 5.53. Detecting and Managing Fatigue
headingPath: Belconnen United FC – Player Development Curriculum (Master Document) > 5. Season Planning > 5.5 Managing Load, Fatigue, Enjoyment, and Variety > 5.53. Detecting and Managing Fatigue
content:
Croatian methodology views fatigue as both physical and cognitive.Belco coaches monitor both through behaviour, tempo, and body language.
Signs of Fatigue:
- Loss of decision clarity (slow reactions to Open Ball).
- Reduced communication or pressing speed.
- Increased technical errors in basic actions.
Coaching Adjustments:
- Reduce pitch size or duration to manage load.
- Use positional or transition games instead of full-intensity formats.
- End sessions with low-pressure finishing or 3v3 duels to restore enjoyment.
- Allow more rest or walkthrough tempo during tactical review.
Belco Cue: “Fatigue shows in decisions before it shows in legs.”

heading: 5.54. Keeping Sessions Enjoyable and Competitive
headingPath: Belconnen United FC – Player Development Curriculum (Master Document) > 5. Season Planning > 5.5 Managing Load, Fatigue, Enjoyment, and Variety > 5.54. Keeping Sessions Enjoyable and Competitive
content:
Enjoyment drives engagement — and engagement builds learning speed.Belco coaches are expected to keep intensity competitive but joyful.
Strategies:
- Use scoring and reward systems (points for pressing wins, assists from cut-backs).
- Keep game flow high — fewer stoppages, more guided feedback between rounds.
- Include duel-based competitions regularly (defending and attacking).
- End sessions with purposeful games (4v4, 6v6) to anchor energy and emotion.
- Celebrate individual bravery — players who act decisively in Open Ball or Duel moments.
Croatian academies emphasise intrinsic motivation through challenge — games that make players think, compete, and enjoy learning.

heading: 5.55. Maintaining Long-Term Variety
headingPath: Belconnen United FC – Player Development Curriculum (Master Document) > 5. Season Planning > 5.5 Managing Load, Fatigue, Enjoyment, and Variety > 5.55. Maintaining Long-Term Variety
content:
Belco variety comes from contextual design, not random change.
The same principle is repeated through fresh problems:
Belco Principle | Example Variations Across the Year
Build Through Thirds | 7v7 positional build-up → 9v9 under pressure → 11v11 tactical rehearsal
Overloads (Drive–Draw–Play) | 3v2 → 4v3 → 6v4 with moving zones
Compactness | 4v4 + 2 floaters (small) → 7v7 block (medium) → 11v11 defensive line control
Assist Zone | 4v4 wide-channel game → 6v6 attacking shape → full 11v11 chance creation
Pressing | 5v5 transition → 7v7 traps → match scenario with pressing triggers
Repetition through variation = identity through freshness.

heading: 5.56. Protecting Performance and Joy
headingPath: Belconnen United FC – Player Development Curriculum (Master Document) > 5. Season Planning > 5.5 Managing Load, Fatigue, Enjoyment, and Variety > 5.56. Protecting Performance and Joy
content:
Belco’s rhythm protects both the body and the mind.
- Load is managed inside the football, not outside it.
- Fatigue is treated as feedback, not failure.
- Enjoyment is the fuel for consistency, not the distraction.
- Variety comes from how the same ideas are explored differently every week.
Belco Summary:Our program trains smart, not heavy.
Rhythm replaces fatigue.
Freshness keeps identity alive.
Every session should end with energy — players walking off thinking, “Can’t wait for the next one.”
• Integration of Matchplay Learning
This bridges the philosophy and day-to-day coaching.

heading: 6. Game Model Detail (part 1)
headingPath: Belconnen United FC – Player Development Curriculum (Master Document) > 6. Game Model Detail
content:
Perfect. We’ll build this cleanly, deliberately, and Croatian in logic — not rushing into prose before the spine is right.
We’ll do this in layers, exactly how Croatian curricula are built.
Chapter 6 – Belco Game Model
(Draft – Structural Build)
6.1 Purpose of the Game Model
The Belco Game Model defines how our teams behave in matches — with and without the ball — across all age phases.
It is not a system, formation, or pattern book.It is a shared reference picture that connects:
- our principles of play (Chapter 2),
- our player development phases (Chapter 3),
- our training methodology (Chapter 4),
- and our season planning logic (Chapter 5).
The Belco Game Model ensures that:
- all teams pursue the same football idea,
- behaviours mature with age rather than change,
- players arrive at senior football already fluent in the model.
The idea stays the same.The execution matures.
6.2 The Belco Game Model – Overview
Belco football is organised around:
- Four moments of the game
- One identity
- Three developmental expressions
The Four Moments
- In Possession (BP)
- Out of Possession (BPO)
- Transition: BP → BPO (loss)
- Transition: BPO → BP (regain)
What Never Changes
Across all ages, Belco teams aim to:
- Control tempo through intelligent possession
- Create superiority rather than force play
- Stay compact and connected
- React aggressively to transitions
- Compete with courage and clarity
What Evolves
What evolves is:
- speed of recognition
- coordination between units
- decision efficiency
- game management responsibility
This progression is captured in Table 6A, which forms the backbone of this chapter.
6.3 Table 6A – Belco Game Model Progression
This table defines what Belco football looks like in each moment of the game, and how that behaviour matures from Technical → Tactical → Professional phases.
This is a reference tool, not a checklist.It answers one question:
“What should we recognise and try to do in this moment, at this age?”
Table 6A: Game Model Progression
IN POSSESSION (BP)
Phase | Behavioural Intent | What We Prioritise | What Success Looks Like
Technical Phase (U10–13) | Learn to play with the ball, not around it | • Playing through thirds in small formats | 
• Bravery to receive, dribble, and combine |  |  | 
• Early recognition of open ball |  |  | 
• Supporting close to the ball | • Players want the ball under pressure |  | 
• Ball moves with purpose, not panic |  |  | 
• Forward play appears naturally when space opens |  |  | 
• Team stays connected behind the ball |  |  | 
Tactical Phase (U14–15) | Learn when and why to progress | • Structured build-up through units | 
• Creating overloads through movement |  |  | 
• Timing runs on open-ball moments |  |  | 
• Tempo variation (circulate → accelerate) | • Team progresses through thirds with control |  | 
• Players recognise open / closed ball cues |  |  | 
• Overloads are created, not accidental |  |  | 
• Possession serves positional and tactical purpose |  |  | 
Professional Phase (U16+) | Control the game through possession | • Manipulating tempo deliberately | 
• Sustaining positional superiority |  |  | 
• Accessing assist zones with coordination |  |  | 
• Decision efficiency under pressure | • Team dictates rhythm of the match |  | 
• Progression is patient or vertical by choice |  |  | 
• Attacks arrive with structure and balance |  |  | 
• Possession protects and creates simultaneously |  |  | 
OUT OF POSSESSION (BPO)
Phase | Behavioural Intent | What We Prioritise | What Success Looks Like
Technical Phase (U10–13) | Learn to defend together, not chase | • Staying close and compact | 
• Immediate reaction to loss |  |  | 
• 1v1 engagement with recovery effort |  |  | 
• Delaying rather than diving in | • Team stays connected behind the ball |  | 
• Players react quickly after loss |  |  | 
• Duels are competed for with intent |  |  | 
• Opponent progression is slowed |  |  | 
Tactical Phase (U14–15) | Defend as a coordinated unit | • Compactness between lines | 
• Pressing on triggers, not emotion |  |  | 
• Cover and balance behind the press |  |  | 
• Protecting central corridors | • Pressing actions are synchronised |  | 
• Team shifts together laterally and vertically |  |  | 
• Central space is protected consistently |  |  | 
• Opponent is forced wide or backward |  |  | 
Professional Phase (U16+) | Control space and rhythm without the ball | • Organised pressing schemes | 
• Line coordination and rest defence |  |  | 
• Trigger-based aggression |  |  | 
• Managing defensive tempo | • Team dictates where opponent can play |  | 
• Pressing traps are executed collectively |  |  | 
• Compactness is maintained under fatigue |  |  | 
• Defensive actions protect transitions |  |  | 
TRANSITION: BP → BPO (Loss of Possession)
Phase | Behavioural Intent | What We Prioritise | What Success Looks Like
Technical Phase (U10–13) | React instantly after loss | • Immediate pressure on the ball | 
• Nearest players engage |  |  | 
• Simple recovery runs |  |  | 
• Emotional reset | • Players react without hesitation |  | 
• Ball carrier is delayed or forced error |  |  | 
• Team regains shape quickly |  |  | 
Tactical Phase (U14–15) | Decide press or recover | • Counter-press with nearest three | 
• Collapse space around the ball |  |  | 
• Recover compactly if press fails | • Team either wins ball quickly or regains shape |  | 
• Transitions are short and controlled |  |  | 
• No emotional chasing |  |  |

heading: 6. Game Model Detail (part 2)
headingPath: Belconnen United FC – Player Development Curriculum (Master Document) > 6. Game Model Detail
content:
Professional Phase (U16+) | Kill or control transitions | • Collective counter-pressing | 
• Rest defence protection |  |  | 
• Risk management based on game state | • Opposition counters are stopped early |  | 
• Team controls chaos moments |  |  | 
• Tempo is restored quickly |  |  | 
TRANSITION: BPO → BP (Regain)
Phase | Behavioural Intent | What We Prioritise | What Success Looks Like
Technical Phase (U10–13) | Look forward if free | • Head up after regain | 
• Simple forward action if open |  |  | 
• Secure if pressured | • Players attempt to attack space |  | 
• Ball is not cleared blindly |  |  | 
• Confidence after regain |  |  | 
Tactical Phase (U14–15) | Decide in the first seconds | • First pass forward when possible | 
• Support runs ahead of the ball |  |  | 
• Secure possession if opponent is set | • Team counters with structure |  | 
• Or calmly resets shape |  |  | 
• No rushed decisions |  |  | 
Professional Phase (U16+) | Manage momentum | • Secure or strike decision-making | 
• Tempo control after regain |  |  | 
• Positional reorganisation | • Team exploits disorganisation |  | 
• Or slows the game intelligently |  |  | 
• Momentum stays under control |  |  | 
Perfect — we’ll keep momentum and build the rest of Chapter 6 cleanly and consistently.You can read it fresh tomorrow as a complete, flowing section.
I’ll continue in the same Croatian logic + Belco voice you’ve established.
6. Game Model Detail
This chapter explains how Belco teams behave in real match moments.It translates our principles into observable behaviours, aligned to the senior model and scaled across the development phases.
The Game Model is not about systems or formations.It is about recognition, decision, and collective action.
6.4 In Possession (BP): Build-Up, Creation, Finishing
Belco’s in-possession model is built on control with intent.
We don’t keep the ball to look good.We use possession to:
- manipulate the opponent,
- create superiority,
- control tempo,
- and arrive in the final third with structure.
Core In-Possession Principles
- Build & Control the Tempo
- Create Superiority
- Compact Support Behind the Ball
- Open-Ball Recognition
- Duel Mentality in Attacking Actions
6.41 Build-Up: From Back to Middle Third
Belco intention: progress through the thirds with clarity, not speed.
Non-negotiables (all phases):
- Structure before acceleration
- Support underneath the ball
- Goalkeeper involved as a footballer
- Calm circulation until the open-ball moment appears
Phase Expression
U10–13 (Technical Phase)
- Build in small formats (3v3–5v5).
- Teach “Back → Mid → Front” as a habit, not a rule.
- Encourage centre players to receive under pressure.
- Allow dribbling to fix opponents (Drive–Draw–Play).
Success looks like:Players ask for the ball, recycle calmly, and play forward when space opens — not because they’re told, but because they see it.
U14–15 (Tactical Phase)
- Introduce clear build-up structure (pivot, width, depth).
- Create numerical or positional superiority in the first line.
- Teach when to circulate vs when to break lines.
- Recognise open / closed / return-ball cues.
Success looks like:The team progresses through thirds with control, not bypassing midfield under pressure.
U16+ (Professional Phase)
- Manipulate opponent press through tempo and positioning.
- Use rest defence to protect against transitions.
- Adjust build-up based on opposition shape and match state.
- Build patience into pressure moments.
Success looks like:The team dictates rhythm from the back and never looks rushed or disconnected.
6.42 Creation: Middle Third Play & Open-Ball Moments
Belco intention: create advantage before attempting penetration.
The middle third is where Belco football becomes visible.This is where we:
- provoke pressure,
- create overloads,
- and accelerate only when conditions are right.
Key concepts:
- Open Ball = trigger to attack
- Overloads through movement, not standing positions
- Third-man and bounce play
- Tempo variation
Phase Expression
U10–13
- Encourage freedom and movement.
- Teach players to scan and support the ball.
- Reward forward actions when the ball carrier faces forward.
- Keep distances short to enable quick combinations.
U14–15
- Introduce rotation and third-man logic.
- Teach how to create +1 centrally or wide.
- Use positional games to reinforce spacing and timing.
- Begin linking midfield behaviour to forward runs.
U16+
- Manipulate defensive blocks through coordinated movement.
- Recognise when to fix defenders and release runners.
- Control rhythm through pauses and accelerations.
- Use positioning to prepare defensive security behind the ball.
6.43 Finishing: Final Third & Assist Zone Behaviour
Belco intention: arrive with structure, finish with control.
We do not attack the final third with chaos.We value:
- assist zones,
- cut-backs,
- second-line arrivals,
- and calm finishing under pressure.
Non-negotiables:
- At least one underneath the ball
- Multiple runners attacking different lines
- Decisions over desperation
Phase Expression
U10–13
- Encourage varied finishing (both feet).
- Introduce byline and pull-back concepts naturally.
- Reward bravery and repeated attempts.
- Keep finishing grounded and game-real.
U14–15
- Teach timing of runs into the box.
- Introduce second-line finishing and blind-side movement.
- Combine quickly in tight areas.
- Link finishing to prior build-up behaviour.
U16+
- Coordinate attacking structure across the front five.

heading: 6. Game Model Detail (part 3)
headingPath: Belconnen United FC – Player Development Curriculum (Master Document) > 6. Game Model Detail
content:
- Attack assist zones deliberately.
- Manage risk vs reward based on match context.
- Finish with composure — “small touch, big finish.”
6.5 Out of Possession (BPO): Pressing, Compactness, Organisation
Belco teams defend forward.
We don’t sit and wait.We organise, squeeze space, and decide when to press with collective intent.
Core Defensive Principles
- Collective Compactness
- Pressing on Triggers
- Protection of Central Corridor
- Duel Mentality with Cover and Balance
6.51 Pressing Philosophy
Belco intention: make the opponent predictable.
Pressing is not about running — it’s about timing and coordination.
Triggers we coach:
- Closed ball
- Return ball
- Bad touch
- Sideline isolation
Phase Expression
U10–13
- Press the ball together.
- Teach recovery runs and effort.
- Emphasise staying close rather than chasing.
- Reward immediate reaction after loss.
U14–15
- Introduce pressing roles (1st, 2nd, 3rd defender).
- Coordinate front and midfield lines.
- Teach when not to press.
- Emphasise compact distances.
U16+
- Execute pressing traps deliberately.
- Protect rest defence behind the press.
- Manage pressing intensity across the match.
- Adjust pressing height based on game state.
6.52 Compactness & Defensive Organisation
Belco intention: control space, not chase the ball.
Compactness is the foundation of:
- effective pressing,
- transition control,
- and defensive resilience.
Key ideas:
- Vertical and horizontal distances matter.
- The team moves as one unit.
- Central spaces are protected first.
Phase Expression
U10–13
- “Stay close, defend together.”
- Small pitches to teach natural compactness.
- Encourage communication and effort.
U14–15
- Teach line distances.
- Introduce shifting as a unit.
- Protect the central corridor consistently.
U16+
- Synchronise all lines.
- Defend forward with organised rest defence.
- Maintain compactness under fatigue and pressure.
6.6 Transitions: The Decisive Moments
Croatian methodology views transitions as the truth moments of football.
Belco places huge emphasis here because:
- transitions reveal decision speed,
- emotional control,
- and team connection.
6.61 Transition: BP → BPO (Loss)
Belco intention: react before the opponent settles.
- First action is always forward.
- Nearest players engage immediately.
- If regain fails, shape is restored quickly.
Progression:
- U10–13: react and chase together
- U14–15: counter-press or recover intelligently
- U16+: kill the transition or control it
6.62 Transition: BPO → BP (Regain)
Belco intention: exploit disorder or secure control.
The first seconds after regain define the moment.
Decision:
- Strike if space exists
- Secure if opponent is organised
Progression:
- U10–13: look forward if free
- U14–15: support runs and decision clarity
- U16+: manage momentum and tempo
6.7 Set Pieces: Belco Philosophy
Belco treats set pieces as:
- organised moments, not rehearsed theatre.
Guiding Principles
- Structure before creativity
- Clarity of role
- Aggression in duels
- Readiness for second balls and transitions
Coaching Focus
- Simple routines
- Clear triggers
- Collective responsibility
- Defensive readiness after attack
Set pieces reinforce:
- Duel Mentality
- Compactness
- Transition awareness
They are trained inside football rhythm, not isolated blocks.

heading: 7. Evaluation & Progression (part 1)
headingPath: Belconnen United FC – Player Development Curriculum (Master Document) > 7. Evaluation & Progression
content:
7.1 Why Evaluation Matters in a Development Curriculum
In a true player development environment, evaluation is not an endpoint — it is a guiding mechanism.
At Belconnen United FC, evaluation exists to help us understand:
- how players are learning,
- how effectively our football ideas are being absorbed,
- and whether our environment is supporting long-term development.
This reflects the Croatian view that development cannot be separated from observation.Players do not develop in straight lines, and progress cannot be reduced to match results or short-term performance indicators.
Instead, evaluation must answer deeper questions:
- Is the player understanding the game more clearly?
- Are decisions improving under pressure?
- Is behaviour becoming more consistent across different situations?
In this curriculum, evaluation is therefore positioned as a continuous, reflective process, embedded in daily coaching practice rather than a formal grading system.
7.2 Croatian Development Logic: Evaluation as Observation
In Croatian methodology, evaluation is rarely presented as a standalone process.It is woven into coaching itself.
Coaches are trained to observe:
- what players recognise,
- how they respond to changing game situations,
- and whether correct actions repeat when conditions become more demanding.
This approach prioritises:
- behaviour over outcome,
- decision quality over execution perfection,
- learning readiness over chronological age.
Belco’s curriculum adopts this same logic, but articulates it more explicitly so that:
- coaches with different backgrounds share the same reference point,
- expectations remain aligned across age groups,
- and progression decisions are made with clarity and consistency.
Evaluation, in this sense, is not something done to players — it is something coaches do with attention and intent as part of the learning process.
7.3 Evaluating the Player: Behaviour, Not Labels
Player evaluation within the Belco curriculum focuses on what the player does in the game, not what we call them.
Rather than categorising players by roles, positions, or outcomes, we observe how they behave across the four moments of the game:
- in possession,
- out of possession,
- during transition to attack,
- and during transition to defence.
Across all phases, evaluation considers four interconnected dimensions:
- technical behaviour under pressure,
- tactical recognition and decision-making,
- physical expression within football actions,
- and psychological response to challenge, error, and competition.
These dimensions are never viewed in isolation.They are always interpreted inside the game context and through the lens of Belco’s principles.
Technical Development Phase (U10–13)
In the technical phase, evaluation is centred on confidence and fluency.
The key concern is not whether actions are successful, but whether players:
- want the ball,
- attempt solutions under pressure,
- and show increasing comfort solving football problems through technique.
Mistakes are expected and accepted.A player who repeatedly attempts to receive, dribble, or combine — even imperfectly — is often developing faster than one who avoids involvement.
The Croatian question at this stage is simple:
Is the player becoming braver and more coordinated within the game?
Tactical Development Phase (U14–15)
As players enter the tactical phase, evaluation shifts toward understanding and application.
Coaches begin to observe whether players:
- recognise key cues such as open and closed ball,
- adjust positioning in relation to teammates,
- and apply principles consistently within team structure.
Here, evaluation is less about individual moments and more about pattern recognition over time:
- Are decisions improving?
- Is timing becoming more refined?
- Does the player understand their role within the collective idea?
This phase represents the transition from instinctive play to intentional football thinking.
Professional Development Phase (U16+)
In the professional phase, evaluation focuses on consistency and control.
Players are assessed on their ability to:
- manage tempo,
- execute decisions efficiently under pressure,
- and maintain performance standards across full matches and demanding contexts.
At this stage, Croatian methodology expects players to:
- self-regulate,
- communicate effectively,
- and contribute to the collective organisation of the team.
The defining question becomes:
Can the player influence the game responsibly, not just express themselves within it?
7.4 Evaluating the Team: Expression of Identity
While individual development remains central, the Belco curriculum also evaluates team behaviour as a reflection of shared understanding.
Teams are not judged primarily on results, but on how clearly they express the game model:
- Does the team build with structure?
- Is compactness visible without the ball?
- Are transitions managed with clarity and intent?
- Does the team recognise when to accelerate and when to control rhythm?
This perspective allows coaches to distinguish between:
- teams that are progressing but still inconsistent,
- and teams that are winning without expressing the intended football identity.
This distinction is critical in protecting long-term development.
7.5 The Role of Data and Performance Indicators
Where performance data is available, it is used carefully and contextually.
In Croatian methodology, data never replaces observation.It serves only to:
- support reflection,

heading: 7. Evaluation & Progression (part 2)
headingPath: Belconnen United FC – Player Development Curriculum (Master Document) > 7. Evaluation & Progression
content:
- raise questions,
- or confirm patterns already seen in the game.
At Belco, this means:
- data is secondary to football behaviour,
- physical metrics are interpreted in relation to tactical demands,
- and statistics are never used in isolation to judge players or teams.
The game remains the primary source of truth.
7.6 Coach Reflection as a Development Tool
Evaluation within the Belco curriculum extends beyond players to include coaching practice itself.
Croatian coaching culture places strong emphasis on reflection:
- How was the learning environment designed?
- Did players have to think, or simply follow?
- Were interventions timely and purposeful?
- Did the session connect to match reality?
Belco coaches are expected to engage in this same reflective process, using the curriculum as a reference point rather than a script.
This ensures that development remains:
- intentional,
- aligned,
- and responsive to the needs of players.
7.7 Progression Between Phases
Progression through the Belco pathway is based on developmental readiness, not age alone.
Movement between phases considers:
- technical security under pressure,
- speed of tactical recognition,
- psychological resilience,
- and physical capacity expressed within football actions.
When progression is managed correctly:
- players arrive prepared,
- expectations feel familiar,
- and learning continues without disruption.
This mirrors the Croatian belief that development should feel continuous, not segmented.
7.8 Closing Perspective: Measuring What Truly Matters
This curriculum measures development not by trophies or tables, but by clarity of behaviour over time.
Progress is visible when:
- players need fewer instructions,
- decisions improve under pressure,
- teams express shared principles instinctively,
- and the game begins to manage itself.
In that sense, evaluation is not an external judgment — it is a mirror held up to our environment.
If the football looks more intelligent, more connected, and more controlled year after year, the curriculum is working.
That is the Croatian standard.That is the Belco aim.

heading: 8.1 Frameworks
headingPath: Belconnen United FC – Player Development Curriculum (Master Document) > 8. Supporting Resources > 8.1 Frameworks
content:
Frameworks are one-page reference tools that define and simplify Belco’s key principles of play.
Each framework explains what the concept means, why it matters, and how it looks on the pitch.
They translate complex tactical ideas into clear, coachable language — outlining player behaviours, key coaching messages, and example training applications.
Together, these frameworks act as a bridge between the Belco Curriculum and the training ground, ensuring that every coach, across every phase, speaks the same football language.
Each framework defines player behaviours, coaching messages, and training examples that bring the Belco identity to life.
Coaches use these one-pagers to connect daily sessions to the long-term playing philosophy — ensuring consistent language and learning across all phases.
Belco Framework Index
In Possession Principles
Theme | Purpose | Core Connection
Open Ball | Identify and exploit moments when a player has time and space to drive or penetrate. | Foundation of Belco attacking identity.
Overloads | Create numerical or positional advantages to progress or break lines. | Links to Building Through the Thirds and Drive–Draw–Play.
Building Through the Thirds | Develop composure and structure in progressing from back to front. | Reinforces positional discipline and freedom within structure.
Drive–Draw–Play | Encourage players to commit defenders before releasing the ball to the free player. | Central to decision-making and creating advantages.
Cut Backs / Assist Zone | Enter the “gold zone” to create high-value scoring chances through low cut-backs. | Attacking efficiency; modern alternative to traditional crossing.
Level and Outside | Maintain width and horizontal balance, providing options to face forward or stretch the opponent. | Supports Open Ball and Overloads.
Control the Tempo | Manage game rhythm through intelligent possession — when to accelerate, when to retain. | Tactical maturity; linked to senior control principles.
Out of Possession Principles
Theme | Purpose | Core Connection
Compactness | Maintain minimal vertical and horizontal gaps to reduce opponent space. | Core defensive identity; foundation for pressing traps.
First Defender | Establish immediate pressure on the ball, dictating direction and intensity. | Triggers collective defending.
Duel Mentality | Win individual battles — physically, technically, mentally. | Defines Belco’s competitive character; “one-v-one all over the park.”
Pressing | Collective high or mid-block pressure to regain possession in advanced zones. | Links to Drive–Draw–Play through aggressive mentality and positional courage.
Closed Ball |  | 
Return Ball |  | 
Transition Principles
Theme | Purpose | Core Connection
Counter Pressing | Regain possession immediately after loss to sustain pressure and control territory. | Connected to Compactness and Duel Mentality.
Secure or Counter |  |
